information:

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Cumming testified that on February 26th, around 10:30 PM, he was stopped by a group of 7-8 men he believed to be a press-gang. They surrounded and hustled him, and immediately after, he discovered his watch was missing from his pocket.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Upon his arrest, prisoner Smith produced a paper stating he was authorized by a Mr. Whiteway to press men. Smith was described as the master of the gang.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Sarah Willoughby, Smith's landlady, testified that Smith was at home very soon after ten o'clock on the Wednesday night of the robbery and did not go out again.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Gilbert testified that the prisoner Ennis was at his house from about half past nine until about half past ten on the night of the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The victim, William Cumming, stated he did 'not know any thing of the other prisoner,' referring to Thomas Brewer. The only evidence linking Brewer was the watchman seeing him flee with Ennis.

Description: Officer John Armstrong testified that on March 6th, he found Richard Blakesley and Ann Hale in a room in Gloucester-court, along with accomplice William Wright and two other men.

Description: Officer Armstrong found more of the stolen goods, including the carpet and another bed, in a different room in Red-lion-court.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Christian Gayner, the prosecutor's wife, testified that on Monday morning, November 17th, she discovered a bed, bolster, and blankets were missing from a locked ground-floor room. She had last seen the items the previous night.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mrs. Gayner positively identified the bed, blankets, and bolster recovered from the prisoner by the watchman as the property she had lost from her house.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mrs. Gayner testified that the room from which the items were stolen was locked at 11 p.m. In the morning, she found the door half open with a different key in the lock, suggesting a break-in.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The prisoner's defense was that a man offered him one shilling to carry the bed, and that this man ran away when the watchman appeared.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Mrs. Gayner testified that the prisoner lodged in the house, on a different floor from where the theft occurred. This suggests he had legitimate access to the building's common areas.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: James Kennedy, an officer, testified that although the house had two doors (one common to lodgers), it was legally considered a single dwelling house where the landlord resided, which is a requirement for a burglary charge.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The prisoner's uncle, Joseph Austin, testified that the prisoner was a shoemaker and he had never heard anything against his character before.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prisoner's uncle also testified that Mrs. Gayner's house was 'full of bad women' and that the prisoner had been there with them.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The final value of the stolen goods was assessed as 39 shillings, just under the threshold for a capital offense.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Two other witnesses, aside from his uncle, were called and gave the prisoner a good character.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On December 9th, around 6 p.m., William-Allen Thackthwaite and Mr. Lifford were stopped in their chaise on the Uxbridge-road by a single highwayman on horseback. The man presented a pistol, demanded their money, and subsequently took Mr. Thackthwaite's metal watch, steel chain, and key.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mr. Thackthwaite testified that it was very dark, and as a result, he could not swear to the identity of the man who robbed him. Mr. Lifford gave similar testimony, stating he could not distinguish the person of the man.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Daniel Griffiths, a Bow-street patrolman, testified that around midnight on December 9th, he and his partner apprehended the prisoner, William Cross, at the Hammersmith turnpike-gate. Griffiths felt a pistol in the prisoner's coat pocket and hauled him off his horse.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: In his defense, William Cross claimed that he had gone to Hounslow to seek a job. While there, he went to the necessary (toilet) and found the watch and pistol tied up in a handkerchief.

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: John Powell, a customs tide-waiter, testified that his entry book showed 126 bags of coffee were delivered from the Minerva into the lighter.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Peter Dewte, an eyewitness on board the Minerva, testified that he saw the prisoner take a full bag from the lighter, sling it with ropes, and with the help of a waterman, place it into a wherry.

[EVIDENCE ev10]

[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: John Chamberlayne, who had management of the Timothy lighter, testified that the prisoner was in his employ at that time.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George Laport, the victim, testified that his watch went missing around 5 p.m. at Hendon fair. He noted there was a great deal of bustling, crowding, and pushing at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The witness Mr. Gearish saw the chain of Laport's watch hanging out and warned him to take care of it just five minutes before Laport discovered it was missing.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mr. Gearish testified that he observed the prisoner, Thomas Houghton, standing next to Mr. Laport for five minutes, continuing until just two minutes before Laport missed his watch.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Two key witnesses, Wicks, who helped detain the prisoner, and Bumby, who allegedly found the watch on the prisoner, were bound over to testify but were absent from the trial.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prisoner claimed in his defense that the watch was not found on him, but rather that 'a person outside of the crowd held up the watch.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Henry Lovett, an officer who took the prisoner into custody, testified, 'I saw a countryman, who had searched his pocket, give it into the hand of Mr. Gearish.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: George Laport positively identified the recovered watch as his property by its seal and specific markings.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The prisoner called seven witnesses who gave him a good character.

The theft allegedly occurred after Foster lured the boy into a public house, the Six Cans and Punch-Bowl, under the pretense of buying him a drink, then sent him on an errand and absconded with the box.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Judge, the owner of the stolen property, testified that he sent the boy, Thomas Sansum, to retrieve a box of his clothes on Saturday, August 8th.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: John Sweet, the publican, positively identified the prisoner, stating, 'I am positive it was the prisoner.' He confirmed seeing Foster and the boy enter with the box.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Sweet also testified that he saw the prisoner go out, saying he would 'come in again immediately,' just before the boy returned to find his box gone.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Richard Jones, a customer in the pub, corroborated the testimonies of both Sansum and Sweet, stating he saw the prisoner and the boy bring the box in, and saw the prisoner send the boy for cakes before leaving.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Ann Huddy testified that the prisoner, Mr. Foster, sent for her and asked her to pledge the stolen items for him, which she did, believing they were his own.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Thomas Judge positively identified the pawned items as his property, stating that most of them were marked with his name.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: John Clarke, an officer, testified that upon searching the prisoner, he found 'an amazing quantity of duplicates' (pawn tickets).
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Officer Clarke took a shirt from the prisoner's person at the time of his arrest, which Mr. Judge later identified as his own stolen property.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner claimed, 'They were sent to me from Bristol by a man who owed me some money.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On September 10th, the prisoner, John-Webb Elkington, came to the shop of William Molloy, a shoemaker, to purchase three dozen pairs of shoes.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The prisoner was accompanied by a Mr. John Nabbs, a Manchester gentleman whom Mr. Molloy knew and trusted.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mr. John Hornidge, clerk for Esdaile and Co., testified that the acceptance on the bill was not the handwriting of any partner or authorized clerk.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Molloy testified that about seven days after the first purchase, the prisoner returned, paid the expenses for a misdirected delivery, and collected the shoes.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The prisoner returned to Molloy's shop a second time to purchase more shoes, for which he offered another bill of exchange, this time for fifteen pounds.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: During cross-examination, Mr. Molloy admitted that upon subsequent inquiry, the account the prisoner gave of himself was found to be true.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner told Mr. Molloy that he had received the bill from a person named Carter.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The bill was endorsed by the prisoner in his own name, 'John-Webb Elkington'.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mr. Molloy testified that he recovered the shoes that Elkington had purchased, with the exception of one pair.

public.game_background: William Pearce, also known as Benton, was indicted for unlawfully and maliciously shooting at Ann Houghton with a pistol loaded with ball on the 6th of October.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. William either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Ann Houghton testified that the prisoner, Mr. Pearce, came out of the house next door (No. 38), passed her on the step, took a pistol from under a loose coat on his arm, and fired at her. She stated her skull was shattered to pieces.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Ann Houghton stated the shooting occurred at about half-past eight in the evening. She also denied having been drinking that day.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The surgeon, George-Frederick Furnival, testified that the victim's skull was excessively fractured by a hard body and that the wadding of a pistol alone would not have caused such an injury.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The prisoner, in his defense, stated that on the evening of October 6th, he went home at twenty minutes past eight, sat down to supper, and remained by the fire-side smoking his pipe until after nine.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: James Mash, who lived at the same address as the victim, testified that he witnessed the entire event. He stated the shooting occurred at ten minutes after nine o'clock.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: George Fellows, a watchman, testified that he saw Ann Houghton at her door around a quarter to nine. He stated she was "very much intoxicated" and surrounded by a "great mob of men, women, and children."

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Osborne, the younger, testified that his firm was missing one sack of wheat from a waggon on their wharf on the morning of March 15th. He stated that suspicion fell upon two boats which had access to the wharf.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Radley, a fourteen-year-old boy, testified that he saw the prisoner take a sack of wheat from Mr. Osborne's wharf onto the boat at about half past ten at night.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: On cross-examination, William Radley admitted that he himself was accused of taking the property, and only after being taken into custody did he charge the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Radley also admitted that he never told anybody about the theft until after he was taken a prisoner himself.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Radley claimed that the other three men in the boat did not see the prisoner take the sack because they were with the horses.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Under questioning, Radley admitted that for the prisoner to get the sack, the boat must have been stopped, the horses must have been standing still, and there was a man at the helm who would have known the boat stopped.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Benjamin Neal, the master boat-man, testified that he accompanied the barge the whole way and never saw the prisoner bring any sack on board or take one out at Ganford-green.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: On cross-examination, Benjamin Neal admitted he was ashore for half an hour to fetch horses, which was enough time for a sack to be brought on board. He also admitted he was not on board at four in the morning.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Thomas Marlborough, another boatman, testified that he was in the boat with the prisoner the whole time and there was no sack of wheat on board. He stated the prisoner was in bed and could not have gotten out without his knowing.
[EVIDENCE ev11]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Officer John Armstrong testified that on Feb 17th, he and other officers went to the prisoners' apartment. He found the main house door and the door to their room open. The prisoners, Pagett and Rothwell, were inside and were civil, with Pagett telling them, 'warrant or no warrant, you are welcome to search.'
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: In an armchair in the prisoners' room, Officer Armstrong found a leather bag containing 59 uncoloured counterfeit blanks the size of a shilling, along with 'sissil' (waste metal from which blanks are punched). He also found pliers, cork, sandpaper, file-dust, and six iron punches in the same area.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Officer John Vickery testified that he found numerous counterfeit coins in the room. This included seven counterfeit sixpences in a tea caddie and a drawer containing counterfeit half guineas, seven-shilling pieces, shillings, and dollars. Some of these coins were described as 'fit for circulation.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Officer Daniel Bishop found a vial of vitriol and, hidden under rags and a washing-tub, a vial containing aqua fortis. Bishop, a former dyer, testified he was certain the substance was aqua fortis from its taste and his decade of experience using it.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: During the search of the apartment, Officer Armstrong heard Elizabeth Pagett say, 'take care of my goods,' in reference to the items in the room.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Officer Armstrong testified that during the search, James Rothwell remarked that 'he was unlucky that he had just come up.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The landlady, Eleanor Bromage, testified that she had let the apartment to Elizabeth Pagett and another woman. She stated that Rothwell did not live there, but was merely a visitor whom she had seen there on occasion.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: During the cross-examination of Officer Armstrong, the defense established that no files were found during the search. The defense counsel suggested that files were a necessary instrument for preparing the blanks and making the tools complete.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: In her defense, Elizabeth Pagett stated, 'I am innocent of the charge that is laid against me.'

public.game_background: Sergeant Hugh Mack was indicted for the wilful murder of Timothy Kirby, a deserter under his escort. The charge stems from an incident at Barnet on March 23, 1806, where Mack struck Kirby on the head with a halbert following a dispute, leading to Kirby's death several days later.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The party of deserters, including the deceased Timothy Kirby, halted without orders at Barnet. They stated they were unable to continue to Highgate without refreshment, having had none since noon the previous day.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: According to civilian witness Matthew Smith, as Sgt. Mack raised his halbert to strike the fatal blow, he said, 'I will murder you, you b-r.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Surgeons who examined Kirby confirmed that an instrument had penetrated his skull and entered the brain. The surgeon who performed the post-mortem had 'no doubt at all' that this wound was the cause of death.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Civilian eyewitnesses Thomas Webb and Matthew Smith both testified that Kirby had made no physical threat or motion to strike Sgt. Mack before the blow was delivered. Smith stated any such claim was 'a very false thing.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Officer Wiltshire, who had received Mack as a volunteer from the militia, testified that he came with 'an exceeding good character' and that in his experience, 'he has always behaved well; I never found him inhuman.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Under cross-examination, escort soldier Thomas Wilson admitted that his reason for believing Mack only meant to strike with the wooden part of the halbert was because Mack had told him so after the incident.

public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Edward Basset either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Elizabeth Ibbett, wife of the property owner, testified that on Sunday, March 2nd, she and her husband left for a funeral, leaving the prisoner, Edward Basset, as the only person in the room. When they returned, they discovered three blankets and a sheet were missing from his bed.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Elizabeth Castletine, a neighbor who lived opposite the lodging house, testified that she saw Basset come down the stairs with a bundle on the afternoon of the incident. She stated, 'I saw something like a blanket, there were holes in the bag.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Patrick Mackcaller, who lodged in the same room, testified that he saw Basset come into the room 'of a bustle and went to the cupboard, after that he went to his box, and took something out,' although he did not know what it was.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mrs. Ibbett testified that when the defendant returned to his lodgings on the night of the incident, 'he did not ask after the blankets.' He was arrested the following night.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: During cross-examination, Mrs. Ibbett confirmed that Basset had lodged with her for six to eight months and had always paid his rent regularly.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Mrs. Ibbett stated that the room contained four beds and that seven people slept in it, although the prisoner 'mostly had a bed to himself' at that time.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mrs. Ibbett testified that the defendant 'complained in the middle of the night that he was cold.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Meyer, the victim's brother-in-law, testified that Mr. Steele left London on November 5th, 1802, for his property in Feltham. After Mr. Steele failed to return, Meyer initiated a search on Hounslow Heath.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Benjamin Hanfield, an accomplice granted a pardon, testified that John Holloway approached him to commit a 'footpad robbery'. Hanfield stated Holloway identified Owen Haggerty as the third conspirator, and they all met at the Black Horse pub before heading to Hounslow Heath.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: John Smith, a coachman, testified that on the night of the murder, as his coach passed between the trees and the eleven-mile stone on Hounslow Heath, he heard a man groan twice. He stated the first groan was louder and the second was more faint.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Multiple witnesses, including officers and publicans, testified that Holloway, Haggerty, and Hanfield were frequently seen in each other's company at pubs in the Dyot Street area around the time of the murder.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Magistrate John Nares testified that both defendants made false statements during their examinations. Haggerty denied knowing Hanfield and gave a false alibi of working for a Mr. Smith, which Smith refuted. Holloway also denied being at Hounslow and provided several false work alibis that were disproven by the named employers.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Officer Daniel Bishop testified that he overheard conversations between Holloway and Haggerty in their separate lock-up rooms. They discussed Hanfield's testimony and the lies they had told the magistrate (e.g., Holloway: "I denied it entirely..."; Haggerty: "So did I."), but at no point did they protest their innocence to each other.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Under cross-examination, Hanfield admitted to a life of crime, stating he got his living 'Sometimes by thieving and sometimes by industry.' He also stated his confession was due to 'compunction of conscience,' despite four years of silence and the existence of a reward.

Description: The prosecutor, Peter Muller, testified that the prisoner, James Watley, accosted him claiming to be a watchman. Watley then took him into a public house against his will.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The publican, William Screen, testified that Muller and Watley 'came in very quietly together' and that he believed the prosecutor called for the drinks. He observed only 'good nature' between them.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Nicholas Argent, the prosecutor's landlord, testified that he heard a thump at his door and a fiddle playing. Looking out his window, he saw two men and a fiddler with Muller, who promptly left when Argent opened the window.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Nicholas Argent stated that when Muller came inside, he immediately began to cry and complained of being robbed.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Argent testified that Muller was 'more sober than he was in liquor.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The officer, John Griffiths, apprehended the prisoner in a room with 'two girls of the town.' Upon searching him, he found two guineas and a seven shilling piece.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The publican, William Screen, positively identified the prisoner, Watley, as the man who was in his establishment with the prosecutor.

public.game_background: Caroline Cooper was indicted for burglary and theft in the dwelling house of Mary Waylett on October 3rd, 1808. She was accused of stealing a pocket book, cash, and various small items after taking a lodging for the night in Mrs. Waylett's house.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Caroline Cooper either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_mw_01]
Description: Mary Waylett testified that on October 3rd, she placed a pocket book containing several bank notes and six or seven seven-shilling pieces into an iron-hooped trunk in her chamber. She stated she locked the trunk.
[EVIDENCE ev_mw_02]
Description: Mrs. Waylett stated that Caroline Cooper arrived at her house between seven and eight o'clock on the evening of the theft, asking for a lodging. An arrangement was made for her to share a room with another lodger, Elizabeth Black.
[EVIDENCE ev_mw_06]
Description: Mrs. Waylett claimed that on October 7th, she confronted Cooper and asked for her pocket book. She testified that Cooper confessed, saying 'I will go and fetch it,' and then retrieved it from a basket of rags.
[EVIDENCE ev_absence_money]
Description: No witness testified to finding any of the stolen bank notes or coins. The recovered pocket book contained only 'duplicates and receipts.'
[EVIDENCE ev_defense_01]
Description: The prisoner said nothing in her defence.
[EVIDENCE ev_defense_02]
Description: The defendant called two witnesses who gave her a good character.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: David Samuhda testified that he and a companion were stopped in their chaise by four footpads, two of whom had pistols. The men forcibly removed his companion, Mr. Swaby, from the chaise and demanded his money and watch.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: David Samuhda identified the prisoner Carr as the man who held a pistol to him and took his property. He stated, 'I am sure of Carr to the best of my knowledge and belief,' and noted Carr was wearing the same jacket in court.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mr. Swaby, the second victim, testified that he was dragged from the chaise by the robbers. He stated, 'I took notice of one man in particular; his name is Kelly. I am quite sure he is one.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Officer Charles Frost testified that about an hour after the robbery, he and other patrols stopped four men. The last man, who Frost believed to be Carr based on his dress, 'gave a jump and went towards Newington Green' when told he would be searched.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Officer Lloyd testified that he apprehended Michael Conner, one of the four men stopped by the patrol. Conner was holding a parcel which was identified by the victim, David Samuhda, as the one stolen from his chaise, as it contained his name and address.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mary Kaley, Carr's landlady, testified that on the evening of the robbery, Carr was at her house at six o'clock. She stated, 'at half after six he drank tea with me and my husband... He went to bed as the watch was crying ten o'clock.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Eleanor Kain testified that just before the physical altercation, she had a verbal dispute with Conner, accusing him of 'trying to make words between man and wife' because he was whistling and singing while she and her husband were arguing.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Under questioning, Eleanor Kain admitted that her family and the Conners were not good neighbors, stating, 'We quarrelled once before.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Officer Francis Freeman testified that he apprehended Driscoll the following day and, upon searching him, found a half guinea in his possession.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: John Kain mentioned that the morning after the incident, 'Conner got a warrant against me and my wife.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Defense counsel highlighted that despite ten to twenty people gathering during the incident, Mrs. Kain did not call out to any of them that her husband had been robbed.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: John Kain, himself a watchman, did not report the robbery to an officer that night. He went to his watchhouse, found his post filled, returned home, and only laid an information at the magistrate's office the next morning.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prosecutrix, Sarah Biggins, testified that she locked her room on July 4th, leaving the key in its usual hiding spot. Upon her return within the hour, the key and her husband's silver watch were missing.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The prisoner, Susannah Beeson, claimed in her defense that the prosecutrix had given her the watch to pawn for money.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Richards, a pawnbroker, testified that the prisoner came to his shop on the afternoon of July 4th and he lent her one pound one shilling on the stolen watch.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The landlord, Henry Dunkin, testified that he confronted the prisoner, who was very intoxicated. She initially denied knowing anything about the watch.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prosecutrix stated that the landlord, Henry Dunkin, was at work in his shop within the same house when she went out, as she could hear his weaver's shuttle.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Henry Dunkin testified he saw the prisoner at her father's house between seven and eight o'clock at night, and 'she could hardly stand she was so much intoxicated in liquor.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Robert Wilks testified that on the morning of September 7th, he discovered his warehouse had been broken into by removing a pane of glass in a window. He found that nineteen reams of a special, patented printing paper were stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mr. Wilks stated that the defendant Daniel Bracken was his warehouseman, had the key to the warehouse on the day of the robbery, and did not show up for work the following day until the evening.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Denham, a printer, testified that on the day after the robbery, a stranger sold him twelve reams of the same type of paper for six guineas, a price far below its market value of twenty-six or twenty-seven guineas.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The defendant Williams stated in his defense that his coach and horses were stolen from him at Temple Bar around 11 o'clock at night. He claimed he spent the subsequent hours searching for his coach and reported its loss to his employer.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Bracken stated in his defense that he had returned his keys to a servant as usual. He claimed he was at Bartholomew Fair with a friend, John Rivers, until nearly 4 a.m., after which he went with a girl.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Two witnesses provided a good character reference for the defendant John Williams.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Three witnesses provided a good character reference for the defendant Daniel Bracken.

information:

Description: On the night of February 6th, the dwelling house of Thomas Sedgwick, esq. was broken into and robbed. Mr. Sedgwick heard a noise around two or three in the morning but did not investigate, attributing it to a neighbor's carriage.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: In her defense, Susannah Thurston stated she was not at home at the time of the robbery, but was in the country, one hundred and twelve miles away.

[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: In his defense, Jeremiah Thurston stated, 'I remember the time of my father giving her the gowns, I know nothing of them; I know nothing of the charge laid against me.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Boswell testified that the prisoner, Samuel Walker, came to his cottage around 5:30 PM on December 23rd. Walker asked for potatoes and then a drink of water, and was subsequently invited inside to have some sprats which the family was cooking for supper.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Both Thomas and Hannah Boswell confirmed in court that the prisoner told them he 'had a partner on the road with a horse.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Stephen Hale, a farmer, testified that he was sent for the next morning and found the prisoner tied up in Boswell's house. He searched Walker and found gunpowder upon him.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Stephen Hale testified that he received two pistols from Mr. Boswell. Upon examination, he 'observed the muzzles full, within about three quarters of an inch of both of them.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In his defense, Samuel Walker claimed he never offered to fire any pistols. He stated he went to Boswell's house because he was 'informed that he wanted to buy pistols' and took one out of his pocket to show him.

[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George Ross testified that the prisoner, James Dunbar, was his clerk. In October, Ross left London for Lisbon.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mr. Ross testified that the items were left in his house when he departed and that he discovered they were missing in December.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Ross confirmed that an 'extent commission' was taken out against him, meaning the government had a right to seize the property if it had been returned.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The prisoner claimed, 'I meaned to return them.'
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The prisoner claimed in his defense that 'It is a malicious act.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Melinda Mapson was hired as a servant by Mrs. Dignam and began her service on the evening of the robbery, February 10th, 1809. She was the only servant in the house.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The next morning, Mr. and Mrs. Dignam discovered the prisoner was gone. The street door was open, and a large quantity of property was missing from multiple rooms, including linens, silverware, clothing, and a timepiece.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mrs. Dignam found a candlestick on the kitchen table where the candle had burned completely down, cementing the candlestick to the table with grease. She also noted drops of tallow grease in various parts of the house where items were taken.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Margaret Garey, the previous servant, testified that she had left her locked box in her former bedroom. Upon returning, she found the box broken open and her clothes, valued at fifteen guineas, were stolen.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mrs. Tidball testified that on the afternoon of February 16th, the prisoner came to her house to purchase twine. He asked for the bill of parcels to be made out in the name of 'Williams' and purchased three dozen.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The servant, Sarah Foster, testified that she saw the prisoner take a note from her mistress's hand. She confirmed it was a note, although she did not see it opened and did not know its value.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Officer Thomas Sapwell testified that he arrested the prisoner on March 13th, nearly a month after the incident. He found the prisoner in possession of a pocket book, but no ten-pound note was found on him.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner stated, 'When I was taken up... the woman first said that I snatched the note out of her hand afterwards she said she gave it me... I had the note from her, but not violently.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner gave a lengthy account of his long and decorated career in the Royal Navy, detailing his service in major conflicts and mentioning that he was wounded. He stated he is now 'old and worn out, being between sixty and seventy years of age.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mrs. Tidball testified that she recognized the prisoner by chance in a pork shop in Bishopgate-street, at which point she 'gave the alarm, and had him taken.'

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Jonathan Whittaker asserted his sobriety, stating, 'I was perfectly sober. I had only a share of a pot of porter that afternoon.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Immediately after Whittaker called for the watch, the prisoner Mary Murphy ran away from the scene into a nearby house.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Stafford, a soldier, testified that he met Robert Butt, Joseph Cope, Mary Walton, and Sarah Slater at a public-house where Butt asked if he was 'down to the queer,' a slang term for being involved with counterfeit money.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Stafford admitted under questioning that he was arrested as a deserter on June 25th. Subsequently, he wrote to the secretary of state and began acting as an informant under the direction of the solicitor of the mint.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Officer John Armstrong testified that at Butt's house he found a cutting instrument with a cutter that matched the size of a counterfeit sixpence. This physical evidence was presented in court.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The landlady, Mary Quin, testified to a strange smell she had attributed to a nearby slaughterhouse. When shown aquafortis at the magistrate's court, she said the smell was 'something like it.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: When asked if Charles Walton was present during the criminal conversations, the prosecution's main witness, William Stafford, testified, 'No, I do not recollect he was.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: David Phillips, the victim, testified that he was alerted in the morning to find his shop's shutters shoved aside and a pane of glass broken, allowing a hand to be put through. He stated he generally shut the shutters himself but could not swear he had done so on that specific night.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: John Murphy, a patrolman, testified that between 2 and 3 AM, he and his partner Owen Duncan observed a group of six men, including the three prisoners, acting suspiciously and pretending to be intoxicated. He saw them pass Mr. Phillips's house several times.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: James Manyough, a watchman, testified that he saw the three prisoners together appearing intoxicated. He later saw Prescott alone cross the street to Phillips's shop, heard a 'wrench of a shutter or a door,' and heard Prescott say 'all is well' before he fled. Manyough then pursued and apprehended Prescott.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Hopkins's defense was that he was passing by and saw a bundle on the ground, which he picked up just before the watchman stopped him.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Jones's defense was that he was taken to the watchhouse before the circumstance happened, pointing out that the watchman had looked at Mr. Phillips's house before taking him.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Prescott's defense was that he had been to the play, got locked out of his home, and was wrongly apprehended by the watchman.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The defense called a total of eleven witnesses who gave good character references for the three prisoners (six for Prescott, two for Jones, and three for Hopkins).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Baldwin, the receiver for the police offices, testified about the standard procedure for settling accounts. He stated that the chief clerk (Skene) submits a quarterly statement of expenses, and after receiving funds from the treasury, Baldwin pays the clerk the balance upon presentation of corresponding vouchers or receipts.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: William Holmes, a clerk for coal merchants Soulsby and Allison, testified that the actual amount of their bill for the quarter was £17 9s., not the £36 8s. claimed on the receipt.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Joseph Downes, the printer, testified that the amount of his demand for printing for the quarter was £4 14s. The receipt submitted by Skene was for £11 10s.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Michael Sandys, a partner at the stationery company, testified that his firm's demand for the quarter was £5 16s. 9d., which was the exact amount listed on the receipt Skene submitted.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: John Lee, a law stationer acquainted with Thomas Rider for over thirty years, testified that he did not believe the signature on the £20 rent receipt belonged to Mr. Rider.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner did not provide a defense himself, instead leaving it to his counsel. The defense called eight witnesses who gave him a good character.

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Morgan testified that Wharton came to him in Hastings and proposed a plan to feign bankruptcy and conceal goods, stating that 'eight hundred pounds might be put in my pocket.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Wharton wrote a letter to Morgan on September 14th, stating, 'Our shop has been robbed of nearly every thing that is in it'.
[EVIDENCE ev6]

[EVIDENCE ev7]

[EVIDENCE ev9]

[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Sowerby testified that Morgan only told him about the authority to pawn goods after a large quantity of pawned items were discovered, and that Morgan's explanation of a £60 limit seemed 'very disproportionate' to the amount of goods found.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Sowerby stated that he and the other creditors initially instituted the prosecution, but 'in consequence of what you have since learned you have dropped it, and left Mr. Morgan to go on with it.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Simon Bathgate testified that he was in the Marquis of Granby public-house with two men. During a purported demonstration of a gambling trick, one of the men took his £10 note out of his hand, claimed to have won it, and handed it to the other man.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: When asked to identify the second man involved in the trick, Simon Bathgate stated, 'I cannot positively say whether it was the prisoner or not. ... I do not recollect the man.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Simon Bathgate testified that he was 'perfectly certain' of the other man, identifying him as James Wheeler, the prisoner's brother who was previously tried for the offense.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: James Hodges, a beadle, testified that around one o'clock on November 24th, he saw the prisoner, Charles Wheeler, and his brother James coming out of the Marquis of Granby public-house.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: John Rose, a clerk at Messrs. Barclay's, testified that the prosecutor reported the lost note. About half an hour later, a young woman brought the note to be changed, and he detained it.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: William Mann, the publican, testified that Simon Bathgate and James Wheeler came into his pub, and about five or ten minutes later another man walked in, whom he believed to be the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner stated, 'I know nothing of the transaction. I was not in the house that day.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The prisoner argued that because he and his brother were twins, it would have been 'impossible for me to represent myself as a stranger' to his brother, and therefore the victim should have remembered him if he were present.

public.game_background: James Leary was indicted for the murder of Edward Clifford on July 26, 1813. The victim's body was found bludgeoned to death in a brick-field pond. information:
[EVIDENCE ev1_body_found]
Description: The body of a man, later identified as Edward Clifford, was found in a pond in a brick-field. The body had 'great cuts on the side of the head,' indicating a violent death.
[EVIDENCE ev8_leary_incriminating_statement]
Description: Officer Thisselton and Thomas M'Carthy testified that Leary, while in custody, stated that if he could get someone to prove he was in bed at 10 o'clock 'it would be all right,' but admitted 'that is where I should be lost.' He then said the only way to get through it was 'to fix it on some one else.'
[EVIDENCE ev10_burke_quarrel]
Description: Hyam Barnett, the hatter who sold Clifford his new hat, testified that Clifford 'seemed terrified' of Mary Burke when she entered the shop and that 'they seemed as if they had been quarrelling before they came in.'
[EVIDENCE ev12_no_money_found]
Description: When Leary was apprehended at his workplace the day after the murder, he was searched and found to have only an 'eighteen-penny piece' on him. No part of the victim's stolen money was found on his person or in his lodgings.
[EVIDENCE ev13_leary_good_character]
Description: David Helston, Leary's employer, testified that he had a 'good character' with him and described him as a 'quiet, sober, industrious man.'
[EVIDENCE ev14_burke_hammer]
Description: Officer Thisselton testified that he found a second hammer, different from the one in Leary's room, in Mrs. Burke's apartment in Fitzroy-market.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_01]
Description: Charles James Ashley, the person on whose account the bill was drawn, testified that the signature on the bill was 'most assuredly not' his.
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_03]
Description: Mr. Ashley testified that he had once left his check book on a table and left the prisoner, Botterill, alone in the room.
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_04]
Description: Officer John Collingbourne testified that Botterill confessed to him, stating he 'had taken several checks from the book' when visiting Mr. Ashley.
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_05]
Description: William Ayre, the cashier, testified that Ann Sinclair presented the bill, and he paid her £47 because the imitation of the signature was so correct he initially believed it to be genuine.
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_06]
Description: Ann Sinclair testified that on the day of the crime, Botterill said 'he must get Phoebe or me to go to the banker's.'
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_08]
Description: Phoebe Evans testified that the prisoner gave her the bill of exchange. She then gave it to Ann Sinclair to be cashed at the bank.
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_10]
Description: Phoebe Evans testified that she delivered a letter from the prisoner to a Mr. Voss.
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_11]
Description: David William Voss testified that he received the letter from Phoebe Evans, has seen the prisoner write, and believes the letter to be in Botterill's handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_12]
Description: The letter, attributed to Botterill, contained the statements 'she is not to blame; it is me' and 'If you think it best I will throw myself upon the mercy of Mr. Ashley and the bankers.'
[EVIDENCE ev_whb_13]
Description: Officer Collingbourne testified that when he apprehended the prisoner, Botterill put out his hand and said, 'Collingbourne, you have me; it is the happiest moment I have in my life.'

public.game_background: Thomas Tyler, James Knight, Samuel Knight, and Jane Stamfield were indicted for burglariously breaking and entering the dwelling-house of George Collins on the night of April 9th, and stealing a large quantity of furs and skins, including bear skins, hare skins, and a fox skin.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George Collins, the victim, testified that on the night of April 9th, he fastened his premises securely. He was alerted at 4:30 AM that his property had been broken into, apparently by force using crowbars.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: John Woolfe testified that he let a stable in Hoxton to the defendant Thomas Tyler in late April. The key for the stable hung over the gate for common access.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: John Wittam, a porter, testified that defendant James Knight gave him three skins on May 7th, asking him to find a buyer and claiming they belonged to a Russian ship.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: John Pook testified that Jane Stamfield brought three of the stolen skins to his shop to sell. She first claimed her husband brought them from abroad, but when confronted, changed her story to say she received them from an unknown Jew on London Bridge.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: James Knight's Defense: He claimed he received the skins from his brother Samuel, who told him they came from a Russian captain, and he was merely trying to help sell them.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Samuel Knight's Defense: He claimed a man at a pub who knew a Russian merchant gave him the skins to sell on commission, and he asked his brother James to help find buyers.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Rebecca Fisher, who lived with James Knight, provided an alibi, stating he was home in bed on the night of the burglary. She claimed she locked the only door and would have known if he left.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Two witnesses, Samuel and Henry Shaler, testified that Samuel Knight was with them in Little Chelsea on the evening of Friday, April 29th, and the pub landlord confirmed he saw Knight go to bed around midnight.

Description: Amelia Bailey, a grocer, testified that on the same evening, October 7th, the prisoner came to her shop and attempted to pay for tea and sugar with a shilling. Mrs. Bailey perceived it was bad, returned it to the prisoner, and told her to go away.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Officer Dickens testified that he searched the prisoner and found one bad shilling in her pocket, which he kept separate from the shilling the landlord gave him.

[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: In her defense, the prisoner stated, 'Please your honour, these two men when they took me before, a woman brought me the bad money.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The prisoner stated, 'I am a poor desolute woman. If you are going to hang me, hang me out of the way at once, and do not use me in this manner.'

information:

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Thomas Martin, clerk of the parish church of Whitechapel, produced the official register book of marriages from October 1794.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Benjamin Hayes testified that he was present at the prisoner's marriage to his cousin, Ann Hayes, on September 20, 1808, at Northwood in the Isle of Wight.

public.game_background: Patrick M'Court, John Breeze, Robert Rhodes, and Michael Eagan, all soldiers, were indicted for stealing a trunk containing money, bank notes, a watch, and other valuables from the dwelling of Corporal Thomas Endfield and his wife, located within the Hounslow military barracks on April 22, 1819.

public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mary Endfield, wife of the victim, testified that on the night of April 21st, a locked box containing bank notes, money, a watch, and other valuables was stolen from under their bed. The next morning, she discovered the trunk broken open in a passage with the money and watch missing.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Sergeant Gannon testified that the defendant Breeze also told him money was in Captain Mortray's stable. However, when Gannon took Breeze there, Sergeant Lloyd had already discovered the items based on M'Court's earlier confession.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Sergeant Lloyd testified that after telling the defendant Eagan it would be 'better to tell the truth,' Eagan stated his wife had £12. Lloyd retrieved this money from Eagan's wife.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Adjutant William Glanville testified that on the night of the robbery, M'Court was on guard, while Rhodes, Breeze, and Eagan were in the barracks. He stated that all the prisoners could have had access to Mrs. Endfield's room.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas M'Donald, an assistant in the pantry, testified that he saw the prisoner, John Butler, coming out of the pantry at about half-past three o'clock in the afternoon.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Revell, a coachman, testified that he saw the prisoner running down Cleveland-yard with the plate under his coat, and then saw him hide behind a cistern.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: M'Donald testified that upon being caught, the prisoner fell on his knees, begged for pardon, asked to be beaten well, and promised he would never do it again.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Officer Green stated that the stolen plate weighed twenty ounces and a half and was worth about 5 pounds, confirming its value.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner's only statement in his defense was to 'throw myself on the mercy of the Court.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Richard John Draper, the prosecutor, testified that his house was secured with a spring-lock at 9:30 AM on July 25th. Upon returning at 9:30 PM, he found the house had been entered without forced entry on the door, and a large quantity of property, valued at over £100, was missing from multiple rooms.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Sarah Remmington, a publican next door, testified that around 6 PM she saw a hackney chariot at the prosecutor's door, which was wide open. She witnessed bundles being placed inside the chariot.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Sarah Remmington positively identified the prisoner, John Jones, as the man she saw at the scene. She stated he shut the chariot door, told the coachman to drive on, pulled the prosecutor's door to, and ran away across the street.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William Day, another witness at the scene, testified that he saw two men come out of the house but could not identify the prisoner as one of them. He was certain that the man who got into the coach was not the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The prosecutor, Richard Draper, testified that upon apprehension, the prisoner stated he lived at 'the second house on the left hand' in Swan-place. This is the exact location where the coachman testified he dropped off the stolen goods.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Deborah Robinson, a neighbor at the drop-off location, saw the goods being unloaded. She testified that the man directing the unloading was 'much stouter than the prisoner' and that the prisoner did not fetch the goods from the coach.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: A search of the prisoner and the house he claimed to live in yielded none of the stolen property. The stolen goods, which included numerous items of glassware, clothing, and money, were never recovered.

public.game_background: Henry William Bull, Frederick Bull, Samuel George, and John Gomm were indicted for stealing a bill of exchange for £100 from the person of Henry Joll on October 21st, 1819. The prosecution alleges that when Joll presented the bill for verification of its acceptance, Henry Bull snatched it, and with the help of the other defendants, Frederick Bull destroyed it in a fire. The defense contended that the bill had been fraudulently obtained from them by a third party, and their actions were to prevent a crime, not to commit one.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Henry either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Joll testified that he went to the Bulls' office to verify the acceptance on a £100 bill. After showing the bill to Henry Bull, Bull made a snatch at it, took it from him, and ran into the clerk's office.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Joll testified that he had discounted the bill from a man named Crawley, an uncertificated bankrupt who was a prisoner in the Fleet. Joll stated he paid £71 in cash and a promissory note for the balance.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: During cross-examination, Joll admitted he had previously taken benefit of the Insolvent Act, was not a housekeeper, and had received the £71 cash from his father to discount the bill.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The defense lawyer asked Joll if the defendants told him 'they had been swindled out of this bill and 2000 l. by Crawley, and from your manner they considered you as a coadjutor'. Joll denied this was said.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Joll repeatedly and emphatically denied under oath that either of the defendants had offered to provide him with a copy of the bill or to repay any money he had advanced on it.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: It was established that the defendants had sought Joll to serve him with notice of trial for a previous session but could not find him. Joll also admitted to shutting the door on Henry Bull when he came to serve him with the notice.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: William Underwood, the defendants' brother-in-law, testified that he said to Joll, 'you were told before, if you had given a fair consideration for the bill Mr. Bull would have paid you the amount.' According to Underwood, Joll did not deny this at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The judge's concluding observation was noted in the transcript: 'that though they had acted imprudently in destroying the bill, they certainly were free from any criminal motive.'

The charge stems from a street altercation where Skelton's cart obstructed Turney's waggon. The prosecution alleges that after a dispute, Skelton deliberately knocked Turney under the wheels of his own waggon and prevented his rescue, leading to Turney being run over and killed.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Joseph Skelton's dust-cart, along with a potato-cart, was obstructing a narrow part of Chandos-street, leaving insufficient room for William Turney's coal-waggon to pass.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Before the coal-waggon arrived, a Hammersmith stage-coachman also argued with Skelton about moving the cart. Skelton refused and knocked the coachman under his horses' feet.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Following Skelton's refusal, Turney's waggon attempted to pass. The waggon touched the hind part of Skelton's cart, causing it to swing around towards the pavement without significant injury.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Witnesses stated that after Hughes was struck, the general 'whooping and hallooing' from the crowd caused the young horses to move forward, and the waggon wheels ran over the deceased.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Henry Bond, the house surgeon, confirmed that the deceased died from a severe compound fracture of his left leg and arm, and that the 'injury he received was the cause of his death.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Defense witness George White, the cook-shop owner, testified that he has known the prisoner for two years and that Skelton is an 'inoffensive man, and acted with forbearance.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Stephen Skinner testified that he is a milkman and lives at No. 44, South-street, Grosvenor-square, which is his own dwelling-house.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Stephen Skinner testified that around 8:30 PM, while it was dark, he heard a noise in the parlour, which was rented to his sister, Mrs. Williams, who had been out since noon.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Upon investigating the noise, Stephen Skinner went up the area steps and saw the prisoner, Morris Jones, jump out of the parlour window and fall into the area.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mrs. Williams testified that upon her return home, she found a bundle moved, and two shifts and a bedgown were gone.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner stated, 'I was going home, and saw something laying down, I picked it up and put it into my hat.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Stephen Skinner testified that the prisoner gave conflicting addresses, first saying he lived with his sister in St. Giles's and later telling the Magistrate he lived in Cato-street.

public.game_background: Arthur Thistlewood and ten co-conspirators were indicted for High Treason. The charges included conspiring to depose the King, levying war against the King, and subverting the Constitution. Overt acts listed in the indictment included conspiring to murder members of the Privy Council, providing arms for this purpose, conspiring to seize weapons, burn barracks, and inciting insurrection.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Arthur either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Testimony from Joseph Hale (Brunt's apprentice) and Mary Rogers (landlady). They confirmed that Brunt hired a back room in Fox-court for a man named Ings. This unfurnished room became the regular meeting place for the conspirators, where they were heard hammering and sawing.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Testimony of Thomas Hiden, a cow-keeper. Hiden stated that conspirator Wilson recruited him, describing the plot to destroy the ministers with hand grenades at a cabinet dinner. Hiden was told to meet at the Horse and Groom public-house in Cato Street.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Testimony of Lord Harrowby. He confirmed his intention to host a cabinet dinner on Feb 23rd. He also testified that he was approached in the park by Thomas Hiden, who gave him a letter warning of the plot, which prompted the cancellation of the dinner while allowing preparations to continue as a trap.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Testimony of John Monument, a shoemaker. Monument testified that Brunt and Tidd recruited him on Feb 22nd, giving him the password 'B.U.T.' to which the reply was 'T.O.N.'. The next day, he went with Tidd to the Cato Street stable, and Tidd was carrying a pistol and pike heads.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Testimony of Bow-street officers Ruthven and Ellis. They described raiding the loft in Cato Street, announcing themselves as officers, and being met with armed resistance. They stated that Thistlewood retreated into a small room and then lunged forward, stabbing and killing Officer Smithers with a sword.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Testimony from multiple officers regarding arrests. Tidd was apprehended outside the stable after a struggle in which his pistol discharged. Davidson was caught fleeing armed with a carbine and sword, exclaiming 'D—n the man that would not die in liberty's cause'. Ings was caught after firing at an officer and shouting he wished he had killed him.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Physical evidence presented by Officer Taunton. A large cache of weapons and ammunition was found at Tidd's lodgings, which Adams had identified as the 'depot'. The find included 965 ball cartridges, ten grenades, bags of gunpowder, and twenty-seven pike handles.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Physical evidence presented by Officer Taunton. Two baskets found in the back room at Brunt's lodgings contained fire-balls, grenades, bags of powder, and bullets. Brunt denied knowledge of them.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Testimony of artillery sergeant Edward Harrison. He examined the seized hand-grenades and fire-balls. He described the grenades as containing 3.5oz of gunpowder and pieces of iron, designed to be highly destructive in a room, and the fire-balls as capable of burning for several minutes and setting a building ablaze.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Testimony of defense witness Thomas Chambers. Chambers claimed that Robert Adams and a man named Edwards had tried to recruit him, with Adams stating they were going to 'kill His Majesty's Ministers' and would have 'blood and wine for supper.' He also claimed they tried to leave a bag of pistols with him.

Description: The victim, farmer Edward Boards, testified that he missed one sheep on the morning of March 31st. He stated that the sheep was safe with the others in Mr. Corbett's marsh at six o'clock the previous evening.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mr. Boards testified that all three defendants had previously worked for him and were therefore familiar with his premises and the location of his livestock.

Description: The sole eyewitness, Joseph Day, testified that he was on the Chingford-road between three and four o'clock in the morning when he met the three prisoners and a fourth man.

Description: Day stated he had no quarrel with the prisoners, but also testified that his wife is separated from him and now lives in the house with the defendant, Harvey.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edward Brown testified that on the morning of January 31st, he discovered his mare was gone from his enclosed stable-yard in Ashwell, Norfolk. He had last seen it safe the previous day.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Edward Brown, Jun., the prosecutor's nephew, stated he saw the prisoner at Ashwell on January 29th, the day before the mare was stolen. He noted that the prisoner was visiting his mother but could not be found after the theft.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The prisoner's defense stated that he was at his home in Bethnal-green, London, on the night of January 30th. He claimed he bought the mare on the Romford road.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Hurlings Moore testified that upon learning of the charge, the prisoner went to the office with him willingly and had proposed it himself.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Samuel Howard also testified that after the prisoner left his employ, he went into business for himself and had two horses. This suggests the prisoner had the means and business context to purchase a horse.

public.game_background: John Bates was indicted for feloniously and maliciously setting fire to his public house, which was in his possession, with the intent to defraud the Sun Fire Office Company, with whom he held a policy for £2100. The prosecution alleges that Bates deliberately set multiple fires in the under-furnished property to claim the insurance money. The defense argues that the fires were accidental and that Bates has a solid alibi for the time the fire started.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. John Bates either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Upon entering, witnesses discovered two separate and distinct fires. One was in the bar under a cask, and another was a pile of wood burning at the bottom of the cellar stairs against a partition.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: When questioned by a fireman, Bates admitted he was the last person in the house, leaving around 8:30 PM. When asked for the key, he first said his wife had it, before exclaiming, 'Oh, dear! Mrs. Bates has got it,' and then implying he must have it.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Bates held an insurance policy for £2100. James Toplis, a surveyor for the Sun Fire Office, testified that he valued the entire contents of the house, including apparel, at only £115 15s.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Robert and Eliza Jones provided a detailed alibi for Bates, testifying that he left his house with them at 8:00 PM and remained in their company continuously until after midnight, when news of the fire arrived. Robert Jones added that Bates fastened the front door from inside and exited through the back.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Eliza Ryder, a former servant, testified that firewood and coals were normally kept in the cellar, and that the cupboard where another fire was found was used to store things like candles and rags for cleaning candlesticks.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Both Eliza Ryder and a fireman, John Rose, testified that they saw a cat in the house the day after the fire which had the hair on its back burnt.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: A neighbour, William Biddulph, who was in the house early, testified that the fire in the cupboard appeared to have been burning for no more than a quarter of an hour. He opined it must have started after people had already entered the house.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: A magistrate testified that the prisoner's public house license had been suspended a year prior due to his wife's misconduct. The annual licensing day to potentially renew it was the day after the trial began.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: According to the Lucetts, William Burn, who was the last guest to leave, turned back from the door in fear, stating that there was a man in the passage with a sword and he was afraid for his life.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: James Burt, a watchman, testified that after securing the prisoner, he found a naked sword in the prisoner's room. He stated that the sword 'had fresh marks of blood on it.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: In his defense, Dennis Shehee stated, 'I never had the sword in my hand - they must have cut themselves. They were laying a wager that they were worth more money than I was, and struck me with a candlestick and fender.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The watchman, James Burt, testified that the prisoner 'was scarred, as if he had been pulled about, and rolled in the kennel.' This indicates Shehee had visible injuries consistent with a physical struggle.

Description: The butler, James Spalding, also confirmed that the silver plate, including the items listed in the indictment, was locked securely in a cupboard before he went to bed.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mary Capling, wife of the owner, testified that on Sunday morning, May 27th, a box in her bedroom contained £100 in bank notes, eighteen sovereigns, and a 7s piece. The next morning, she discovered the box had been broken open and the contents were gone. The prisoner, Sarah Fletcher, was a servant residing in the house at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mary Ann Smith, another servant, testified that she saw the prisoner in her mistress's room between three and four o'clock on Sunday afternoon. She stated the prisoner had the lid of the box open and immediately shut it when Smith entered the room.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In her defense, the prisoner stated, 'I do not think my mistress ever lost any thing, she is a very suspicious woman.' She offered an anecdote about her mistress once misplacing her husband's shirt.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Wessen testified that the prisoner, Matthew Lynch, was a former labourer of his. Wessen had previously ordered Lynch off the premises after Lynch entered his wife's room with the intent to steal something.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: When questioned, the prosecutor John Wessen stated, 'I believe he was mad drunk.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Wessen testified that he found no weapon of any kind on the prisoner. He also stated that nothing was removed from the house because he came downstairs immediately as the prisoner got in.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: In response to the jury, Wessen claimed that the prisoner's only tools on his property were two gravel sieves in the yard, worth about 2 pence, and that the prisoner knew they were not in the house.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: John Cheese, the toll-gate keeper, testified that the prisoner came to his toll-house, forced him to open the door, seized a shovel, and then went to Wessen's house and took a lighted candle.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: John Cheese, the toll-gate keeper, testified that the prisoner appeared to him to be 'as perfectly in his senses as I am.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prisoner stated that he broke the door open because he 'was in a passion' after Wessen refused to let him retrieve his working tools, which he claimed were inside the house.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: William Camplin, a defense witness, testified that for two or three days before the incident, the prisoner 'ran about like a madman, night and day, without his shoes,' and that this behavior began after he was drawn for the Militia.

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Samuel Hall, a servant, testified that he saw the prisoner in the cellar with his master, Mr. Tedd. The prisoner said 'Good morning' to his master before going upstairs.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: John Scott, an officer, testified that upon searching the prisoner, he found a bag containing a half-sovereign, 15 shillings, and a small book in his breeches pocket, as well as a small medal.
[EVIDENCE ev7]

[EVIDENCE ev10]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Francis Pontet, Jun. testified that on May 18th, the prisoner, calling himself Charles Thompson, bought snuff for £6 18s. and paid with a cheque for £17 6s., receiving £10 8s. in change. Pontet positively identified the prisoner in October, though the prisoner denied it.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Harding Thomas, a butcher, testified that on October 13th, the prisoner ordered meat and offered a fraudulent cheque for £16 drawn on the same bank. Suspicious, Thomas had the prisoner wait while he checked with a bank, leading to the prisoner's arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: William Hook, a hay salesman, testified that on May 5th, the prisoner paid for hay with another fraudulent cheque for £9 10s., drawn by 'H. C. Lloyd' on the same bank. Hook received change but the cheque was not paid, and he expressed no doubt as to the prisoner's identity.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: When asked for his defense, the prisoner stated, 'I throw myself on the mercy of the Court.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The gardener, William Duckett, testified that he secured the house at 10 p.m. on January 15th. After midnight, he was awoken and confronted by two armed men who had broken in. He later found a scullery window broken, which was the apparent point of entry.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mrs. Maria Kelly testified that the defendant Edwin Cochran had been in her service for seven months. He was therefore familiar with the layout of the house, including a concealed door to the cellar where candles were kept, which were used during the burglary.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Officer William Scott testified that on January 21st, five days after the robbery, he observed defendants Cochran and Osborne, along with another man, talking together multiple times, which aroused his suspicion and led to their arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Officer Robert Chapman searched John Clare's apartment and found several pieces of ribbon in a chest.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: A pistol was found in Clare's apartment. However, George Crockford, an apprentice coachmaker, testified that the pistol was his and he had given it to Clare, a painter, to stain the handle a fortnight before.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: George Crockford, Clare's colleague, testified that Clare attended his work as a painter as usual during the investigation, even after his lodgings had been searched.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Sarah Bishop, a widow, testifies that she lodges in the parlour of John Hemmings's house. On the day of the robbery, she went out at 8 AM, having locked her door and shut the window down close.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: James Matthews testifies that between 7 and 8 PM, he saw three men, whom he positively identifies as both prisoners and a third person, walk by his house near the crime scene.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: James Matthews testifies that he told a boy to fetch a constable, and that the prisoners, who were standing near enough to hear, 'ran off directly' upon hearing this.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Thomas Wardle testifies that around 8 PM, he saw three young men standing at the house, with two of them lifting the third one up to the parlour window.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Officer William Davenport testifies that the day after the robbery, when he called out to Yates, Yates 'kicked his shoes off his feet, and ran off as fast as he could.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: In his defense, Donahow states, 'I was only walking by.'

[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Richard Ives testified that Ramsay, who was in front of him, and Greenwood, who was behind him, accosted him. After Greenwood coughed, Ramsay rushed forward, struck him, and snatched his property before running down a court.

public.game_background: John Benjamin Brookes is indicted for forging the endorsement of William Clarke on a £10 cheque. The prosecution argues that Brookes forged the signature to support a fraudulent set-off claim in a prior civil suit. The defense contends that William Clarke genuinely signed the cheque in the presence of witnesses.

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: During a previous civil trial between William Clarke and John Brookes, the defendant Brookes produced a cheque for £10. Brookes personally delivered this cheque to his counsel, Mr. Sergeant Pell, who used it as evidence for a set-off claim.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Richard Hill, attorney for the prosecutor Clarke, admitted under cross-examination that he has expressed a determination to bring Brookes to a public trial. Hill also stated that Brookes had threatened him and raised mobs against him.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Donald testified that he accompanied William Clarke to Brookes's office to discount a bill. Clarke went in alone, and upon coming out, they went directly to Clarke's house where Clarke gave Donald the proceeds, including the £10 cheque. Donald stated Clarke had no opportunity to write on the cheque while they were together.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: George Ward, a hatter acquainted with Clarke's handwriting, testified that Brookes showed him the cheque and asked his opinion on the endorsement. Ward stated, 'it was nothing like Clarke's hand-writing,' an opinion his wife shared. Ward noted Clarke's writing was a 'loose straggling hand' and he seldom wrote his Christian name in full.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Thomas Goldsmid testified that while both were in prison, Brookes approached him and said, 'My life is at stake; will you swear that is Clarke's hand at the back of the cheque?' Goldsmid, having never seen the cheque closely, refused.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Joseph Bicknall, a clerk, testified that he accompanied Clarke to visit Brookes in prison. During the visit, Brookes agreed to deposit money to pay Clarke's expenses, provided Clarke did not attend the next hearing. Bicknall received £150 from Brookes for this purpose.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: James Turner, a clerk to both Isaacson and Brookes, testified that he personally witnessed the transaction. He stated Brookes wrote the cheque, gave it to Clarke, said 'Clarke, you had better endorse this cheque,' and that Clarke then endorsed it in his presence.
