information:
[EVIDENCE ev_beckett_1]
Description: William Beckett, age 11, testified that the prisoner, William Tucker, first asked to shoot at his hat. When Beckett refused, Tucker took the hat from his head, threw it into the air, and shot two holes in it as it lodged in a hedge.
[EVIDENCE ev_coleman_william_1]
Description: William Coleman, Jun., who was in a nearby boat, heard the gun fire and then heard Beckett shout, "Oh, my eye." When he met them on the bridge, Beckett said, "Take me home, or this boy will kill me."
[EVIDENCE ev_prisoner_defense]
Description: The prisoner, William Tucker, claimed the shooting was an accident. He stated that as he was getting over a stile, the butt-end of the gun knocked against it, causing it to discharge accidentally.
[EVIDENCE ev_trumper_1]
Description: Mr. Trumper, the farmer who employed Tucker, testified that the gun was old and 'went off very easy at full cock.' He also gave character testimony that Tucker was a 'humane boy, and particularly fond of children.'
[EVIDENCE ev_coleman_thomas_1]
Description: Thomas Coleman, an independent witness, testified that the prisoner brought the injured boy to him, stating the 'gun had gone off unawares.' He observed Tucker to be 'very attentive' and 'appeared sorry.'
[EVIDENCE ev_bell_1]
Description: Press Bell, the boy's guardian, admitted on cross-examination that he had told a third party that if the prisoner paid the doctor's bill and other expenses, he 'should not trouble him further.'

public.game_background: William Pearce, aged 15, was indicted for stealing thirteen pieces of silver, valued at 3 pounds, from the Royal Mint on December 10, 1822. The Mint was legally considered a dwelling-house of the King, making the charge a capital offense.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mr. Henry William Atkinson, the senior moneyer of the Mint, testified that he had observed a deficiency in the bullion several times prior to the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mr. Richard Franklyn, Jun., another moneyer, testified that on December 10th, he weighed the silver at the end of the day and discovered a specific deficiency of about 1 lb. 4 oz.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner initially explained his possession of the keys by stating he 'found the keys in his way to the Mint.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Regarding the silver blanks, the prisoner claimed that he 'supposed somebody must have put them into his pockets.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mr. Powell, from the solicitor's office, testified that before the Magistrate the prisoner changed his story, stating, 'Samuel Marlow gave me the keys... For the purpose of opening the strong places.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The court transcript notes, 'The prisoner made no Defence.'

public.game_background: John Strong, a former employee, was indicted for stealing a large quantity of money and bank notes from the dwelling-house of his former employer, William Donovan. The defense challenges the credibility of Kenaley and presents evidence contradicting his account.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Charles Kenaley, the prosecutor's errand boy, testified that the prisoner, John Strong, met with him on the Friday and Saturday before the robbery. Kenaley claimed Strong planned the theft, arranging for Kenaley to let him and an associate named Bob into the house on Sunday evening while their master was at chapel.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Thomas Cook, a carpenter, examined the drawers from which the money was stolen. He testified that there were marks of two different chisels on the drawer.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Donovan testified that the prisoner, Strong, was a former employee who might have known where he kept his money. Donovan also stated it was his constant habit to attend chapel on Sunday evenings, a fact known to Strong.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William Donovan, the prosecutor, testified that his suspicions first fell on Kenaley. Furthermore, Kenaley admitted on cross-examination that he has been held in the House of Correction ever since the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Thomas Griffiths testified that during the evening, Kenaley left him alone in the house for about twenty minutes while he went out to deliver some oysters.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: William Donovan testified that he had a 'very sharp dog' in the house that would bark at any stranger. He stated the dog knew Strong and would 'run at him now'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner John Strong made a simple declaration to the court, stating: 'I am entirely innocent.'

[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The prisoner made no Defence in court. However, nine witnesses were called who gave him a good character, describing him as a 'good tempered inoffensive man.'

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: John Scrivener testified that two days before the main incident, the prisoner, whom he knew, asked him to change a £5 note, claiming he was selling cloth on commission and giving a false address of 'No. 27, Tottenham Court-road'.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Joel Cox testified that he believed the prisoner to be the man who came to his shop on November 21st, purchased stamps with a £5 note, and gave the false name 'Mr. John Price, No. 37, St. John-square'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: John Ward, a publican, identified the prisoner and testified that nearly five months prior, Hill passed a £5 note at his house, telling a fabricated story about a new job to gain trust and arrange for partial change.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: John Lees, an inspector of notes for the Bank of England, testified as an expert that all four notes tendered in the separate incidents were 'forged in every respect' and were all printed from the same counterfeit plate.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: John Chew, called for the defense, testified that he had known the prisoner for fifteen years and had 'never knew a stain in his character.'

[EVIDENCE ev02]
Description: The prisoner, John Robinson, was James Dawn's servant and had access to the room containing the desk. Dawn was in the habit of leaving his keys in a table drawer in the same room.
[EVIDENCE ev04]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Dundee, the property owner, testified that he discovered a small fire in Haley's shop over the kitchen staircase, which was quickly extinguished. About half an hour later, a second, much more severe fire erupted from the kitchen below, bursting through the shop floor.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Dundee and Mr. Crombie testified that Haley had a severe dispute with Dundee over the lease, stating he considered Dundee his 'greatest enemy' and would spend 'every sixpence' fighting him. This establishes a motive of animosity.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: James Fosbury and Martha Tulley testified that Haley had attempted to borrow small sums of money (10 pounds and two sovereigns) in the days immediately preceding the fire, indicating he was in 'great distress'.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Watchman William Sharrow testified that on the night of the fire, Haley gave him sixpence for 'a glass of stiff grog', noting that Haley had given him money before for waking him, but never for grog, and never more than threepence.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Priscilla Redman testified that two of Haley's children came to sleep at her house on the Saturday before the fire, and a third joined them on Sunday, the night of the fire. She stated they had never slept there for two nights together before.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Watchman William Sharrow testified that he was alerted by Mrs. Haley screaming from the parlour window that they were 'almost suffocated' by smoke. He was handed an infant and saw the family inside the room in their night clothes.

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Thomas Brown, a watchman, testified that at the watch-house, Larkin produced the pin and asked the prosecutor if it was his. She did not, in his hearing, say she found it on the ground.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Watchman Brown also testified that on the way to the watch-house, Larkin asked Allen if he would 'make it up' if he got his pin. Allen refused.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The prisoners' written defense stated that the prosecutor had accosted Lloyd, accompanied her to Larkin's room, and remained for half an hour. A dispute arose when he only gave her 1s. 6d. after promising half-a-crown.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Daniel Allen testified that Peter Hill, a friend of Larkin, offered him 10s. at the police office to say that he had lost the pin.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Peter Hill, a defense witness, testified that he had a long conversation with the prosecutor, who told him 'he did not know, but he might have dropped the pin'.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The prosecutor's deposition, taken before the Magistrate shortly after the incident, was read in court and found to be 'precisely to the same effect as his evidence in Court.'

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mary Meredith, the prosecutor's wife, testified that on the evening of February 8th, days before the theft, the prisoner William Probert visited their farm, inquired where they kept the mare, and was told it was sometimes in the fold and sometimes in the orchard.

Description: James Stammers, a hostler in Kensington, testified that on February 16th, Probert brought a tired-looking black mare to his stables, claiming to have ridden it 40 miles that day.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William Cozens testified that a note given to Dixon's repository, authorizing the sale of the mare in his name, was a forgery written in Probert's handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Andrew Meredith and his son both positively identified the mare recovered by the officer in London as the one stolen from their property. The son pointed out a white star on her forehead and a specific saddle sore.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Officer James Ellis testified that he apprehended Probert and found bills on him with "Dixon's repository, Barbican" written on the back, which led him to find the stolen mare.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Andrew Meredith testified that he never saw Probert in the local neighbourhood again after the mare went missing on the morning of Friday, February 11th.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Both Andrew Meredith and his wife Mary Meredith testified under oath that they never, on any occasion, lent or offered to lend the mare to Probert.

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The hen house is located in the garden, which is enclosed with the main house. The garden itself is surrounded by a wall on two sides and a fence on the other.
[EVIDENCE ev3]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Joseph Woolf, the victim, testified that his house was secured on the night of June 23rd. He was alarmed by the watchman at 4 a.m. and discovered his shop had been rifled, with property valued at over 100 l. stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Rottenbury, the watchman, testified that around 1:40 a.m., he saw a young man he believed to be William Rippen, the Younger, coming out of Hop-gardens, where the cow-house was located. He knew Rippen Jr. associated with other youths he had seen in the same area moments before.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Joseph Carcass testified that when he informed Rippen Jr. of the search at his father's house, Rippen Jr. responded, 'he thought how it would be,' and then admitted that John Reedy, John Flannergin, and Davy were involved in the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The stolen property was found in the cellar of the elder prisoner's house. Some goods were in a large grape jar, and the rest were in a sack and bundles in a dirty recess.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Philip Woolf, the victim's son, testified that while watching the Rippens' house around 5 a.m., he heard a female voice say, 'They are on the watch now,' and a male voice he believed to be Rippen Sr.'s reply, 'D--n their eyes, let them watch - we have got it safe enough here.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Harriet Tulip, a lodger on the floor below Rippen Sr., testified that she sleeps very lightly and heard Rippen Sr. come downstairs about a quarter past six, stating she 'must have heard if any one had come down' earlier.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Richard Ponting testified that he saw Rippen Jr. with three other boys standing close by the prosecutor's door on the night of the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Several witnesses, including the prosecutor himself, gave the elder prisoner, William Rippen Sr., an excellent character, describing him as an 'honest industrious man' and a 'very honest worthy character.'

public.game_background: Charles Shaw was indicted for stealing a mare valued at 50 l., the property of Henry Tubbs. The prosecution argued that after being entrusted to sell the mare, Shaw refused to hand over the payment, absconded with the mare itself, and disappeared for several months, demonstrating felonious intent. The defense contended that this was a civil dispute over sales commission and payment, and that Shaw's actions did not constitute larceny.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Tubbs and his brother Charles delivered a mare to the prisoner, Charles Shaw, a horse-dealer, with instructions to sell it for no less than 70 l.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: On Sunday, Shaw refused to give the payment order to Charles Tubbs, becoming 'very noisy' and asking if Tubbs thought him dishonest. Shaw attempted to get the order cashed but failed as it was a Sunday.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: There was a dispute over Shaw's compensation. Officer Ballard testified that Shaw claimed Tubbs owed him an account and had promised 3-5 guineas to the General's servant, but Tubbs only thought half a guinea was enough.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: At General Conrad's stables, Shaw got on the mare without a saddle. When Henry Tubbs laid hold of the bridle and said 'Give me up my property,' Shaw made no answer and rode away very fast.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Shaw did not go to his stables as promised. Charles Tubbs testified that he did not see Shaw again until his apprehension on September 23rd, over four months after the incident on May 16th.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Defense witness William Austin testified he has known the prisoner for twenty years, and that Shaw 'kept the stables in Barlow-mews for three months after that' and that Austin had been there with him a month after the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: When the officer asked Shaw where he lodged, he was denied at the address he gave, but it later 'turned out that he did lodge there'.

Description: Alfred Richard Cocker testified that on the night of December 11th, before 11 PM, he personally examined the street door and ensured it was bolted. His family and servants had already retired for the night.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Patrol James Stevens affirmed his identification of the prisoner, stating, 'I had a good view of his features by the gas-light, and am certain he is the man.' He later confirmed the identification at the watch-house.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Watchman William Cummins, who was also on duty, testified that he saw the two men run past him in Gerrard-street. He stated, 'I saw the prisoner's face... and am certain he is the man,' noting a gas-light shone in their faces.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Watchman Cummins also testified that during the pursuit, he saw the prisoner run past Litchfield-street and stop 'up against some shutter, to hide himself, as a watchman was coming by.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Watchman Silas Leach testified that during his pursuit, he found a crow-bar and a lantern on the left-hand side of Grafton-street, which was part of the escape route.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Green testified that he knew the prisoners and that Rigaud orchestrated the signing of an affidavit and a power of attorney by the female prisoner, who used the name Eliza Foster. Green stated he witnessed both signings at Rigaud's request, believing her to be entitled to property.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mr. Watson, a proctor's clerk, testified that the defendant Rigaud personally gave him instructions to obtain letters of administration in the name of Eliza Foster of Dover, naming himself and John Green as sureties.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: A letter signed 'Mary Collins' was read. It was addressed to Eliza Foster and informed her of the death of her aunt, Mary Foster, and the existence of a small sum in the Bank, advising her to administer the estate.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner Rigaud claimed he was innocent and had acted merely as an agent for John Green. He alleged that Green had introduced a different woman to him in Dover as Eliza Foster, and that Green was the true orchestrator of the entire affair.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: In a written statement, the prisoner Baker claimed her only fault was her attachment to Mr. Rigaud. She denied signing any fraudulent papers, stating she only ever signed letters to her mother and sister.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Abraham Ruffell, a former servant of Rigaud, testified that after an unrelated arrest at the house, the prisoner Baker opened Rigaud's desk, gave him papers, and instructed him to 'bury or burn them'.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Henry May, a Bank of England clerk, confirmed the account of Mary Foster existed and that the funds were successfully transferred on August 11, 1825, to 'Eliza Foster, administratrix,' via the power of attorney in question.

Description: Joseph Hart testified that he and his wife left their servant, Ann Thomas, in charge of their house. They returned at three o'clock in the morning to find a watchman at the door, who informed them the house had been broken open.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The servant, Ann Thomas, stated that she had secured the house for the night. Specifically, the kitchen window was hasped, and its inside shutter was closed and fastened with a wooden bar. She also bolted and locked the front door.

Description: Ann Thomas testified that around 2 a.m., she confronted an intruder standing behind a door between the parlours. She asked what he was doing there, and he replied, 'I beg your pardon.' She stated she saw him clearly by the light of the candle in her hand and was sure the prisoner was this man.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Ann Thomas found a pair of men's shoes on the floor of the back kitchen after the intruder had fled.

The defense argued the accident was caused by the reckless driving of the gig's driver, Mr. Farbridge.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The victim's husband, Henry Farbridge, testified that he was driving on the correct (left) side of the road, within three feet of the footpath, leaving ample room for the mail coach to pass him safely.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: George Hudson, the post-master at Barnet, testified that he saw Mr. Farbridge's driving style and feared an accident might happen as he seemed 'not accustomed to drive'.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: John Allsop, a farmer who witnessed the collision from behind, testified that there was 'plenty of room for the mail to have gone more to the right' to avoid the gig.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Edward Rans, the guard on the mail coach, testified that he blew the horn continuously from 150 yards away and that the defendant also shouted a warning to the gig.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Elizabeth King, an eyewitness, testified that Mr. Farbridge's gig was on its wrong side of the road, and that she heard a man named Jervis shout at him 'three or four times to come on his own side'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mrs. Betty, another eyewitness, testified that the mail coach was on its proper side, as near as possible to the church-yard railing, and that Farbridge's chaise 'instantly flew back towards the coach doors'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: James Clark, a public-house keeper, testified that he saw the gig 'dart towards the other side' and that the man in the gig pulled the rein, which turned the back of the gig into the coach.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: John Clay testified that immediately after the accident, he measured the distance from the mail's hind wheel to a post on its side and found there was only the length of his foot between them.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The defendant, Richard Wells, stated that he was driving steadily, that he shouted, and that the gig's wheel became entangled with his coach's wheel.

[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mrs. Dover, a shopkeeper near the crime scene, testified that the alarm was raised around ten minutes or a quarter past eight o'clock, not at 7:30 PM as Fuller claimed.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Lydia Bender testified that the prisoner Bishop was with her at the Hospital public-house from 8:00 PM until 10:00 PM on the night of the robbery, providing him with an alibi.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William Adey and Thomas Sidebottom testified they were with Downes, Ore, and Hackman at the Angel and Trumpet pub from around 8:15 PM until 10:30 PM, providing a consistent alibi for the three men.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: John Lessent and Henry Bond, two weavers, testified they were near the scene, saw the entire robbery, knew some of the actual perpetrators by sight, and swore on their oath that none of the four prisoners were involved.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Greig testified that the prisoner, William Hackwell, had been his groom for fourteen months. He positively identified the cloth found on the prisoner as his property, stating it was taken from his warehouse, which is part of his dwelling-house, and that it had been safe within the last six weeks.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Greig mentioned that the prisoner's friends are respectable and that he believes this to be his first offence.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The victim, Henry Harman, testified that he was at the Black Dog public-house and identified the four prisoners as being present. He stated that three of them sat at his table, while Lovell acted as a waiter.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: At the watch-house, prisoner Lovell said to prisoner Raymond, 'You know who has got the sovereign as well as I do,' to which Raymond replied he did not.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: A search of the prisoners at the watch-house revealed 'several shillings' on Cowley, but nothing on the others. The stolen sovereign was not found.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The defense stated that Harman invited Raymond to drink and was sitting between two 'girls of the town,' directly contradicting Harman's testimony.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Thistle testified that while in a field adjoining his house, he heard a door creak. Upon investigating, he saw the prisoner, John White, about twenty yards from the house with a bundle under his arm.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: After the prisoner was apprehended, Thomas Thistle went back to his house and found one of the ground-floor windows open, with enough room for a person to get in.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Ann Thistle, the prosecutor's daughter-in-law, testified that when she left the house that morning, the windows were both nailed down and she had bolted all the doors.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Ann Thistle identified the two gowns and handkerchief in the bundle as her property, and the shirt as her father's, stating she had seen them safe in the drawers that day.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: James Payne, a constable, testified that he produced the two gowns, shirt, and handkerchief, which were brought to him with the prisoner and had been in his possession ever since.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner claimed he met a man named Milton, who came out of a house with the bundle, said they were his clothes, and asked the prisoner to carry them, promising to overtake him.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Two witnesses were called who gave the prisoner a good character.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Under cross-examination from the prisoner, Thomas Thistle denied having seen another man running away or asking anyone if they had been with the prisoner.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Joseph Whaller, a grazier, testified that on Sunday, July 8th, he saw his four black bullocks safe in his field at Abbey Marsh, Essex. He provided detailed descriptions of each animal's unique markings, such as a white ring around one's tail and a piece chipped off another's horn.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Joseph Pilgrim, a toll-gate keeper, testified that between two and three o'clock on Monday morning, four dusty and panting bullocks passed through his gate. He identified the prisoner Tarnan as the man driving them.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Thomas Harrison, who worked at the Newport-market slaughter-house, testified that the foreman, Saville, woke him at 3:30 AM on Monday to get the keys to the cattle pound, which was an unusually early time to do so.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Harrison testified that about three weeks prior to the incident, Saville had mentioned that 'he expected a countryman was going to fetch him four beasts out of the country.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: John Baker, the clerk for the slaughter-house, testified that he had never known four beasts to be brought in together to be killed for a stranger in his five years there. He also noted the recovered hides lacked a customary 'clip'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Tarnan's defense was that Pilgrim's identification was false and that he had never been in the slaughter-house in his life.

public.game_background: William Baker was indicted for feloniously assaulting Richard Luscomb in Hyde Park on October 29, 1827, putting him in fear, and taking from his person a purse containing 4 sovereigns and 4 shillings, an umbrella, a handkerchief, and a glove.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. William Baker either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Richard Luscomb testified that a man, whom he identified as the prisoner William Baker, approached him near the bason in Hyde Park, insisted he change his route, and when Luscomb refused, the man swore at him, forcibly wrenched his umbrella away, and ran off toward the Serpentine River.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Luscomb positively identified the prisoner in court as the man who robbed him, stating that it was a moon-lit evening and he had a clear view of his face during their encounters.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Under cross-examination, Luscomb admitted that he was a servant who had been out of a situation for about three days at the time of the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Luscomb's reason for being in Hyde Park was that he was going to Cadogan-place to pay a debt, but decided he was too tired and turned back. This was presented as a potentially weak or inconsistent explanation.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: When the prisoner was apprehended and searched at the watch-house, none of the stolen items (purse, sovereigns, shillings) were found on his person.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Abraham Brown, a tailor and independent witness, testified that he was at Cumberland-gate and saw Luscomb raise the alarm. He then saw the prisoner arrive, scuffle with Luscomb, and saw an umbrella and hat fall to the ground before the prisoner ran off.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Abraham Brown testified that at the watch-house, the prisoner publicly offered Luscomb money to be let go, saying 'I will give you 10l. or 20l. or 30l. if you will let me go - I can easily raise it.'
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The witness Abraham Brown noted that the prisoner appeared before the Magistrate the next morning wearing a different coat ('a blue straight coat') than the 'loose great coat' he had on during the night of the robbery.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mary Ann Clements testified that her house was robbed on October 31st while she was away. She listed numerous stolen items including a gold watch, bracelets, twelve silver forks, eight table-spoons, and clothing, estimating the total value at £130 to £140.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mary Ann Green stated that after Aldridge went out, leaving her alone in the house, the prisoner returned about half-past two o'clock, knocking at the door for her to let him in.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: According to Green, Sutton then asked her if she could get him a silver spoon to pledge for money, as he was 'very much distressed.' She refused, stating it would get her and the servant into disgrace.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Green testified that Sutton then went into the parlour, took a basket of silver plate from the sideboard, and went upstairs for a 'good sweep.' He came down five minutes later with two bundles and left the house.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Green stated that before leaving, Sutton instructed her on how to feign a robbery. He told her to clap her hands when the servant returned and exclaim, 'Oh! Bella, I am sure there are thieves in the house, for there is such a noise up stairs.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Both Green and Aldridge testified that immediately after Aldridge discovered the robbery, Sutton appeared and knocked at the door. He then advised Aldridge to 'keep all silent, and to go for an officer,' directing her to a specific butter-shop.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Isabella Aldridge stated that Sutton told her he would wait until she returned with an officer, but he left while she was gone. He also told her 'not to say that he had been to the house.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Officer George Avis testified that when he apprehended the prisoner at Clerkenwell prison visiting Mary Ann Green, the prisoner gave his name as Williams.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Officer Avis stated that upon being arrested, Sutton claimed he was innocent, knew nothing of the robbery, and 'was never in the house in his life.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Mary Ann Green testified that when Sutton saw her in Clerkenwell prison, 'he begged of me to keep my own counsel.'
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Mary Ann Clements stated in court that her wardrobe had not been forced open. When shown her deposition where it stated the wardrobe was forced, she claimed, 'it must be a mistake' and that she never said it.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Two witnesses were called who gave the prisoner a good character.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Victim Charles Chalk's testimony: The prisoner, George Lemon, took a heated cabriolet step out of my hand and struck me with it. About half a minute later, I received a blow to the head which I believe fractured my skull.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Surgeon Edward Headlam's testimony: The prosecutor received a wound on his head from an obtuse cutting edge, like the chisel produced. The blow produced a concussion of the brain, which is attended with danger of life.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mr. Thomas Field Savory testified that the prisoner, Adam Wright, had been in his service for eleven months. He stated that his plate was entrusted to Wright, and to him only.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Savory stated that on April 30th, the prisoner was drunk and excessively abusive. When Mr. Savory mentioned sending for an officer, Wright left the house.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: John Kerry, a pawnbroker's assistant, testified that on April 18th, twelve days before the alleged theft, the prisoner pawned a pair of silver candlesticks and some spoons.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Officer Benjamin Schofield testified that he apprehended the prisoner on Sunday, May 4th, after watching his residence for two days. The prisoner denied the charge upon arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner submitted a written defense. It stated that his character was unexceptionable and that at the time of his apprehension, the prosecutor was in possession of all his property.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Bullock Watts, the victim, testified that his brother, George Watts, after demanding money, fired a pistol while standing within four feet of him. Thomas stated he heard the report, saw the flash, and heard a 'wizzing noise' by the side of his head before grappling with the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The victim, Thomas Watts, testified that he believes his brother 'has been decidedly insane for the last twelve or fourteen months.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Dr. Edward Thomas Munroe, an expert on lunacy, testified that he examined the prisoner and found him to be of 'unsound mind,' believing the condition was of 'some standing' and that the prisoner was insane at the present moment.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: John Fenner, a surgeon, testified to treating the prisoner for a violent epileptic fit in April 1827. He stated that at that time, a year before the incident, the prisoner was 'decidedly insane' and he had warned the family of potential consequences.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Edward Watts, another brother, testified that the prisoner was known to have carried pistols for three years. When asked why, the prisoner gave a delusional reason: 'to prevent his being thrown into a large furnace' where they boiled horses.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Multiple witnesses, including the gaol surgeon William Henry Box and acquaintance William Hull, corroborated the view that the prisoner was insane. Mr. Box found him of 'unsound mind' upon entering prison, and Mr. Hull observed his 'insane state of mind' as early as 1826.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: In his own defense, the prisoner delivered a long, disjointed statement accusing his brother of driving him 'out of my mind' and deceiving him in legal and financial matters. He claimed he had been 'worse than death for years' due to his brother's actions.

information:

Description: William Wall, the prosecutor, testified that having previously missed money, he took measures to discover the thief by setting a trap.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Wall testified that a master-key, which unlocked both his desk and his clerk's desk, was also found on the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: A duplicate for a silver snuff-box was found on the prisoner. Mr. Wall identified the box, which had his name engraved on it, as his property which he had lost at an unknown earlier time.

[EVIDENCE ev14]

information:

Description: Mr. John Lawrence testified that on Sunday, November 9th, he left his chambers at No. 52, Lincoln's Inn-fields locked. When he returned that evening, the door was open, his drawers were ransacked, and a significant portion of his wardrobe and silverware was missing.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Lawrence identified the prisoner, Armstrong McCausland, as the servant of Mr. Gough, another resident of the same building, confirming the prisoner had legitimate access to the premises.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Diana Martin, the housekeeper, testified that on the evening of the robbery, the prisoner borrowed her bunch of keys, claiming he had lost his own. She stated that her keys could open all the chamber doors she attended.

[EVIDENCE ev11]

[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The transcript notes that 'The prisoner received a good character.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The defendant, William Cass, was present at the King's Head public-house from approximately twelve o'clock noon until nine o'clock at night on the day of the theft.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The back door leading to the yard was always kept locked, but the key was kept inside, so anyone within the pub could have opened it and gone out.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Cass was found the next morning at a different pub, the Flask, some 300-400 yards away, and not in the company of Flinn or Cantwell.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense, Cass stated that he left the pub at twenty minutes to nine o'clock through the front door.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Cass stated there were twelve or fourteen navigators in the house. The publican stated only Cass was there in the evening, but also said he didn't think more than four or five persons had been there all day.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Lady Ann Bentinck testified that she had a house in George-street which she had let to a Mr. McNamara. She had left jewels, including a watch, rings, and brooches, in a cabinet in the house. Upon returning on July 2nd, she discovered these items were missing.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Bridget Delany confessed at the second examination that she had stolen the items herself. She claimed a man named Phillips saw the items lying about and encouraged her, saying, 'Take these things, for her ladyship will never miss them.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Officer Goddard, accompanied by Officer Ballard, searched Bridget's room at Kendal-mews and found several pairs of drawers, pantaloons, and waistcoats. Several of these items bore the name of the late Lord Bentinck.

The theft occurred at the Lord High Admiral public-house, where the money was taken from a locked chest in a bedroom adjacent to a club-room the prisoners were occupying.

public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Johanna Budden, wife of the proprietor, testified that the two prisoners came to their public-house separately on Sunday, June 28th, but joined company. She saw them at the house on each of the four following days, where they had refreshments together.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Johanna Budden testified that on the day of the robbery, she went into her bedroom to get money to pay a servant. At that time, she is positive that only the two prisoners were in the adjoining club-room, and that nobody else went upstairs before the money was missed.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Upon trying to enter her bedroom after the prisoners left, Mrs. Budden found she could not insert the key. She then found the door was unlocked, a skeleton-key fell out of the key-hole, her strong chest was burst open, and a crow-bar lay on the floor.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Jeremiah Cochrane, the barman, testified that upon finding Thomas Walker at Fairlop Fair, he gave him into custody. Thomas then rushed through a crowd of men who made way for him, saying he needed to get his umbrella, and fled, with Cochrane in pursuit calling 'Stop thief!'.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Richard Chapman, a waterman, testified that he saw the prisoner John Walker on the coach stand in Aldersgate-street with his coach between three and four o'clock on the afternoon of the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: William Barnett, a constable, testified that on the day of the robbery, Thomas Walker came into his shell-fish shop in Borough-road between half-past three and four in the afternoon and stayed for nearly half an hour. Barnett remembered the time and day because it was the day before Fairlop Fair, and he had been on duty and heard of the arrest on the following Saturday.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Constable William Barnett testified that Thomas Walker's brother, William, is 'a deal stouter' but 'very like him in the face'. He stated that 'any one who knew them would know them to be brothers from their faces.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Nicholson testified that while he was out of town, his locked portmanteau in his room at the Manchester coffee-house was broken into. Property worth more than 10l., including a military cloak, was stolen from the portmanteau and from unlocked drawers in the room.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Caroline Yeo, daughter of the coffee-house keeper, testified that the prisoner was in the shared room for most of the day he left. She saw him leave in the afternoon with a middling-sized bundle.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Both Caroline Yeo and her brother Frederick testified that the bundle the prisoner carried out was not large enough to contain a military cloak, one of the key items reported stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: James Strowger testified that when he encountered the prisoner four months after the robbery, the prisoner denied knowing the Manchester coffee-house.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: James Strowger stated that the prisoner, when confronted, also falsely claimed to have been abroad for eight months and to have just arrived in England.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The owner's son, William Yeo, testified that the name 'Manchester coffee-house' is painted on the house.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Witnesses for the prosecution stated under cross-examination that the bedroom door was never locked and that a chambermaid who cleaned the room had since been discharged, although not on suspicion.

The defense claims the sheep in question was a stray that wandered into the flock, which Holbeck separated with the innocent intention of returning it to its rightful owner.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Testimony from Thomas Procter, a master drover, stating that the prisoner, Thomas Holbeck, had worked for him for two to three years. On November 2nd, Procter left Holbeck in charge of 205 sheep in Cock-lane, Smithfield, for about an hour.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Henry Simmons, who works in Cock-lane, testified that Holbeck came to him and asked to put a sheep in their shop, claiming his young master had given him permission.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Holbeck stated that the red mark down the face was common on 'thousands of sheep' and denied there was a mark on the nose, attempting to weaken the specific identification.

Description: Mr. Grant stated that although he wore green spectacles for a slight inflammation, his 'sight was as perfect as it is now' as the inflammation was on the side of his eye, not in the pupil.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Daniel Benjamin Leadbitter, a marshalsman, testified that when he apprehended the prisoner, he searched him and 'found nothing on him but 1s. and a pocket handkerchief.' The diamond pin was not found.

Crane was apprehended in possession of the stolen goods. Saggers was accused as an accomplice due to his role in employing Crane as an occasional porter.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Constable John Salter testified that on the morning of January 22nd, he met Jeremiah Crane carrying a large load tied in a sheet. Crane claimed he was going to Islington but had no invoice and did not explain his possession of the goods. The sheet was found to contain seven pieces of flannel.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Hill, an employee at a wine-vaults, testified that Crane came into his shop with the load and said he was going to Shoreditch. This contradicts Crane's statement to Constable Salter that he was going to Islington.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: In his defense, Crane claimed he was hired by a man in Cripplegate-buildings who looked like a porter. This man allegedly paid him 6d. to carry the load to Goswell-road and promised another 6d. upon arrival.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Tweedale testified that Thomas Saggers was his employee, and that Saggers was the person who typically employed and paid occasional extra porters, including Jeremiah Crane, whom Tweedale had seen on the premises before.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Tweedale testified that Saggers went willingly to Guildhall on two separate occasions at his request, before being taken into custody. Saggers did not manifest any unwillingness to assist in identifying Crane.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Tweedale confirmed that he went to Saggers' lodgings, but found nothing belonging to the firm there.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The warehouseman, Henry Weston, admitted under cross-examination that he could not swear the flannel had not been sold by the firm's clerk, John Brenan, who was not present at the trial. Weston also stated the goods might never have been missed due to the extensive stock.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Four witnesses were called who gave the prisoner Crane a good character. Additionally, William Taylor testified that he had known Crane for two or three years and considered him an honest man.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mr. Tweedale testified that Saggers himself had stated that the wrapper the flannel was found in belonged to their firm.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: On cross-examination, John Sheering admitted his profession was a 'resurrectionist' (body-snatcher) for the past five years. He also admitted to being in trouble 'four or five times,' including for vagrancy and obtaining money under false pretences.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Wix testified that upon returning to his office, he found the latch of the door was forced and the drawer was broken. He produced the broken catch in court.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Wix testified that the prisoner Nash had been employed at the hospital to assist in carrying coals and 'knew the place very well indeed.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: John Gill testified that on May 27th, he saw Nash with two men, one of whom he believed to be Hurley. He heard Nash say, 'The time to do the job is between nine and ten o'clock in the morning.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: William Glyde, a hospital porter, testified that he saw Nash and Sheering outside the hospital's center gate between nine and ten o'clock on the morning of the robbery.

Description: William Benfield, the owner, testified that the articles listed in the indictment were used in his business and were safe in his shop a quarter of an hour before they were taken. He also confirmed that the shop is part of his dwelling-house, where he resides.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Sanderson, the shopman, stated: 'I saw the prisoner going out of the shop, with the articles in his possession - I had not seen him come in, as I was curling a child's hair.' He testified that he immediately pursued the prisoner.

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: A second witness, William Arnsby, testified that he was passing by and saw the prisoner 'skulking' under the shop window just before the incident.

[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: John Moye testified that the prisoner offered to sell him the horse and chaise-cart for thirty-four guineas, stating explicitly that the property 'belonged to him'.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Trueman Machin, a stable-keeper, testified that the prisoner brought the horse and cart to his stables on May 10th (the day they were due back to Macbeth) and kept them there until May 18th, paying for their keep as if he were the owner. The stables were within a mile and a half of Macbeth's own stables.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: John Macbeth testified that when the chaise-cart was returned, his name and address, which had been painted on it in full, had been removed.
[EVIDENCE ev9]

[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: During cross-examination, the prisoner attempted to discredit John Moye by accusing him of being a 'notorious swindler' who goes by the name of Smith and whose stock consists of 'brick-dust and sawdust'. Moye denied all accusations.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner questioned John Macbeth whether they had been 'in treaty between two and three years ago, to purchase the horse and cart'. Macbeth denied this, stating there was no bargain and the prisoner had only asked if he would part with it.

Description: Thomas Jefferson Holt, the homeowner, testified that his house was secured around 11 PM. At about 2 AM, an alarm bell rang, and upon investigating he discovered a lit dark-lantern in the cellar.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Holt stated that the cellar's iron grating had been secured by a strong iron chain, which he found broken by violence. The curb of the grating was also pressed, seemingly by a crow-bar, indicating a forcible entry.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: In the cellar, tools including a crow-bar, a centre-bit, and a chisel were found by John Smith and Inspector John Laws.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Upon being searched at the watch-house, the prisoner was found to have five skeleton-keys, a bag containing the stock of a centre-bit, a chisel, a gimlet, a knife, and a phosphorus-box with matches.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: On cross-examination, Mr. Holt stated that the inner door leading from the cellar to the upper part of the house had not been broken or opened. Access to the main living quarters and the jeweller's stock was not achieved.

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Watchmen Thomas Nevell and Edward Ganer testified that at around 3:15 AM, they saw two or three men exit the front door of the victim's house, each carrying a bundle. They found the door wide open and a lantern on the floor, which raised their suspicions and caused them to give chase.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On the night of the incident, the prisoner, William Parrott, called on Ann Chell, appearing sober. He said he was expecting a letter or message from his wife, Harriet Parrott.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: When Mrs. Parrott arrived, the prisoner got up, caught her by the hand, pushed her into a corner, and asked her to live with him again.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mrs. Parrott refused to live with him, stating she could not because she was in danger of her life. The prisoner was in the kitchen at the time but could hear what was said.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mrs. Parrott's throat was bleeding profusely. She cried out, 'The instrument is in my throat!' The prisoner replied, 'No, it is not,' and then threw a razor away, which was later found by the officer to be bloody.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Ann Chell, an eyewitness, testified that the prisoner did not cry out, 'Oh, God! what have I done?' immediately after the act.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: After his wife was seated and bleeding, the prisoner approached her and said, 'Good bye, perhaps we shall meet again in another world.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: While waiting for the doctor, the prisoner sat down opposite Ann Chell, 'took up the beer, and drank the officer's good health.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The surgeon, Richard Bradford, testified that the wound was four or five inches long and laid the large vessels bare. He stated that if the wound had not gone upwards, it 'must have caused instant death.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Richard Bradford, the surgeon, testified that he heard the prisoner say 'I committed it' in the room.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Harriet Parrott, the victim, testified that her husband had previously been in a 'flighty and deranged state of mind when he has had a little to drink' and that his actions were 'like a mad man sometimes'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Harriet Parrott testified that the prisoner attempted to destroy himself five weeks before the attack, the day after she had left him.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The prisoner told the officer he had borrowed the razor from his employer, Mr. Sidney, to shave himself.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The Jury found the prisoner Guilty but recommended him to Mercy 'on account of his former apparent aberration.' The Prosecutrix (the victim) also joined in this recommendation.

He sealed and directed it to Mrs. Elizabeth Carkeet in Falmouth and placed it in the office's letter-rack for the messenger.

Description: Elizabeth Carkeet of Falmouth, the intended recipient, testified that she never received the letter or the two Bank post-bills.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: William Baker, a messenger at the General Post-office, produced the attendance-book showing that John Barrett was on duty on the evening of November 21st, employed as a sorter of paid and franked letters.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Ann Brunt testified that on December 7th, the defendant Barrett came to her house with a bundle under his arm, which he gave to her to take care of. He and Kay slept in her house that night.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense, Barrett stated: 'I merely wish to say I am not guilty of stealing from the office, nor of breaking the seals, but I received them out of the office from another person.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: A Post-office clerk, McMahon, testified that letters 'are all sorted promiscuously by different people.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: John Barrett was also indicted in two other cases (294, 295) for embezzling letters from the post office on November 21st and 22nd, to which he pleaded guilty. This information was available to the court.

Description: A letter, purportedly from the prisoner and containing a forged bill of exchange for £200, was delivered to the counting-house of Fletcher, Son, and Co. on October 9th, 1829.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. John Myrie Holl, a partner at the firm, identified the prisoner as a person he had seen at their counting-house before, despite significant changes to his appearance (shaved whiskers, different wig).
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The prisoner had previously conducted business with Fletcher & Co., specifically receiving a £500 advance on a bill of lading which he endorsed.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Fletcher testified that he had seen the prisoner write on two occasions, including endorsing the previous bill of lading.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Testimony was given that James Anderton of Berwick-upon-Tweed, the supposed drawer of the bill, could not be found.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Gabriel Meyer, formerly a clerk for Makin and Co. (the drawees), testified with certainty that the acceptance on the bill was not the handwriting of either partner in the firm.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mr. Meyer testified he had known the prisoner for nearly twenty years and that the prisoner was aware of Makin and Co. through his father's business.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William Coates testified that he took the letter to Fletcher & Co. at the prisoner's specific direction.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The banker's clerk, looking over the books, stated 'we appear to have paid bills drawn on them by Anderton,' which contradicts other testimony.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner claimed he was an innocent party, having been deceived and 'plundered' by a man named Usher who subsequently fled to America.

The prosecution alleges the will was fabricated after Friend's death to fraudulently claim his estate, while the defense maintains it is a genuine document.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Beams, a proctor's clerk, testified that Andrew Morgan brought the purported will of George Friend to his office to obtain probate. Morgan described himself as the executor and a relative of the deceased.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mary Ann Pringle, whose name appears as a witness, testified that she never saw the deceased. She stated that Morgan brought the will to her ready-written with her name already signed, and asked if she could write like the signature, which she could not.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Elizabeth Harding, who knew the deceased for forty years, testified that in January 1832, George Friend was extremely infirm. She stated he walked with two sticks, his knees lapped over one another, and could 'scarcely drag one foot after the other,' making a trip from his home to Whitechapel highly improbable.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Elizabeth Harding testified that the evening before Friend died, he told her he had made no will. She also stated he had no nephew, and his only known living relatives were two female second cousins.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Ann Stackwood testified that two days after Friend's funeral, she met Morgan and Fallon at the Seven Stars pub. After she mentioned her husband was guarding a deceased person's property near the Minories, Morgan became inquisitive and asked for a newspaper.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: John Cowderoy testified that the defendant Dalton asked him to receive a letter at his lodging-house under the false name 'Teighson.' When the letter arrived from North Shields, Dalton opened it in Cowderoy's presence, and it was completely blank inside.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: James Notman, a relative of the next-of-kin, testified that Thomas Fallon came to him and admitted the will was a forgery. Fallon stated it was written on March 24th and that Morgan and the other parties had never seen the deceased.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Officer James Lea testified that a search of defendant Dalton's trunk revealed blank forms for seamen's wills, other forged or partly-filled wills, and a memorandum of expenses. The memorandum listed costs for 'the letter 4l. 12s. 6d., for the witnesses 3l. 2s. 6d.'

[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On April 13, 1832, a proposal was made in the prisoner's name to the British Fire-office for a £700 insurance policy on household goods, stock, and fixtures at the premises. The premium was paid, making the policy active even though not yet delivered.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The prisoner was in arrears for rent. After a distress was levied, he still owed £20 and was given a final deadline to pay on Monday, the day of the fire.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The prisoner brought two sacks of shavings to the house on Sunday. He was observed clearing out the vault and arranging old boards, boxes, and the shavings in the kitchen and vaults.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: A full wine-bottle of turpentine was on the kitchen mantelpiece. On Sunday evening, the servant Sarah Smith noticed a very strong smell of turpentine on the stairs, which she said was not present on Saturday.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Witness Henry Cadel and fireman William Moakes testified there were two distinct and separate fires: one in the back vault, and another at the bottom of the kitchen staircase.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner was first seen in the area under the neighboring shop. He was unburnt and asked to be let out, but did not ask the witness to alarm the house where five or six females were sleeping.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Lodger Samuel Davis testified that when Mrs. Smithies met the prisoner in the yard after escaping, she said, 'You wretch... you have not been in bed all night; you have set the house on fire on purpose to murder me.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The prisoner told police he believed the fire started when veneers he placed near the kitchen fire ignited. However, witness Davis, a cabinet-maker, stated that wood for veneers is best kept in a hot, dry place.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The servant Sarah Smith testified that when she went to bed at 11 p.m. on Sunday, there was no fire at all in either the kitchen or parlour grate.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev01]
Description: The key testimony came from Joshua John James, the 15-year-old son of the prosecutor's clerk. He confessed his involvement and detailed the entire plot, stating that Richard Smith and George Coney planned the robbery, instructed him to get an impression of the warehouse key, used his information to prepare, and that he ultimately left the house door ajar for them to enter on the night of the crime.
[EVIDENCE ev03]
Description: Nicholas James, Joshua's father, testified that a week before the robbery he found a tin box of soap in his son's pocket. Joshua snatched it away and ran out. A toy cart made from the lid of a tin box was later seen in the house, and Officer Forrester found a corresponding piece of tin at the prisoners' residence at 47 Red Lion-street.
[EVIDENCE ev04]
Description: Housebreaking tools, including a crow-bar, skeleton keys, a dark-lantern, and whipcord, were found left behind in the warehouse. Joshua James testified that Coney had told him they would leave their tools behind 'to wipe off the suspicion from my father, and to show that it was done by the profession.'
[EVIDENCE ev05]
Description: Two odd worsted socks were found at the crime scene. At the prisoners' residence at 47 Red Lion-street, officers found the bottoms of two stockings with the tops cut off. A hosier, Thomas Clark, testified that the pieces found at the two separate locations 'undoubtedly belonged' and originally formed a single pair of stockings.
[EVIDENCE ev06]
Description: The family dog, which was usually kept inside, was found outside in the morning with a cord tied around its neck. A piece of cord, which officers testified was 'precisely the same sort of cord', was found in a shed at the prisoners' residence at 47 Red Lion-street.
[EVIDENCE ev07]
Description: A silver waxer and two silver bodkins were found at 47 Red Lion-street several days after the robbery. Mr. Wheeler, the owner, testified with high confidence that they were his property, as they exactly matched the unique manufacturing dies he used in his business.
[EVIDENCE ev08]
Description: Multiple witnesses (Mary King, Hannah Wrenalds, Thomas Dugard) established that Richard Smith was an acquaintance of Joshua James and was present at the Wheeler house the day before the robbery. Multiple other witnesses established that both Coney and Smith frequented 47 Red Lion-street.
[EVIDENCE ev09]
Description: James Cronin, a warehouseman, testified that on the Monday morning after the robbery, Richard Smith came to the premises asking for Joshua. Smith initially claimed he hadn't seen Joshua since Saturday, but later admitted to Mr. Wheeler that he had met him on Sunday. Cronin reminded him of the contradiction, and Smith claimed it was a mistake.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: On the morning after the robbery, two witnesses saw a coach arrive at 47 Red Lion-street, from which a trunk was brought out and placed in the coach. Shortly after the coach departed, Richard Smith was seen exiting the house.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Nicholas James testified that on the night of the robbery, he was unwell and asleep for the first few hours. He admitted under cross-examination that his son, Joshua, *might* have been able to get out of the bed they shared for an hour or two without his knowledge.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Seven witnesses gave the prisoner Smith a good character. Three witnesses gave the prisoner Coney a good character.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Around 4:30 PM on April 17th, George Newport, the homeowner, saw two men crossing the road from his house. One wore a great coat with a fur collar similar to his own, and the other carried a bundle. He could not positively identify them at this time.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mr. Newport testified that he recognized two of the prisoners, Guest and Richardson, as they had been in his shop on the Saturday week before the robbery. He also stated that Richardson had been on his staircase on the Monday prior, asking for a lodger.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Sarah Ann Newport, the daughter, testified that around 4:00 PM, she looked into the yard and saw a man she positively identified as Thomas Guest. She recognized him from his visit to the shop on the Saturday week previous. About ten minutes later, her father raised the alarm.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: After being apprehended, stolen coats were found being worn by the prisoners. Officer Bolton found a stolen coat on Guest. Officer Pocock testified that Richardson was wearing two stolen body coats.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: At the Bow-street station, Officer Pocock observed Richardson concealing a skeleton key under his seat. Upon searching Richardson's hat, Pocock found eight silver spoons and a set of beads, all later identified as stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The coachman, Charles Saggers, identified Richardson as the man who gave confusing and contradictory directions for the coach's destination, changing from Portland-road to Waterloo-road, and then demanding to be turned around to go over Blackfriars-bridge.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: A large collection of property found in the coach and on the persons of Guest and Richardson was positively identified by George Newport and Sarah Ann Newport as the items stolen from their home.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Jones submitted a written defense claiming he met the others by chance, agreed to help them carry bundles, and put the coats on their backs for convenience. He stated he knew nothing of the robbery and ran from the coach out of fear and to avoid disgrace.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Character witnesses were called for Guest and Richardson. Charles Kay and Maria Swintall deposed to the previous good character of Guest, and John Wildsmith testified for Richardson.

[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: John Rutland, a waiter, testified that on the morning of March 18th, Belcher came into his public-house and offered to sell him a scent-bottle with a silver top for 6d.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Watchman John Alsford saw Belcher on the morning of March 18th looking at the ground with something glistening in his cap. He later saw Belcher in the company of Thomas at a public-house.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Sergeant James Izatt testified that while escorting Capel, Capel confessed that only himself, Paradise, and Belcher were involved in the crime, and said they broke the bolts 'with his hands'.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Watchman William Ward testified that upon being arrested for a separate assault, Barrett said, 'I know more than they do, for Thomas knows nothing about it.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Sergeant-major George Chapman questioned Paradise, who admitted being with Belcher on the night of the 17th but denied being in the area of the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Constable William Thomas Chittenden testified that, based on information from Thomas, he went to where Thomas slept and it was confirmed that he had slept there on the night of the burglary.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Watchman John Noyes testified to seeing Thomas with Belcher and another man. When Noyes approached, the other two ran away, but Thomas did not attempt to flee.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Pawnbroker Edward Charter stated that a marine offered him two brooches for sale. He believed it was Barrett but admitted he 'cannot positively swear it'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Langham M'Lin testified that he and his wife left their apartment on the evening of April 9th, between eight and nine o'clock. His wife locked the door and took the key with her.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: M'Lin testified that upon returning home around ten o'clock, he found the padlock's staple drawn and the door half open. He listed numerous items that were stolen, including clothing, a pistol, a watch, and a £5 Bank-note.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: M'Lin recounted going to Mary Townshend's residence on Sunday. Townshend initially claimed Fry and Castle were not there but had gone home. However, upon entering, M'Lin found Fry, Rearden, Castle, Townshend, and Finnagin all present.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police Sergeant Richard Hancock testified that during the initial search of Townshend's room, where all five prisoners were present, nothing was found on any of them.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Policeman George North testified that he returned to Townshend's house, and in a lower room for which Townshend provided the key, he lifted a hearthstone and found a bundle of the stolen property, including clothes, gowns, and a £5 note.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Policeman George North stated that he found the chisels at the house of the prisoner Fry's mother. He also noted that the chisels were 'not an uncommon size.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: It was revealed during cross-examination that William Hermitage had previously been sentenced to three months at Coldbath-fields for an offense involving wax, and had been taken up on suspicion before that.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The defendants offered defenses. Fry and Rearden stated they knew nothing about the crime. Townshend and Finnagin claimed ignorance of how the property came to be in the house.

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mary Creighton, the prosecutor's wife, testified that Sullivan warned her in the pub, 'Keep your husband out of our way; if you don't, as sure as he has got a head on he will suffer for it.' This was said outside of her husband's hearing.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Defense witness Catherine Kelly testified she overheard Creighton provoke Sullivan in the street after they left the pub, calling him a 'cuckold' and claiming he often slept with Sullivan's wife for a glass of gin.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner, Michael Sullivan, stated in his defense that Creighton followed him home, wanted to fight, and struck him first. He claimed he only hit Creighton in the face, causing him to fall because he was very drunk.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: On cross-examination, Michael Creighton admitted to having been confined for one month for stealing sugar eight or nine years prior.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mary Creighton admitted that she and her husband had been drinking at two other pubs prior to the Cross Keys, but claimed her husband 'was none the worse for liquor.' Sullivan's defense claims he was 'so drunk, was not able to stand.'

Description: Testimony of Mary Ann Thomas: Her husband, the prisoner, was drinking at a public-house. On their way home across a field, he struck her on the head with his fist and threatened her, saying, 'Bloody into you, I will have your life, or you shall have mine.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Testimony of Mary Ann Thomas: She stated the prisoner was 'not so very drunk but he well knew what he was doing.' She also testified to a history of severe abuse, stating, 'I have suffered a martyrdom by him.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Prisoner's Defense: The prisoner stated, 'She has taken every thing from me that would fetch 2d—I never hit her, nor nothing—it is all false what she has been swearing.' He later added that she sells and pawns his belongings.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Testimony of Policeman Jonathan Young: The prisoner admitted to a minor assault ('I only gave my wife a spat on the nose'), then stated he hoped she was dead so he would 'be hung for it,' and later threatened, 'If I get over this here, I will buy a gun, and I will blow her brains out with it.'

[EVIDENCE ev1]

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Thomas Young, a clerk at the Bank of England, testified from the stock-ledger that the sum of £7814 5s. 9d. stood in the three trustees' names until December 7, 1835, when it was transferred to Henry Bilke.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Rev. William Addison Fountaine, one of the trustees, testified that the signature 'Wm. Fountaine' on the power of attorney was not his writing and that he never executed such a document.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prisoner's counsel, Mr. Bodkin, stated to the court that it was the prisoner's wish to admit his guilt, there being sufficient legal proof, and that he had only abstained from pleading guilty to explain the circumstances.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner stated that his crime was driven by 'necessity, and not inclination,' due to immense pecuniary difficulties that threatened him with prison, professional ruin, and distress for his family.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The prisoner explained he used the money to discharge debts of over £4700 and invested nearly £2000 in a discount trade, believing he could realize a handsome income to replace the stock.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Rev. Fountaine, one of the victims of the fraud, testified that he had known the prisoner for twenty years and that he had always moved in respectable society and borne a good character until this transaction.

[EVIDENCE ev1]

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Richard Greening, the victim's son, testified that he had worked in the shed until eight o'clock on the night of the robbery. He stated he fastened the door with a bolt and bar and confirmed everything was secure, including the roosting fowls, when he checked again around half-past eleven.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Eleanor Cooper, a washerwoman, stated that on the evening of the robbery, she had washed the silk handkerchief and left it hanging wet on a line inside the shed.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Thomas Balliston, a watchmaker, identified the recovered handkerchief as his property, pointing out his initials 'T. B.' marked on it.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Police officer Joseph Fearne testified that while being taken to the station-house, Cockett said, 'It was Yarroll led me into this,' and that he would confess the whole matter before the Magistrate.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Elizabeth Ann Yarroll, the defendant's sister, testified that she heard Officer Fearne state, in relation to a different case, that 'the next he got hold of he would hold pretty tight, and would not be humbugged out of them.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Defense witnesses James Ward and Richard Spencer, both neighbors, testified that they had frequently seen the prosecutor's fowls wandering into their own yards. Ward stated he once found them in his water-closet.

Description: The prisoner's defense was that he never went into the house at all. He claimed he heard a noise, went down into the area, found the great coat on a step, and went to sleep in the coal cellar where the policeman found him.

Description: Mary Ann Austin testified that on the Tuesday night she was staying with the prisoner, he came back upstairs after pretending to go to the yard. She said to him, "You have not been down the yard," to which he replied, "No, it was too cold." He then cut her throat without any further words or provocation.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: James Thomas Vallance, a surgeon, testified that upon examining the prosecutrix's wound, he concluded from its nature that she did not inflict it herself. He attended her for nineteen days.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Vallance testified that the prisoner's wound was worse than the victim's, with his windpipe nearly half divided. When asked by the jury if he could tell from its appearance, he confirmed the wound was self-inflicted.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Surgeon James Thomas Vallance testified that he saw no symptoms of insanity in the prisoner's conduct and noted that he was not drunk on the night of the incident. He observed no signs of madness in the prisoner's conversation once he was able to speak.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner's defense statement was, "I have no knowledge of the crime; I had no malice against any one."

Description: Ebenezer Ward, the innkeeper, testified that he did not hear anyone knock at the door after he shut up the house. He stated, 'I sleep in the front of the house, and generally hear if any body knocks.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Ebenezer Ward also testified that around midnight he heard Thorpe 'hallooing about the town.' He thought the man was calling 'Ward' to awaken him, not 'Watch'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Innkeeper Ebenezer Ward corroborated Thorpe's testimony that the prisoners were in the house with him and that they all left together around eleven o'clock. Ward also stated that Thorpe was sober.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Constable Charles James Murray testified that when he found the defendants, Kedge was in bed and Stevens was lying on the floor at the same lodging.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Constable Murray stated that when Thorpe saw Stevens, he immediately recognized him, saying, 'That is the man that put his foot on my breast, and took my bag from me'.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Constable Murray testified that as he was taking Stevens to the cage, Stevens said, 'I know what you want me for, you want me for the highway robbery.'

The prisoner slept alone in the captain's state-room, which was where the wooden case represented to contain silver plate was stored.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: David Lee, the steward, testified that on the evening of August 5th, he went down to the cabin and saw the prisoner with the lid of the box partially off. Lee stated he saw the prisoner using a knife to pry the rest of the lid off, with a hammer lying nearby.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On October 21st, Michael Harris, appearing respectable with a silver guard-chain, entered the pawnbroker's shop. He offered a watch, a ring, and a brooch, asking to borrow £10 on them.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The watch was identified as a metal 'duffin watch' made to deceive, with forged Goldsmith's Hall marks. Its value was estimated at 25s to 50s. The ring and brooch were also of low value, around 12s-15s each.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Testimony established that No. 1 Woodstock-street, Knowles's real address, is not easily mistaken for No. 8 Paddington-street. Woodstock-street is an inferior street, and a dead wall separates the two locations.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Pawnbroker George Webb testified that the same watch was a 'duffin watch' made for deception. He had previously been deceived by it himself, advancing £6 on it before it was sold unredeemed at auction for £2 9s.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Knowles claimed he owed Harris £8 and told Harris to pawn the items and pay himself from the proceeds.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Upon his arrest, Knowles told the police officer that regarding the watch, he 'believed part of it was gold, and part was not.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Several witnesses testified to Knowles's good character, stating they had known him for years as an 'honest, respectable tradesman.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: A defense witness, Roger Brown, testified that he did not think Knowles was a good judge of a watch because 'he is very short-sighted—he has had very bad eyes.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
