[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Tea-dealer Coates Fennell testified that a woman representing herself as Boone's wife ordered tea worth nearly £6 on credit from his business. The goods were delivered, but no payment was ever received.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Grocer James Bennett testified he was defrauded of goods worth over £14, first by a woman claiming to be Boone's mother, and then by Boone himself who promised payment but failed to provide it.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Coates Fennell testified that when he apprehended Boone, Boone denied knowing anyone named 'Quantock' but admitted he knew 'Collier'. Later, at Guildhall, after Quantock's real name was revealed, Boone was seen whispering with him at the bar.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Josiah Dimsdale, landlord of No. 9, Fann-street, testified he let the house to a 'Thomas Collier' based on an excellent character reference given by defendant Quantock. 'Collier' later absconded without paying rent.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: James Baldwin, a paper-hanging maker, testified that Quantock gave a strong positive reference for a man named 'Collier,' persuading Baldwin to provide goods on credit. This 'Collier' also absconded without payment.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Witnesses Charles Barnard and Robert Fenton testified that Boone, using the alias 'Thomas Collier', set up another fraudulent shop on St. Andrew's-hill. He obtained goods on credit from them before absconding again.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prosecutor, John Starkey, testified that after detecting his servant, Elizabeth Starkie, stealing, he watched her and eventually traced her to an area where the prisoner, John Bell, lived. He observed Bell assemble a truck, bring two large boxes from a nearby house (No. 5, Anthony-street), and transport them towards St. Katharine's Docks.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Elizabeth Chason also testified that Bell himself had brought the boxes to her house on the preceding Sunday, four days before he came to take them away. She overheard conversations between Bell and Starkie about going to Hull to get out of the country.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police-sergeant William Sawyer testified that at the station-house, he heard Starkie say to Bell, 'These things that are marked 'J. B.' are your property.' Bell replied, 'Oh, you d—soft one—if I am asked the question I will tell the truth... you have no right to bring any more into It than are really guilty,' and cautioned her against talking.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Officer James Lea testified that he found two keys hanging over the chimney-piece in the room where Bell slept at No. 2, Lower Chapman-street. These keys opened both of the boxes that Bell was transporting.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Henry Fowler, a pawnbroker's shopman, testified that he had a pair of shoes pawned with him by 'Ann Bell' on the 19th of February. These shoes were among the articles stolen from one of Mr. Starkey's guests.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: James Gill, who took possession of the prisoner's premises, found fourteen pawn duplicates hidden in the oven, which he gave to Officer Lea.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner's defense was that he knew nothing about the matter and was simply hired for 18 pence to transport the truck.

[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: John Perryman, one of the debtors, testified that he had already paid his debt of 4s. 4d. in full to Hurren by January 29, 1838, months before Hurren took money to summon him.
[EVIDENCE ev7]

information:

Description: John Goodman, foreman to the tailor Mr. Wood, testified that he met the prisoner, an old acquaintance, who claimed he was going to Jamaica. Goodman offered to make him an outfit, and the prisoner ordered several items, agreeing it would be a 'ready money order.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Inspector Symons testified that he made inquiries and confirmed that the Northern and Central Bank at Liverpool had stopped payment in January 1837, over 18 months before the prisoner wrote the cheque.

Description: Skidmore Ashby, a banker, testified that on March 22nd, the prisoner George Pizzey brought a bill of exchange for 50l with John Cole's mark as acceptor and asked the bank to discount it. The bank paid the money to the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Ashby stated that the bank had discounted several previous bills for the prisoner with the same mark. All of these prior bills had been paid by the prisoner himself.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The banker, Mr. Ashby, admitted that the firm had suspicions about the authenticity of the acceptance on the bill immediately preceding the one in question.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Cole, the farmer whose name is on the bill, testified that he cannot write and uses a mark. He stated he did not make the cross on this bill of exchange, nor did he authorize the prisoner to do so.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: John Cole acknowledged that about eighteen months prior, the prisoner had asked to use his name to get money from a loan club, but Cole refused permission.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William Cole, the son of John Cole, testified that the signature 'Witness to the mark of John Cole, William Cole,' was not written by him and that he knew nothing about the bill.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: John Anning, a schoolmaster, testified that he drew up the body of the bill of exchange at the prisoner's request. At that time, the names of the acceptor (John Cole) and witness were not on the document.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Anning, the schoolmaster, stated that he had known the prisoner for about a year and a half and had never heard anything against him.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: James Pizzey, the prisoner's 16-year-old son, testified that he overheard a conversation where John Cole told his father he could not write, but gave him permission to 'sign his hand on a bit of paper for him.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: A witness named William Rose, a cattle-dealer from Sunbury, was called by the defense and gave the prisoner a good character.

Description: Duffey admitted under cross-examination that he had known Sievers for eight or nine years and their business had always been conducted honorably until this incident.

[EVIDENCE ev17]

public.game_background: James Young was indicted for a misdemeanor, accused of fraudulently obtaining one sovereign from William Mole by presenting a forged document purporting to entitle a sailor, John Freeman, to £75 in prize money, and falsely representing that he had verified the document's authenticity.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. James Young either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_wm_main]
Description: William Mole testified that James Young and a sailor came to his house. Young produced a paper stating the sailor, 'John Freeman', was due £75 prize money and asked for a sovereign loan until December 12th, promising 10s for the loan. Young claimed he had been to Mr. Goode's office to check the books and that 'it was quite right', which induced Mole to give him the sovereign.
[EVIDENCE ev_make_up]
Description: James Mole and policeman Lambert Wilkinson both testified that after his arrest, Young offered to pay back the sovereign to 'make it up' and avoid further trouble.
[EVIDENCE ev_poverty]
Description: Young argued in his defense that he was destitute ('had not a shilling to bless myself with') and thus could not have claimed to have paid 2s to search the agent's books. William Mole confirmed Young's financial distress, having recently redeemed a pawned brooch for him.
[EVIDENCE ev_young_defense]
Description: The prisoner, James Young, claimed he had no idea the certificate was fraudulent and was only trying to help a man in difficulties. He stated he advised William Mole to go to the agent's office to verify the certificate himself, but Mole declined, saying 'There is no occasion for that.'
[EVIDENCE ev_davis_role]
Description: Testimony from both sides indicates a man named Davis, who lodged with the Moles, introduced Young and the sailor. Young claimed Davis initiated the idea of asking Mole for a loan. Mole confirmed Davis told him that Young needed money badly and wanted the sailor to borrow it so Young could have it.
[EVIDENCE ev_just_testimony]
Description: A defense witness, William Just, testified that he was in Young's house after the arrest and heard William Mole say, 'If you can make up the money it will prevent the prosecution.' Just claimed Mole even admitted it would be a 'compound felony' but said he would take the money anyway.

public.game_background: Thomas Spicer is charged with wilful and corrupt perjury. The prosecution alleges that Spicer falsely testified under oath during the civil trial of Harris v. Pridmore. In that trial, Spicer claimed he had tendered a payment of £2 18s. to the plaintiff, Charles Robert Harris, on August 15th, 1839, which Harris had refused. The prosecution aims to prove this testimony was a fabrication.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Thomas either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Mountain, a clerk from the Sheriff's Court, testified that he administered the oath to Thomas Spicer on the trial of Harris and Pridmore. He confirmed Spicer was examined as a witness for the defendant in that case.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The prosecutor, Charles Robert Harris, testified that he had never seen Spicer before the trial. He stated that on August 15th, Spicer did not come to his house, and no one tendered him £2 18s.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Harris provided an alibi, stating that on Thursday, August 15th, he left his house at 6 a.m. to work at a job in Highgate. He claimed he was there with his men all day and did not return home until around 9 p.m., making it impossible for Spicer to have seen him there at 4 p.m.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: John Chapman, a painter, testified that Harris was at Highgate all day on August 15th and walked part of the way home with the workmen, leaving them around 8 p.m. Chapman remembers the date because he had written a letter on the 13th, the day before he started the job.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Charles Harrison, the brother of Mrs. Pridmore, testified that he was present after the original trial and heard Harris say, 'I will indict Spicer for perjury; I was at Gravesend at the time he says he called on me.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Thomas Peacock, a cheesemonger, testified that he was in court after the trial and heard Harris tell Mr. Wood that he would indict Spicer for perjury, 'for the day he said he tendered the money, he was at Gravesend.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Thomas Peacock also testified that he heard Spicer, on cross-examination, say 'to the best of his knowledge he was the man' when asked to identify Harris positively.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mary Pridmore, the defendant from the original case, testified that she had offered to pay Mr. Harris the rent due (£2 18s.) before she left the premises, but he refused to take it. After his refusal, her husband directed their attorney, Mr. Wood, to pay the money to Harris.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Edward Harding Wood, who managed the defense for Pridmore, testified that he employed Spicer on August 15th to take the sum of £2 18s. and tender it to Mr. Harris at his home.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Mr. Wood testified that he distinctly remembers Spicer being cross-examined and saying, 'I can't swear positively, but I believe him to be the man.'
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: The original action brought by Harris against Pridmore was for £20 for the occupation of rooms. The defense's plea was that only £2 18s. was due, and that this sum had been tendered on September 13th, 1839.
[EVIDENCE ev19]
Description: A new trial was granted in the original case of Harris v. Pridmore. This was confirmed by Samuel Stone and a rule from the court directing the verdict to be set aside.
[EVIDENCE ev20]
Description: Henry Hall, a witness for Harris, testified that he did not hear Spicer qualify his identification of Harris, but admitted, 'I do not remember distinctly what I did hear.'

information:

Description: Donald Robertson testified that on January 25th, he was delivering linen and left his coat in his cart on Broad-street for approximately ten minutes. When he returned, his attention was called to the fact that his coat was missing.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The defendant, John Noyes, put in a written defense declaring his innocence and stating that the witness, Sarah Knight, must be mistaken in identifying him as the person who took the coat.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Smith, the victim, testified that on the night of the robbery, January 24th, he went to bed around 11:05 PM. Before retiring, he personally secured all the bolts and locks of the doors, including the street-door.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: John Smith testified that upon coming downstairs the next morning, he found a door leading to his shop broken open. A square of glass had been cut away, and the bolt was entirely broken away by force, indicating a burglary had occurred.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: John Smith stated that he discovered property was missing, including cloth, clothing, and silverware. The total value of the stolen goods was approximately £110.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Smith described a coal-hole in front of his house covered by an iron plate. He explained that a boy could get down through it and enter the kitchen via an unfastened window, establishing a plausible method of entry.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Police Sergeant George Weston testified that when Bird realized he was being arrested, he said to Hall, "you b——, if I had known you were going to split on us, we would have murdered you." Weston also stated Bird resisted arrest violently.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Sergeant Weston testified that in the public house, Hall asked Bird, "Where is Jack?" to which Bird replied, "So help me G—he is pinched for a coat since that night." This implies knowledge of another accomplice involved in the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Police Constable William Montgomery, also present at Bird's arrest, confirmed Bird's reaction. He testified that Bird said to Hall, "You b—if I had thought you had split I would have murdered you."
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Lydia Thorpe, a barmaid at the Duke of Argyle, testified that she saw Saunders come to the pub on a Friday in January and call Hall out. However, she did not see Bird there at that time and had never seen all the alleged conspirators together.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Robert Clark, a laborer, testified that Saunders and Bird frequented the Duke of Argyle public-house, but he had never seen Hall there with them. However, when cross-examined, it was revealed his testimony contradicted his earlier deposition where he stated he had seen all three together.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: William Finch, a former police constable, testified that on the night of the robbery, between twelve and one o'clock, he saw the defendant Saunders in Tavistock-street, a few doors from the prosecutor's house.
[EVIDENCE ev19]
Description: Defendant John Bird stated in his defense that Hall and two men disguised as sailors tried to entrap him into a different robbery. He claimed his angry outburst upon arrest was because Hall had tried to set him up, not because he was involved in the Smith burglary.
[EVIDENCE ev20]
Description: A character witness was called for the defendant Richard Saunders, who received a good character in court.

information:

Description: Julius Butcher, the cabin-boy, testified that three men came aboard the vessel. He stated, 'I am quite sure the prisoner was one of them... I saw them take the pork, put it into their boat, cover it with a handkerchief and row away... I am almost sure the prisoner is the man—I am quite sure he is.'

Description: Julius Butcher mentioned that 'the Custom-house officer was near, and saw them.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Osmond, a butcher, testified that he sent a hamper to London on May 7th containing five sides of veal, two pigs, and three calves' heads. He noted his specific practice of placing the calf's cauls inside the kidneys, which he stated was the usual course for butchers in his county.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: George Mogan, a wagoner's clerk, testified that on May 9th, an undelivered hamper with no direction was unloaded at Newgate-market. Upon opening it to find a note, he saw it contained sides of veal, two pigs, and three calves' heads.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Williams, a market salesman, testified that he saw defendants Hutton and Austin put a hamper from Blake's stand onto a truck and take it away.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Frederick Fireman, a butcher, testified that all three prisoners—Hutton, Tack, and Austin—arrived together at Simmonds's stand carrying packs of meat fully wrapped in cloths, with no parts visible.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Fireman observed that the veal was dressed in an unusual manner, with the caul 'stuffed in under the kidneys,' and he called Mr. Simmonds's attention to it.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Joseph Wells, an employee at Simmonds's, testified that Hutton and Tack delivered meat consisting of five sides of veal, two pigs, and three calves' heads. He also stated he later returned seven meat cloths to them.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Joseph Simmonds, the salesman, testified that defendant Austin arrived first at his shop with the meat packed in cloths and asked for payment for 'pitching' it, which Simmonds gave him.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Simmonds stated that Hutton and Tack arrived later that day. Hutton inquired if the meat was sold, then bought one of the pigs and directed the cost to be deducted from the total sales, claiming ownership.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Thomas Herdsfield, a City officer, testified that he stopped Hutton and Tack in Newgate-market, each carrying a side of pork, which he suspected to be stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: William Sales, from the Rose Inn, testified that no wagon from a person named Smith had arrived at the inn on Saturday, May 9th.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Benjamin Dowling, the intended recipient of the meat, confirmed that he was expecting two hampers from Mr. Osmond but only received one. He stated the missing hamper was supposed to contain five sides of veal, two pigs, and three calves' heads.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Pare, a partner in the firm, testified that Samuel Jenkins entered their service in January 1839. He stated that he employed Jenkins to collect debts in Midsummer 1839, despite a written agreement.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Thomas David Taylor, a solicitor, testified that he prepared the written employment agreement between Jenkins and the firm. The agreement included a clause stating that Jenkins was not to collect any monies without express written permission.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Benjamin Allen, a customer, testified that Jenkins came to him in September and November 1839 representing Pare & Co. and collected a total of £5 12s. 6d. from him, for which Jenkins provided a receipt.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William Harrison, another customer, testified that on January 10th, 1840, he paid Jenkins £20 12s. 6d. for an account of £20 17s. 5d. on behalf of Pare & Co.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Pare testified that on one occasion in February, he gave Jenkins permission to use some of the money he might receive for expenses while Pare was away, although Jenkins did not end up collecting any money during that time.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Pare denied ever offering to settle the case for a sum of money, specifically denying he asked for £96 or £100 from a Mr. England to stop the proceedings.

public.game_background: John Gurdon was indicted for wilful and corrupt perjury for his testimony in a civil case, Curlewis v. Cox. Gurdon testified under oath that he witnessed his employer, Mr. Curlewis, lend Mr. Cox a £50 note. The prosecution, led by Mr. Cox, alleges that this was false, that the money was an advance on behalf of a third party (Captain Henningsen), and that Gurdon was not present at the transaction nor in Mr. Curlewis's employ at the time.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Testimony of William Sayer, reading from his notes of the prior trial: John Gurdon swore that he was called by his employer, Mr. Curlewis, to witness him giving Mr. Cox a £50 note as a loan. Gurdon testified that Curlewis said, 'You see me give Mr. Cox a 50l. note,' and asked for it back soon, to which Cox agreed.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Testimony of William Sayer, from the prior trial: Gurdon swore under cross-examination that he was in Mr. Curlewis's employment from January 1838 to April 1839.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Cross-examination of Thomas Pinn: Pinn admits to pawning goods belonging to Mr. Curlewis without his permission and keeping the money, stating he was not paid honorably.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Testimony of Ralph Hindmarsh: Shortly before the original trial, Gurdon told him he did not know Mr. Cox and had never seen him.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Testimony of Richard Wright: He was the holder of Captain Henningsen's bill. Cox paid him £50 towards it on Feb 2nd, and the payment was made in multiple notes, specifically five £10 notes.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Cross-examination of Henry Charles Curlewis: He admits to having had dozens of lawsuits filed against him, having men in possession at his house multiple times, being a bankrupt, and having answered to the alias 'Garth'.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Cross-examination of Henry Way: Way admits to writing a letter to Cox's attorney on Curlewis's behalf but without his authority, and to having acted as a fake sheriff's officer to prevent a genuine one from taking possession.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Testimony of Thomas Cox: Cox confronted Gurdon in a public-house after the trial, calling him a 'villain' for swearing falsely, to which Gurdon made no answer and 'laid his head down'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Samuel Twaites testified that he executed a warrant of attorney on September 9th, 1836, at the office of his solicitor, Mr. Kearns. He stated that the date was clearly '9th' and the word 'nineteenth' was decidedly not present at the time of signing. Execution was issued based on this warrant on December 7th, within three months of the original date.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Charles Innes, Kearns's clerk, testified that another clerk named Barton was 'personally concerned in the transaction' and had likely prepared the draft of the warrant. Innes also stated that Barton had since gone abroad.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Charles Innes testified that the defendant, Mr. Kearns, went to France a few days after September 9th and did not return until the end of October 1836. The warrant of attorney was filed at the Warrant of Attorney Office on October 7th, 1836, during Kearns's absence.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William Ewing, manager of the London and Westminster Bank, testified that he knew Mr. Kearns's handwriting 'perfectly well.' After examining the alteration on the warrant, he stated, 'I have a great question in my own mind if it is his writing—I do not believe it to be his.'

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Gaston, the van driver, testified that he knew the prisoner, his brother-in-law, and met him in Blackman-street. Gaston stated that he gave Farrell the smock-frock containing the money-bag to hold for approximately a minute and a half while he placed another parcel in the van.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Inspector Charles Walter testified that when he first questioned Farrell, he asked if he was on the Croydon-road that evening, and Farrell said, 'No.' He then admitted to being with his brother-in-law as far as the Elephant and Castle.
[EVIDENCE ev6]

[EVIDENCE ev10]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On February 3rd, Mary Ann Franklin entered the shop of pawnbroker Joseph Knowles and attempted to pawn a ring for £4, claiming it was a diamond ring.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Testimony from William Martin showed that on January 23rd, Harding, accompanied by a woman, attempted to pawn a fake diamond pin for £4, but returned the money when challenged by the pawnbroker.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mary Evans, a manufacturing jeweller, identified the ring and all the pins from the various pawnbrokers as imitation pieces made by her firm. She testified they were sold for very low prices (e.g., the ring for £1 7s 6d) to a man who called himself Frenaby.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Landlady Mary Scurry testified that Frenaby and Franklin lived together as 'Mr. and Mrs. Frenaby,' and that they brought Harding to the house to work with them.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: A fellow lodger, Joseph Houghton, testified that he saw Franklin give Frenaby £4 and a pawn ticket. Franklin told Houghton she was pledging items for 'Jew merchants' upstairs and admitted the articles were manufactured imitations worth no more than 5 shillings.

public.game_background: Henry Allshorne was indicted for stealing 31 saddles and 1 pad from his master, George Buist. His father, James Edward Allshorne, was indicted for feloniously receiving the same goods, knowing them to have been stolen. Henry Allshorne was found guilty of theft, while James Edward Allshorne was acquitted of receiving stolen goods.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Henry Allshorne either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George Buist, a saddle-maker, testified that Henry Allshorne worked for him occasionally for three to four months. The last wages were paid on December 5th. Buist confirmed that numerous saddles shown in court were his property.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police-constable Stephen Creed testified that when he went to James Allshorne's house, he was denied entry three times by James's wife. Upon gaining entry, he saw James throw a parcel containing seven pawn duplicates behind a door.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: George Tolley and his son both testified that they had known James Allshorne for one to two years as the master of the Queen's Grammar-school at Kew and always understood him to be a very respectable man.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_cherry_1]
Description: Edward Plant testified that he saw the prisoner, Edward Cherry, in a public-house with a parcel. When Plant identified the contents as mouslin-de-laine with a torn end, Cherry became defensive, put it in his pocket, and said, 'You shall not see this.'
[EVIDENCE ev_cherry_4]
Description: City Police Constable William Leigh Wood testified that when he stopped the prisoner, Cherry claimed, 'it is my property, I bought it from the foreman of the warehouse, we are allowed to purchase these things, and make as much by them as we can.'
[EVIDENCE ev_cherry_8]
Description: Constable Wood testified that the prisoner 'made a very violent resistance' during his arrest, to the extent that 'it required five of us to hold him down.'
[EVIDENCE ev_cherry_9]
Description: Thomas Beynon, the warehouseman, testified that before this incident, they 'had no reason to doubt his honesty.' The Prosecutor and Jury also recommended the prisoner to mercy.

[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Robert Reid, who witnessed the assault from a third-floor window, testified that he saw both prisoners strike the deceased, but he did not see Covington have the deceased's stick or strike him with it.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Eleanor Pettit Maddox, the victim, testified that the prisoner, John Comber, came to her stable three times in June. On the 26th of June, he agreed to purchase two ponies for £62, plus a dog, paying with a cheque from the London and Westminster Bank after claiming to be a respectable farmer who always used cheques for safety.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Police-sergeant Thompson testified that upon being apprehended, the prisoner stated, 'Then it is all over with me.' On the way to town, he also admitted the ponies were sold and asked the officer if he could 'make it right,' implying a desire to bribe him or settle the matter out of court.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The court record notes that the prisoner had a prior capital conviction for horse stealing in 1830. This information was not presented to the jury but was known to the court.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Eliza Street testified that on May 22nd, the defendant approached her at a public-house after hearing her inquire for the barrister Mr. Phillips. Henson allegedly told her he was Mr. Phillips's clerk and that Mr. Phillips would handle her son's defense for a fee of three guineas.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Daniel Mead, a baker who accompanied Eliza Street, corroborated her account. He testified that Henson said he 'done business for Mr. Phillips as his clerk' and also mentioned that if he took the money by false pretenses, he would be 'taken off the rolls.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: William Street, the prisoner's son, testified that Henson visited him in prison three times. During these visits, Henson explicitly stated, 'I am Mr. Phillips's clerk,' and assured him that Mr. Phillips would be his counsel.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Charles Phillips, Esq., the barrister in question, gave definitive testimony on the matter. He stated that the defendant, William Henson, 'was never my clerk in any shape whatever.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. William Cope, the keeper of Newgate prison, testified that Henson signed the prison's book as 'attorney for Street.' This confirms Henson was publicly representing himself as the attorney in the case, not merely as a clerk.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Barrister Mr. Horry testified that a brief for William Street's defense was delivered to him by an associate of Henson's. This indicates a genuine attempt was made to secure counsel for the trial.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mr. Cope, the gaol keeper, testified that the court was determined to finish business on the day of Street's trial as it was the last day of the Sessions. He believed the court would not have waited for counsel who was absent.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: David Arnold Donaldson, the prosecutor, testified that he packed a white deal box with clothes and other articles, including three coats, trousers, boots, and a hat. He nailed the box down, corded it, directed it to a workshop at 18 Wild-court, and sent it from Tunbridge Wells on Monday, August 4th.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Patterson's defense argued that a man named Goodrich, who worked for him and lived with Clark, had received the box. It was further stated that both Goodrich and Clark had absconded from their lodging the same week and that the police were searching for them.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense, Patterson stated that there was no lock and key to the workshop, implying that anyone could have entered and taken the box.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Charles Sells, the pawnbroker's assistant who took in the stolen boots, testified that while he believed Ashton was the person who pawned them, he 'should not like to swear to her.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The defendant Henry Patterson received a good character reference in court.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Police-constable Pocock testified that when he arrested Patterson and informed him of the charge, Patterson denied the charge altogether.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On November 21st, the prisoner Lee entered Sarah Bradley's shop. Shortly after, the prisoner Roberts entered, and Lee greeted him, 'Captain Roberts, when did you arrive?'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Thomas Rolls, the prosecutrix's brother, testified, 'I never saw Roberts, and never did work for him—I know no Captain Roberts.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police Inspector Robert Valentine testified that the watch is 'very inferior,' made of metal, does not work, and that the watch and ring together are 'not worth 5s.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mrs. Bradley testified that Roberts had whiskers when he came to her shop but has none at the trial.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mrs. Bradley stated that both men were 'dressed more like gentlemen' at the time of the offense than they were in court, and that 'Roberts looked like a captain.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James M'Donald testified that the prisoner, Louisa Stansfield, was his laundress and that when he went away for an extended period, he left her with the key to his plate-chest.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Upon his return, Mr. M'Donald noticed imitation silver forks on his dinner table. This prompted him to check his plate-chest, where he found a large portion of his silver forks and spoons were missing.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Before producing the imitation forks, the prisoner asked Mr. M'Donald if he would advance £5 on her wages, which he hesitated to do.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: On cross-examination, the defense established that the prosecutor's full name was James Benassy M'Donald, a fact he had almost forgotten and a name he had not used since 1826.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mr. M'Donald testified that he had previously held a 'very high opinion' of the prisoner and that she came with a 'very good character.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: After the verdict, the prosecutor recommended the prisoner to mercy, stating he believed her to have acted under the influence of bad advisers.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Michael Hart testified that on Dec 20th, Archibald Leslie, with a man named William Bouch, presented a paper he called an 'award paper.' Leslie stated this paper represented a valid claim of £65 14s. for Bouch on the Portuguese government, and that he knew both the man and the claim to be correct.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Aaron Josephs testified that when he presented the award paper and power of attorney at the Commission office, the clerk, Mr. Younghusband, checked the register and stated 'there was no such name as William Bouch' and that claim number 254 was also incorrect.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Police-constable William Isaacs testified that when he arrested Leslie, the prisoner stated it was 'through being a friend to another man, who had gone to Bristol to get a ship'.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: William Brown, who was an adjutant in the same regiment as Leslie, was called by the defense. He testified that Leslie's character 'was always honest and trustworthy' and that he recalled a man named Bouch serving in the regiment.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prisoner, using the name Gillingham, offered to sell 'Reading sauce' to Mr. Harker's clerk, James Hayes, at a price 15% below the manufacturer's (Cocks') standard selling price.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: In a separate incident, the prisoner approached another tradesman, Mr. Cuthbert, offering Cocks' sauce at a reduced price and explicitly stating it was the genuine article.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Cuthbert, having grown suspicious after a second offer of sauce at an even lower price, confronted the prisoner by comparing the sold sauce with a genuine bottle.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Charles Cock, the manufacturer, testified that he had never had any barter transaction with the prisoner, John Simmonds, whatsoever.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mr. Cock examined the bottles sold by the prisoner and confirmed that both the sauce and the labels were spurious and counterfeit, though the labels were a very close imitation.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mr. Cock testified that he had a transaction for soy with a person named Gillingham, and that this Gillingham had proposed a barter for sauce, but the deal never took place and the Gillingham he saw was not the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Mr. John Hanbury, a grocer who had known the prisoner for 14 to 15 years, testified on behalf of the defense that he believed the prisoner to be honest.

[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: After the prisoner fled, the servant and police found that drawers in the bedroom and parlour had been forced open, and the wine-cellar door was broken open with its padlock forced off.

public.game_background: Edward Munn, Henry Rouse, and William Barton were indicted for conspiracy. This forged document, purportedly witnessed by Rouse and Barton, was then presented by Munn as genuine evidence in a civil lawsuit between Timewell and Munn regarding unpaid rent.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Prosecutor James Timewell testified that he hired the defendant Edward Munn to manage his corn-chandler business, not as a partner. The agreement was that Munn would receive a share of the net profits for his services.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Joshua Baker, a hay-salesman, testified that Timewell had personally introduced Munn to him as 'his partner' and stated he would be responsible for whatever goods Baker supplied to Munn.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The defendants' affidavits, which were read in court, contained their sworn statements. Munn's affidavit stated he was in a partnership with Timewell and that the agreement was a genuine document outlining the terms of the lease for the Stanhope-terrace premises, which were a joint venture.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Howe, the wagoner, testified that he gave the prisoner, William Brown, a ride in his wagon. This placed the prisoner in the vehicle where the money parcel was being transported.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Thomas Westall testified that he packed the parcel with two £5 notes, twenty-six sovereigns, thirty-two half-crowns, and other coins, and sent it to be delivered. The wagoner, James Howe, later confirmed the parcel was missing.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The wagoner, James Howe, testified that the parcel was certainly in the basket just before he left the wagon, as he had just given the prisoner bread from it. This narrows the timeframe of the theft to the period when the prisoner had sole access.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The wagoner, James Howe, testified that he actively assisted the police in their search for the prisoner. The police sergeant confirmed that the wagoner 'appeared to be active in searching for the prisoner.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: No witness testified to seeing the prisoner physically remove the parcel from the basket. The prosecution's case rests entirely on a chain of circumstantial evidence.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The defense implied that the wagoner had initially told his master he had brought no man in the wagon. The wagoner denied this, creating a potential conflict in testimony that could impact his credibility.

public.game_background: The defendants, Perceval Chamberlain, George Fuller, William Reeve, and their wives, are indicted for conspiracy. The prosecution, led by Thomas Alley Jones, alleges that after Jones sued Chamberlain for a significant debt, the defendants conspired to maliciously and falsely prosecute him for theft and fraud related to Fuller's bankruptcy. This allegedly led to Jones's wrongful arrest and subsequent trial, where he was ultimately acquitted of all charges.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. The either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Alley Jones testified that in December 1841, he commenced an action against Perceval Hamilton Chamberlain to recover approximately £637 which he had advanced to or paid on behalf of Chamberlain.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Charles Lawrence testified that defendant William Napier Reeve visited him and called Jones 'a scoundrel, a villain, and a thief,' adding, 'we are determined to transport him for stealing property from Fuller's creditors.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Charles Anslipp, a solicitor and cousin of the Lawrences, testified that George Fuller admitted to him that he had perjured himself, but that 'the creditors, through Mr. Reeve, had promised to protect him if he would turn round against Jones.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Ellen Pinner, a laundress, testified that defendants Reeve and Fuller visited her and attempted to persuade her to falsely state that she had washed two sets of curtains for Miss Lawrence, including an embroidered set she had never seen, offering to pay her expenses and 'make you a handsome present'.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Jones admitted under oath that Chamberlain's execution on Fuller's goods was 'put in with my concurrence; I knew of it, I counselled it, and proposed it,' in order to defeat other creditors. He also admitted striking a docket against Fuller for his father, a fiat which was later set aside.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Under cross-examination, Jones was evasive about his past, including whether he had acted as a paid legal adviser before being articled. When shown a receipt for a pig received for 'law business', he would not swear he didn't write it or receive the pig, stating 'you take me back twelve or thirteen years'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The warrant for Jones's arrest was applied for on Saturday, June 18th, the same day that Jones's perjury trial against Fuller, which was set for trial by a special jury, was postponed until the following Thursday.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Lucy Lawrence testified that the china pomatum pot shown to her at the police court in June was not the same one she had seen at the Bankruptcy Court in January, which had been taken from Jones's house.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Mountain, from the Sheriff's Office, produced a valid writ of capias ad satisfaciendum against Martin Hussey. This established the legal authority for the officers to attempt an arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Thomas Meredith, a merchant at the same wharf, testified that he saw Gibbs with a 'dreadful wound on his head' with blood flowing copiously, and heard him crying for assistance.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Meredith also testified that he heard Hussey call for a knife and a hammer 'to do for him' (Gibbs), which caused him to fear murder and go for a policeman.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: A surgeon, Thomas Hopkins, testified that Gibbs suffered a 2.5-inch incision on the top of his head which exposed the skull bone. He concluded it was caused by a severe blow from a blunt instrument, consistent with a poker.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Robert Webling, a well-digger who was in the yard, testified that he saw Officer Gibbs go to a stable, get a pitchfork, and use it to wrench open Hussey's front door.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Mary Smith, who was inside the house, testified that she personally bolted the front door after letting the Husseys in, shortly before the officers arrived.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_gm1]
Description: George Matthews, the landlord, testified that he let the house at No. 185 Ratcliffe-highway to a man named Edward Williams about four months prior. He saw the prisoner, Stevenson, there several times and considered him to be a servant. The shop closed around September 10th, and Matthews found the premises empty.
[EVIDENCE ev_jm1]
Description: John Mallison, an outfitter, testified that an elderly man named Edward Williams introduced the prisoner as his son-in-law, for whom the shop was intended. Together, they selected goods amounting to between £80 and £90.
[EVIDENCE ev_jp1]
Description: John Priest, a foreman for outfitter Robert Popplewell, testified that he went to the shop to take an order, where the prisoner identified himself as 'Mr. Williams'.
[EVIDENCE ev_pp1]
Description: Peter Popplewell, a button merchant, testified that his cousin Robert Popplewell inquired about a 'Mr. Williams of Ratcliffe-highway'. Knowing only the established outfitter at No. 20, he gave a positive reference based on that mistaken identity.
[EVIDENCE ev_tw1]
Description: Thomas Williams testified that he is a long-established tailor and outfitter at No. 20 Ratcliffe-highway, opposite the prisoner's shop, and has no connection with the prisoner or the shop at No. 185.
[EVIDENCE ev_jh2]
Description: John Hussey, a traveller, testified that on September 9th, the prisoner identified himself as 'Mr. Williams' and claimed to have another shop across the street. He placed an order for goods worth £27-£28 and promised to pay cash.
[EVIDENCE ev_cc1]
Description: Charles Chambers, the arresting policeman, testified that when he took the prisoner into custody, the prisoner first gave his name as Williams, and 'directly after that it was Stevenson'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Walter Cotter, a clerk of judgments for the Court of Queen's Bench, testified that a judgment was indeed signed in the cause of Watts against Wittenbury on August 27, 1842, and was subsequently struck out by a Judge's order. This established the existence of the judgment central to the case.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Samuel Gibbs, a clerk to Mr. Justice Cresswell, produced several documents that had been ordered to be impounded by the judge. Among these documents was the specific affidavit made by the prisoner, William James, which formed the basis of the perjury charge.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The prisoner's affidavit deposed that on August 26, 1842, he accompanied James Wittenbury to the office of attorney Samuel Hanson Williams, where Wittenbury consented to a judgment, drew up an order, and served it the same day.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Wittenbury testified that his initial action upon receiving the writ was to hire attorneys to defend the action, as he believed the underlying debt was invalid. He was informed no action could be taken until late October.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Joshua Morris, the sheriff's officer who arrested Wittenbury, testified that the purported attorney, Samuel Hanson Williams, does not live at the address listed in the legal documents. Morris stated another individual, Munyard, lives there, and he had never heard of Williams in Woolwich.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Joshua Morris, the sheriff's officer, also testified that he saw the prisoner, James, in conversation with Simmers, the attorney's clerk, in the hall of the judge's chambers on the day the fraudulent judgment was being contested and set aside.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: On cross-examination, Wittenbury mentioned a book kept at his work site in Hockley that details his expenses and dates of travel, which he claimed would confirm his presence there. The book itself was not produced in court.

public.game_background: Henry Simmers, a managing clerk for an attorney, was indicted for wilful and corrupt perjury. The charge stems from an affidavit he swore to obtain a judgment against James Wittenbury in a civil action. Simmers's affidavit claimed that Wittenbury, accompanied by a man named James, visited his employer's office on August 26th, 1842, and consented to a judge's order for payment, which was subsequently not honored. The prosecution contends that this statement was entirely false, that Wittenbury was elsewhere on that day, and that the entire process to obtain the judgment was fraudulent.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The sworn affidavit of Henry Simmers, dated after August 26, 1842. In it, Simmers states that James Wittenbury and a man named James called at his employer's office in Woolwich on August 26, agreed to pay 3l. in costs, and said they would go to London to draw up the order. Simmers claims that because they failed to do so, he signed judgment against Wittenbury on August 27.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: A Judge's order, purportedly from Lord Denman and dated August 26, 1842, was presented. It stated that by consent of both parties, legal proceedings would be stayed upon payment of 5l. debt and 3l. agreed costs. This order formed the legal basis for the subsequent judgment.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Thomas Hawker testified as a character witness for the defense. He stated he had known Simmers for several years, had employed him to collect debts, and always found him to be a 'truth-speaking, honest man' who never deceived him.

[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Alexander Needham, a boat-builder who knew Champ, testified that he believed the signatures on the writ and affidavits to be Champ's handwriting, although he noted slight differences and stated he would 'not like to say positively.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]

[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: It was established that Myers had previously seized goods belonging to Benham. Shortly after, Benham took the benefit of the Insolvent Act with debts around £4000, and Myers, a creditor, received no dividend, establishing a history of significant financial conflict and animosity.

Description: Police Constable Saunders testified that after Champ was arrested, he stated that 'if he was to suffer, he hoped others would as well as himself,' and pointed out Benham's location. He did not protest his innocence but expressed a wish to be transported.

[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Richard Lebas, a coal-merchant, testified that on March 5th, he missed large lumps of Derbyshire coal from his heap. The surface of the coal was covered with hoar frost from a sharp frost that morning, and the pieces later shown to him retained this frost, fitting perfectly into the vacant, frost-free spaces on the heap, indicating they were recently taken.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Police-constable James Thorn testified that around 5 a.m. on March 5th, he saw the prisoner on the wall of Mr. Lebas's wharf with a piece of coal. Upon seeing the officer, the prisoner laid the coal down and ran away. Thorn stated he knew the prisoner well and did not lose sight of him until he was close enough to be certain of his identity.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The prisoner stated, 'I was not out at all that morning.' He added, 'There are two more lodgers in the house besides me, and they have the run of the house,' implying someone else could have brought the coal.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Richard Roadnight, a policeman, produced a certificate of the prisoner's former conviction for felony from January 1842. He testified that he was present at the trial and knew the prisoner to be the same person.

[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Flanders, the prosecutor, testified that the two cog-wheels shown to him by William Ives were his property. He identified them by a unique flaw, a crack in the pattern, which corresponded with other wheels in his stock.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Flanders stated that the prisoner, Bassingthwaite, was in his employ as a smith for about fourteen months and had left his service approximately fifteen months before the trial.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: During cross-examination, Mr. Flanders admitted he had missed wheels but could not say how many or when he had missed them, only that his stock was smaller than it should be.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mr. Flanders testified that when he confronted the prisoner, Bassingthwaite denied having sold any cog-wheels to Mr. Ives and claimed to know nothing about them.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: William Ives, a wheelwright, testified that the prisoner, about thirteen months ago, offered to sell him a pair of wheels and subsequently brought them, for which Ives paid him 5 shillings.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Ives further testified that within the last two months, the prisoner saw the wheels, hid them under a bench, and warned him that if his uncle saw them, he would know where they came from.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: On cross-examination, Ives admitted that the prisoner had made an 'unjust claim' for 9 pounds against him and had brought an action against him for the money.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: When questioned about an illness that caused the trial to be postponed, Ives was evasive, stating he couldn't tell what was wrong, didn't know the doctor's name, and was back at work in about two days.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Under cross-examination, Henry Beale was questioned about owing the prisoner money. He was evasive, stating, 'I do not know that I do, if we settled,' and denied owing a specific amount.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Mr. Flanders stated under cross-examination that a person named Wilson, who used to work for him, had set up in the same business.

Description: David Samuels, an employee of Samuel Levy Samuels, testified that in February, the prisoner claimed to be from Mr. Nathan and requested four umbrellas on credit, stating Mr. Nathan would pay the next morning. Believing him, Samuels gave him the umbrellas.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Sarah Hobbs testified that on September 29th, the prisoner came to her shop, claimed Mr. Nathan had sent him for boots, and stated he had been living with Mr. Nathan for the last six months. Believing him, she let him take the boots.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Moses Nathan stated that the prisoner had been in his service twenty months prior to the events. He also confirmed he never sent the prisoner to the prosecutor's, either when he was in or out of his employ.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: James Martin, a policeman, testified that he apprehended the prisoner.

information:

Description: William Phillips, an employee of the shop, testified that he observed the prisoner for five to ten minutes. He stated the prisoner took two pairs of trousers from a display, put them under the tail of his coat, and left.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Samuel Skinner confirmed that two pairs of trousers were missing from the shop and identified the recovered pair as being one of them.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Police-constable Robert Redgrave testified that upon being arrested, the prisoner immediately claimed he was not the person and that he had not had a coat on all afternoon.

The prosecution alleged that Green, in concert with others operating a fraudulent firm named 'Hodges, Parker, and Co., Merchants and Bankers,' obtained goods and credit from various tradesmen by using the fictitious firm as a credible reference.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Findon visited 10, Austin-friars, where a person claiming to be a partner confirmed Green kept a regular account and was trustworthy. Green also provided a second reference, a coach-builder named Thompson, who gave him an excellent character.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Reassured by the references, Findon gave Green the cabriolet in exchange for a 50l bill drawn on Hodges and Parker. The bill was later returned dishonored, and Findon never received payment or his property back.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Findon testified that he later met Green, who acknowledged the failure of Hodges and Parker and offered another suspicious bill on a 'Captain Farquharson' as a replacement, which Findon refused.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Hamilton testified that Green was present at the Austin-friars office, acted like a partner by intervening to secure credit, and that the firm subsequently absconded without paying the £60-£70 bill.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Winkworth stated that Green absconded without paying rent and later sent a fictitious bill for £29 17s. 4d. drawn on a 'Thomas Farquharson', who could not be found.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Charlotte Jones, the landlady of the offices at 10, Austin-friars, testified that she let two rooms to a Mr. Parker for 'Hodges, Parker, and Co.' in June 1842, and the name was written on the door.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Jones testified that Green was present at the offices 'several times,' 'took a great deal of authority,' and 'had mutton chops and lunch there,' which were charged to the firm’s unpaid account.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Jones testified the firm appeared to be carrying on business as merchants and bankers, with samples, cards, and checks on the premises, but no actual goods. They left around Michaelmas owing rent, which she recovered by seizing the office furniture.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Charles Corbyn testified that he was the holder of the 50l bill from Findon. When he went to present the bill at Austin-friars on the day it was due, he found the firm had gone.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Robert Clavering Savage testified that Elizabeth Brown was his housekeeper. On December 26th, he left her in charge of his house and returned a few hours later to find her gone. He discovered that property valued at £500-£600 had been stolen from his plate-chest, the key to which had been left on a mantel-shelf.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: On cross-examination, Mr. Savage admitted that he and Brown had lived as man and wife thirteen years prior and that he had sought her out to return to his service after not seeing her for five years. He also admitted that the co-defendant, Maller, is Brown's sister.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Police-constable William Lee testified that he encountered the prisoners on the night of the theft, about four and a half miles from Euston-square, though they were asking for directions to the station there. Brown was intoxicated.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Trichler, a clock-dealer, testified that in April 1842, he employed the defendant, William Christey, as a Custom-house agent to clear three cases of clocks that he was expecting as a consignment from the Continent.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Ignatius Brunner, who received one of the cases (H S), testified that it contained 106 common wooden clocks and one musical clock.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Thaddaus Gaunter, the consignee for another case (G and C), testified that it contained 253 clocks.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: It was established that if the Crown suspects goods are undervalued, it can seize them by paying the declared value plus 10%. This option was not exercised.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: John Trichler testified that he believed it was Christey's custom to receive money from him before paying the duty, as he understood Christey was not in very good circumstances.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Northern testified that he took a house in his own name for the prisoner Bottrell, as the landlord objected to Bottrell as a tenant. Northern stated he went to live in the house himself after asking Bottrell to send in furniture to secure the rent.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The day before the alleged break-in, Bottrell had summoned Northern before a Magistrate to compel him to give up the goods. The Magistrate, however, made no order.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Under cross-examination, Northern admitted to a contentious financial relationship with Bottrell. He claimed Bottrell owed him perhaps £100 but also confirmed that Bottrell held his financial acceptances.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Elizabeth Shibley, a widow who lived nearby, testified that she saw three or four men with a van push the door two or three times until it was broken open. She also saw a fender thrown out of the house.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Robert Quarman, the next-door neighbor, heard a noise he believed was the door being broken open and saw goods being removed. A man he could not identify told him he had a warrant.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: George Hambrook, the van driver, testified that Bottrell hired the van and met him at the location. He stated that both Bottrell and the other prisoner, Hurrel, went into the house and directed the removal of items.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: George Hambrook testified under cross-examination that he heard Bottrell give 'strict orders that they were to take nothing that belonged to Northern'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Elizabeth Haines, the prisoner's landlady, testified that about a fortnight before the murder, after the deceased had left the prisoner, he said, 'I will lie awake for her, and give her a good hiding; and if I had her here, I would cut her throat with a razor.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Thomas Tuffnell, a surgeon, testified for the defense that he treated the prisoner in 1838 for a severe concussion of the brain. Tuffnell stated that due to this injury, if the prisoner took liquor, he would not be of sound mind and might 'get furiously mad'.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The deceased, Frances Crouch, was described as a 'very well-conducted, moral, proper person' and 'extremely kind' to the prisoner.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Duggin testified that near midnight, Ann Mergrove accosted him, saying, 'My dear, will you come home along with me?' He stated he did not explicitly agree but went with her to a house in Parr's-place, Cottage-lane.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Under cross-examination, William Duggin admitted he had been drinking but maintained he 'knew pretty well what I was about.' He claimed that all he had that evening was 'half a glass of gin and water.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: James Stevens, an innkeeper, testified that Duggin had been at his establishment earlier and had 'a glass of gin and water and a pint of ale.' Stevens stated Duggin was 'sober enough to know what occurred' and confirmed he was wearing his union pin at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mergrove's written defense claimed Duggin refused to pay a promised half-crown, became abusive, and took up a poker, swearing he would 'knock my brains out.' Her defense states she acted to prevent him from leaving while he was being violent.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Police-constable John Redman testified that when he found Duggin, he 'was the worse for liquor—he seemed to understand what he was about pretty well.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Officer Redman testified that the two female prisoners were searched, but nothing was found on them. He stated, 'I found no pin, watch, or money.'

Description: Jacob Dennis Thomas, a dock labourer, testified that he saw the prisoner take a handful of tobacco from the end of a hogshead, turn quickly, and conceal it under the tail of his coat. Thomas was confident that no tobacco was hanging out of the cask beforehand and immediately told his colleague, Smith.

public.game_background: James Cockburn Belaney is indicted for the wilful murder of his wife, Rachel Belaney. The prosecution alleges the prisoner intentionally poisoned his wife with prussic acid to inherit her substantial property. The defense maintains that Mrs. Belaney's death was a tragic accident, resulting from her mistakenly drinking prussic acid that the prisoner used for a medical condition.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: On Thursday, June 6th, two days before Mrs. Belaney's death, the prisoner purchased one ounce of Scheele's prussic acid from a surgeon, Mr. Donahoo. The prisoner stated he was in the habit of taking it for a stomach complaint.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: A letter from the prisoner dated June 8th (posted Saturday evening after the death), which arrived on Monday, stated Mrs. Belaney was 'dangerously ill' with a 'diseased heart' and attended by 'two medical men.' These claims were entirely false.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: A post-mortem examination of the deceased's body found the vital organs to be generally healthy. However, the stomach contained fluid 'strongly impregnated with the odour of prussic acid.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prisoner, claiming to be a doctor, did not apply any of the known effective remedies for prussic acid poisoning, such as cold water affusion or ammonia. Instead, he put his wife's feet and hands in warm water, which medical experts testified was a 'loss of time' and of little use.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Numerous witnesses, including friends and employees, testified that the prisoner and his wife appeared to be on 'extremely affectionate terms' and were a happy couple. The prisoner was described as being known for his 'kindness and humanity.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: On the morning of the death, the prisoner rang for hot water, a tumbler, and a spoon. Shortly after, he came out of the bedroom and told the servant he was going to write, which he proceeded to do.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: When the prisoner was apprehended, he claimed his wife had committed suicide, which was not the case.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Charles Talbot Carter testified that he complained about a dirty pudding he was served by the prisoner, William Ward. He claimed Ward snatched it, threatened him, then ran from behind the counter to kick him in the bladder and knock him into the street, where he kicked him several more times.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: George Birch Oram, a tailor in the shop Carter fled to, testified that Ward came across with pudding on a fork. Oram stated Ward 'ran the pudding into his face, not intending to run the fork into his nose; but, in the heat of passion, it went into his nose.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Henry Edward Davies, a druggist who treated Carter, described the injury as 'a slight wound,' a 'slight puncture' on the side of the nose which he did not think went through. When asked if it was a wound that would cause someone to keep their bed for a week, he replied, 'I think not.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: An advertisement was placed in the Times newspaper for 'three superior horses;... all warranted sound'.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Professor Charles Spooner of the Veterinary College examined the three horses and testified that all were unsound. One was a 'roarer' with spavin, another had chronic lameness, and the mare had lameness with severed nerves in her fore-feet to prevent the sense of pain.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Police-sergeant Jonathan Whicher testified that he made inquiries in and around Stratford-upon-Avon and could find no family by the name of Lloyd that matched the prisoners' description.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The groom, William Whistler, heard the elder prisoner say upon being taken into custody 'that he knew he owed the man the money, and he meant to pay it to him as soon as it was convenient.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Police-sergeant Frederick Shaw testified that when he arrested the younger prisoner and charged him with the offense, he replied that he 'knew nothing at all about it.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: William Whistler testified that during the initial sale, the elder prisoner asked for Mr. Angerstein's name and commented that his master 'wanted a great deal of coaxing.'
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Edward M'Keon, a bank clerk, testified that Mr. Angerstein's cheque for £115 10s. was cashed on the day it was drawn, 'very soon after two o'clock.'

public.game_background: William Hunt was indicted for forging and uttering an undertaking for the payment of £1739 10s., purportedly signed by Thomas Rogers, with intent to defraud. Rogers claimed the IOU and other documents were forgeries, while Hunt maintained they were genuine and that Rogers was indebted to him.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Rogers testified that he had known the prisoner for many years and had lent him money on various occasions, but had never received any repayment. Rogers stated he never owed the prisoner any money and that the IOU for £1739 10s. was a forgery.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: On cross-examination, Thomas Rogers admitted he was declared bankrupt in 1838 and was later imprisoned in Newgate for not giving satisfactory answers to commissioners, particularly regarding £12,000 in sovereigns he claimed to have lost.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Rogers was cross-examined about multiple fires at properties in which he had a financial interest, including some for which he received insurance payouts. He accused the prisoner of setting the fires but admitted he had not made this accusation until legal proceedings began.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Cranch testified that in 1842, Hunt instructed him to bring an action against his brother's assignees and to hand over half the money recovered to Mr. Rogers in payment of a debt he owed him.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Cranch admitted on cross-examination that during a prior civil trial at Kingston, a dozen independent witnesses were called for Hunt who testified that they believed the signatures on the bills to be Mr. Rogers's genuine handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Cranch also admitted that after taking handwriting experts to inspect the bills in the civil suit, he applied for leave to add a plea that the bills were for accommodation. This new plea would be an alternative to the claim of forgery.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: At the jury's request, Thomas Rogers wrote his name on a piece of paper in court. Witness Samuel Holmes, who had just sworn the signatures were 'nothing at all like' Rogers's writing, was shown the paper and testified, 'No, I believe it not to be his handwriting.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Witness George Wintle Roberts, an accountant, also testified that the forged signatures were bad imitations. However, when shown the in-court sample written by Rogers, he also stated, 'I should say that that is not Mr. Rogers's handwriting.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Elizabeth Ann Hughes, a housemaid, went to room No. 6 at about 10:10 PM and found the door unlocked, which she testified was unusual as it was always kept locked.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Warrener testified that he had received prior information and was on alert in the hall, which allowed him to be certain that no one else entered from the street to get onto the stairs after the screams began.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Felix Gatayes, the occupant of room No. 6, testified that he had locked the room and that two rings, worth about two sovereigns, were missing from a drawer in his dressing-table.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Police Constable Thomas Burt found four keys hidden in the coat lining of prisoner Howell. The hotel landlord, Warrener, and the police inspector later confirmed that one of these keys opened the door to room No. 6.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police Constable John Brett testified that upon searching prisoner Smith, he found a skeleton key and two latch keys on his person.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: PC Brett testified that while being taken to the station, prisoner Smith said, 'I am the bl--y thief; would not you go into a house if you thought you could get anything?' and 'D--n it, you have got the lot'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Cab driver Henry Taylor testified that he drove prisoner Franklin and two other men to George-yard around 9:10 PM. While he could not swear to their identities, he believed the other two were Howell and Smith.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Edward Fitzgerald, a waiter, saw Franklin inside the servant's hall of the hotel around 9:50 PM. Franklin gave the excuse that he mistook it for a beer-shop.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: PC Crowe testified that he saw Franklin following the other two prisoners on the opposite side of the street as they were being taken to the station in custody.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Mr. Warrener re-examined, stated that the dressing-table where the rings were kept was in the part of the room belonging to the parish of St. Michael's, not St. Edmund the King and Martyr as laid in the indictment.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Smith testified that on Saturday, March 29th, he and his wife left their room locked around 7 PM. Upon returning at 10 PM, they found the door halfway open and a bag containing over 100 pairs of socks was missing.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: John Smith stated that the prisoner, Elizabeth Craddock, was employed by him to knit socks. He added that he previously had a very good opinion of her.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Catherine Smith testified that on the evening of the robbery, Elizabeth Craddock brought home four pairs of socks. After being paid, she came back and asked if Mrs. Smith's husband was going out with her.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Catherine Smith stated that the bag of socks was visible in the room when Elizabeth Craddock was there. She also testified that Elizabeth had previously helped her move a box from which a tablecloth was stolen, indicating knowledge of its location.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mary Elizabeth Easton testified that she saw a female standing on the Smiths' doorstep between seven and eight o'clock. She stated, 'by her appearance it was the prisoner Elizabeth, but I did not see her face.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Constable John Cale testified that he went to the house of Susannah Craddock, where Elizabeth Craddock was also present. The stolen bag, containing thirteen dozen pairs of socks and a tablecloth, was produced from behind a curtain.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In her defense, Elizabeth Craddock stated, 'What the policeman says, is false, my sister is innocent, and knows nothing about it.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: A voluntary statement made by Elizabeth Craddock before the Magistrate was read in court: 'I went up to speak about some work; I found the door partly open; I went in and took the property; I did not take more than they have found.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edmund Hearne testified that the defendant Willis approached him, claiming to have a friend who could discount two promissory notes issued by John Mytton. After agreeing on terms, Hearne entrusted the notes to Willis, who failed to return with either the money or the notes, offering various excuses.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Dr. John Ryan testified that the defendant Goldsmid confessed to him about a 'scheme' to get bills from Mr. Hearne. Goldsmid detailed how he had employed Willis to pretend he could discount the bills, receive them from Hearne, and then run off with them.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Mytton testified that he signed and delivered the promissory notes to a firm called 'Smith and Co.' based on their promise to provide him with a £1500 loan. Mytton stated he never received any money and that the firm disappeared, leading him to believe he had been robbed of the bills.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Evidence revealed that Hearne's partner, Mr. Bartlett, who supposedly provided £600 of the cash advance, had an execution out against him for a debt of around £400. Hearne admitted to requesting that the execution be withdrawn shortly before the trial.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Hearne's financial credibility was further damaged by his admission that he owed money from a prior bill transaction to a Mr. Scanlan, which he was paying off in small weekly installments of 5 shillings.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: On cross-examination, Mytton admitted his acquaintance with both defendants, that he had previously employed Willis to discount bills, and that he was paying for their legal defense.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: A dispute arose when Charles Ratherbee demanded back a piece of land he had rented to Joseph Harvey. Harvey had already begun sowing seeds, while Ratherbee wanted the land to plant potatoes.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: A neighbor, Elizabeth Wiffin, corroborated the prosecution's account, stating she saw Harvey approach the victim with his elbow raised and then saw the victim lying on the ground.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: A witness, Mr. Lester, testified that he saw the victim make the first blow at Harvey with a spade.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: A third witness, Charles Wenman, testified that the victim made the first blow with a spade and that Harvey did not strike him at all.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Medical testimony from Dr. Otway stated that a blow, as alleged by the prosecution, could have ruptured a blood vessel in the victim's diseased lungs, accelerating his death.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Medical evidence confirmed the deceased had a severe, long-standing lung disease that would have eventually proved fatal, and that he had suffered a similar hemorrhage months before from a non-violent cause.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Surgeons who conducted the post-mortem examination found no external marks or bruises on the victim's chest, which they stated was surprising if he had received a blow forceful enough to knock him down.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Richard Wiffin testified that his granddaughter, prosecution witness Elizabeth Wiffin, was standing fifty yards from the altercation and could not possibly have heard the conversation she reported.

information:

Description: Alfred Charles Smith testified that he was employed by the Society for the Suppression of Vice to go to the defendant's shop and ascertain if indecent prints were sold there. This establishes the context for his actions and his role as an agent for the prosecution.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Police-sergeant William Chadwick testified that he thoroughly searched Mr. Smith before he entered the shop on July 5th and confirmed he had no prints on him. Chadwick then waited outside, never lost sight of Smith, and received the marked prints from him immediately upon exiting.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Edward Cooper testified that he is employed by the property owner and that he receives rent for the shop at 103, Strand directly from the defendant, Alfred Carlile.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Thomas Protheroe, an assistant in the Court of Exchequer, testified that the defendant had lodged with him for nearly a year and had 'borne a most unimpeachable character.' Charles Gould also deposed to the defendant's good character.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Both Sergeant Chadwick and Edward Cooper testified that they had never observed any indecent prints being openly exposed for sale in the shop window or public areas.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Barrett testified that a month prior to the assault, on September 6th, the defendant, William Bassett, threatened him on a bridge, stating, 'Nobby Barrett, I will do for you some of these days.'
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Multiple witnesses, including William Westcoat, Mary Barrett, William Hibbart, and Henry Pratt, testified that William Bassett was heavily intoxicated at the time of the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: William Hibbart, a laborer for the prosecutor, testified that while he saw Bassett strike Barrett on the nose with his hand, he did not see anything in Bassett's hand at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev8]

[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: During cross-examination of the victim, the defense introduced the possibility that the injury was caused by a jagged branch of an elder tree near the fence where the confrontation occurred.

While the initial coroner's inquest concluded suicide, Browning was later charged after she was found in possession of the victim's property and made multiple confessions to the crime.
