information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mary Cheshire, a neighbor lodging in the next room, testified that around seven o'clock on the morning of the death, she was awoken by the victim's voice crying 'Murder!' twice, followed by 'What are you doing? what are you doing?'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: In her statement at the coroner's inquest, the prisoner, Martha Browning, claimed the deceased had a fit around seven o'clock, screamed 'Murder!' and 'what are you doing to me?', and that she (Browning) left to fetch the victim's daughter.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Ann Gaze, the victim's daughter, found her mother dead, lying on a box in her night clothes. A cord was tied twice around her neck with a tight knot at the side.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Edward Gaze, the victim's son-in-law, testified that when preparing to lay out the body, he found the bed was wet on the under side, but not the top. He concluded the bed had been turned over to hide the wetness.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Surgeon John Charles Atkinson testified that the victim died from strangulation. He stated the two distinct lines in the front of the neck and only one at the back made it improbable that she could have done it herself and suggested force was applied from behind.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Ann Gaze, the victim's daughter, testified that her mother had once taken poison about three years prior, though the victim claimed it was by mistake. This suggests a history of self-harm or suicidal ideation.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Two days after the death, the prisoner was in possession of a £5 note which the victim's daughter and son-in-law identified as belonging to the deceased due to distinctive grease spots.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Inspector Partridge found two duplicates in the prisoner's box for items pawned on the 24th and 28th of November, just before the murder, for small sums of money. This suggested she was in financial distress.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Edward Gaze testified that while he was escorting the prisoner to Bedford-street, she fell back into his arms and confessed, 'I cannot keep it no longer; I murdered the old woman, and deprived your wife of her mother.'
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Multiple witnesses described the prisoner as being in a fainting, agitated, and hysterical state when confronted about the murder and while being taken into custody, tearing her hair and appearing in convulsions.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Auctioneer Thomas Doggett testified that when he hired Henry Spicer to assist in a distress for rent, he gave him a copy of the warrant with the explicit instruction: 'On condition you do not move or do anything, but leave the man in possession, till you let me know'.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The tenant, Elizabeth French, testified that she was not violent towards Spicer, did not use ill language, and did not threaten to break the furniture.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Charles King, a friend of the Frenchs, corroborated Doggett's account, stating he was present, heard the arrangement, saw Stephenson agree to it, and heard neither conspirator 'say a word about 5 l. or 5 l. 12 s. 8 d.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Under cross-examination, Doggett admitted that he had been trying to get into the house for 'a length of time, and had never been able to get in,' but Spicer succeeded on the same day he was employed.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Doggett admitted under cross-examination that he had 'not paid Spicer a farthing for what he did on this occasion.'

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The apprentice Barry Yelverton testified that he heard Thomas Rason say that if they made land, he would report the crew's treatment and ensure the captain 'would be no more captain of the Tory.' Yelverton reported this threat to the captain.
[EVIDENCE ev7]

[EVIDENCE ev11]

[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The ship's carpenter, Alex Sinclair, testified that before the mutiny reports, the captain had 'conducted himself quietly and kindly.' His behavior only became agitated and violent after he believed his life was threatened.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Charles Maymot, the surgeon of Horsemonger-lane-gaol, testified that the prisoner suffered from fever, delirium, and dysentery. The surgeon stated that such a physical condition 'might have committed this act in a state of delirium.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The defendant, Mary Ann Perry, filed a petition for protection from process in the Court of Bankruptcy. In the schedule, she disputed a debt to George Levy for £45 5s. 6d., stating she had never signed the promissory note he held.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Couch, sen., testified that Ms. Perry owed him a balance of £45 5s. 6d. for over two years of board, lodging, and other expenses. He stated that this balance was agreed upon after comparing account books.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mary Ann Strickland, a former servant, testified for the defense that she was present in the kitchen when a note was signed. She stated the amount discussed and read aloud was for £15, not £45, and that it was requested by Couch to show to his creditors due to his financial 'great difficulties.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: During cross-examination, it was revealed that in a prior civil trial at Bristol concerning the same promissory note, the jury found in favor of the plaintiff (Couch/Levy), suggesting they did not believe Mary Ann Strickland's testimony.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Couch, sen., was cross-examined about burning the indentures of his apprentice, M'Carthy, after erasing a previous apprentice's name and writing M'Carthy's in. Couch admitted to this but claimed it was to cancel the indentures, not to hide a faulty stamp.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: On cross-examination, Timothy M'Carthy admitted that Mr. Couch had been supporting him in London for three weeks prior to the trial, paying for his food and lodging and giving him money.
[EVIDENCE ev13]

public.game_background: Edward Morgan, George Bethell, and Thomas Bullard were indicted for stealing a carriage valued at £100, the property of Richard Keene. The prosecution claimed that a man named Worthington had legitimately purchased the carriage from Morgan and later transferred it to Keene as security for a debt. The defense contended that the carriage was merely hired to Worthington, who therefore had no title to transfer, and the prisoners were repossessing their own property.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Worthington, a cab proprietor, testified that he made a bona fide purchase of the carriage from Edward Morgan for £110. He claimed he paid part of the price down and was to pay the rest in installments, but that the bargain was not reduced to writing.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: A written agreement was produced, bearing Worthington's signature, which detailed a transaction to hire the carriage for £5 down and £5 per month. This directly contradicted Worthington's testimony of an outright sale.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: On August 10th, the three prisoners went to the stable where Keene kept the carriage. Using a pretense that Bullard was showing the carriage to Bethell as a potential buyer, they gained access to it, and Morgan provided horses to drive it away.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: William Smith, a coach-maker, testified that shortly after the transaction, Worthington told him the carriage was 'on hire' and not purchased. Smith stated Worthington said this to avoid paying a debt he owed Smith.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Richard Keene's memory proved unreliable on the stand. He initially forgot about and even denied signing the agreement he made with Bullard to sell the carriage, only recalling it when the document was produced in court.

Description: William Smith Adams, a warehouseman for a tea-dealer, testified that the tea was in fact Bohea, a common black tea disguised to look green, and was not Young Hyson. He stated a person acquainted with tea would find it 'impossible' to mistake the two.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Expert witnesses William Smith Adams and Leonard Wrightson testified the tea was 'the veriest rubbish' and essentially worthless. After accounting for the required duty of 2s. 2¼d. per pound and dock rent, the tea was '8d. worse than nothing' and 'unsaleable in the market'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]

[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Both expert witnesses confirmed that the tea chests were marked with the words 'Young Hyson'.

public.game_background: James Harrisson was indicted for stealing approximately 3 cwt. of lead, various metal fixtures, and 5 gallons of fine port wine from a house belonging to David Hickinbotham. Harrisson had been hired by Hickinbotham to guard the unoccupied house over a weekend.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prosecutor, David Hickinbotham, testified that he hired the prisoner, James Harrisson, to take charge of his unoccupied house at No. 4, Manchester-street from Saturday night, June 12th, until Monday morning.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Police-constable Rumball found ten bottles of the stolen wine hidden in the wash-house on the premises.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Frances Curtis, the prisoner's landlady, testified that on the Sunday he was guarding the house, he was very tipsy, the stairs were wet with red wine, and bottles were on his table.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Police-constable Rumball testified that after the prosecutor said he suspected him, the prisoner came to the station to ask if there was a charge against him and 'express his readiness to meet it.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: On cross-examination, Hickinbotham admitted that just before hiring the prisoner, he had turned out a woman who had lived in the house for seven months and who knew about the port wine.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Hickinbotham also admitted that another man, Thomas Hawkins, had previously stolen a key to the street door and that the police considered Hawkins's residence 'a brothel.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prosecutor admitted that the initial charge against the prisoner before Magistrate Rawlinson was dismissed after hearing from him and Sergeant Rumball.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Police-constable Rumball testified that a search of the prisoner's lodging immediately after the complaint found 'nothing to indicate either wine or bottles.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Police-constable Rumball testified that the prisoner 'did run away' after the prosecutor found the matching bottle, but also that he later 'came to the station, and expressed his willingness to meet the charge.'
[EVIDENCE ev17]

[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The goods were carried away from Taylor's shop in a cart belonging to Joseph Price, who is Evans's brother-in-law. Price received the goods into his cart as Evans brought them out.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: John Purdy testified that Evans and Price approached him and offered him a sovereign to play a role. He was to pretend to be a man named 'Ratford' who had received the goods from Evans and had since disposed of them.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: John Payne, a horse-dealer, testified that he was present at the staged meeting. He confirmed that Evans's anger was feigned and that afterwards Evans told Purdy 'it was capitally done' and paid Purdy a sovereign and Payne himself 10 shillings.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Taylor testified that Evans wrote him a letter, pretending to be working on Taylor's behalf against 'that scamp' (Purdy/Ratford), which arranged the meeting where the deception took place. This letter was presented as being in Evans's handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Ann Brent, Mrs. Taylor's sister, testified that she saw the goods being moved and heard Taylor ask Evans, 'Are they perfectly safe?' to which Evans replied they were.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: A police sergeant testified that when he arrested Evans, he asked him where the things were, and Evans replied 'he knew nothing about them.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Evans received a good character reference in court.

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Gardiner stated that he went to the sugar-house on August 21st and saw Wilson on the premises acting as a 'foreman or master over the men,' who were at work pulling down the building.

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Police Constable Ebenezer Luff testified that he was called to the scene and found two men holding the prisoner back. He heard the prisoner shout, 'Let me go, I will smash her, I will do for her before Monday.' Luff testified that he had to persuade the prisoner to leave on three separate occasions within a short period, as Fell kept returning to challenge George Scott to a fight and make a disturbance.

information:

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Smith, another employee at Barclay's, testified that on August 25th, Bishop brought two more barrels with two duplicate porterage-tickets, allegedly from customers Otterway and Phillips. Smith paid the porterage and issued receipts for Bishop to collect payment.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: James Elliott, a customer of Barclay's, testified that he returns barrels every week but never receives money for them. He stated he never authorized anyone to deliver a barrel and receive £1 on his behalf.
[EVIDENCE ev9]

[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: David Dally, an employee of customer Mr. Phillips, testified that he authorized no one to take a cask and receive money. He stated he is the only one who handles cask returns.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Martin Moore, who runs the Talbot booking-office, testified that Arnall was his employee but Bishop was not. He stated neither had authority to issue tickets, and that if casks were sent back, he should have received the porterage.
[EVIDENCE ev13]

[EVIDENCE ev14]

[EVIDENCE ev15]

information:

Description: The victim, Emma Underwood, met the four prisoners and another female in a public house in Whitechapel. She bought them half a pint of gin after they offered to show her the way to Holborn.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mrs. Underwood testified that after leaving the pub, Jones struck her in the mouth, throwing her to the ground. She stated Douglas held one of her arms while Jones knelt on her chest and took her purse. She claimed all prisoners were present during the attack.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police Sergeant Robert Cole testified that around the time of the robbery, he saw Douglas, Jones, and Samuel Smith with three women near Finsbury-square. Shortly after, he heard a scream, turned back, and saw Smith and Jones passing him.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The pub landlord, Thomas Fairhead, confirmed that Jones and Samuel Smith were with Mrs. Underwood in his pub. He testified that Smith held a 'private conversation' with her before taking her by the arm and leading her out, with Jones following.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: A surgeon, John Bubbers Mather, testified that Mrs. Underwood's dress was 'gorged with blood' and she had 'serious contusions about the head and face,' a mouth injury, loosened teeth, and an injury to her ear.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Henry Douglas stated in his defense that he was a stranger to the other prisoners and was merely passing by after the theatre. He claimed he saw Mrs. Underwood making noise, and was helping her up from the ground when the policeman arrived and arrested him.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Samuel Smith's defense was that he accompanied Mrs. Underwood from the public-house to Aldgate Church and then left her there.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Thomas Jones's defense was that he went with Mrs. Underwood from the pub to Houndsditch and wished her good night there.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Elizabeth Smith's defense was that she was at home at the time of the incident. She was found not guilty.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The victim, Mrs. Underwood, stated at the police station that Samuel Smith was 'an old man, and had neither knocked me down nor robbed me.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Evidence was presented that Samuel Smith had a previous conviction in April 1845, for which he was confined for two years.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: A collision involving the York up-train occurred at Leighton Buzzard on the morning of September 5th, 1848. This was confirmed by William Bosford, a guard on the train.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: On October 26th, a solicitor named John Harrison, acting on Robert Duncan's instructions, sent a letter to the railway company. The letter claimed Mrs. Duncan suffered a miscarriage and was confined to bed, and Mr. O'Brien suffered a bruised knee, severe chest injury, and was spitting blood as a result of the collision.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Catherine Sinfield, who lodged in the Duncans' house from September 5th for a month, testified that she saw Mrs. Duncan 'pretty well every day' and she 'appeared to be well' and was performing her household duties. She only heard of an illness in the last few days of the month.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Susannah Young, a servant at the Duncans' house until late September, testified that her mistress was 'up and about as usual' on the day of and day before the alleged accident, and was never confined to bed while she was there.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: James Berridge, a local greengrocer, testified that Mrs. Duncan came to his shop on Saturday, Sept 2nd, and he went to her house on Monday, Sept 4th, where she paid him. On both occasions, she appeared in her 'usual health'.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Robert Darke testified that O'Brien asked him to copy a letter, claiming to be a witness to the accident. O'Brien told him the real witness was 'gone abroad, or was dead,' and that this fabricated letter would get the compensation paid immediately.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Upon their arrest, officer Donald Murray testified that Robert Duncan said, 'I ought not to take his wife, for she had acted under his direction.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Officer John Wright testified that when O'Brien was arrested and told Duncan had partly admitted his guilt, O'Brien responded that Duncan 'was a traitor big enough for anything.'

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: At the station, both Harrison and Officer Batton testified that Smith apologized, claimed he had made a mistake, and said he had taken Harrison for another gentleman. Smith offered to make compensation if Harrison would forgive him.
[EVIDENCE ev4]

public.game_background: Robert Dailey was indicted for wilful and corrupt perjury. He is accused of making a false affidavit to secure the arrest of his former employer, Frederick Bennett. In the affidavit, Dailey swore that Bennett owed him £27 3s. 4d., was preparing to flee to Australia to avoid his creditors, and had sold his business, claims which the prosecution asserts are entirely false and were made maliciously after Dailey was discharged from employment.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: An affidavit signed by the defendant, Robert Dailey, was presented as evidence. This legal document formed the basis for a writ of capias, leading to the arrest of Frederick Bennett.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Frederick Bennett testified that he discharged Dailey on Saturday, 30th June, for using insulting language. When discharged, Dailey allegedly threatened him, stating, 'Then, by God! I will ruin you.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Frederick Bennett testified that he never had a man named John Redding in his employ, nor any person who went by that name, and that the prisoner Dailey must have known this.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Frederick Bennett testified that he never had the slightest intention of quitting the country, never sent any boxes to Mr. Rawlings for shipment to Australia, and had not sold his business or property.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Under cross-examination, Frederick Bennett admitted that he had borrowed money from Dailey 'more than once' and had 'occasionally given him I O U's'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Bennett testified that a former employee named John Cooper, who Dailey knew, had been discharged twelve months prior. Cooper never went by the name of Redding.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Alfred Jones, a solicitor, presented Bishop's signed examination from the bankruptcy court. The statement declared that Bishop purchased Park-farm for £299 5s. with money saved by himself and his wife, a £98 prize from a Derby sweep, and that no part of the money or property on the farm was from the bankrupt, Hugh Swan.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Stephen Woodland, the former proprietor, testified that Bishop paid him £349 5s., including a £200 note. He stated he once saw Bishop and Swan together before the purchase and that Bishop later told him Swan had lent him 'a little money' for the farm.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Joseph Parrington testified that when confronted with a warrant to seize the farm, Bishop eventually stated, 'I have no property belonging to Swan; this is all my property here.' When asked if Swan had advanced the money, Bishop replied, 'Whatever has passed between the bankrupt and me has nothing to do with the property here.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: John Falshaw Pawson, a creditor and assignee, admitted under cross-examination that the assignees had previously indicted individuals for an assault related to the farm (who were acquitted), had brought a civil action against Bishop, and that Bishop had in turn sued him. The perjury indictment was brought after these other legal conflicts.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Adele Fleyen, the cook, testified that she secured the kitchen on the night of December 6th. The next morning, she discovered the drawing-room had been ransacked, with drawers pulled out and papers scattered on the floor.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The cook, Adele Fleyen, testified that an iron bar on the kitchen window was bent and loosened from the stonework. This created an opening large enough for a person to enter.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Moses Roberts, a beer-shop keeper, testified that the two prisoners were at his establishment in Notting-hill with two other persons between nine and ten o'clock on the night of the burglary.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Police-Sergeant William Glenister stated that he and another officer encountered and apprehended the two prisoners, Frewin and Crewe, together on the morning of December 7th, about two hours after the burglary was reported.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Sgt. Glenister testified that as he was escorting Frewin to the station, Frewin turned towards Crewe, who was following with another officer, and shouted 'Ding it'.

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Relying on Willis's assurances that the receipt would be paid without hesitation, Thomas Thomas gave Willis the difference between the receipt's value and his hotel bill (£3 8s. 6d.), amounting to over £11 in the current coin of the realm.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Christopher Hodgson, the treasurer of Queen Anne's Bounty, testified that the office opens at ten in the morning, not eleven, as the prisoner had claimed.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mr. Hodgson testified that while Willis was indeed the incumbent of Minster, he was not entitled to the sum mentioned in the receipt on April 11th because the living was under sequestration. The money had subsequently been paid to the sequestrators.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: A letter written by the prisoner was received at the Bounty office on the morning of April 11th. The letter, dated the previous evening, stated that he was 'recovering from a state of mind almost bordering upon insanity' and had distributed more receipts than one, and therefore 'none will be attended to.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Testimony revealed that in April 1848, the prisoner had presented three separate receipts for a single half-year's dividend. When questioned by Mr. Hodgson, Willis wrote a letter blaming the incident on wrongly worded receipts and a misunderstanding.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John William Jones, a solicitor's clerk, testified that the prisoner, John Lawrence, offered him £10 to 'get a counterpart lease away from your office.' This meeting was allegedly set up by an unidentified young man.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Police Sergeant Thomas Hardwick testified that he was given the memorandum by Jones but lost it in court during the previous session. Despite searches, the original document could not be found.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Walter Southwood, one of the solicitors, testified that he, his partner, and the client Mr. Smith, consented to Jones handing over the deed. This was done for the purpose of 'ascertaining what the prisoner intended.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Sergeant Hardwick testified that he was in the public house and saw Lawrence and Jones in conversation. He witnessed Lawrence looking at the deed and heard a 'rattling, apparently of gold.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Sergeant Hardwick testified that when he stopped Lawrence after he left the public house and asked what he had, Lawrence claimed 'he really did not know' and did not know the party he got it from.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: During cross-examination of Jones, the defense lawyer suggested an alternative scenario: that Lawrence arranged to take the deed home 'for two or three days on his lodging 10l. with me as a security that he would return it.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: It was established that the deed was damaged in the Royal Exchange fire and that much of it was obliterated, making it difficult to read.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: On cross-examination, Jones admitted he did not express any 'abhorrence or detestation' at the criminal proposition and remained calm. He stated, 'I did not feel indignant at having such a proposition made to me, and do not now.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Sergeant Hardwick testified that he could not overhear what Lawrence and Jones said because of 'the louder talking of the other persons' and because 'they were speaking very low indeed.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mr. C. P. Bartley, a solicitor for Seth Smith, testified that leases for similar shops in the same street had increased in value since 1830, with premiums of £300 being received on the same rent.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The prisoner received a good character reference in court.

information:

Description: Josephine Leantare, the victim's niece, testified that on June 12th, a distress for rent was put on her aunt's property. The defendants, George Moody and John Jones, were the bailiffs placed in possession of the two rooms.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: After the goods were taken to the cellar, Ms. Leantare saw a hat belonging to Moody left on the floor. Inside the hat was a tablecloth belonging to her aunt, which she had seen shortly before in another room.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Ms. Leantare testified that after she returned from a brief errand to the landlord, she noticed that Moody's pockets appeared more bulky than they did before she left. During her absence, only Moody and Jones were present.
[EVIDENCE ev4]

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: During her visit to Moody's house, Ms. Leantare saw Mary Ann Moody wearing a shawl belonging to her aunt.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: At the same time, Ms. Leantare saw John Jones outside Moody's house wearing a distinctive pink cotton handkerchief belonging to her aunt.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: A search of Moody's premises revealed several other stolen items, including neck-ties, an eye-glass, salt-spoons, scissors, and a toilet-cover, hidden in a box over the water-butt in the yard.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: A search of Jones's residence revealed a card-rack, padlocks, a crucifix, and other stolen items belonging to Madame Lebas.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Thomas Butler, whose step-daughter was married to Jones, testified that he received a watch and several other stolen items from Jones's wife around the time of the theft. Some she sold to him, and others she left for him to take care of.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The landlord, Thomas Westlake, testified that the goods he seized under the distress warrant were old and of very little value, and did not include a watch, scales, or any of the other valuable items that were stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev15]

[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The prisoners received good characters.

[EVIDENCE ev7]

[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Thomas Green, Walstab's former partner, testified that he personally took an order from Allen in November 1850, and that Allen later paid him 9s. 8d. and gave him a further order on December 12th. Green was certain that the defendant was the person he dealt with.

Description: In his testimony at the County Court, Allen claimed he had let the business in June 1850 to a man named Husband. He stated his name remained on the shop for only a month or six weeks after that, long before the goods in question were delivered in December 1850.

information:

Description: William Curtis Crutchley testified that the prisoner, James Kimble, confronted him, took him by the collar, and demanded he retract his words, saying, "Now, prove I am a liar!"
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: An independent eyewitness, John Lambert, testified that he saw one young man (Kimble) point an object at the other's (Crutchley's) face, lower it, and then raise it again and fire it directly in his face.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Frances Elizabeth Payne testified that about a fortnight before the incident, Kimble said he had only made up with Crutchley 'to get what he could out of him' and that he would have a 'serious quarrel with him' soon.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Thomas Howard testified that he lent the pistol to Kimble on the morning of the incident, that it was unloaded at the time, and that he warned Kimble not to fire it or 'play any games with it.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Dr. George Crutch, a surgeon, testified that the victim's face had numerous small punctured wounds and that black specks embedded in his eye and skin would be permanent. This established a lasting injury.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The surgeon, Dr. Crutch, stated the injury was likely caused by the discharge of a pistol 'not loaded with ball.' The wadding from the pistol also struck a bystander, Richard Stubbings.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Richard Stubbings, who was present, testified that he had known the prisoner for about a year and that he 'has been a good-natured boy; I never found anything else.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police Constable Richard William Key testified that on February 3rd, the prisoner came to a public house and 'wished to give himself into custody, for shooting a young gentleman.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Jones testified that he saw a placard for the 'Gold Mineral Company' and met with defendant Tripe, who stated, 'We have a vessel going,' named the Camilla. Tripe provided a dietary scale that included a bottle of wine per week and a pint of beer a day, which Jones found attractive.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: James Gowland testified that Tripe told him the company had 'chartered' the ship 'Medicis' for Australia. Based on this representation, Gowland paid a £9 deposit for his nephew's passage.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Robert Ewart Shaw, a clerk for the ship brokers of the 'Camilla,' testified that his firm had only a preliminary discussion with the defendants about chartering the vessel. He stated no agreement was ever concluded and that he 'had never given any authority, either to Tripe or Montague, to engage berths.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Richard John Edwards, ship broker for the 'Medicis,' testified that negotiations with the defendants to charter the ship 'went off for want of a sufficient guarantee.' He stated, 'I never authorized Montague or Tripe to dispose of berths on board the Medicis.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Shaw (broker for the Camilla) testified that when he confronted Montague about passages being sold without authorization, Montague 'regretted it, and would take care it should not happen again, and that the money should be returned.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Charles Dawson, an attorney, testified that although he was spoken to about forming a company, 'no company was ever formed, to my knowledge.' He also stated that his name was used without his authority on a prospectus as the company's solicitor.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Henry Tucker, a clerk from the Registration Office, testified after searching the register that 'no such company as the "Australian Gold and General Mining and Emigration Company" has been registered at any time, provisionally or otherwise.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: During cross-examination, the solicitor Charles Dawson confirmed that Major Hawkes and Mr. Lutwyche, named as directors, were men of apparent respectability and wealth. He also stated that Major Hawkes introduced Montague to him as the company's mineralogist.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: James Barker testified that before he paid his money, he was aware that Mr. Greenwood, the secretary, was consulted about reducing the fare for his party, and that Greenwood agreed to the reduction. This suggests a hierarchy and process within the office.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: John Railton, a shipping agent, testified that he understood Tripe acted in a 'perfectly subordinate capacity,' merely as the 'servant of those gentlemen' (the directors). He also testified that Montague represented he was 'going out under the company's service as a mineralogist.'

Description: Francis Disney, the shop owner, identified the recovered shoes as his property. He testified they were in his shop on Saturday evening and were gone on Monday, and he knew them by their make.

Description: Mr. Disney re-examined, testified he had known both prisoners from infancy, had never known them do a wrong action, and believed they had been drinking or they would not have done it. He, along with the Jury, strongly recommended them to mercy.

public.game_background: William Jarrett and Henry Keene are charged with unlawfully obtaining goods (flour, oats, and hay) from William Pinnock by false pretences. The prosecution alleges that Keene falsely represented Jarrett as a wealthy and trustworthy businessman, thereby inducing Pinnock to sell goods on credit, which the defendants then allegedly misappropriated without intention to pay the full amount.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. William either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Pinnock testified that Henry Keene approached him in July, claiming to have left his former employer and to be living on an income of £100-£200 a year. Keene offered to recommend good customers, promising to bring only 'the very best'.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: A few days later, Pinnock asked Keene what he thought of Jarrett. Keene responded that Jarrett was a 'man of plenty of money' and claimed to have seen him with 'three 50l. notes the night before'.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Jarrett came with a man named Nicholls to buy fifty sacks of flour. Nicholls offered to lend Jarrett £50 for the transaction, creating an impression of financial backing. Later, when questioned by Pinnock, Nicholls admitted he had only known Jarrett for about a month.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Henry Greatorex, a neighbor, testified that Jarrett's shop oven was lit only twice, and goods like flour and oats were removed soon after delivery. Pinnock confirmed that on visiting the shop, he found it in disarray with almost no stock, indicating it was not a functioning business.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Thomas Terry, Keene's former employer, testified that Jarrett had previously ordered flour from their firm and failed to pay. Terry stated he had told Keene at the time that he believed Jarrett 'was a swindler,' proving Keene was aware of Jarrett's dishonest character.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Vale, the landlord of the shop Jarrett rented, testified that Jarrett occupied the premises for about two months and paid nothing for rent.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Corn dealer William Brown testified that Keene and Jarrett sold him forty quarters of oats for £31 10s. The oats were in sacks marked 'Pinnock' and had been sold to Jarrett on credit for approximately £40.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Henry Cook testified that around August 1st, in Keene's presence, Jarrett claimed he 'had not a shilling' to repay a £15 debt and had to borrow 1s from Keene.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Police-sergeant George Quinnear testified he arrested Keene in Gloucester, where he was living under the alias 'Wright'. When informed of the charge, Keene stated, 'Jerrard is a great swindler, and I wish I had never seen him.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Pinnock testified that after the delivery of fifty sacks of flour, Jarrett came and paid him £50 on account. This payment led Pinnock to supply a further order of oats.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Thomas Franklyn, a local victualler whose brother-in-law previously ran the shop, testified against Jarrett. Franklyn admitted to being 'very active in this matter,' making inquiries, introducing Pinnock to a solicitor, and transporting witnesses in his carriage.

The affidavit claimed that five named witnesses from Emscote, Warwickshire, had attended the trial for two days and had been paid specific sums for their expenses.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Sparks further testified that a month after the trial, Steadman told him he had charged for his attendance and instructed him to say 'yes' if asked if he was at the trial. Steadman then had his man, Yorston, take Sparks, Miller, and Williams to Westminster to see the court, so they could truthfully say they had been 'at the Court.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Under cross-examination, William Sparks admitted that he is now employed by Mr. Knight, the prosecutor in this case and the defendant in the original civil suit, and is earning a higher wage (33s. per week, up from 24s.).
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William Shadrake, Steadman's foreman, testified that Steadman was too ill to manage his business affairs due to an accident. Shadrake was tasked with subpoenaing the witnesses but failed to travel to Emscote as he missed the train.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Dr. Edward Digby, a surgeon, testified that he attended Steadman for serious injuries to his head and shoulders sustained in an accident. He stated that at the time of the trial, Steadman was in a 'very unfit state to attend to business.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Stone testified that he paid John Allen £1 4s. 11d. for goods on or about April 12th. He detailed that his wife fetched a sovereign, Allen receipted the bill, read it aloud, and then Stone paid him a sovereign and 5 shillings, receiving a penny back.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Stone testified that at the County Court trial, he heard the oath administered to Allen and heard him deny signing the bill for £1 4s. 11d. and deny ever having received the money.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: During William Stone's testimony, the prisoner, John Allen, interjected, stating, 'I am no scholar, I cannot read.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William Stone testified that on August 17th, he brought out several paid receipts to prove to Allen that no money was owed, but the receipts went missing a few minutes after Allen left the house.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: A certified copy of the judgment from the Clerkenwell County Court showed that the judge found a verdict against Stone for the amount claimed by Allen.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: On cross-examination, William Stone admitted he could not say precisely how much he owed Allen at the time of the disputed £1 4s. 11d. payment, stating 'it is a long time back.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The prisoner, John Allen, received a good character reference at the end of the trial.

[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mary Ann Tarron, a co-worker, testified that on a Saturday she saw the prisoner take up two unfinished shirts, put them in a handkerchief, and leave the room with them.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The foreman, Samuel Kohen, testified that about a fortnight before the incident, he told the prisoner he would not give her any more work to do at home because she once failed to bring a shirt back on time.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Upon being found by the foreman and a policeman, the prisoner immediately said, 'You know I brought three shirts away to finish on Saturday night, which I intended to bring back this morning, but I could not get them done.'

Description: The prisoner did not return to work on the Monday following the Saturday she took the shirts.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Mary Ann Tarron testified that one of the shirts produced was one the prisoner had been making, identifying it by a peak on the wristband, though she admitted she had not paid particular attention to it before.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Caroline Burton testified that she was introduced to Frederick Crutchley and engaged in an initial loan of £18 for a £20 bill, describing it as a 'joint concern' with either Crutchley or another man named Stanborough.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Witness John Chapman testified that he knew William Clarke Wright and stated, 'he is certainly not a man of fortune, or a man with landed estates... he is a very poor man,' having seen him in the Queen's Bench prison.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Witness John Bert, an attorney, testified that while Wright 'used to have an estate in Northumberland... he has not had it for many years' and is 'decidedly not a person in thriving circumstances.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Letters written by Morton to Wright were read in court. They detailed plans for fraudulent bills, mentioned 'Fred' (Crutchley), and instructed Wright on how to create false backstories for their targets, with one letter stating, 'He thinks I am your man of business.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The defense submitted to the judge that there was no evidence Morton received any of the money obtained from Ms. Burton and argued the letters did not prove a conspiracy with Crutchley. The Court considered the case sufficient to be decided by the Jury.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Caroline Burton testified that the defendant, Crutchley, was introduced to her in 1852 by a Mr. Stanborough. The first financial transaction between them was Burton discounting a £20 bill for Crutchley as a personal favour.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Caroline Burton testified that she had recently seen the real Mr. Wright, describing him as a 'vulgar, low man' who told her he 'had not 20s. in the world'. She stated that if she had seen him once, she never would have lent the money.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: John Bert, who knew Wright for about twenty years, testified that during 1852-1853 Wright was not a person of any property, had been bankrupt five or six years prior, and was a man of no means at all.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Letters between Morton and Wright were read in court, detailing instructions on how to create the fraudulent bills and write letters to deceive. The letters refer to 'Mr. C' (Crutchley) and include Morton's comment, 'He thinks I am your man of business.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Under cross-examination, Caroline Burton admitted to having occasionally speculated in shares and discounted a few other bills out of 'kindness and charity', contradicting her initial claim of being a complete novice to such transactions.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Burton testified that she was aware Crutchley was not of legal age during most of the transactions, stating 'I know you are not of age, and therefore I have no legal claim on you.' Crutchley came of age in March 1853.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Anthony Boucher, an attorney, testified that Crutchley had a future inheritance worth £2,000-£4,000 but could not access it until age 24. He also stated he understood Crutchley had raised money against this inheritance to go into partnership with Mr. Burton, the victim's husband.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The prosecution's solicitor testified that William Clarke Wright, the man falsely represented as wealthy, had been bound over to appear at the trial but had since disappeared and could not be found.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Testimony revealed that William Morton listed a £2,000 bill drawn by W. Wright on Captain Bulkeley in his bankruptcy balance sheet. This was one of the fraudulent bills that had been passed to Mrs. Burton.

Description: In response to a direct question from the defense counsel, Mrs. Inglis testified that her husband never told the prisoner that if he gave information, he would not be prosecuted. Mr. Inglis also testified that neither he nor his wife (in his hearing) told the prisoner it would be better or worse for him to confess.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: To clarify a point of confusion in Mr. Inglis's testimony, the Court re-examined Mrs. Inglis, who confirmed that her husband's first question was "How much have you taken?" and that the prisoner's first answer was "Eight shillings."
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Inspector Henry John Teague testified that upon being taken into custody and charged with stealing the £7 5s 10.5d, the prisoner stated, "I did not steal the whole of it."
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The prisoner's statement before the Magistrate was read in court, in which he said, "I took the money to send over to her [my mother in the country, very badly off]."
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mr. Inglis stated that the prisoner's wages were 3s 6d a week plus board and lodging. He also testified that when he hired the prisoner four months earlier, Wright said he only had 10s and no friends or relations in London.

[EVIDENCE ev14]

[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Testimonies from William Smith (grocer) and Henry Heed (baker) showed that they too had advanced money and goods to Mead based on similar claims of a large inheritance from the East Indies.

[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Alfred Trevor Crispin, a solicitor, testified that the newspaper advertisement Mead had used to convince his victims actually referred to an intestate estate worth only £130.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Policeman Joseph Huggett testified that upon arrest, Mead said everyone would have their money in a fortnight. Huggett also discovered that Mead was a farm labourer from Essex.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In his written defense, Mead claimed his inheritance claim was legitimate, originating from a Times advertisement in 1843 concerning his uncle, Samuel Mead of Bengal. He asserted his claim was admitted in 1845.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Victim Jeremiah O'Keefe testified that he went to Keate's court with a young woman of 'loose character' to a 'bad place'. While waiting for her, he saw the three prisoners emerge from an opposite doorway.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: O'Keefe testified that he identified all three prisoners to the police after their arrests. He picked out Rogers and Merritt at the station from among other men, and later identified Bedborough, who was found in bed.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Policeman George Covill testified that when he went to arrest Bedborough and told him it was on suspicion of stealing a watch, Bedborough responded, 'What, for that souper up the court?' Covill explained that 'souper' is a slang term for a watch.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The mothers of two prisoners and a friend visited O'Keefe's home and offered money (£2 6s. up to a requested £3) for him not to appear in court. O'Keefe stated he said nothing, but his wife discussed the terms in his presence, though he ultimately refused the offer.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Policeman Covill testified that the three prisoners were known to him and that he had seen them together on several occasions, particularly Rogers and Merritt, at a pub at the corner of Keate-court, near where the robbery occurred.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The police searched all three prisoners after their arrest, but the stolen watch, chain, and key were not found on any of them.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Jeremiah O'Keefe stated that he was sober at the time of the attack. Policeman Covill testified that when O'Keefe came to the station to report the crime, he 'was then perfectly sober.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: All three prisoners denied the charges upon their arrest. Rogers and Merritt said O'Keefe must be a 'bad one' to accuse them, and Bedborough said he 'knew nothing about it; he was not there.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_01]
Description: Jane Barnes testified that last Saturday month, she saw the prisoner, Mary Gollacher, at the Benbow public house. When Barnes and her companion Thomas Clynes exited, Gollacher was waiting for them on the kerb.
[EVIDENCE ev_03]
Description: Thomas Clynes testified that about ten minutes before the incident, the prisoner's brother (aged 17) struck him on the arm with a poker. Clynes stated he did not strike the brother back but merely took the poker from him.
[EVIDENCE ev_06]
Description: Jane Barnes testified that as soon as she stepped between Clynes and the prisoner, she was stabbed with a knife in the left eye. She stated the effect of this was to "burst my right eye out."
[EVIDENCE ev_13]
Description: The prisoner claimed she had the knife in her hand when she went out because she had been cutting some bread for her tea at that moment.
[EVIDENCE ev_15]
Description: The jury found the prisoner GUILTY of unlawfully Wounding, a lesser offense than the original charge of stabbing with intent to do grievous bodily harm.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The court heard Louisa Harrisson's original sworn testimony from the trial of Charles Mallett, in which she gave a detailed account of being robbed and violently assaulted in her home. She described the attacker striking her, stealing her money, tying her hands with a clothesline, and threatening her baby before fleeing.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Emma Ferguson and Officer Little both testified that Harrisson's husband, upon arriving at the scene, stated in her presence that she had told him at tea-time she 'had had a dreadful dream' and 'was sure something would happen before night.' Harrisson later repeated this herself.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Charles Mallett testified that he was completely innocent. He provided a detailed alibi, stating that at the time of the alleged robbery (6:30-6:45 PM), he was at his place of work, a penny theatre in Whitechapel, where he was employed to keep order.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The crime scene at Bull Inn yard, Aldgate, was established to be a 'five or six minutes' walk' from the penny theatre where Mallett and his witnesses placed him.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Mallett admitted to going by the name 'Charles Mallett' instead of his real name, Simon Marriott. He claimed he adopted the alias because he had been a 'fighting man' and did not want to 'disgrace his friends.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The standard procedure for payments at the company involved the prisoner, as secretary, preparing cheques for approved accounts. The Board would sign them, and the prisoner would then countersign and deliver them to the intended payees.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Dr. Thomas Crocker, the company's medical officer in Bristol, testified that the company owed him sixteen guineas in December and a total of £38 19s 6d by March. He was not paid until receiving a cheque for £40 on August 31, 1854, long after the cheques in question were issued.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mr. Thomas also testified that on June 7, 1854, at the prisoner's request, he signed another receipt for Dr. M'Dermott's fees of seventeen guineas under a similar promise that a cheque would be sent. This payment was also not made at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Mr. Cracknell, a director, admitted on cross-examination that the directors had publicly thanked Mr. Bevan for his exertions, which had raised the company's business from just two policies to around £6,000.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Cracknell testified that the Board generally signed cheques prepared by the prisoner 'without much discussion' and that the company's accounts were only audited once a year.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: When irregularities were first discovered in July 1854, Mr. Cracknell confronted the prisoner, who admitted to 'great irregularities' but denied any intent to defraud the company. He then resigned at the director's suggestion.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: It was revealed during cross-examination that the prisoner had been tried at the last Session for stealing other cheques and had been acquitted.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: George Knox, the accountant, testified that the prisoner went over the accounts with him and admitted to deficiencies amounting to about £600 before submitting his counter-claim.

information:

Description: William Edroff testified that while at the bar of the Eagle Tavern, he observed the prisoner Jones put his hand into his left waistcoat pocket and take his watch.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: During cross-examination, Roland Hurst stated he was not positive about what he saw, admitting, 'I did not see anything distinctly' and that Jones's hand was closed.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Pointer testified that when he caught Jones, Jones said, 'You fool, what did you catch hold of me for? I was running after the thief.'

Description: Testimony from Edroff and Hurst established that the bar was very crowded, with fifty or sixty people present.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Edroff testified on cross-examination that he briefly lost sight of Jones during the chase when a cab passed between them.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Edroff testified that after the chase, he returned to the tavern and found Roberts still standing at the bar.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The stolen watch was never found or recovered.

information:

Description: Virtue Thirkettle, an attendant at Hampton Court Palace, testified that it was her duty to attend the room containing the model. On the day in question, she was having dinner in a small adjoining room.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Ms. Thirkettle heard footsteps, opened her door, and saw the prisoner as the only person near the model. He was at the back part of the model, where a person standing in front could not see what was done.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The witness saw the prisoner walk up to the model, put his hand on a piece of the wire railing, feel it, take his hand off, and then put it on again.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Ms. Thirkettle testified she was sure the wire was not on the boards, as she had passed the model less than half an hour before and the railing was perfectly safe.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The policeman took the prisoner back to the room and fitted the wire to the model. The prisoner said nothing at that time.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: While being conveyed to the House of Detention, the prisoner gave his correct name and address, and asked the policeman what punishment he thought he would get.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: In cross-examination, the policeman stated the prisoner's friends 'seemed very decent, respectable people.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Phillips, a court clerk, read the sworn information of Sarah Browning dated Aug 15, 1855. She stated that Henry Watts entered her room, shook his fist in her face, laid hold of her, and tore her finger, causing it to bleed, after she refused to pay a disputed bill.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Policeman Henry White, who was called to the scene, testified that he 'saw a graze on her thumb, and she had a rag round it, which had a little blood on it.' He stated he saw no drops of blood.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: It was established that Sarah Browning had given evidence against William Watts's son in a manslaughter case. Watts subsequently hired the same attorney who defended his son to prosecute Browning for perjury.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Prosecution witnesses gave conflicting accounts of Watts's location. Target claimed Watts never went into the room. Webb said he was in the passage. Knapp said he was about three feet from Browning. This contrasts with defense witness Sutton, who claimed Watts dragged Browning from the fireplace inside the room.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prosecution's own witness, Francis Webb, admitted that when Mrs. Browning went to the street door and called 'Police', he 'went and shut it... because there should be no piece of work.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George James Ward testified that the prisoners accosted him, with Boyce pinning him against railings by the throat, rendering him helpless, while Rushworth tore his watch from his pocket and took money.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Reynold Eastland, a policeman, testified that he heard cries of 'Police!' and 'Stop thief!' and saw Ward and Rushworth running toward him. Ward was covered in mud and dirt.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Latter, a surgeon residing at Adelphi-terrace, testified that the defendant collects dung in barges which remain for eight to ten days. He stated that the manure smells very badly and the stench is a great inconvenience all over his house.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Latter described the manure as an 'omnium gatherum,' including stable manure, cow dung, and vegetable matter. He stated that there was a strong suspicion of human ordure from the 'intolerable stench,' and recalled an instance of decomposing carrots that 'very nearly destroyed us.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: As a medical man, Henry Latter stated that he considers the stench injurious to health. He claimed he could prove it has been 'particularly so in our neighbourhood.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: David Gordon Laing, a decorator whose workshop is ten yards from the wharf, testified that an apprentice of his was 'taken with a slow fever' and another workman 'cannot stand' the smell. He described the smell as being 'more like night soil than anything else.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mr. Laing also testified that dung is sometimes dropped along the ride (a slope) and 'lies there till it festers, and the whole ride is covered with it,' sometimes for a week, before being gathered into the barge. He stated this process makes the smell worse.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Charles Elder, proprietor of the Adelphi Hotel, testified that the nuisance caused him a 'serious loss' of business. He stated that 'many families have complained of it, and left through it.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Elder claimed the defendant 'manufactured the stuff into manure in the barges' by mixing wet and dry materials to aid decomposition. He stated this process, along with turning the mixture over, made the smell worse because it was 'steaming up.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: According to Mr. Elder, when he complained to the defendant about the 'insufferable' smell, the defendant threatened him, saying 'if I would go down he would put my head into it.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Alfred Dickins, a superintendent inspector for the Board of Health, inspected the wharf on January 9th. He saw 'barges there loading with apparently reeking stable manure, the smell from which was very offensive, and quite perceptible from the terrace and the streets adjoining.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Henry Longman, who lives directly above the wharf, testified that he saw men moving the manure about in the barge. He stated 'it was so short they could not turn it over with their forks, they were obliged to take shovels,' indicating it was highly decomposed.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: George Hancock, the parish inspector of nuisances, stated that the problem has become 'very much worse in the summer, and worse since Gore's wharf has been done away with; for I think the defendant has taken a portion that Gore used to have.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: On cross-examination, surgeon Henry Latter admitted the presence of four common sewers near his house. He conceded that 'no doubt we smell the sewers sometimes' and that the smell is blown directly onto the terrace when the wind is south-west.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: George Bessant, who purchases the material from the defendant's barge for use as manure, testified on behalf of the defense. He stated that for the last five years he has bought the whole of it, and for the last twelve months, it consisted of 'horse dung, nothing else.'

information:

Description: On November 23rd, City policeman William Shaw, who was watching the prosecutors' premises, saw the prisoner William Griffiths leaving with a bag.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: When the policeman stated that Griffiths must return to the warehouse, the prisoner asked 'whether anything else would not do,' which was interpreted as an attempt to avoid returning.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Under cross-examination, Millington admitted it was 'the constant practice' for employees to buy goods from the establishment for themselves and to sell again, although it was 'not allowed by our employers.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The cashier, Charles Coles, testified that the prisoner did not pay him for the forks, night lights, or slate pencils, and that there was no record of payment for the lamps in his book for that day.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: James Camp, an assistant, testified that he saw a box of night lights in the prisoner's cupboard and slate pencils in his drawer a few days before the arrest, and that these items were gone after Griffiths was taken into custody.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: On cross-examination, witness James Camp admitted that he had previously been 'decidedly suspected of dishonesty' by his employers in a case involving a significant sum of money missing from the cash till.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: During cross-examination, the arresting officer confirmed that the property found at the prisoner's house was 'readily shown' to him and there was 'no disguise about it'.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The prisoner received a good character reference in court.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Lloyd Jones testified that while looking at a placard in a shop window, he felt something move in his left waistcoat pocket where his watch was kept. He stated he had felt his watch was safe just a few minutes before.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: City Policeman Robert Lilley searched the prisoner upon taking him into custody but found nothing on him except a knife. The stolen watch was not recovered from his person.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Approximately ten minutes after the arrest, Policeman Lilley returned to the scene. He searched the area and found a ring belonging to the stolen watch on the pavement in front of the shop.

Description: When Policeman Lilley asked the prisoner for his address, he gave a vague and unhelpful answer, stating he lived at a coffee shop in the Westminster-road but did not know the name or number.

public.game_background: George Sandford Keymer was indicted for embezzling three sums of money (£18, £28 14s 8d, and £15 10s) from his masters, William Manoah Chambers and another, proprietors of a wine merchant business. The prosecution argues Keymer was a salaried employee whose duty was to collect and immediately remit payments. The defense contends the relationship was more akin to a partnership or a debtor-creditor arrangement, and that the non-payment was a matter for a civil accounting, not a criminal charge.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Abbott, a licensed victualler, testified that he paid £18 to the prisoner on account of Messrs. Chambers and Ellwood. He stated he dealt primarily with the prisoner, whom he presumed was their traveller, and knew little of the firm itself.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Alfred Grace, another victualler, testified he paid the prisoner multiple sums on account of Chambers and Ellwood. During cross-examination, he admitted he knew nothing of the relations between the prisoner and the firm and did not know if he was a traveller or a partner.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Manoah Chambers, a partner in the prosecuting firm, testified that the prisoner was employed as a 'town traveller' on a salary, and his duty was to pay over any money he received on the same or the following day.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: A document, described as an 'account,' was provided by the prosecutors to the prisoner's cousin. This document calculated the total profits from the prisoner's business, halved them, and listed 'Cash drawn' by the prisoner and 'Accounts received and not accounted for' as debits against his share.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Ellwood, the other partner, testified that the firm's bookkeeping was poor. He stated that payments to the prisoner for salary and expenses were often entered as lump sums at year's end based on 'little slips' of paper which were subsequently destroyed, and he could not produce detailed vouchers.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Chambers testified that in 1853, a previous defalcation by the prisoner amounting to over £470 was discovered. He stated, 'I said to him, "You must have taken some of these moneys; but if you will go through the ledger with me, and not keep one item back, I will forgive you"... that sum was treated by us as a debt owing by him.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner's statement before the Magistrate was read, in which he claimed: 'when I joined Chambers and Ellwood, Mr. Chambers made an agreement with me that I should have half the profits upon all the connection I introduced, and that I should bear half the loss of any bad debts incurred, and that separate books should be kept for that purpose.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mr. Chambers was cross-examined about whether he initiated the prosecution before or after receiving a letter from the prisoner's attorney threatening an action for slander. Description: A solicitor, Mr. Fesenmeyer, testified that he had prepared a draft employment agreement for the prisoner, which stated he was to be a clerk. However, the agreement was never executed because the prisoner failed to find a security.

public.game_background: William Palmer is charged with the wilful murder of John Parsons Cook in November 1855. The prosecution alleges that Palmer, motivated by financial desperation, poisoned Cook with strychnine to steal his recent race winnings. The defense argues that Cook's death was due to natural causes, exacerbated by his poor health and the general illness prevalent at the time.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: At the Shrewsbury races on Wednesday, Nov 14th, John Parsons Cook drank a glass of brandy and water in the company of William Palmer. Immediately after, Cook complained of a burning sensation in his throat and became violently ill with vomiting.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Several witnesses, including Ishmael Fisher, mentioned that many people attending the Shrewsbury races fell ill with sickness during that week. Fisher noted a friend was 'rather poorly there'.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Dr. Gibson, who attended Cook at Shrewsbury, testified that Cook's tongue was 'perfectly clean.' He stated that in a bilious attack, the tongue would typically be discolored.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: On Sunday, Nov 18th, chambermaid Elizabeth Mills tasted about two tablespoons of broth sent by Palmer for Cook. Within an hour, she became violently ill with vomiting that lasted all afternoon and confined her to bed.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: On Monday, Nov 19th, Charles Newton, a surgeon's assistant, sold William Palmer three grains of strychnia at approximately 9 PM.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: On Tuesday, Nov 20th, Palmer bought six grains of strychnia and two drachms of opium solution from a druggist's apprentice, Charles Roberts. Roberts testified Palmer acted suspiciously, luring another person out of the shop before making the purchase.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The post-mortem examination of Cook's body revealed no natural cause of death. However, analysts Dr. Taylor and Dr. Rees failed to find any strychnine in the stomach, though they noted the stomach was delivered to them in a very unfavorable condition for analysis.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Witnesses for the defense, including George Myatt, testified that Cook was 'the worse for liquor' on the Wednesday night at Shrewsbury, contradicting Fisher's testimony. Myatt also testified that Palmer was never out of his sight that night.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: A shorthand writer's transcript of Joseph Hague's sworn testimony from a bastardy appeal on April 25th was read in court. In it, Hague denied being the father of Emma Plaister's child, denied ever having a sexual relationship with her, denied visiting her father's house more than once, denied walking with her, and provided an alibi for the evening of November 12th, 1854.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Emma Plaister testified that Joseph Hague is the father of her child. She stated their first sexual encounter was on Sunday, November 5th, 1854, after he came to her house around 10:30 PM and they went to a nearby plantation. She claimed her sister Ann was home at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Tiley, a millwright and friend of Hague, testified that on Sunday, November 5th, 1854, he was with Hague at the Cross Lances public house from 6 PM until midnight. He stated they stayed in the bar with the landlord after the house closed at 10 PM and he heard the church clock strike 12 as they left.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: William Henry Plaister, Emma's father, testified that on Sunday, November 12th, 1854, at 11:30 PM, Joseph Hague opened his door and called for 'Emma.' When confronted, an intoxicated Hague claimed he wanted bran for eggs and later apologized for being in liquor.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: James Tiley, brother of John Tiley, testified that he was with Joseph Hague the entire day and night of Sunday, November 12th, 1854. He stated they went to Hampton-court, returned late, and he slept with Hague at his mother's house that night.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William Henry Plaister testified that in March, after his daughter told him she was pregnant by Hague, he confronted Hague. He stated Hague denied it, saying 'I know who it is,' but looked confused and walked away.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Harriet Lane testified that one Wednesday evening in November 1854, she was walking with Emma Plaister when Hague appeared. She stated that Emma ran away from her to meet him, and they went into the plantation together.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Henry John Bennett testified that he saw Emma Plaister and the prisoner together on two separate occasions between Michaelmas and Christmas, 1854. He saw them standing and walking together near Mr. Plaister's house in the afternoon.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mary Ann Davis testified that on Sunday, November 19th, 1854, she saw the prisoner with his arm around Emma Plaister's neck, kissing her, under the saw mills near Plaister's house. She was certain of the date because of an arrangement to collect laundry.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: John Clifford, Emma's step-brother, testified that he was at the Plaister house one night in October or November 1854 when Hague came in. He stated Hague asked him to fetch some bran, gave him 2 pence, and he left Hague in the house with Emma Plaister.

He was staying in room No. 46 at the Great Northern Hotel and had locked his bedroom door, leaving the key in the lock.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Thomas Lissaman, a waiter at the Great Northern Hotel, testified that he saw the prisoner Kingston breakfasting in the coffee room on the morning of March 20th, the morning the theft was discovered. Kingston was with another man named Branch.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Charles Althon, a hotel porter, and Benjamin Moran, from the American legation, testified to the close association of Howard and Kingston. They stayed in adjacent rooms at Morley's hotel, associated constantly, left for Paris together, and obtained passports with consecutive numbers.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: George Bransome, hall porter at the Great Northern Hotel, identified Howard as having taken a room at the hotel on April 10th and 11th under the false name 'West'.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mary Ann Johnston, a chambermaid in Manchester, testified that after a separate robbery committed by Kingston, she found a pair of pliers under a mat. Inspector John Mayberry testified that he could turn the key of the robbed room in Manchester from the outside with these pliers.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: John Scott, a detective officer, arrested Howard on April 19th aboard the ship 'Persia', which was under way to New York. Howard had booked his passage under the name 'D. Allen'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Martin Burke, an attendant at the Strand Theatre, testified that on April 4th, he confronted Henry Anderson for selling tickets outside the theatre. An altercation ensued where Anderson struck Burke, and Burke pursued and held him for a constable. Anderson then accused Burke of stealing his purse and 4 shillings.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Samuel Sawyer testified that as a former warder at the House of Correction, he had seen Anderson in prison on 'a great number of times,' possibly seven or eight, under the name of Barney Aaron or Barnett.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Richard Lambert, a warder at the House of Correction for over 25 years, testified he knew Anderson by the name of Barney Aaron and had seen him in prison several times. He stated he was in court during Burke's trial and passed a note to the judge identifying Anderson as a former prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: George Lockyer, another warder, testified he knew Anderson in prison under the name William Barnett. He specifically recalled receiving him into Cold Bath Fields prison on 1st August 1855, for assaulting a man named Bath.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Policeman William Twiss testified he knew the prisoner by the name Henry Barnett and had seen him selling 'flash sovereigns and flash rings in the street on several occasions'. He also recalled a specific incident where Anderson gave the name Henry Barnett after his window was broken.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police sergeant Edwin Townshend produced a charge sheet from January 28th showing Anderson had signed his name as 'Henry Barnett'. On that occasion, Anderson had reported the theft of a box of jewellery and gave his occupation as a 'hawker of jewellery'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Thomas Stone and Judith Clarke, who occupy and work at No. 6, Wellclose Square, both testified that the prisoner did not live or lodge there on May 16th, and they had never known him to live there.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Solicitor John William Allen produced a certified copy of a summary conviction from 1843 for a man named Barnett Aaron. Former policeman Henry Cotton then identified the prisoner, Anderson, as the person who was convicted under that name.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Martin Burke admitted under cross-examination that he had been imprisoned twice before, once for assault and once for breaking a man's leg, for which he was sentenced to twelve months.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Sarah Catharine Aaron, the prisoner's mother, testified that her son lived at No. 6, Grace's Alley, Wellclose Square, and worked in the dentist profession. She denied ever knowing him to go by the name of Moses Aaron.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Moses Aaron testified that he is a separate individual who was imprisoned under the name Moses Aaron. He stated he knew the prisoner, Anderson, and had never known him to use that name.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Frederick Behling, a clerk at a sugar refinery, testified that on May 6th he delivered 20 tittlers (loaves) of sugar belonging to Cooper & Co. in fulfillment of a delivery order signed by the defendant Charles Harrison.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Thomas Cooper, Harrison's employer, testified that the May 6th order was unauthorized and not for sold goods. He showed that Harrison had falsified the corresponding counterfoil in the order book to read as an order for raisins, thereby concealing the theft.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Cooper stated that Roger Durant, a former employee, knew the firm's business practices well. He also testified that Roger had introduced 'Mitchell' as a customer, whom Roger later admitted was his brother-in-law, Ryder Durant.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Peter Mossop, a clerk at a second refinery, testified that on May 22nd he delivered 40 tittlers of Cooper & Co.'s sugar based on another signed order from Harrison. The loaves were marked with the number '85'.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Henry Blatch, the Durants' porter, testified that Ryder Durant instructed him to put blue paper on the tittlers and then said, 'We have cleaned the bottoms off, and they cannot swear to them now.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Blatch also testified that after Harrison was arrested, he asked Ryder Durant if he knew anything was wrong about the sugar. Ryder replied, 'Of course I did.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Detective Henry Webb testified that when questioned, Ryder Durant denied having any dealings for 'tittlers' with Mr. Cooper or Harrison. Ryder claimed his brother was the buyer and he knew nothing of the transactions.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Detective Webb also observed that after the investigation began, the name 'Roger Durant and Co.' over the shop was erased and replaced with 'William Mitchell'.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Thomas Davis gave character evidence, stating that he had known both Durant brothers for years and they had always borne the character of 'honourable and respectable' young men.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Thomas Davis also testified that Ryder Durant told him he had his father-in-law's permission to carry on the business in the name of 'Mitchell'.

public.game_background: Edward Hastings is indicted for wilful and corrupt perjury. He swore in an affidavit for a lawsuit between Price's Patent Candle Company and Bauwens Patent Candle Company that the iron pipes in Bauwens' apparatus were kept ordinarily red hot and were exposed to the direct action of the furnace fire. The prosecution aims to prove this statement was false, while the defense maintains it was true.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Edward either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
