information:
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Robert Wilson, the solicitor for Price's Patent Candle Company who initially took Hastings's statement, testified, 'I think I never met with a witness who has been more consistent in his testimony from beginning to end.' No inducement or reward was offered for his statement.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: John Henry Japp, a former stoker at Bauwens', testified that when he assisted in installing a new coil of pipes, the top of the furnace was removed and there were no Stourbridge tiles between the pipes and the fire. He stated he knelt down inside and put his hand underneath the coil, confirming there was nothing but a space to the fire.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: William Reece, a former stoker, testified that on one occasion he looked in at the furnace door and 'saw the pipes were more than red hot; they were between a white and a red heat.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Thomas George Prigmore, a former engineer at Bauwens', testified that a stoker named Dawson lifted the furnace door to show him the coil of pipes, which were red hot. Dawson allegedly told him that 'they could not work the stuff unless they were so, and it was all right.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Warren Delarue, F.R.S., and Dr. Hofmann, expert chemists, testified that their experiments proved that working with superheated steam (requiring pipes heated above the boiling point of the fat, likely red hot) was 'the most beneficial mode.' Compared to using sub-heated steam, it resulted in a 5% greater yield and a better quality product with less waste.

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Henry Pengilly, a Board of Trade manager, testified that the official log book entry regarding Eastick's death was 'very unsatisfactory' and that the captain's subsequent statement was also unsatisfactory, prompting him to call the police.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: On cross-examination, four key prosecution witnesses from the crew—Murdock, Montague, Stowe, and Way—all admitted to having had disputes with Captain Doyle over wages or character references. Several had summoned the captain before a magistrate.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_tm_01]
Description: Victim Theresa M'Dermott testified that Ann Mulvey accosted her, saying 'You old b——, I shall pay you,' then struck her in the face and bit her thumb through. She stated this was in response to an unfounded accusation that she had called Mulvey's daughter a 'w—'.
[EVIDENCE ev_tm_06]
Description: Theresa M'Dermott insisted she was 'quite sober' at the time of the assault and had not had anything to drink until afterwards.
[EVIDENCE ev_fo_01]
Description: Francis Owen, the house surgeon, confirmed that a portion of Mrs. M'Dermott's left ear was missing and that a recovered piece of ear corresponded to the injury.
[EVIDENCE ev_tb_01]
Description: Police Constable Thomas Blow testified that when the prisoners were given into his custody, the victim Mrs. M'Dermott was in a 'drunken, muddled state,' as was her husband. He stated this was due to drink, not the effect of blows.
[EVIDENCE ev_tb_02]
Description: Constable Thomas Blow testified that the victim 'did not complain of losing her ear that night' and that he 'saw no blood.' The piece of ear was only shown to him the next morning.

public.game_background: Robert Buckland, a former horse keeper for Francis Gurney, was charged with feloniously setting fire to a hay stack on his former employer's property. The prosecution's case rested on evidence of motive from a prior dismissal, a direct threat witnessed by a third party, and footprints matching the defendant's shoes leading from his cottage to the scene. The defense argued that Buckland was the one who raised the alarm and that another employee, discharged on the very night of the fire, presented an alternative suspect.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Robert either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prosecutor, Francis Gurney, testified that the prisoner, Robert Buckland, had been his horse keeper for six to eight months but was discharged about a year prior for taking his employer's hunters out and riding them without permission.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Francis Gurney stated that it was the prisoner, Robert Buckland, who gave the alarm about the fire. Buckland appeared at Gurney's house, 'rather excited, and out of breath, as if he had been running,' shouting that the ricks were on fire.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The morning after the fire, Mr. Gurney saw footmarks which he traced from the rick yard, through hurdles into a meadow, and over a stile to within ten feet of Buckland's cottage. He noted the tracks went both there and back and were not on the public path.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Policeman Cooper testified that the prisoner claimed to have first seen the fire from a boiler at the back of his cottage. Cooper stated that the prosecutor's house was much closer to the stacks, and the 'better course would have been to go to the prosecutor's and announce the fact, instead of going to his cottage first.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William Price testified that he met the prisoner coming from his house shouting 'Fire!', but from that location, Price was 'forced to go to the middle of my master's yard before I saw it'. This implies the flame was not visible from where Buckland raised the alarm.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Upon arrest, the prisoner stated, 'That is all one gets for giving the alarm of fire; and the next time I see a rick on fire I will go home and go to bed; they may put me on my trial for it, but I am innocent.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: During cross-examination, Francis Gurney admitted, 'I discharged a man from my service on this very night; we had a trifling dispute about wages.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prosecutor, Francis Gurney, acknowledged that a public path crosses the meadow near his property, leading from West End hamlet to Northolt village and passing within eighty yards of his rick yard.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Till, a printer, testified that on October 30th, the prisoner John Smith came to his premises and ordered 10,000 labels for baking powder and 10,000 for egg powder. Smith provided patterns and asked for the labels to be printed as near as possible to the originals.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: George Borwick, the proprietor, testified that the powder sold by the prisoner was of 'very inferior quality' and would not work. He also confirmed that he gave no authority to anyone to print the labels.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The defense argued that a fatal variance existed between the genuine and forged baking powder labels. The genuine labels contained the warning, 'the public are requested to see that each packet is signed George Borwick, without which none are genuine,' which was omitted from the prisoner's labels.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The defense counsel, Mr. Sleigh, argued that this was not a forgery at common law, comparing it to Reg. v. Closs where writing a painter's name on a picture was not held to be forgery. He contended the name 'Borwick' was a mere arbitrary mark, not a legal document.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The Jury found that the prisoner issued the printed papers with intent to defraud. However, they recommended him to mercy on the ground that they believed he was not aware he was criminally responsible for his actions.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Younghusband stated that while it was not the prisoner's duty to be in the office at night, he came in every morning to light the fires. The envelopes were kept in a desk in that office, which was generally locked but might have been left open by accident.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: On cross-examination, Mr. Younghusband confirmed he has forty men in his employ. He also stated that when pieces of brass are taken off old harness, they are put into his office.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The defense questioned the age of the harness fittings. Mr. Younghusband admitted one piece was very old, but pointed out another was 'very new' and that when they sell old harness, they always remove the brass on their own premises.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mr. Younghusband testified that the prisoner was suspended on Saturday, January 30th, 'in consequence of a quantity of property being stolen.' The stolen items were found at the prisoner's residence on the following Monday night, February 1st.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Under cross-examination, PC Bailey stated that when he searched the room, a woman who 'represented herself as his wife' and a child were also present.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Thomas Jary, the landlord, also testified that he had known the prisoner for seven or eight years and 'always believed him to be an honest, respectable man.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: After the guilty verdict, Mr. Younghusband stated that he had been robbed to the extent of £70 during January alone, and that the prisoner was the only watchman on duty at night.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Susan Dean testified that the defendant, Mrs. Laigh, beat her almost daily from the second week of her service. She claimed the beatings were administered with various implements including a poker, a chopper, fire-tongs, and brushes, often on her head.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Louis Ash Israel, son of the prosecutor, testified that he locked up the house on March 8th around 11 p.m. and was awakened by police at 3:10 a.m. to find the street door and tills broken open.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Louis Ash Israel testified to missing several items, including a purse containing £12 10s., a gold watch and chain from his room, and a great coat. His father, Henry Ash Israel, missed a purse containing £5 and a 20-dollar piece.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Louis Ash Israel stated that the iron safe was opened with a key that had been taken from his pocket while he slept. He had left his bedroom door open.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Louis Ash Israel confirmed that the prisoner, Huckle, worked for his father on the premises 'some years ago'.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: During cross-examination, Thomas Walker admitted he had been out of employment for five months after being discharged by Mr. Israel. He stated, 'we had a bit of a bother, and I said that I would leave'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Henry Ash Israel stated that they have had a dog for about three years and that the prisoner Huckle has not been employed in the house within that time, but 'has been about the premises'.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The police officer, William Dudley, testified that he found Mr. Israel's door open between five and ten minutes past 3 o'clock in the morning and aroused the house.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Northway, a chemist, testified that the prisoner, George Burchett, came to his shop on August 5th and asked him to purchase a pawnbroker's ticket for 12 shillings.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Northway testified that he later found out the pawnbroker's ticket was forged.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Samuel Readfern, a partner at the pawnbroker's, testified that the writing on the ticket was a forgery and not written by anyone in their employ.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Readfern stated that the ticket was white, but all of their tickets for items pawned for over 10 shillings are yellow.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Readfern also testified that his firm had no paper in pledge during August of the previous year.

Description: The arresting officer, James Threadgold, stated that when taken into custody, the prisoner refused to provide his name and address.

[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: After the guilty verdict, evidence of a prior conviction was introduced. A certificate was read stating George Burchett was convicted in September 1856 of 'forging a request for the delivery of goods.'

It was also alleged that her husband, Frederick Hodges, manufactured the lockets and sent her to pledge them.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On March 9th, Emily Hodges offered a locket in pledge to James Hall, a pawnbroker's assistant, asking for 4s. When asked if it was gold, she replied, 'Yes, the best gold,' and claimed it was a gift from an old sweetheart. Relying on this representation and her respectable appearance, Hall lent her the 4s.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Archibald Douglas, a working jeweller, testified as an expert. After testing the lockets, he concluded they contained a miniscule amount of low-carat gold (worth about 5d-6d each) and were 'made for the purpose of deception'.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Frederick Hodges, the male defendant, stated to a police inspector, 'I am the manufacturer of these lockets; I prepared them, and I sent my wife to pledge them.' He repeated this admission in court, but denied any fraudulent intention, claiming it was 'not an offence in law'.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: James Hall admitted under cross-examination that he did not test the lockets with aqua fortis when the money was lent. He stated, 'I trusted entirely to her statement; the statement about its being gold, coupled with the respectability of her appearance, induced me to lend her the money.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: James Hall testified that Emily Hodges did not use the same name on all occasions, using 'Harnett once, Hardnet once, and Horton once'. He still believed it was the same person because he 'knew the article'.

Description: Charles Sadler, the brother of the first wife, testified that he was present on October 30th, 1842, when the prisoner married his sister, Mary Ann Sadler, at the parish church of Upper Chelsea. He stated they lived together for about four years and that he had no doubt the prisoner was the man.

[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Anna Maria Froggatt testified that she married the prisoner at Gretna Green on November 15th, 1847. She stated he used the alias 'Marco Emile de St. Hiliare', declared himself a single man, and assured her the marriage was legal. They lived together for four years and had a child.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Margaret Murray testified that she married the prisoner in Sligo, Ireland, on March 5th, 1853. She stated he represented himself as a single man and was using the name Dr. Tucker, though he married her under the name Borromeo.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner denied that he was the person who married Miss Sadler in 1842. He claimed that he was in Paris at the time, but stated he was not allowed time to prove it.
[EVIDENCE ev12]

Description: The prisoner asserted that the present case, as well as a previous prosecution, was the result of a conspiracy against him by members of an Italian confederation whose enmity he had aroused.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mary Smith, a boarding-house keeper, testified that she observed 'improper familiarities' between the married prisoner and Miss Bankes. Due to this, she asked Miss Bankes to leave her establishment, after which the prisoner also decided to leave, abandoning his wife.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mary Smith also testified that Miss Bankes was 'very delicate,' suffered from bile, nausea, and was sick on several occasions at her house. She noted that Miss Bankes could not ride in an omnibus or carriage without being sick.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Ann Robertson, a lodging-house keeper, testified that when the deceased and prisoner first came to her house on Feb 4th, the deceased 'appeared to be quite well' and was in the habit of going out for walks of two or three hours.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The prisoner administered all nourishment (e.g., beef-tea, arrow-root) to Miss Bankes. The landlady, Ann Robertson, would prepare the food and leave it in the parlour, from where the prisoner would take it to the deceased. The prisoner kept the key to the cupboard where food items were stored.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When a witness wondered why the sickness was not stopping, the prisoner explained that Miss Bankes 'was a very bilious person, and there was some more bile to come.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mrs. Wheatley, the second landlady, offered to sit up with the patient at night to give the prisoner some rest. He refused, stating he could not afford a nurse and that 'no one could do so well as himself for her.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner directed his landlady to keep one of the deceased's evacuations for Dr. Todd to examine. He also personally gave a sample of an evacuation to Mr. Bird for examination.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner wrote to the deceased's sister, Louisa Bankes, repeatedly discouraging her from visiting, claiming the excitement would be detrimental to the patient's health and that doctors had forbidden visitors.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: When Louisa Bankes suggested that their relative, Mr. Lane, a surgeon, should examine the deceased, both the prisoner and the deceased declined the offer.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: During a visit from the sister, the prisoner went outside the bedroom to mix a saline draught, returned, and gave it to the deceased. She was sick immediately after taking it.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Dr. Julius, the first attending physician, testified that after observing the case from April 3rd, he formed the opinion that 'there was something being administered which had a tendency to keep up the irritation existing in the stomach and bowels.'
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Dr. Julius testified that the prisoner told him Dr. Todd's prescription (pills of sulphate of copper and opium) had caused 'intense burning... constant vomiting, and fifteen bloody motions.' Dr. Julius stated unequivocally that the medicine could not have produced those effects.
[EVIDENCE ev20]
Description: Dr. Alfred Taylor, a leading toxicologist, analyzed several samples. He found no poison in one evacuation (No. 1), but did find a small quantity of arsenic in another (No. 2). In the post-mortem analysis of the body, he found no arsenic but did find a small quantity of antimony in the intestines, one kidney, and traces in the blood.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Hannah Edwards testified that water was thrown from the prisoner's second-floor window, wetting one of her children who was playing in the court before the school.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Edward Langford, a fishmonger, testified that he saw Mrs. Edwards struck by the prisoner. He later assisted her into his shop and 'drew the dagger from her side,' corroborating that she was stabbed.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Sarah Loom testified that she saw the prisoner take out the life-preserver, strike Mrs. Edwards, and that the blade was out when he struck Mrs. Edwards a second time.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Dr. Thomas Langdon, a house-surgeon, confirmed that Hannah Edwards had several bruises and a punctured wound on her chest, about three-quarters of an inch deep, in a dangerous location near the heart. He stated such a blade as the one produced would cause such a wound.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: During cross-examination, Hannah Edwards admitted that she had been previously taken before a Magistrate on a charge of assaulting the prisoner and was bound over to keep the peace.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Policeman James Noble testified that he found a 'large crowd' of 90 to 100 people and 'a very serious riot going on.' The prisoner's clothes were torn, and he had evidently been 'badly used by somebody.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Adolphus Hoffman, a defense witness, testified that he had observed children creating a 'constant noise and annoyance' in front of the prisoner's school-house, including scratching off newly applied paint.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Defense witness Adolphus Hoffman stated that Mrs. Evans and Mrs. Edwards began 'threatening him likewise with her fist' and that Mrs. Edwards 'flew at him in the face,' and he only struck her to defend himself.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: James Leach, a bookbinder living nearby, testified that he went out after hearing a noise and saw 'fifty or sixty people there... very noisy and fighting with the prisoner,' and that 'they all got on him.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Edward John Watchurst, a bootmaker, testified that he saw the prisoner in a 'fearful state,' being 'unmercifully' beaten by several men. He stated his impression was that 'if they could have got him down they would have killed him.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Several witnesses were called who deposed to the prisoner's good character. Edward John Watchurst described him as a 'well-ordered, well-conducted man' who 'bears a respectable character in the neighbourhood.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: In late August, the prisoners visited the jeweller's shop, made a small exchange, and claimed they would return to buy wedding presents for a brother arriving from Australia. This established a pretext for a future, larger visit.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mary Evans, the prisoners' landlady in Oxford, testified that they lodged with her for three weeks, paid their rent, and behaved like 'gentle people'. She also stated they spoke 'very poor English'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The landlady, Mrs. Evans, testified that she saw a chain, or one like it, in a little box on the table in the prisoners' room some days before they left Oxford.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mrs. Evans testified that when Mr. Wells came to her house inquiring about the prisoners, he said 'he could not say that they had robbed him, or that they had not', but was concerned about losing money on items he had to specially order for them.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Louis Solomon, a jeweller with a shop near Mr. Wells, testified that the prisoners had visited him often, perhaps once or twice a week, and that they had gold chains they offered to sell.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Under cross-examination, Mr. Wells admitted that he had not taken a full inventory of his stock for the last two or three years.

Description: Policeman James Thorndike testified that he saw Edwards loitering near the passage, and then saw Smith emerge and join Edwards, whereupon they both crossed the road in a 'hurried manner'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On Saturday, October 22nd, thirty-seven casks of bristles were shipped onto the barge 'Barber' at St. Katharine's Docks. The barge was securely covered with tarpaulins, which were nailed down, and was left safe at Galley-quay on Monday evening, Oct 24th.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: John Edwards, who had known Taylor for twenty years, testified that he saw Taylor driving a two-wheeled cart containing two bristle-like casks near Brewer's-quay at approximately 8:30 AM on October 25th, the morning of the theft.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: James Hunt testified that on the morning of the theft, he saw a four-wheeled van near the barge and two men with it. He stated that 'to the best of my belief one was Taylor'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Upon being taken into custody for receiving stolen bristles, Nicks stated, 'It is a bad job; I have expected it.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Thomas Best, Taylor's employer, testified that during the two years Taylor had been with the firm, he had 'borne a good character' and was trusted with a good deal of property.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: A great many witnesses deposed to Nicks's good character. It was also noted that his warehouse was a large one where an 'extensive business is carried on,' a place where one 'should not have expected to find stolen goods.'

Description: Mr. Robertshaw further testified that the prisoner had previously communicated with him and obtained goods using other names, such as Mr. Wilmot and Mr. Jones.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Under cross-examination, Bikher admitted that an action of ejectment was brought against him by Beeston, that a jury gave a verdict against him, and that his attorney had informed him the Sheriff had given the prisoner possession of the house.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Bikher admitted under cross-examination that he, along with Hempson, was tried at the last Middlesex Sessions for 'riot and assault' in relation to this incident and was found guilty. He stated he was currently under recognisance to come up for judgment.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Bikher admitted his friends were at a nearby pub just before the incident and came to his aid when he shouted 'Murder'. He also admitted he resisted being taken out of the house.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Bikher was forced to admit that the magistrate at the police-court hearing did not commit the prisoner or hold him to bail, and that the magistrate had stated that he was satisfied the prisoner was 'perfectly justified'.

The prosecution alleges they fabricated a series of false stories about their identities, relationships, and financial distress to induce Mr. Stohwasser to provide them with substantial sums of money and support.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Joseph Stohwasser, chairman of the German Benevolent Society, visited Eliza De Welzenstein after a referral from the embassy. She claimed her husband, an Austrian officer, had gone to America, leaving her and their child destitute. Believing her story, Stohwasser gave her money personally and arranged a grant from his society.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: A man named Corn informed Stohwasser that the woman's story was a fabrication, that 'Sternfeldt' was actually her husband, and that the child she presented was borrowed.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Augusta Bordfeldt, the mother of the child used in the deception, testified that Madeline Jourdan instructed her to say the child was not hers 'for a joke' in front of 'the old gentleman' (Stohwasser). She confirmed her child was coached to call Eliza 'mamma'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Charles Kieber testified that Edward De Welzenstein told him he had letters in his possession that would destroy Stohwasser's reputation and that 'if he was a wise man, he would not stick at the paltry amount which they had asked him for finally,' which was £50 or £60.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Multiple handwriting experts testified. Witnesses for the prosecution, including a bank cashier, stated the incriminating letters were not in Stohwasser's hand. Witnesses for the defense, including a church clerk, stated the writing resembled his.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Lady Stanley of Alderley testified that she had known Madeline Jourdan (as Poninska) since 1852 as an 'honourable and respectable woman.' She also stated that she introduced Eliza De Welzenstein to Jourdan as an object of charity in 1857.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Edward Bordfeldt, the child's father, testified that Eliza De Welzenstein asked him to deny that the child was his if Stohwasser asked. She stated that Stohwasser 'took interest in her, and if ever he found out that stories had been told him, that he would draw back his hands from them.'

information:

[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Jacob French, a jeweller, testified that in April, Pearce came to him under the name 'Roberts' and provided loose diamonds and rubies, valued around £60, to be mounted into rings. Pearce gave his address as 4, Albion-place, Stoke Newington, where he was later arrested.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: City-policeman Theodore Foulger testified that he was watching the prosecutor's wharf in Upper Thames-street on the morning of June 14th.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Officer William Smith, also on surveillance, saw the prisoner Martin arrive at the mews with two empty sacks on his arm and go towards the cart.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: In the stable, Officer Foulger found an empty sack with the name 'Green and Sedgwick, Rutland Wharf' printed on it.

Description: When questioned, Avery admitted his destination was Mr. Henderson's in the Haymarket, saying, 'Well, it is no use denying it.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]

[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: A statement was made to the court that Martin had received a good character.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Policeman Greathurst testified that on the morning of June 19th, he observed the prisoner Lamb throwing five empty sacks from a coke-van onto the top of the coal-van, which was loaded with twenty sacks of coal. He stated this aroused his suspicion and caused him to follow the van.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Mr. Millaret testified that the prisoner Jones solicited the order by claiming to be from the 'Great Northern Coal Company' and that the agent was 'Mr. Clarke.' Millaret stated he believed this and gave the order based on the company's reputation.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: David Wells was not arrested with the other prisoners on the day of the incident. He was summoned to appear before the Magistrate the next day to answer the charge, at which time he claimed the horses and vans.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: At the conclusion of the evidence, it was noted that 'The prisoners received good characters.' This implies that witnesses testified to their general honesty and good reputation prior to this incident.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Police-sergeant Sutherland testified that on the morning of August 30th, he found a hole bored through the shutters and two iron plates of Mr. Carpenter's shop. He also found sawdust on the pavement directly below the hole, and a pane of the plate-glass window was broken.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Policeman Franklin, watching from a doorway opposite, saw all three defendants arrive. He witnessed Homewood and two others at the shutters and heard glass fall, while stating Timothy and Kelly were positioned nearby as lookouts.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Policeman Walker, arriving from the back of the premises, saw Homewood about to strike Franklin with a life-preserver. Walker intervened, striking Homewood with his staff and causing the weapon to be dropped.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Policeman Walker found two caps on the ground after the scuffle. He testified that he had repeatedly seen the prisoner Kelly wearing a cap like one of those found within the last six months.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: A screw auger, identified by police as a type used by burglars, was found by a citizen, George Newcomb, hidden in a hole under the pavement about 50 yards from Mr. Carpenter's shop on the morning of the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Sarah Ann Ryan testified that the prisoner Kelly was in her room from about 11:45 PM until 2:30 AM for a wake for her deceased child. She stated that when police arrived at 3 AM, they found Kelly in his bed.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Timothy's mother, Susan Short, testified that her son went to bed at 9:45 PM and she was awake in a room below until 2 AM, and would have heard him if he left. Another lodger, Mary Ann Hartman, testified she saw him in bed at 11:15 PM.

The prosecution alleged that Wilson, along with an unidentified accomplice, concocted a story about a horse sale to trick Woodford into giving them money, after which they fled.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Woodford testified that William Wilson approached him on the street, claiming a prior acquaintance through his brother's pub. Wilson initiated a conversation, establishing a pretext for further interaction.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Wilson showed Woodford a pocket-book containing what appeared to be at least £300 in notes. He offered to give the money to the groom, who refused, suggesting that if Woodford paid the money, the transaction would not seem improper to his master.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The victim, John Woodford, positively identified William Wilson in a line-up of five or six men at the Worship-street police court. He expressed complete certainty that Wilson was the man who had defrauded him.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: James Frost, an ostler at Yeoman's livery stable where the horse was allegedly located, testified that there was no horse belonging to a Quaker on their premises at that time.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prosecutor, James Haynes Haynes, testified that on July 11, 1856, after he remonstrated with his wife, she became violent. He stated she went into a dark wash-house, returned with something concealed in her dress, and then threw a burning fluid from her right hand into his face, causing blindness and agony.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The prosecutor testified that they never kept sulphuric acid or other burning materials in the house, as they had no saucepans or coppers to clean that would require it, implying the acid was brought in for the attack.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The prosecutor stated that while he was recovering on the sofa, the prisoner threatened him, saying, 'I have put one eye out, and now I will put out the other.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The prosecutor testified that his wife had been guilty of 'innumerable acts of violence' towards him, including taking a knife to him and throwing a large glass stand at his head.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Constable Stephen Colebrook testified that upon her arrest, the prisoner appeared in 'great grief and agitation.' She claimed that her husband had 'aggravated her because he had taken up a carving knife to her.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Surgeon Joseph Bailey testified that it was the prisoner who came to fetch him after the incident. He also confirmed she had no shoes on, suggesting haste.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: In cross-examination, the prosecutor admitted that he struck his wife on one occasion in 1852, a blow which 'blacked her eye.' He justified it by saying his cousin told him she 'richly deserved it.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prosecutor admitted that at the time he renewed the prosecution in 1860, he was living with another woman who was passing as his wife, with whom he had 'two or three children.' He turned the prisoner out into the street upon her return.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prosecutor did not immediately press the felony charge in 1856. He stated this was 'in consideration of the child' and he only had her bound over to keep the peace a month after the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prosecutor testified that lodgers, Mr. and Mrs. Moore, lived in the house and 'had a charwoman about once a week.' The surgeon noted the acid appeared as if 'used for cleaning grates or metal utensils.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On December 15th, the prisoner, Schmidt Schotte, entered George Fitch's shop to look at signet rings, stating he wanted three of a sort. Mr. Fitch showed him a tray of rings, which the prisoner said did not suit him.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Fitch acknowledged a lady was also in the shop, a respectable person he knew. He claimed she was not as near to the rings as the prisoner, though he conceded she could have reached them.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The missing ring was never found. Fitch speculated that the prisoner swallowed it or threw it away during the struggle.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police Constable Richard Loughboro searched the prisoner and found on him two brass imitation wedding-rings, a brass pin, and a brass chain, but no money.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Thomas Emberson, another jeweller, testified that the prisoner had been in his shop on November 17th. He stated the prisoner gave a false name and address ('Brown, 21, Chiswell-street').

public.game_background: James Thomas, alias Patrick Bowling, was indicted for burglariously breaking and entering the dwelling-house of the Baroness Talbot de Malahide on November 30th, 1860, and stealing a diamond necklace and other valuable articles, with a total value of £2,000.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. James either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Sarah Ives, the housemaid, testified that at 7:30 PM on November 30th, she discovered Lady Talbot's bedroom in disarray, with wardrobes open and the jewel-case missing. Upstairs in the attic, she found a broken jewel-case, diamond trays, and a 'jemmy' (a burglar's tool). A spare room window had a pane of glass broken near the lock, indicating the point of entry.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Barnes, a foreman working at the adjacent house (No. 7), found a hat in the gutter on December 3rd. He gave it to the police. The hat was traced back to the prisoner via a repair ticket with his name and address ('Mr. Thomas, 40, Great Cambridge-street') that had been mistakenly left inside the lining by the hatter.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William Hunt, the butler, identified the prisoner as a man who had come to the house on the morning of the robbery or the day before with a falsely directed letter, ostensibly making an inquiry. Hunt spoke with him for three minutes and later identified him in a room containing a hundred people.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Policeman Alexander Huddy testified that he saw the prisoner at 5 PM on the evening of the robbery, standing about 150 yards from the house with another man. This was approximately two hours before the burglary was discovered.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Upon being arrested on December 26th, the prisoner made several incriminating statements. To Sergeant Potter, he said, 'Very well, Mr. Potter, don't expose me here.' During a search of his house, he said, 'I know what you are looking for now, you are looking for tools, but I have done away with them years ago.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: When asked for his profession at the police station, the prisoner stated he was a 'lapidary' (a person who cuts and polishes precious stones).
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: William Coombes, a police officer, stated after the guilty verdict that the prisoner had been in custody thirteen times previously. The prisoner also pleaded guilty to a prior conviction for housebreaking in 1858, for which he received four years' penal servitude.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On August 12th, Reverend Thomas Gregory's black leather bag was taken from the luggage labeling area at the London-bridge terminus. An almost empty bag of a similar appearance was left in its place.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Reverend Gregory and his servant, Charlotte Greasby, positively identified a coat, trousers, boots, slippers, and other personal items found in the recovered bag as belonging to Mr. Gregory and having been packed for his trip.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Harriet Young, whose mother was the prisoner's landlady, testified that the prisoner came home on the evening of August 12th with a heavy bag that looked like the one produced in court.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Officer William Harris testified that on August 23rd he took the prisoner into custody at the Victoria station and confiscated from him the bag produced in court.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: A key provided by Mr. Gregory was found to open the bag that was taken from the prisoner by the police.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The prisoner stated in his defense that the bag claimed by the prosecutor was his own property, and that he had owned it for eleven years.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner stated that his poor health and physical infirmity would have rendered him incapable of carrying away Mr. Gregory's heavy, full bag.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Reverend Gregory could not positively swear that the bag was his. He stated, 'it strikes me it is like my bag, more I cannot say.' He also mentioned his bag had 'one canvas division in the centre'.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Police-constable John Parish testified that when he checked the prisoner's information regarding his lodging, it was 'perfectly correct.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas William Murton, a clerk from the Court of Bankruptcy, produced the official proceedings showing John Corss Smith was adjudged a bankrupt on August 13th, 1861. The summons required him to surrender on September 2nd or September 26th.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Richard Willis, the petitioning creditor, received a letter from Smith on August 12th, stating: 'I must request you to make me bankrupt and enforce your deed at once... I have been most shamefully robbed, but shall be ready to explain all when wanted.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Richard Willis estimated that between £1,000 and £2,000 worth of property disappeared from Smith's shops between Saturday and Monday. Employee John Tozen confirmed the safe at one shop was emptied, estimating the loss there at £400-£500.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Employees testified that Smith was 'very much pressed' financially, had been served with many writs, and that there had been an execution in the house the week before he left. One employee stated Smith was 'afraid of being arrested, it quite frightened him out of the shop.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The defense cross-examination and witness testimony suggested Smith's financial troubles were largely caused by his dealings with a Mr. Jenks. Employee Tozen stated, 'Mr. Jenks was the main cause of it,' and Smith himself told the arresting officer he had been 'fearfully robbed' by Jenks.

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The prisoner's employer, John Crowther, testified that he had instructed Gibbs to ask the deceased to leave the kitchen, as he did not want drinking on the premises.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Witness Frances Crisp testified that she saw the prisoner and the deceased scuffling together before the blow was struck. She saw Gibbs catch hold of Pratt's shoulder during the scuffle.

[EVIDENCE ev9]

[EVIDENCE ev10]

[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Both William Francis and the prisoner's employer, John Crowther, testified that Gibbs was a 'quiet, inoffensive man.'

public.game_background: Robert Francis Fairlie was indicted for unlawfully taking a false oath before a surrogate to obtain a marriage license. He swore he had the consent of George England, the father of the bride, Eliza Ann England, a minor, when in fact he allegedly did not.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Robert either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Watts, a record-keeper, produced the affidavit signed by Robert Francis Fairlie, dated 4th January, 1862. Watts stated that he made out the marriage license based on this sworn affidavit.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John McMahon, an acquaintance, testified that the defendant was a person of high position and respectability. McMahon frequently saw Mr. Fairlie showing attention to Miss England in the presence of her parents, which she appeared to accept willingly.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: George England, the father, testified unequivocally: 'I never gave my consent to the marriage of my daughter... most positively to the contrary.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Under cross-examination, Mr. England admitted that he himself had eloped with his current wife while his previous wife was still alive.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The defense argued that the oath was on an immaterial fact. As Eliza Ann England was an illegitimate child, her natural father, George England, had no legal power to consent to her marriage under the Act, making the statement about his consent legally irrelevant.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: A letter from the defendant, written from Cadiz after the marriage, was produced. In it, he requested pardon for the marriage, stating it was only done because 'there appeared no hope of obtaining the prosecutor's consent.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The Recorder pointed out a potential defect in the indictment, which averred that George England was the 'lawful and natural father,' whereas the evidence suggested he was only the natural father.

information:

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: On Tuesday, September 16th, around 2 a.m., the prisoner came to the pub again and purchased gin. She paid with a five-shilling piece.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Richard Reed, a seaman, testified he met the prisoner after 2 a.m. on the Tuesday morning and she asked him to have a drink.

Description: Policeman Matthew Bullock stated he received the prisoner in custody from Mr. Ellis along with the counterfeit crown and half-crown.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William Webster, an inspector of coin from the Mint, testified that both the half-crown and the five-shilling piece were counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In her defense, the prisoner stated, 'I know nothing about the half-crown.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Policeman Matthew Bullock testified that the prisoner gave her correct address upon being taken into custody.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Testimony from John Cooke, a short-hand writer, who confirms that the defendant, William Bailey Bray, was sworn in during the trial of 'Bray v. Waterlow' and testified that he did not sign the agreement in question, had no interest in the Haford mines, and was a member of the Institution of Civil Engineers.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: An agreement dated 30th April, 1858, is produced in court. It states that John Arthur, William Bray, and James Crane are mutually interested in the profits from the Haford mines and bears the signature 'William Bray' and initials 'W. B.' against an alteration.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Charles Manby, honorary secretary of the Institution of Civil Engineers from 1839 to 1860, testifies that the prisoner is not a member and is not the 'William Bailey Bray' who is on their register.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Charles Rees, a solicitor's clerk, testifies that he found a duplicate of the signed agreement (ev2) and Bray's incriminating letter (ev5) in a box belonging to the deceased co-signatory, John Arthur.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The prisoner received a good character during the trial. The jury also strongly recommended him to mercy on account of his good character.

public.game_background: John Gleeson was indicted for robbery on Frederick Allen, stealing part of a watch chain from his person on the morning of February 4th, 1863. The prosecution presented eyewitness testimony from the victim and police, while the defense presented an alibi supported by four witnesses.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Victim Frederick Allen testified that the prisoner, John Gleeson, grasped him by the throat while another man struck him and broke his guard chain. Allen stated he was sure of the identification because a lamp was nearby, allowing him to see the prisoner's face.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Policeman Richard Fenn testified that on the night of the robbery, he saw the prisoner running towards him and shouting 'Stop thief'. Finding this suspicious as no one was ahead of the prisoner, Fenn detained him for 20 minutes before letting him go, stating he took a 'good look' at him.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Policeman William Ackrill testified that when he went to arrest the prisoner, he found him hiding up a chimney. Upon being told the charge, the prisoner allegedly said, 'I know I shall have ten years for what I am going for,' and then violently attacked the officer.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Defense witness Thomas Robinson provided an alibi, stating the prisoner was with him on the night of the robbery. He claims they traveled from Gleeson's parents' house to his own home in Notting-hill, where Gleeson stayed until February 5th.

information:

[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: A certificate of the prisoner's bankruptcy and a deed of composition with his creditors were entered as evidence. The deed showed he paid 1 shilling in the pound.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: John Fergusson, a neighbour at Upper Eaton-street, testified that the premises had not been a monetary advance office before Lewis took the office in June 1862. He stated the board claiming establishment in 1849 was put up only after Lewis arrived.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Simeon Jones, the landlord of 1, St. James place, Pall Mall, testified that the prisoner never resided at his house and had no authority to have letters addressed there for his 'M. P.' advertisement.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Benjamin Hazell applied for a loan of £100 in December and paid a fee of two guineas. The loan was not granted, and only one guinea was to be returned.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Samuel Cooper, an attorney, testified that he had known the prisoner as a loan-office keeper since 1852 in Fetter-lane and Blackfriars-road, had many transactions with him, and considered him to be 'perfectly honest'.

[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Wycherley's landlord, William Frost, testified that the prisoner asked him to change a £5 note. A bank clerk later identified this note (No. 34,513) as one of those stolen from Mrs. Levy.

[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Dr. Harry Simpson testified that death was caused by suffocation from blood in the lung, resulting from external violence that caused a large bruise under the right collar-bone.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: George James, the errand-boy living in the house, testified that the deceased was a habitual and heavy drinker, stating, 'she was drunk on the Friday night... and on the Saturday, Sunday, and Monday morning... I have frequently seen her drunk.'

public.game_background: Jesse Woodgate, a pawnbroker's manager, was indicted for feloniously receiving 9 oz. of stolen gold, valued at approximately 40 l. The prosecution argued he bought the gold under suspicious circumstances and knew it was stolen, citing an altered date in the purchase ledger. The defense contended Woodgate acted openly, sought to have the gold assayed, had a non-criminal reason for the ledger alteration, and that the true value of the gold was not obvious without an assay.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Police Sergeant Thomas Evans testified that when questioned, the prisoner stated he bought the gold after business hours from a working man he had never seen before and whose name he did not know.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: George Buckland, a gold-flatter, testified that he recognized the gold brought in by the prisoner on June 12th for an assay, as he had flatted it for the owner, Mr. Summers, the previous day.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The gold's market value was established as approximately 38 l., while the prisoner had purchased it for 28 l. The prosecution's witnesses, being gold-beaters, argued that any experienced dealer should have known its approximate value.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: William Summers testified that on the evening of June 11th, he was carrying the gold when a companion, McDougal, asked to carry it and then disappeared. McDougal has not been seen since.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: John Savage, the prisoner's employer, testified that he believed the prisoner to be a 'highly respectable young man' and that the prisoner could not have personally profited from the transaction, as any gains went to the business.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Alfred Spencer testified that on Friday, the prisoner showed the gold to another person in the trade, named Davis, who then recommended he take it to Mr. Buckland to be melted.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: James Johnson, a gold-refiner, testified that a person would not be able to tell the actual value of gold between 63s. and 80s. an ounce by tests, and would need an assay. Thomas Redfern, another jeweller, corroborated this.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edward Merry, a clerk from the Bankruptcy Court, testified that Behrens was adjudicated bankrupt on May 27th, 1863, with debts of about £740 and no declared assets. Behrens failed to surrender to the court.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: John Harrison, cashier for the petitioning creditors, stated that on April 16th, Behrens' account was overdue. Behrens promised to settle it but failed to do so. When Harrison visited on May 6th, he found Behrens in bed claiming to be ill, which Harrison believed was 'assumed'.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: In cross-examination, John Harrison admitted that Behrens had told him the goods were for the 'German trade' and that he was going to fairs in Germany. Behrens told him on April 16th he had just returned from Germany and would go again soon.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Thomas Toovey testified that he purchased Behrens’ ironmongery business on May 5th, finalizing the deal with Mrs. Behrens. Behrens had told him he could make more money by travelling on the Continent. Toovey did not receive any of the business's account books.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Shipping agent William Soundy testified that on April 27th, Behrens came to him pretending to be an employee of 'Mr. Behrens'. He arranged to ship numerous packages to Cologne, claiming his master was ill and a 'great blackguard' who owed him money.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Charles Burton, an auctioneer, testified that Behrens had a history of depositing goods with him for cash advances and then selling them at auction. Between January and May 1863, goods to the value of £1112 were sold this way. This practice was described as 'very common in the trade'.

The defense, led by Mr. Best, argued that the relationship between Farnham and Hannam was more akin to a partnership, which would negate the charge of embezzlement.

[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Charles William Blythe testified that Hannam was present when Blythe let a house to Farnham. Blythe stated Hannam later told him to 'look to him for the rent, that he wished to assist Farnham.'

public.game_background: William Peet Harris, a 55-year-old tailor, was indicted for stealing a 50l. bank note from his lodger, John William Abrahams, within his own dwelling-house. The prosecution argued that Harris, having been refused a loan by Abrahams, forced open a locked wardrobe, stole the note, and changed it at the Bank of England, leaving his handwriting on the stolen note and a replacement 5l. note. The defense countered by highlighting the victim's carelessness, the existence of other potential suspects, and a failure by the bank clerk to identify Harris as the culprit.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John William Abrahams testified that he kept a 50l. note in a pocket-book inside a wardrobe fitted with a Chubb's lock. On October 6th, he discovered the note was missing, replaced by a 5l. note. He stated the wardrobe's internal bolts showed signs of being forced with an instrument, bypassing the lock.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Abrahams testified that in March, about six months before the theft, the prisoner had asked to borrow 50l. to invest in his tailoring business. Abrahams refused the loan, although he had lent the prisoner smaller sums on previous occasions.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: A clerk from the Bank of England, Richard Adye Bailey, produced the stolen 50l. note and testified that it had been changed at the bank on Saturday, September 26th, for forty-five sovereigns and a 5l. note.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner told Detective Palmer he paid a 36l. 10s. bill with a 5l. note, a cheque, and gold. However, Charles Ablett, the cashier for the firm, testified that his record book for notes received contained no entry of such a payment on that day.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The court transcript concludes by stating that 'The prisoner received an excellent character.'

She had held this money for him, returning it in 1859, and last saw the full amount in his possession in March 1862, shortly before he went to live with the prisoner, Maria Read.

Description: Bank of England clerks testified that specific notes issued to Mr. Hankin in 1858 were changed in 1863. A 200l. note was changed with the endorsement 'Maria Reed', two 100l. notes were endorsed 'Maria Read' and 'William Read', and several 10l. notes were endorsed 'H. Read'.

[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Donald Cameron, a local draper who changed notes for Mrs. Read, testified that he had known her for eight years and would not have changed them if he had not known her to be a respectable woman.

[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Elizabeth Harris of the Boot and Slipper beer-shop testified that in late April, the prisoner gave her a shilling for beer which she discovered was bad.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: During cross-examination, Elizabeth Harris stated her belief that the prisoner is a hard-working man.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Policeman Henry Woodbridge testified that upon being arrested and told the charge, the prisoner initially denied it, but then said, "I remember one shilling; I know nothing of the other."
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: James Waters of the King's Arms, Chelsea, testified that on April 29th, the prisoner's companions passed two bad shillings. He then looked in the till and found the coins were bad.
[EVIDENCE ev11]

[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The defense called two witnesses who gave the prisoner a character of an honest, hard-working man.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: In December 1863, the prisoner, Emile Werner, traveled to Paris and informed his employers that his London business was becoming promising. He stated that to conduct a significant business, he required a large stock of goods, and claimed to have brought two buyers for large English houses with him, though he did not introduce them.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mr. Tanco, upon receiving a list of the buyers from Werner, made inquiries with other merchants. He testified, 'within half an hour I found out all about them, and told the prisoner verbally that all those clients were good for nothing... people say they... cannot be trusted to any sum.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Werner defended Lhemann to Mr. Tanco by claiming Lhemann had been 'highly recommended to him by De Vaux and Co., the bankers.' However, Alexander De Vaux, the banker, testified, 'I never gave Werner any reference or recommendation to Lhemann.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Tanco testified that the company's books, kept by Werner, 'were in such a mess that nobody could tell anything; it was a systematic disorder.' Werner had also resisted Mr. Tanco's suggestion to hire a book-keeper.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Charles Russell Curling, a traveller for a Leeds house, testified that in March, Arand gave him a reference to Le Grand, Tanco & Co. Curling stated that Werner falsely claimed Arand had always paid regularly for transactions up to £5,000 and at that moment owed 'not a penny,' when in fact Arand owed thousands.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: During cross-examination, Mr. Tanco admitted that after discovering the extent of the debts around March 16th, the firm did not discharge Werner. Instead, they 'kept him in our service till the day we gave him into custody, some time in June.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Under cross-examination, Mr. Tanco acknowledged letters from the firm pressing Werner for money. A letter from February 29th stated, 'You must positively remit these amounts that you have promised, as we are greatly in want of 5,000l. which we have to pay to our bankers.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: A letter from Werner to his employers dated February 25th was read, in which he stated his intention to continue credit to Lhemann and Arand but 'progressively to decrease it,' arguing that it would be 'imprudent to cut it of short.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The total loss to Le Grand, Tanco & Co. was stated as £32,000. Mr. Tanco testified that out of £40,000 worth of goods consigned through Werner, the firm only received about £10,000 back, and managed to save £3,000 worth of goods elsewhere.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mr. Briggs, the deceased, was in his usual good health and spirits when he left his relatives' house on the evening of July 9th. He was carrying a black bag and was confirmed to be wearing his gold watch and chain, as he referred to it to check the time before departing for the train station.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Around 10:15 PM, a first-class carriage on the 9:50 PM train from Fenchurch-street was discovered empty at Hackney station. The carriage contained spots and trickles of blood, a black bag, a stick, and a hat that did not belong to the victim.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Medical testimony confirmed that Mr. Briggs died from extensive skull fractures and other head wounds. The surgeons concluded these injuries were inflicted with considerable force by a blunt instrument, consistent with a violent assault rather than a simple fall from the train.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Witness Thomas Lee testified that he saw Mr. Briggs alive at Bow Station around 10:00 PM. He claimed Mr. Briggs was in a first-class compartment with two other men, one stout with light whiskers and the other tall and thin. He stated the prisoner was not one of them.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Several witnesses testified that Muller's plan to emigrate to America was well-known among his friends for at least a fortnight before the murder occurred. He had been preparing for the trip and had told his landlady which vessel he would be taking.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Multiple witnesses who knew Franz Muller personally, including his landlady and work colleagues, described him as a 'quiet, inoffensive, well-behaved young man' with a 'kind and humane disposition.'

Description: Colonel Alfred Richards, of the 3rd London Rifle Corps, testified that the order for the cheque book and the five cashed cheques were not written by him or with his authority.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Hoare, the man accused by Lovelock in the original case, testified that he did not take the shovel, was not at the specified location at a quarter to 7 AM, and had no such conversation with Lovelock. He stated the original charge against him was dismissed.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: George Smith, a labourer, testified he was working sifting ballast from 6 AM to 8 AM within ten yards of the area. He stated he had a complete view, did not see Hoare there, and heard no conversation about a shovel until after breakfast.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Hoare testified that the prisoner, Lovelock, had previously been sentenced to one month in jail for assaulting Hoare's son. This suggests a pre-existing animosity from Lovelock toward the Hoare family.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Policeman Joseph Taylor stated that when he tried to serve a summons on Lovelock at his listed address, he was not there, and Lovelock later claimed to have never lived there. Lovelock was subsequently apprehended on a warrant.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Lovelock's original sworn statement was read in court, where he claimed to have seen Hoare throw a shovel from an area at exactly 6:45 AM, and that Hoare threatened him when confronted.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The victim, Theodore Fairhope (known as 'John'), was described by his landlady, Elizabeth Warren, as a gentleman with valuable possessions including six sovereigns, a silver watch, a gold chain, a gold ring, and a portmanteau full of clothes. He had entrusted some of these items to Mrs. Warren for safekeeping.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: On the morning of Thursday, November 3rd, witnesses Henry Lees and Thomas William Hudson saw Kohl and the deceased walking together on the river bank path, heading towards the reed bed. This was the last time the deceased was seen alive.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The victim’s headless body was discovered in the reed bed on November 8th. The head was found the next day, buried in a nearby hole. The location matched where the prisoner and victim were last seen walking.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: On Monday, November 7th, four days after the murder and one day before the body was found, witnesses Mary Jane Cooper and Mary Cooper saw Kohl at the crime scene, jumping over a ditch from the reed bed.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: While in jail, Kohl asked his brother-in-law, Joseph Williams, to tell his cousin Bill that he 'was with me on the Thursday.' When Williams corrected him, stating the cousin was not with him, Kohl insisted, 'He must say so.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Elizabeth Warren, Kohl's landlady for three months prior to the arrival of the deceased, testified on cross-examination that 'he was always a peaceable, quiet man' and she 'never saw anything wrong of him in my house.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The landlord, Frederick Shaw, testified that the prisoner, Seraphini Polioni, came to his pub and made a threatening remark towards him. Polioni allegedly said he could 'kill any such six Englishmen like me.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: A general disturbance began when an Italian named Gregorio struck the landlord, Frederick Shaw. Defense witness Ann Sams corroborated this, stating she saw Gregorio strike the blow and that Polioni was not in the house at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Witness Maria King testified that as she was leaving the bagatelle-room, 'the prisoner knocked me down with his fist, in the mouth.' This initiated the rush into the room.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Inspector Thomas Potter testified that the deceased, Michael Harrington, identified the prisoner at the hospital. When asked to identify his attacker from a group, Harrington pointed to Polioni and said, 'That is the man who did it'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Sergeant George Baldock testified that when he pointed out blood on the prisoner's hands, Polioni said 'I only protected myself.' Later, when charged with murder, he said 'I never used a knife.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William John Weller, a clerk in the Exchequer Office, testified that on October 22nd, a person representing himself as James Edwards swore to an affidavit in the regular form. Weller could not personally identify the defendant.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Prior, a clerk, testified he gave the writ to Edwards, who was not in his employment and for whom this was the first writ served. Prior saw Edwards endorse the writ on Oct 11th, stating it was served on Hooper on the 10th. Edwards described a girl with red hair at the house.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The Reverend Thomas Hooper testified that he was not at his home in Kingsdown on October 10th at the time of the alleged service. He provided a detailed alibi, stating he was in Dartford, about ten miles away, in the company of Mr. Spurrell.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Margaret Godfrey, a relative residing with Hooper, testified that she opened the door to the defendant at 1:30 PM on October 10th. He asked for Hooper, seemed perplexed that he was out, and gave her a closed note to give to him, which she did that evening.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The defense argued that the indictment was fatally flawed. It averred that the summons was to set aside a 'judgment signed' and an 'execution issued', but the prosecution failed to produce any evidence that such a judgment existed, meaning the Judge lacked jurisdiction and the prisoner's evidence could not have been material.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Mr. Senior Dean, a clerk at the Bank of England, testified that on June 22nd, Warren exchanged two £100 notes for gold. To do so, she signed a false name and address: 'Mr. Pawsey, 6, Cambridge-terrace, Notting Dale.'

He also stated the endorsement 'W. D. Preece' was not on the bill when he accepted it.

[EVIDENCE ev11]

Description: Testimony showed that the prisoner received a good character.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Baker, a clicker at Messrs. Rock, testified that on the 11th or 12th of January, the prisoner, his foreman, ordered him to look out two dozen of the best white leathers from Mr. Rock's stock. He was then instructed to cut them up into purse patterns, which he did and gave to the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Detective George Scott testified that upon arrest, the prisoner admitted taking some dark leather to be cut up at the warehouse but stated, 'I gave no orders for any white skins to be cut up.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: James Gerrard, a bookbinder at Messrs. Rock, testified that the prisoner brought him books to bind for his private business, and that he used materials from Mr. Rock's stock, including boards and purple sheep leather, to do the work.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Gerrard testified that there was a system where a boy was placed at the lift-hole to watch for Mr. Rock and shout the code word 'Shears' as a warning. He stated that the prisoner had directed him to use this system.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: After the prisoner was discharged, he instructed witness Smith, 'Why, tell them you have been doing two or three books a week for me,' after Smith had suggested the number was higher. Hall claimed the other books were merely being rectified for defects.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Witnesses Baker and Smith testified that the prisoner's separate business was 'thoroughly well known in the establishment to everybody' and that there was 'no concealment whatever about it' among the employees.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: William Rock stated that the prisoner had 'no authority whatever to take leather or skins from my premises and give them out to workmen to be made' for private purposes.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Henry Baker testified that the white leather was bought from Mr. Wrigglesworth, and that he believed both Mr. Rock and the prisoner dealt with him for leather.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Adolphus Hertzberg, the prosecutor, testified that the 27.5 yards of cloth was his property, valued at £6. He stated that the prisoner, Richard Powell, was his employee but had no authority to remove the cloth.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William John Jones, an employee, testified that on the afternoon of June 21st, he was in the office on the second floor and saw the prisoner take a piece of cloth off a pile near the warehouse door.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The prisoner's statement before the magistrate was read, in which he claimed: 'I did not go outside the door with it.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Police Constable Samuel Knight testified that when he took the prisoner into custody and told him the charge, the prisoner denied it.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: During cross-examination, Hertzberg stated that Powell had been employed by him for about three years. Hertzberg also stated that many persons are about the warehouse.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: William John Jones testified that at the time he saw Powell take the cloth, there was no one else in the warehouse.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Hertzberg testified that he had 'many times missed things from the warehouse.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prisoner, Henry Chambers, stated in evidence before a Select Committee that Mr. Whatman and Mr. Lee came to his forge late one evening before the election. He testified that Mr. Whatman offered him £30 or £40 for his vote, and that Mr. Lee put two sovereigns into his pocket.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mr. Whatman stated that he never canvassed any elector alone in the town and that his canvas was largely over in the week preceding the election. He further testified that after ward meetings, he 'invariably went home.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: James Denton, a solicitor, testified that he had advised Chambers before the committee hearing. Denton warned him that if his statement was not believed and was denied by the MPs, it would be fatal to the petition and would certainly lead to a perjury indictment against him.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Both Mr. Whatman and Mr. Lee confirmed attending ward meetings at the 'Sun' tavern. Mr. Whatman acknowledged that the 'Sun' is 'pretty nearly opposite the end of Pudding Lane,' where Chambers's forge was located.

public.game_background: John Richard Jeffery is indicted for the wilful murder of his six-year-old son, Richard Arthur Jeffery. The prosecution alleges that the prisoner, motivated by marital distress and doubts about the child's paternity, deliberately took the child from his grandmother's care, hanged him in a cellar, and fled. The defense argues that the prisoner was not of sound mind at the time of the offense, presenting evidence of his eccentric behavior, delusions, and paranoia, likely exacerbated by alcohol and his wife's desertion.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Henry Foster, the prisoner's brother-in-law, testified that about a fortnight before the event, the prisoner stated that the boy 'should not be a trouble to any one much longer.' Sarah Foster, the prisoner's sister, corroborated this statement.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police Sergeant James Kay testified that the child was found hanging from a rope in a cellar. The boy's hands were tied behind his back with a handkerchief.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Richard Holden testified that he had told the prisoner that the victim had said he had 'two daddies, Daddy Jeffery and Daddy Salter,' and that 'Daddy Salter went and slept along with his mother.' The boy also related that Salter told his mother to throw him behind the fire.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Defense witness George Corne Barwell testified that he had 'often heard that the prisoner was designated as "Mad Dick"' by several of his shopmates.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Jane Edwards testified that the prisoner, while living with her, once took out a knife, sharpened it, and 'took the boy up and said he would cut him up, and then he would serve me the same.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Gardner testified that on the night of the incident, James Mooney and Welch accosted him, with James Mooney accusing him of insulting some women before striking him. This initial confrontation occurred before the main assault on James Martin.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: In his testimony, Thomas Gardner stated, 'I never saw Walter Mooney.' This is significant as Gardner was a key prosecution witness present during both altercations.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The victim, James Martin, testified that during the altercation outside the 'Crown' pub, two men initially knocked him about, after which Welch 'punched my face two or three times with his fist.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: James Martin stated that James Mooney then came and helped Welch to 'thump me,' and because he could not hit him hard enough, he 'kicked me on my head.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: James Martin identified Walter Mooney as the man who, while he was on the ground, said 'I will show you how to kick the b—s—,' and then 'kicked me under my jaw, which broke the jaw.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Henry Richardson, the landlord of the 'Crown,' provided an alibi for Walter Mooney, stating he was there from six till half-past nine, when he went away with his wife in a state of intoxication and was not seen again.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Defense witness William Reddington testified he saw the row and knew Walter Mooney well, swearing that 'he was not there.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Sarah Brenan Neal, who lived in the same house as Walter Mooney, provided a detailed alibi. She testified that she saw him lying on his bed at 10:30 pm, again at 11:45 pm, and again after midnight, claiming he could not have left without her seeing him.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Mary Ann Macdonald testified that she went to Walter Mooney's house around 9:30 pm and saw him in bed, undressed and 'rather drunk.' She also stated that she and his wife had just assisted him home from the 'Crown' public-house.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On October 14th, police searched Moses Moses's marine store warehouse following a robbery complaint he had made. They found a hole broken through the brickwork from an adjoining empty warehouse. Under tons of rags, they discovered two new saddles, a portmanteau, and other valuable goods.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Edward Harris testified that John Blake was the carman he hired on August 15th to transport cases from Smith and Elder to the East India Docks. Blake was not a regular employee but an 'odd man' hired from a stand.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Edward Harris stated that around October 20th, he confronted Blake about the stolen goods being found. Harris asked Blake to think about the journey and report back the next morning. Blake promised he would, but then disappeared and was not seen again until his arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Upon his arrest, after the charge was read to him, Blake stated, 'Well, I suppose if they have got my name down to them I took them'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Detective John Moss testified that the cart stand in Houndsditch, where Blake stopped, was only 500-600 yards from Moses's warehouse in Black Horse Yard.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Moses himself had reported to police that he had been robbed. The police investigation confirmed a hole had been made from an adjacent empty warehouse into his, and that tools used to make the hole were found.

Description: Police Constable Alfred Urben testified that the prosecutor 'was drunk, but knew what he was doing'.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Frederick Hodges, a gasfitter at the coffee stall, testified that he saw the prisoner 'strike the prosecutor in the mouth, and follow him up very close and keep striking him', causing him to bleed.

[EVIDENCE ev10]

[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Stephen Hodges, the stall keeper, who knew the defense witness Hill 'by sight', testified definitively, 'he was not there'.

information:

Description: George Alfred Morell, an eighteen-year-old employee of silk manufacturers Taylor and Stokes, testified that he stole numerous silk and satin dresses from his employer's warehouse over a period of several months.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Morell testified to a previous, separate receiving by Newman. Morell stated he sold Newman two stolen dresses, worth about £17, for £2, after Newman asked for a dress 'very cheap to give away.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: On one occasion before the main offense, when Morell's father was expected at the Swan pub, Newman warned Morell, 'Your old man will be here to-night; you had better be up stairs.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Police Constable Robert Carter testified that upon arrest, Cuthbert denied knowing the Swan public-house, denied knowing George Street, and denied having received any silk. A receipt for the silk, signed by Morell, was found on his person.
