Description: When arrested, Brown first denied receiving any silk, but then became very excited and said, 'Good God! I will tell the truth; the Swan public-house has been my ruin at last.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Witness Alfred Shakespeare testified that Morell openly stated at the bar of the Swan pub that he sold silk 'on commission.' Frederick Sanderson also testified that Morell told him he sold goods on commission.
[EVIDENCE ev14]

Description: Under cross-examination, Morell admitted to a wide range of thefts beyond what he sold the prisoners, including selling a large amount to a man named Sylvester. He also admitted to once visiting the Swan in a disguise of false whiskers and a moustache.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The company manager, Ernest Alers Hankey, testified that the prisoner, Martin Minns, brought him the cheque for £59 4s. 10d. Minns represented it as being a balance due to Mr. Foster, a wool buyer, which prompted Hankey to sign it.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Manager Hankey testified that the words 'Please pay cash' were not on the cheque when he signed it. He identified this addition as being in the prisoner's handwriting and stated it was unauthorized and not initialed, which was contrary to company practice for such requests.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Bank cashier Lamming provided a memorandum detailing the payment, which included ten £5 notes with specific serial numbers (83351 to 83360 inclusive).
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Detective John Mark Bull testified that upon arrest, Minns stated: 'if I have done any wrong I have been the victim of another man who is dead; he used to come screwy... and I used to sign papers for him.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: On cross-examination, Hankey confirmed that while the cheque counterfoil was in Minns' handwriting, the folio number referencing the cash-book entry ('269') was written by the other cashier, Hammond.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Defense witnesses Rowbotham and Blencoe, both clerks at the firm, testified that Hammond was senior to Minns and that Minns would likely fill up cheques if instructed to do so by Hammond.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Manager Hankey admitted on re-examination that after Hammond's death, defalcations involving Hammond and two other clerks had been discovered.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective William Smith testified that he stopped the prisoner, Henry Ryan, in Bow Lane carrying a large parcel. Ryan claimed it was paper for his trade which he had gotten from his home, and that a receipt was on file there.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: John Lindsay, the manager for the prosecutor, testified that a recent stock-taking showed 485 pieces of the specific paper pattern. After the prisoner's arrest, a recount revealed that the company was short by fifty-five pieces.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Three separate witnesses—Charles Markham, George William, and Robert Turner—testified to seeing the prisoner Ryan drinking with Hooper, a packer from Potter's warehouse, on the day of the arrest and frequently in the preceding months.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: After the jury delivered a guilty verdict on the charge of stealing, the prisoner, Henry Ryan, then pleaded guilty to a previous conviction at the same court in February, 1853.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On March 23rd, 1867, Joseph Moore, a packer, prepared three trusses of carpet worth approximately £38 for J. Easten and Co. He marked them with specific consignment numbers and handed them to the Midland Railway Company for delivery in London.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Froome, a carman for the railway, testified that when he could not find 'Easten and Co.' in Addle Street, he was directed by a person at the corner of Philip Lane to a newly done-up premises at 7, Philip Lane, being told that Easten had moved there.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: John Nelson, a house agent, testified that the defendant Eustace had previously rented premises in Addle Street for a 'Mr. Jennings' but immediately put up the name 'Eustace and Co.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Nelson also testified that Eustace rented the premises at 7, Philip Lane under the false name 'William Lake,' and provided references that did not know him by that name.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: When confronted by Nelson about the false name, Eustace claimed his 'real name' was Shearman and produced a card identifying himself as 'Captain William Eustace Shearman, Royal Dragoons.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: When the railway superintendent, Henry Ralph, visited 7, Philip Lane about a week after the delivery, he found the premises to be entirely empty of goods, with no name up.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: During his visits, Superintendent Ralph was misled by Little, who claimed he was not Little and that Eustace was away, only admitting his identity after being pointed out by the landlord.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Upon his arrest in October, Eustace told the detective a different story, claiming that a person named Prescott took the carpets away and had never paid for them.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: At the police court, Little's solicitor stated that the incident was 'simply a mistake in the delivery, and the goods were to be had if we chose to send for them.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: After a summons was issued, Eustace failed to appear in court. A warrant was issued in June, but he was not located and arrested until October 16th.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The defendant Eustace received a good character reference during the trial.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Magee, the father of Emily Florence Magee, testified that his daughter married the prisoner on December 12, 1859, at Calvary Church in New York.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: James Magee testified that his daughter, Emily Florence, is still alive and has been living at his house since she ceased to live with the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: On cross-examination, James Magee stated that he was requested to keep the engagement secret because the prisoner was not in America at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Robert De Tracy Gould, a member of the American Bar, testified that the marriage ceremony as described was perfectly valid according to the law of New York.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: A letter written by the prisoner in 1865 was read in court, in which he addressed Emily Florence as 'My own wife,' referred to himself as a 'bad husband,' and signed it 'Your undeserving husband, Eardley G. C. Eardley.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Joseph Harris Stretton testified to two legal deeds executed by the defendant. A deed from July 1860 refers to settling money on 'Lady Eardley,' and another from October 1868 refers to 'Emily Florence his wife.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Henry Bannister, the Registrar of Marriages for St. George's parish, produced a register certifying the marriage of 'Eardley Gideon Culling Eardley and Mary Elizabeth Allen' on September 12, 1867.

information:

Description: William Clark, a barman, testified that the prisoner, John McCory, came into the pub and paid for a pot of beer with a florin. This florin was later determined to be counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Policeman Daniel Hill testified that upon searching the prisoner, he found a third bad florin wrapped in paper inside the prisoner's pocket, in addition to some good money.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The prisoner stated that he had received the coins at a public-house in Notting Hill as change for half a sovereign from another man, and he was unaware that they were counterfeit.

[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: During cross-examination, the barman stated that there were 'a good many' people in the taproom and that the prisoner had arrived with 'one or two others' who seemed like travelers.

The discovery was made after a civil dispute over wages between Eliza Chadwick and Mr. Gow. Eliza ultimately pleaded guilty, while the prosecution did not press the case against her husband.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Dan Gow, the prosecutor, testified that Eliza Chadwick was his employee. After a wage dispute, he went to her house with police and found a large quantity of property, valued at £100-£130, which he identified as belonging to his business.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: During cross-examination, Mr. Gow admitted that before hiring Mrs. Chadwick, he had occasionally given her work to make up at her own premises, as she ran her own business making up items for people. He also stated he had bought a sewing machine from her.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The dispute between Mrs. Chadwick and Mr. Gow originated from a disagreement over wages. She had requested an increase, was refused, and left his employ. The matter was before a commissioner on the very day Mr. Gow went to her house with the police.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Gow testified that he buys materials in the open market in cities like Leeds and Manchester, and that other persons may buy similar articles. This admission was made during cross-examination.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Charles Woodbridge, clerk to the Magistrates, testified that the prisoner, Charlotte Stagg, was sworn in during an affiliation hearing on March 30th. Under cross-examination, she stated, 'I did not say to Mrs. Baker that if she would not come to-day and swear she saw Stevens in bed with me I would swear that I saw Stevens in bed with her.' The perjury charge is assigned to this statement.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Sarah Baker testified that on a Friday in March, Stagg visited her and stated that George Stevens was the father of her child. Stagg then said, 'if he does not keep it by fair means he must by foul.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Sarah Baker testified that on the following Tuesday, Stagg asked her to come to Uxbridge and 'speak for me.' When Baker refused, she claims Stagg threatened her, saying, 'If you do not come and swear that you saw George Stevens in my bed, I will swear I saw him in yours.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Under cross-examination, Mrs. Baker stated that during the confrontation in the kitchen, 'the prisoner was in a very great passion, very excited.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Woodbridge testified that at the conclusion of the affiliation hearing where the alleged perjury occurred, the case against George Stevens was dismissed.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mr. Gardener, Stevens's attorney, stated under cross-examination that while he appeared for Stevens, it was from Mrs. Baker that he 'received the information to put the question' about the threat to Stagg.

[EVIDENCE ev1]

The case explores his admission of a much larger defalcation of approximately £3000, his subsequent cooperation, and the firm's delay in prosecuting him while attempting to recover the funds.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Samuel Purvis Rentall, a clerk for Messrs. Tarn & Co., testified that he sent two crossed cheques on July 10th (£20 15s. 6d.) and August 10th, 1867 (£14 17s. 6d.) by post to Messrs. Stead & Co.'s London house. He stated both cheques were paid by his firm's bankers.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: James Harrison, a warehouseman for Messrs. W. & R. Morley, testified that his firm paid £9 14s. in cash to Messrs. Stead on September 9th, 1867. He stated that the receipt for this payment was signed by 'Robert C. Ewin'.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Robert Gibson Hodson, the manager of the main business in Carlisle, described the prisoner's duties. He stated Ewin was to receive money, enter it in the cash-book, and remit balances to the head office, and had no authority to trade on his own account.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The jury found the prisoner guilty but added a strong recommendation for mercy. Their stated reason was 'that if he had had more time, he might perhaps have made the accounts good.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Victim George Joyce testified that he was 'not quite sober' and initiated a physical confrontation by knocking the prisoner, Charles Cardwell, out of his chair three times.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Joyce testified that he did not realize he was wounded until the next morning, after sleeping all night in the same bed with the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Joyce testified that when he accused Cardwell of the stabbing, the prisoner 'made no answer.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Charles Vince noted that after the fight, the prisoner had a 'black eye, and his nose was bleeding.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Police Sergeant George Miller stated that when first questioned, Joyce told him, 'I don't know who has done it.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Sergeant Miller testified that a knife was found on the prisoner that 'corresponded in width with the wound,' but critically, 'there was no blood upon it.'

public.game_background: Noel Desire Luco was indicted for unlawfully obtaining £20 by false pretences from Samuel Konody, with intent to defraud. The prosecution alleged that Luco used a fraudulent bill of lading for a non-existent shipment of potatoes to secure the money. The prosecution also presented evidence of a similar fraudulent act committed against another individual, Charles Atkins, to establish a pattern of behavior.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Konody stated that when he offered to travel to Dunkirk to verify the ship's existence, Luco dissuaded him, saying, 'It is no use to go there and spend 2 l., you had best stop till to-morrow, and the ship will be here.' Luco then showed Konody the bill of lading for the Mary Adelaide.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Konody testified that on the faith of the bill of lading and Luco's promise of the ship's imminent arrival, he handed Luco £20 in gold.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: When confronted, Luco first blamed bad weather for the ship's delay. Later, he claimed that a Mr. Corney owed Konody the money, not him.
[EVIDENCE ev10]

information:

Description: Immediately after the robbery, Mr. Smith described his assailant to the police as 'a dark man with peculiar features, rather a prominent roundish nose, rather a short man and stoutly built'.
[EVIDENCE ev5]

[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: In his defense, Williams claimed he was innocent and had an alibi. He stated he was at Mrs. Perry's public-house at 9 o'clock on the night of the robbery and 'never moved out'.

public.game_background: Joseph Henry Kitchen, a clerk for the Abney Park Cemetery Company, was indicted along with his senior colleague, Samuel Howell Carter (who has absconded), for conspiring to obtain approximately £500 from the company. The prosecution alleges they used a secret, duplicate set of receipt books to collect fees for interments, which they then failed to enter into the official accounts, thereby embezzling the funds.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Joseph either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Multiple undertakers and private citizens testified that they paid the prisoner, Joseph Kitchen, various sums for interments. They confirmed that Kitchen filled out their receipts and they signed counterfoils that were later found to be part of an unofficial set of records.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Heath, the company secretary, testified that after the senior clerk, Carter, absconded, he discovered a hidden rough order-book in the office. This book contained records of payments for burials, with entries in the handwriting of both Carter and the prisoner, Kitchen.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Crucially, Heath testified that all the burials from the hidden book and loose counterfoils were correctly entered into the official Register of Burials. This register was kept exclusively by Kitchen, and the relevant entries were in his handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: When Heath confronted Kitchen, the prisoner claimed he made the register entries using information from official registrar's certificates of death. Heath refuted this, stating that the certificates lacked key details, such as grave depth, which could only have been obtained from the fraudulent counterfoils.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Shortly after being confronted by Heath, Kitchen left the office, claiming he was going to lunch, and did not return. He surrendered to the police about a week later.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The senior clerk, Samuel Howell Carter, held a position of significant trust, had been with the company for 21 years, and was in charge of the cash-book. His salary was £100 per year, while Kitchen, his junior, earned only £1 per week.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Carter, the senior clerk, absconded from his position and had not been apprehended at the time of the trial. The investigation into the company's books only began after Mr. Heath heard rumors that Carter was living extravagantly and had private means.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Heath testified that Carter had been negligent in his duties and had been warned about his conduct. Heath stated he had latterly complained to Carter of his absence from business and inattention.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In a later conversation with the solicitor Mr. Lewis, Kitchen claimed that he had sometimes made entries in the Register of Burials at the direction of Carter, who dictated to him what to write.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Accountant Benjamin Nicholson testified that the prisoner's purchases in April, May, and June of 1869 were £6055, a £4500 excess compared to the same period in 1868. Sales for the same period only increased by £884, creating a large discrepancy.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The accountant, Benjamin Nicholson, stated he had not received any invoices for the year 1869, which had been pasted in a book up to July 1868. Their absence significantly impeded his investigation into the bankrupt's affairs.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: At a creditors' meeting, the bankrupt, Feldman, stated he had not sold any goods at a loss, but rather at an average profit of 7.5%. He was unable to account for the deficiency shown on his balance sheet.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Adolph Stargardter, a next-door neighbor to David Davis, testified that on June 28th, he saw Feldman's van 'crammed full' with goods, valued at about £300, being unloaded into Davis's front door. He stated Feldman and Davis were both present and supervising.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: During cross-examination, Adolph Stargardter admitted to having 'a little animosity' against David Davis, who had forced himself to be an assignee in Stargardter's own bankruptcy. He conceded he 'might have said' he would be revenged on Davis and Feldman.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: John Shea, the foreman for David Davis, testified that large quantities of goods were not delivered from Feldman's in June. He further stated that Davis's warehouse would not have been able to hold goods to the value of £700 or £800 in addition to his regular stock.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: In his bankruptcy examination, Feldman denied sending large quantities of goods to David Davis. He claimed that cheques given to Davis were for loans, not for concealed goods.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Detective Sergeant John Mark Bull testified that a warrant was issued for the arrest of both Feldman and his alleged co-conspirator, David Davis. While Feldman surrendered, every exertion was used to find Davis, but he had 'absconded from the country.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Lord George Francis Hamilton testified that he had previously given the prisoner Godfrey a guinea for a regatta in 1869. He confirmed his signature was on a subscription list from that year, which is now headed 'The Tottenham Annual Rowing Match, 1869', but stated he did not subscribe to any such match.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Policeman Holman stated that after the arrest, Ashton said, 'There is a man down here who gave me this parchment.' Ashton then walked up to Godfrey and said, 'This is a pretty mess, Bill,' to which Godfrey replied, 'It's all right.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: At the police station, Ashton stated that Godfrey gave him the parchments. Godfrey, in turn, claimed another man, whom he did not know, gave them to him.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Policeman Holman testified that he made inquiries in Tottenham at boat-houses and public-houses and confirmed that 'no such thing was ever known as an annual regatta or rowing match.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Thomas Hughes, M.P., testified that a name purporting to be his on the subscription list was not his writing. He also stated that he knew Godfrey by sight from previous requests for subscriptions.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: John Gilbert Talbot, M.P., testified that his signature on the 1869 list was genuine, but he knew for certain that he did not subscribe to the Tottenham Regatta.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Ashton's defense was that Godfrey called on him, asked him to do a job, and gave him the papers. He claimed he thought they were genuine and that Godfrey told him he was one of the committee.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Godfrey's defense was that he received the papers from a man named Charles Brown, who asked him to collect the subscriptions. When he could not, he asked Ashton to do it, and he has not seen Brown since.

Description: George Rotherham Layers testified that around midnight on April 13th, two men rushed out, knocked him into the gutter, and fumbled about his watch and chain, which he then lost. He called out 'Stop Thief!'

Description: In his defense, Bresley claimed he was running away from a girl and was not in that quarter. He accused the officers of committing perjury, stating, 'these officers said they would get it up for me.'

information:

Description: A clerk from the Court of Bankruptcy, Bertrand Robert Johnson, testified that the prisoner's accounts filed in November 1868 showed liabilities of £4118 and assets of only £889, resulting in a deficiency of £3228.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The bankruptcy court clerk, B.R. Johnson, also produced proceedings from the prisoner's previous bankruptcy in April 1866, showing a pattern of financial failure.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The defendant's accountant, Hugh Grosvenor, confirmed that the prisoner bought goods from various creditors and sent them to an auctioneer, Mr. Burton, for sale. An account of these dealings showed a loss of £316 over a six-month period.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: When cross-examined about preparing the account of the auction sales, Mr. Grosvenor stated that the bankrupt 'gave it as far as he could' and 'went to Mr. Burton's for the express purpose of ascertaining' the details.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Accountant Hugh Grosvenor testified that the twenty or thirty business books given up by the bankrupt to the assignees 'were very well kept'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Creditor John Arnold testified that after the bankruptcy, he met the defendant, who said 'he was forced to do it, as his creditors pressed him, so much'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The official shorthand writer's transcript confirms William Heale testified under oath that he had not received any money for bribery at the 1865 election, did not pay money at the Golden Lion, and did not meet Frederick Jarman at the landing.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Clerks from the Crown Office and House of Commons produced official records confirming the 1865 Bridgewater election, a petition alleging an undue election, and the formation of a Select Committee where witnesses, including Heale, were sworn in.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Tromp admitted he was a professional briber paid 250 guineas for his work. He also stated that many witnesses lied to the 1866 Committee and that he himself deliberately 'kept away' from that hearing despite being in London.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Jarman admitted to having been involved in bribery in multiple elections and receiving payment for his actions. He was not examined before the 1866 Committee, despite being available in London.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Witness Philip Gilbert admitted that his 'conscience was awakened' because the £10 bribe was from the 'wrong party' and insufficient. He testified he would have taken £15, and that he later accepted a £10 bribe from the Liberal party.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: A prosecution witness confirmed under cross-examination that Heale had continued to live in or near Bridgewater up to the time of his arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The arresting officer testified that when Heale was taken into custody, he said 'he thought it was all settled, as he had not heard anything since the Taunton trial.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Eleanor Duke, the prisoner's wife, testified that on the morning of the incident, her husband, without any prior quarrel, struck her on the head with a stick three times. He then laid her on the floor, placed a pillow under her head, and proceeded to stab her in the throat and face with a penknife.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The prisoner had been out of work for seven months. During this period, the family had suffered immensely; five of their children were ill with fever, and they had lost one child in October.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The prisoner told his wife, 'I have warned you of this before, as you have been preparing your things to go and leave me; she has driven me to it, to cut her throat and then my own.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When a neighbor, Mrs. Jonah, entered the room, the prisoner said to her, 'Mrs. Jonah, this is all through you.' He repeated this accusation a second time.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Policeman William Wilson, who had known the prisoner for some time, testified that the prisoner 'seemed very calm' during the incident. He also stated he had 'not noticed anything in his manner to lead me to conclude that he is of unsound mind' prior to this.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Richard Thornton, an assistant messenger of the Court of Bankruptcy, produced the official proceedings showing the defendant's bankruptcy petition and adjudication were dated October 21st, 1869. The order for the prosecution was dated July 4th, 1870.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: John Blackshaw, a creditor, testified that in July 1869 he lent the defendant £262 to redeem pawned goods. The defendant promised to repay the money within three days from the proceeds of a sale to Messrs. Ward & Sturt.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Testimony from accountant John Robinson Clark and others established that in a prior composition with creditors in December 1868, the defendant's father, Lawrence Ashton, had secured the last two installments, making himself liable for a sum around £900.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Walter Ongley, the defendant's former clerk, testified that when the goods were proposed to be taken away, the defendant objected and wished his father to retain them, stating the business was good and he could get over his difficulties if given time.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: John Blackshaw testified that weeks after the loan, the defendant's father thanked him for his kindness and told him he 'should not lose a penny, if it was the last stick he had to sell he would pay me'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Edwin Rudd, a creditor who was present at the creditors' meeting in January 1869, testified that while it was agreed the father should become security for the composition, nothing was said at that time about him receiving security from his son.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Phillips stated that an inventory signed by Bennett listed 170 tons of coal and a waggon. However, Phillips discovered there were only about 30 tons of coal, and he was never able to locate the waggon.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Sophia Palmer testified that she was the owner of the disputed land and produced a written agreement showing she had leased it to Bennett. She stated that Bennett had paid her rent quarterly before stopping and declaring he wouldn't pay any more.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Francis Crossley, a former partner, testified that Bennett had induced him to invest £600 using identical false pretenses. Bennett told him the land was his 'own property' by possession, that there was no owner, and that he had never paid rent.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Defense witness John Temperly, a former tenant of the wharf, testified that years earlier he had also held the land in question without paying rent. He stated this was because the person in possession at that time had no power to grant a lease.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: John Ralph, a defense witness, testified that he occupied the land for about six years without paying rent. However, on cross-examination, he admitted that when Mrs. Palmer served him with a notice to leave, he gave up possession to her.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: George Smith, a dealer, testified for the defense that Bennett had given him a waggon to sell and a deposit to buy a new one for £25-£30. He said he sold the old one but had been unable to find a suitable replacement for Bennett.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Phillips testified that of the £90 in outstanding debts shown to him, less than half were actually collectible. He discovered that Bennett had personally collected some of the debts prior to the agreement without accounting for the money.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Alfred England's house at Twickenham was broken into between March 31st and July 23rd, 1870, while he was away. Entry was forced through a small kitchen window. The only item reported missing was a clock.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The defendant, Alfred Healey, had visited Mr. England's house twice, about ten days before the owner left. Healey was in the very room where the clock was kept and would have seen it.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Burnand Scaley, the victim's neighbor, testified that he saw Healey with a parcel containing what looked like a clock with a cracked glass. He stated he then directed Healey to Attenborough's pawn shop at Healey's request for money.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: On discovering the break-in, Scaley suggested going to the police station. He testified that Healey seemed reluctant and said something to the effect of 'Oh, never mind.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Scaley had recently been the primary witness against Healey in a serious but separate criminal charge (concealing the birth of a child). The grand jury ultimately dismissed that charge against Healey.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mr. England testified that in July, Scaley told him that Smith had bought a ticket from Healey and that Smith had been told 'that he [Healey] cracked a crib next to Scaley's house'.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Upon being formally arrested for the burglary, the defendant Healey's only reply was, 'Well, I suppose I must go with you, then?'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Rev. Alfred White testified that on the night of November 3rd, he locked the chapel safely. The next morning, he found several doors and an iron safe broken open, the latter by gunpowder, and a silver monstrance, a thurible, and a chalice worth over 80 pounds were stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Detective McMath testified that he hid in an adjacent cell and overheard a conversation between Jarrett and Palmer. Jarrett admitted, 'they found the b---- tools at my place,' and worried they would 'fit the centre-bit and jemmy to the marks on the doors which we broke open,' while Palmer mentioned getting 'enough out of the b---- stuff to pay for a good mouthpiece.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Detective Watts and Detective Clough both testified that during the arrest, Palmer said to Jarrett, 'Does he want you to round, Harry? don't tell.' This suggested they shared a secret and were concerned about one implicating the other.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Thomas Stevens, a harness-maker, testified that on the foggy night of the robbery, around 12:30 AM, he saw two men near the chapel. He held up his lantern to one man's face and identified him as Palmer, who then went into the chapel.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_nicholson_discovery]
Description: John Nicholson, sub-librarian of Lincoln's Inn, testified that several valuable, frequently used books were discovered missing on July 29th, 1870. Through inquiries with booksellers, he located some of the missing volumes, including 'Fisher's Digest' and 'Shelford on Railways', at the shop of Mr. E.J. Turner.
[EVIDENCE ev_eturner_identification]
Description: Edward James Turner, the bookseller, identified the prisoner, Mr. Lamb, as the person who sold him the stolen volumes on August 4th. He stated that the prisoner brought the books in a blue bag and accepted £6 10s. for them.
[EVIDENCE ev_byerley_bookbinder]
Description: William Byerley, a bookbinder, identified the prisoner as the man who brought the stolen books to his shop to be repaired. He testified the prisoner explicitly asked him to 'put in new end papers... to hide the old name, as he did not care about seeing it.'
[EVIDENCE ev_smith_bookseller]
Description: George Smith, another bookseller, testified that the prisoner, whom he knew personally, had offered to sell him 'Fisher's Digest' a few days before the book was sold to Turner. Smith later pointed the prisoner out to Mr. Nicholson on the street.
[EVIDENCE ev_lamb_initial_explanation]
Description: Mr. Nicholson testified that when he first confronted the defendant about another missing book, the defendant claimed he bought it 'of a solicitor about relinquishing practice, whose name and address he could not give'. This initial explanation did not mention the name 'Langbourne'.
[EVIDENCE ev_alibi_bailey]
Description: William Bailey testified that on August 4th, he was at the office of the defendant's brother and saw the defendant there until about 1:45 PM. This provides an alibi for the time Mr. Turner claims the sale occurred (between 1 and 2 PM).
[EVIDENCE ev_reynolds_motive]
Description: George Reynolds, the prisoner's father-in-law, testified that the defendant enjoyed a private income of £300 a year plus professional earnings, lived economically, and could have obtained any money he needed from relatives. This evidence was intended to show a lack of financial motive for the crime.

Description: Detective Robert Carter testified that he saw the three prisoners in the company of a drunken sailor, John O'Hara. He watched them go into the 'Euston Tavern' together and emerge shortly after.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The victim's right-hand coat pocket, the same one Kelly allegedly put his hand in, was found to be torn down.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The victim, John O'Hara, admitted in his testimony that he was 'quite drunk' and had consumed 'a good deal of liquor' on the day of the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: When arrested by Detective Chamberlain, defendant Roach claimed, 'Good God! Mr. Chamberlain, I know nothing of it; I saw the man lying down, but I don't know anything of it.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: When arrested, defendant Cann stated, 'I don't thieve! I am a prostitute, but you know I don't thieve.' Detective Carter replied, 'I know you do thieve, and you have been convicted for it times.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: When questioned directly by defendant Cann in court, the victim John O'Hara stated, 'I don't know—I know nothing at all about you.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Charles Thompson testified that the prisoner, Arthur Walker, presented himself as an agent for the 'Royal Scottish Plate Glass Insurance Company.' Walker claimed it was a 'sound affair' with substantial directors and the London and Westminster Bank as their bankers, which induced Thompson to pay a premium of £2 17s. 6d.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Thompson testified that the company made good on the first three breakages he claimed, which included replacing glass at two different shops. This satisfied him to the extent that he purchased further insurance policies from Walker.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: One of the signatories on Thompson's policy, Vicomte De Cissy, was identified by a police inspector who testified that the Vicomte was convicted at the Central Criminal Court on April 5, 1870, for an unrelated offense.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: In his defense, Walker stated that the company was started legitimately, that Thompson had initially benefited by over £10, and that shareholders had paid in £250 more than the company's income. He claimed he was not responsible for the directors' actions and acted with 'no guilty intention.'

information:

[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Victim Joseph Greenebaum identified a specific meerschaum pipe, worth six guineas, found in Pearce's room as his property, which was stolen from his house during a burglary in March of the previous year.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Victim Henry West, an optician, identified a compass, a log glass, and other items as property stolen from his shop in October of the previous year.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Victim Moritz Geber identified twenty-eight cigar-tubes and pipes found in Pearce's rooms as his property, stolen during a burglary at his house on January 21st. This was the specific charge of the indictment.

Description: Robert Farmer testified that five opera-glasses, two barometers, and other instruments also found at Pearce's were stolen during two separate burglaries at his employer's premises in 1867 and 1869.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On January 9th, a bank cashier at the London Joint Stock Bank, William Pritchard, cashed a cheque for £315 entirely in gold. The person who presented the cheque was given a bag with the bank's name stamped on it to hold the gold.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Pritchard, the bank cashier who cashed the cheque, testified under cross-examination that it was his belief the prisoner, James Tier, was not the person who presented the cheque.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: James Pike, a partner at Merriman & Pike, testified that the prisoner was their former cashier with access to the cheque-book. He confirmed the forged cheque was from their book and that a page of three cheques, including the counterfoils, was missing.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: John McKenzie testified that in early February, the prisoner came to stay with him, claiming financial difficulty. After a few days, the prisoner fled by climbing over a garden wall when his son warned of someone 'suspicious outside'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Constable Robert Outram testified that upon arresting the prisoner, Tier offered him a bribe of twenty-five sovereigns 'to let me slip out of this,' adding 'I will bolt on the Continent and you can still be looking after me'.

public.game_background: Sarah Simpson, a 53-year-old cook, was indicted for feloniously forging and uttering an order for the payment of £30, with intent to defraud her employer, Mrs. Catalina Isabel Stewart. She was ultimately found guilty of uttering the forged cheque.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Neilson, a bank cashier, testified that the prisoner, Sarah Simpson, was Mrs. Stewart's servant and was in the habit of coming regularly to the bank to transact business for her employer.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: On March 3rd, the prisoner was sent by Mrs. Stewart to fetch a new cheque-book. The cashier confirmed he delivered the book, from which the forged cheque was later taken, directly into her hands.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mrs. Stewart testified that after receiving the cheque-book, she placed it in her desk. While she generally kept the desk locked, she admitted it was possible she had left it open, and confirmed that other servants were also employed in the house.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: An examination of the cheque-book later revealed that four cheques had been torn out, counterfoils and all, indicating a deliberate attempt to conceal their removal.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mrs. Stewart testified that the £30 cheque was not written or authorized by her, confirming it was a forgery.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The cashier, James Neilson, positively identified the prisoner as the person who presented the forged £30 cheque on March 13th. He paid her £25 in gold and a £5 note.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When Detective Dobell confronted the prisoner about the cheque, she denied any knowledge of it, saying 'I know nothing about it'.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The detective searched the prisoner's box and found she possessed only three halfpence. None of the stolen £30 was recovered.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner received a good character reference from her previous employer of five and a half years, Mr. Hyam. The jury and prosecutrix recommended mercy on account of her age and previous good character.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Charles Bellingkrodt testified that on April 5th, his rooms were broken into and a quantity of his wife's dresses and a gold watch were stolen. He identified several recovered dresses as belonging to his wife.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Charles Bellingkrodt testified that he saw a man he believed to be William Corbett loitering near his house some days before the burglary. However, under cross-examination, he admitted, 'I have stated before that I am not quite sure.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: During cross-examination, Policeman John Shannon stated that the prisoner Sarah Inwood also goes by the name 'Porter'.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Policeman John Shannon testified that he overheard a conversation from the cells where William Corbett told Mary, 'you must crack and say I don't know anything about the dress; say I had gone out before you brought it home.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Inspector Wenham testified that on the way to the station, Mary Corbett said 'Countryman knows all about this, and if I have done wrong before, I shall speak the truth this time.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On July 27th, Elizabeth Griffiths, a servant at a dairy, served the prisoner, Sarah Smith, who purchased two eggs and paid with a shilling. Griffiths took the shilling to her mistress, Mrs. Watt, and then went to find a constable.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Sarah Watt, the dairy owner, testified that about six weeks prior, a woman came in, bought two eggs, and gave her a bad shilling. However, Mrs. Watt stated, 'I don't swear that the prisoner was the woman.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Upon searching the prisoner, Policeman Robert French found a significant amount of legitimate currency (two sovereigns, two half-crowns, one florin, eleven sixpences, etc.) but 'No bad money was found on her.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Caroline Spence, a tailoress living in the same house as the prisoner, testified that she was charged with passing a bad shilling on 17th June. Spence is 'positive she was at work for me on that day up till 8 o'clock, and she did not go out of the house at all.'

public.game_background: Daniel Feiler is charged with conspiring with others to obtain large quantities of bricks from the Cowley Brick Company by means of false pretences. The scheme allegedly involved creating fictitious identities, using fraudulent references to obtain goods on credit, and then selling the goods for cash without making payment.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Daniel either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The landlord, Mr. Horncastle, testified that he let the house at 1, Beechey Street to the prisoner, Feiler. This was the same address used by the fictitious 'William Hedges' to order bricks.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Joseph Hiscock, a contractor, testified that he bought thousands of bricks marked 'C.B.C.' (Cowley Brick Company) from a man named Baker for 20s. per thousand, a significantly lower price than the 28s. the company charged on credit.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Herbert Barlee, the victim, testified that his company was defrauded via three separate schemes using the names Hedges, Serle, and Archer. All three used the same method of ordering bricks on credit with false references and then disappearing without payment from addresses that turned out to be empty.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Bargeman George Thomas testified that he delivered bricks for 'W. Hedges' and later for 'Archer'. On both occasions, the same two men, Southwell and Baker, claimed the deliveries.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Elizabeth Mary Edwards, a neighbor, testified she saw Feiler and William Southwell (who she knew as 'Mr. Serle') frequently visiting each other's houses and going out together. 'Serle' was the name used in the second fraudulent brick order.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Edward Yates testified that Feiler rented a shop from him under the false name 'Thomas Fisher' and gave a 'Mr. Zencraft' as a reference. Zencraft was the same name used as a reference for the fictitious 'William Hedges' in the first brick fraud.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Upon his arrest, Detective Peek found Feiler in bed at a new address where he was using the name 'T. Fisher' over a shop that was not open for business. When charged, Feiler said, 'I had nothing to do with it, and know nothing at all about it.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: On the way to the police station, Feiler admitted to the officer, 'I have known Southwell a great many years; I knew him when he kept a public-house in Camomile Street.' Southwell was the individual who used the aliases Hedges and Serle.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: On cross-examination, Herbert Barlee, manager of the brick company, admitted that in these cases he sent off the bricks after receiving only a written reference, without making any further inquiries. He stated, 'it won't be so again.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The prisoner received a good character reference at the conclusion of the trial.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Richmond Tucker, the prosecutor, admitted under cross-examination to being insolvent in 1861 and again in 1867 or 1868, for sums of £500 and £300 respectively, paying nothing to his creditors at the time. He also had several County Court summonses against him.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: On August 4th, Bartholomew's men first attempted to remove the furniture. The issue was resolved when Tucker agreed to provide an acceptance from a friend, Major Gray, for the remaining £10 due.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: On the morning of August 14th, Bartholomew's men entered the house and began removing the hired furniture. Tucker stated that no violence was used by his party at this time.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Thomas Spooner testified that he was in a pub with the defendant Jones, who said they were going to get the things away at night-time, between 8:30 and 9:00, and were going to get 'Head's ladder'. Spooner then informed Mr. Vincent of this plan.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Multiple witnesses, including John Graham, Thomas Spooner, and Police Sergeant Dolphin, saw a ladder placed against the house. They saw several men, including defendants Beer and Green, go up the ladder towards the drawing-room window.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Thomas Spooner, who was wounded, testified that he went up the ladder after Bartholomew's men to 'prevent the things from coming out,' implying he was acting defensively on behalf of Tucker.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Thomas Spooner testified that the morning after the riot, he saw defendants Bishop, Green, and Jones at Bartholomew's shop asking to be 'settling with for what they had done last night.' He then saw Jones pay Green 6s. 6d. and two others 7s. 6d.

public.game_background: John Selby Watson, a 67-year-old former headmaster, was indicted for the wilful murder of his wife, Anne Watson. The prosecution argues that Watson deliberately killed his wife, concealed the crime, planned to dispose of the body, and then attempted suicide to escape justice. The defense contends that Watson was suffering from a severe mental illness, specifically melancholia brought on by the loss of his career, and was not of sound mind when he committed the act.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prisoner and his wife were generally observed to live on very friendly and quiet terms. The house servant, Eleanor Pyne, who had been with them for nearly three years, testified that she never noticed any quarreling or angry feelings between them, and they always appeared to live happily together.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: In September 1870, Mr. Watson was dismissed from his long-held position as headmaster of the Stockwell Grammar School due to a decline in pupil numbers. A letter written by Watson at the time shows he felt this was a sudden and unfair action by the school's committee.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The post-mortem examination revealed eight severe wounds on the victim's scalp, with a skull fracture extending from the top to the base. Dr. Rugg described the injuries as indicating 'extreme and unusual violence, almost the ferocity of violence.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner made two attempts to commit suicide with prussic acid on the Tuesday night and Wednesday morning following the murder. He told Dr. Rugg the first dose, which he'd had for a year, was not strong enough, so he purchased more on Wednesday to complete the act.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: A shirt belonging to the prisoner was found in a drawer with its wristbands cut off. An expert testified that the sleeves had been washed but still showed traces of blood.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Harriet Barrett, the landlady, testified that the prisoner rented the house at 4, Orchard Place, and she saw him there on the morning of the police seizure, September 17th, 1870.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: After the police seizure, the prisoner disappeared without giving notice and could not be found by police officers for over a year, until January 1872.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Officer James Brannan testified that during the seizure at 4, Orchard Place, his team found plaster of Paris moulds that were still hot, a coin within them, a galvanic battery, acids, and all other necessary implements for making and colouring counterfeit coin.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Alfred Redfern, a neighbour, testified that he could see into the back parlour of the house and frequently saw the prisoner melting something in a ladle over a fire and pouring it into what he later found to be plaster of Paris moulds.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Redfern also testified that other persons, who were later tried and convicted for counterfeiting, were present with the prisoner while this melting activity was taking place.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Officer Henry Stirling testified that he saw the prisoner with three women on July 11th, 1870, and watched them go into the Queen Victoria pub, where one of the women, Mrs. Copeland, passed a bad half-crown.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: When Officer Brannan arrested the prisoner in January 1872, the prisoner stated that he "never lived at Battersea, nor had he been there for years."
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner was addressed as Copeland by Officer Brannan and the woman who passed the bad coin was named Copeland, but he gave the name Newman at the police station.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The landlady stated on cross-examination that a man named Taylor and some women were also taken into custody at the house, and that she did not know the prisoner had let part of the house to them.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Upon being caught with stolen cloth, Alfred Claridge confessed to his manager, Charles Gordon, that he had been stealing for 'Only two or three weeks.' He stated that he had sold the stolen items to a Mr. Fellowes and provided his residence.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Gordon testified that when he asked Fellowes what he paid for a piece of blue cloth, Fellowes stated '3s. 1d.' Gordon asserted that the actual value of the piece was 6s.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: When asked by Officer Green for bills or invoices for the goods, Fellowes admitted he had none 'relative to these goods that I have bought.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Manager Gordon stated on cross-examination that Fellowes 'gave every facility for the search.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Gordon testified that company policy allowed trimmers like Claridge to buy goods for their own personal use, for which they were always given a signed ticket and an invoice.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The court transcript notes that 'Fellowes received a good character.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Victim Daniel Jaccard testified that he and the prisoner had words about the French war in a public-house. He stated the prisoner wanted to fight him and went outside three times, attempting to get him to come out.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Policeman Samuel Bond testified that the prisoner was waiting outside the public-house for an hour and a half for the victim. Bond stated the prisoner said, 'I will follow the b—'.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prisoner stated in his defense that he had nothing in his hand and did not stab Jaccard at all with a knife. He admitted only to striking him in the face, which Jaccard returned.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Policeman John Watts, who arrested the prisoner, testified that at the station the prisoner said 'I did it, and if I can get hold of him I would murder him.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Police Sergeant William Peck testified that he searched King Street the following morning and found a knife in the area of the restaurant where the prisoner was taken into custody.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Arthur Henry Savory, a house-surgeon, testified that Jaccard had a 'punctured wound on the upper part of the left side of the chest' measuring between 1 and 1.5 inches in length.

public.game_background: Samuel Sheldrake, Thomas Wallace, and Charles Rowland were indicted for unlawfully conspiring to falsely charge John Moorhouse with feloniously receiving a stolen watch. The prosecution alleges the defendants planted a watch in Moorhouse's coat, which belonged to Wallace, and then had Sheldrake inform the police, leading to Moorhouse's wrongful arrest. The conspiracy was allegedly orchestrated in connection with a Mr. Wilson, who held a grudge against Moorhouse from a previous legal dispute.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1_wilson_motive]
Description: John Moorhouse testified that he had previously been acquitted of stealing a cistern from a Mr. Wilson. At the time of the watch incident, Moorhouse had a pending lawsuit against Wilson for false imprisonment, providing Wilson with a strong motive to retaliate.
[EVIDENCE ev3_moorhouse_discredit]
Description: During cross-examination, John Moorhouse admitted to having previously used an alias ('John Long'), dyeing his hair and whiskers, and having employed a convicted thief named Bottom.
[EVIDENCE ev5_coat_plot]
Description: Witness Charles Ludbrook testified that before the watch incident, Mr. Wilson and defendant Rowland proposed a plan to him. They suggested he leave his door open so a coat could be 'stolen' and then planted in Moorhouse's house to frame him. Ludbrook refused to participate.
[EVIDENCE ev9_suspicious_id]
Description: Sergeant McGillicuddy testified that when defendant Wallace was brought to the station to identify the watch, he exclaimed 'Yes, I know it is' from three yards away, before it was possible for him to properly see and identify it.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Frederick Vivian, a coal merchant, received a letter signed 'John Harvey' from an address at 1, Marion Villas, Dalston, asking for the price of coals. This initiated the transaction that led to the fraud.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: John Hill, the housekeeper at 61 Watling Street, testified that the office of 'Thompson & Co.' was a sham. It was barely furnished, no business was conducted, and the occupants only came to collect letters.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: John Marsden testified that John Garner asked him to go to a hotel to meet a gentleman, impersonate him, and say that he (Garner) had gone to Hull. Marsden refused the offer of payment and declined.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William Day, a publican, testified that he knew all three prisoners and William Hart, and had seen them together on more than one occasion.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Landlords testified that the defendants used multiple aliases to rent houses. Notably, William Garner rented a house as 'John Harvey' and John Garner rented another as 'James Andrews' at the address used by 'Harvey'.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Detective Peck testified that after John Garner's arrest, he went to a pub and overheard Henry Poore say, 'They can't do anything with Jack.' William Garner was also present.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: At the conclusion of the evidence, Mr. Straight, the barrister defending John Garner, stated to the court that he 'could not resist the evidence' against his client.

public.game_background: William Lake, a 24-year-old ledger-keeper and cashier, was indicted for stealing three sums of money (£200, £140, and £205 2s. 6d.) received on behalf of his masters, the firm of Newsom, Burke & Co. The prosecution alleged that Lake received cheques for these amounts, entered them in a preliminary cash-book, and then deliberately struck out the entries and omitted them from the final accounts, thereby pocketing the funds. The defense argued that the firm's accounting was in severe disarray, suggesting that errors or the actions of others could be responsible for the discrepancies.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. William either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Accountant James Baird testified that he found entries for the three specific sums (£200, £140, and £205 2s. 6d.) in the prisoner's handwriting in the rough cash-book. In each case, the entry was uniquely ruled out with two lines and omitted from the page's total, and was never carried forward into the fair cash-book or ledger.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Accountant James Baird testified that the company's books were "very much in arrear." The fair cash-book had not been balanced for months, and the rough cash-book was never balanced in a way he could understand.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: It was established during cross-examination that Mr. Burke, one of the three partners, was not present to testify, despite taking an active part in the matter, being present at police court hearings, and making entries about the received property. His absence had been questioned at previous hearings.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mr. Newsom testified that he found a large number of racing telegrams in the prisoner's desk.

public.game_background: Lewis Smith, a 22-year-old soldier, was indicted for feloniously wounding Mary Ann Webster with intent to do her grievous bodily harm. Description: Under cross-examination, Hammond admitted to being previously summoned and fined for assault on two separate occasions, once for giving a man a 'good hiding' and another for assaulting a trespasser.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Policeman George Warren testified that when he saw the prosecutor around 7 o'clock, he was 'bleeding from the eye, and apparently very weak.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Warren testified that when he went to arrest Linsey, he 'was very violent, and struck at me' and was 'stooping, apparently to pick up the poker.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Under cross-examination, Policeman Warren stated the Heath had been used by the public for 40 years until being closed by the War Department in 1867, and that a footpath existed.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Dr. Joseph Douglas, a surgeon, testified that Hammond's wounds 'might be given by a blunt instrument; a kick might have done it in both cases.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Dr. Douglas stated that the wounds healed in a few days and 'were not dangerous; they were severe scalp wounds.' He noted that scalp wounds 'invariably bleed a good deal.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Dr. Douglas testified, 'I have myself frequently gone over it, both on horseback and on foot—I have never been turned back.' He added it was a well-known shortcut.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Thomas Boughton, Hammond's employer, confirmed that Hammond 'had directions from me to prevent anybody from going across' the Heath, by order of the War Office.

Description: Sidney Smith was employed by the Park Lane Company as a clerk, accountant, and cashier from August 1868 until July 1872. [EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: During cross-examination, Busch admitted that on one occasion he himself was deficient in cash by '10s., or 1l., or something like that'. He described it as an 'accident, in the hurry of business' and claimed he discovered the error himself.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Company books showed that on several occasions, Smith was owed significant sums of money by the company (e.g., £8, £14, £24). Busch characterized this as Smith being 'culpable' for neglecting to claim money due to him.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Busch testified that an estimated £15,000 to £25,000 passed through the prisoner's hands annually. He asserted that the amount was not as high as £40,000 or £80,000.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: After Smith left the company, he started a rival establishment. At the police court, Busch denied saying that he never would have prosecuted Smith but for that fact.

[EVIDENCE ev5]

public.game_background: Michael, Lewis, and Rebecca Goldsmith were indicted, along with the fugitive Montague Goldsmith, for unlawfully conspiring to defraud jewellers Charles Drayson and Henry Charman of articles worth approximately £15,000. The scheme involved obtaining jewellery 'on approbation' under the false pretense of showing it to wealthy clients, then pawning or selling the items at low prices and using forged bills of exchange to maintain the appearance of legitimacy.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Michael, either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Charles Drayson explained his firm's business model, where jewellery was supplied to retailers like Montague Goldsmith 'on approbation.' The items were only invoiced and charged once the retailer reported a sale to a specific customer, a system based on trust.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Montague Goldsmith obtained jewellery worth thousands of pounds from Drayson and Charman by fabricating stories about wealthy customers. Key examples include a £9,000 selection for a supposed Rothschild wedding and items for a fictitious 'double wedding' in the Pakenham family.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Alfred Drayson testified that he frequently spoke to Michael Goldsmith about returning goods. Michael would repeatedly claim the items were 'out at a customer's,' preventing their return when they had likely already been sold or pawned.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: When confronted by Henry Charman, Rebecca Goldsmith confessed to selling the jewellery for low prices and forging the bills of exchange. She stated, 'I did,' and attempted to take the entire blame to exculpate her brother Montague.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Testimony from Alfred Drayson and William Harrison, comparing delivery dates with pawnbrokers' records, showed that jewellery was pawned or sold within days of being obtained. For instance, a butterfly brooch obtained on approbation on Oct 15 was pawned on Oct 17, but only charged to the account on Oct 23.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: To secure credit for a £1,650 bracelet, Montague Goldsmith gave Charles Drayson two bills of exchange purportedly accepted by Sir Sills John Gibbons. Other forged bills were also given to Mr. Charman as security.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Pawnbroker John Rowley testified that Michael Goldsmith pawned multiple items, including a French bracelet and turquoise studs, using the alias 'Michael Phillips.' This activity dated back to March 1872.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Edmund Whitfield testified that Lewis Goldsmith gave him about twenty pawn tickets and asked him to advance money to redeem the jewellery. Lewis told him the goods came from his brother and had been paid for.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The transcript states Montague Goldsmith was 'not in custody,' and Officer Davey testified that he held a warrant for his apprehension since December. Montague's flight from justice serves as evidence of his guilt.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Henry Charman testified that when he confronted Montague and demanded security, Montague asked Michael to get bills of exchange from the safe. Michael retrieved them, checked them against the bill book, and gave them to Montague, who then handed the forged bills to Charman.

Description: On May 7th, Sarah Ann Honey, a Post Office clerk, testified that William Willis purchased stamps and paid with a half-crown and a shilling. She put the half-crown in a tester and it bent, indicating it was counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Robert Frisby, the arresting officer, testified that upon searching Willis, he found two half-crowns, 1s. 6d., and a threepenny piece, all of which were good money.
[EVIDENCE ev4]

[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Sarah Ann Honey testified that the Post Office had received a quantity of bad money, some with the same date (1817) as the prisoner's half-crown, on the day previous to the incident.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: According to Philip William Blunt Burgess from the War Office, the pension commutation process requires an applicant to fill out a form stating whether they are married or single, and to list any dependent children.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The prosecution submitted a marriage certificate for William Berry and Emily Charlotte Hughes, dated June 6, 1858. Witness Eliza Tufnail confirmed she was present at the marriage and that the couple has three children under sixteen years of age.
[EVIDENCE ev8]

public.game_background: Austin Biron Bidwell, George Macdonnell, George Bidwell, and Edwin Noyes Hills were indicted for feloniously forging and uttering a Bill of Exchange for £1,000, with intent to defraud the Governor and Company of the Bank of England. The indictment included additional counts for other forged bills, part of a larger scheme that defrauded the bank of over £100,000.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Austin either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Austin Bidwell, using the alias F.A. Warren, was introduced to the Western Branch of the Bank of England by his tailor, Mr. Green. He opened an account with £1,200, claiming to be an American agent for sleeping cars, thereby establishing a legitimate-seeming front for the operation.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Henry Billinghurst of the London and Westminster Bank testified that the acceptance stamp and signatures on the £1,000 bill from the indictment were forgeries. This was corroborated by representatives from numerous other prestigious firms, including Rothschild & Son and Baring Brothers, who confirmed all bills bearing their acceptance were counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Witnesses from Birmingham identified George Bidwell staying at the Queen's Hotel in February 1873. They testified to seeing him collect mail from the post office and register a letter for America, activities that coincided precisely with the period when forged bills were being mailed from Birmingham to the Bank of England. One witness also saw him with Hills.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Bill brokers from Amsterdam and Rotterdam identified George Bidwell (as Gilbert) and George Macdonnell purchasing genuine bills of exchange. These bills, including some on the same banks that were later forged, were made payable to the alias 'F. A. Warren,' providing the conspirators with authentic models to copy for their forgeries.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Multiple engravers and printers from London identified George Bidwell (using aliases like Brooks and Nelson) commissioning the tools of the fraud. He ordered blank bill forms, endorsing stamps, and engraved plates that mimicked those of various banks, including the specific stamp used on the indicted £1,000 bill.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Handwriting expert Charles Chabot testified that the endorsement 'F. A. Warren' on the indicted bill was written by Austin Bidwell. He also identified George Bidwell's handwriting on the letters from Birmingham and the bodies of the withdrawal cheques, and Macdonnell's handwriting on the body of the forged bills.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Helen Vernon, who was living with George Bidwell, testified to the close and collaborative relationship between all four defendants. She confirmed they used Christian names with each other (Austin, George, Mac, and Ed), and that Hills was introduced to her under the alias 'Mr. Howe' when they all dined together on Christmas Day.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The train accident in France involving Austin Bidwell in January 1873 was confirmed by multiple witnesses. He appeared injured afterward and used the story of the accident in his dealings with Rothschild's bank in Paris to help secure the genuine £4,500 bill that bolstered his credibility with the Bank of England.

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Giffard testified that Williams, whom she knew through her husband, offered to sell her counterfeit coins and she arranged to buy ten half-crowns from him for 3s. 4d.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: At the moment of arrest, Williams shouted, "Call a policeman! this woman has got half a load of bad money!"
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: At the police station, Williams voluntarily handed two additional packets of counterfeit coins (eight florins and eleven half-crowns) from his pocket, stating, "Here is the other stuff because I know you will find it."
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: An expert from the Mint, William Webster, testified that the coins sold to Giffard and the coins found in Williams' pocket were not only counterfeit but were made from the same moulds.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In his formal statement, Williams claimed Giffard had asked him to sell some counterfeit coins for her, as she was 'hard-up'. He alleged she must have planted the coins and the marked money on him in the pub.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Giffard testified that her husband had previously received counterfeit coin from Williams in her presence, establishing him as a known dealer.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Vernon La Motte, a French wine maker, testified that in 1872 he sold and sent 100 cases of his lowest quality sparkling wine, at 15 francs per dozen, to the defendant, Paul Bayard.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Thomas John Ryan, a wharf superintendent, confirmed that 100 cases of wine from the ship Stannington, bearing the invoice mark 'B R', were consigned to P. Bayard. Warrants for their collection were made out to Bayard's order and signed 'P. Bayard'.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Alfred Lightely, the UK agent for Moet & Chandon, examined the wine sold by Bayard. He confirmed it was not genuine Moet & Chandon's wine but was Saumer wine, the labels were forgeries, and the corks were imitation brands. The genuine trade price was 57s 6d a dozen.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Joseph Joanny testified that a man named Le Blanc rented a kitchen from him where bottling machines were kept. He saw Bayard there on one occasion with a truck, taking four cases of 'champagne' which Bayard claimed he had bought from Le Blanc.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Rosina Pone, who rented a cellar to Le Blanc, testified that she saw Bayard there once with Le Blanc. She overheard Bayard say to Le Blanc that the cellar was 'not lightsome enough for his business.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Officer Henry Dawson found a large amount of forgery equipment in the cellars rented by Le Blanc. This included bottling machines, fake labels for numerous wine firms, used corks with La Motte's name, and stamps and dies for branding 'Moet & Chandon' onto cases and corks.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Samuel Read testified that he assisted Le Blanc in a cellar on a Sunday. He described scraping old labels from bottles in ten cases of wine, after which Le Blanc re-corked them with new corks branded 'Moet & Chandon'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Detective Druscovitch testified that he arrested Bayard in Antwerp, where he was working as a manager for a conjuror. When informed of the charge, Bayard stated, 'I have sold the wine and that is all I have done.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Epaminidas Valeriani testified that he had employed Le Blanc to change the labels and corks of 'Justinet' wine to 'Moet & Chandon' at his own premises, and that this was done with his knowledge and consent. He said this operation was unrelated to the one at Portland Street.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The defense counsel, Mr. Williams, submitted to the court that there was no case against the prisoner, arguing that 'the whole of the evidence pointing solely to Le Blanc.'

public.game_background: William Browning was indicted for wilful and corrupt perjury. In a prior assault case against Sarah Legust, Browning testified that on Whit Monday, he saw the original complainant, Sarah Cronin, 'streaming with blood' and crying for help, blaming her husband for the injury. The prosecution alleges this testimony was false, and that Cronin's injury was actually inflicted by Sarah Legust on the following Wednesday.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Pyer, chief clerk at the Thames Police Court, read from his notes Browning's original testimony. Browning stated that on Whit Monday at 11:30 PM, he saw Sarah Cronin 'streaming with blood from a cut of the eye' and heard her vow vengeance against her husband for doing it.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Sarah Cronin testified that on Wednesday evening, May 27th, Sarah Legust struck her on the eyebrow with a street door key, causing a cut that bled very much. She stated it is not true that she was bleeding or cried for help on the preceding Whit Monday, though she did have a disturbance with her husband.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Matilda Watts, a neighbor living opposite the Cronins, testified that on Whit Monday around midnight, she heard a person she identified as Sarah Cronin screaming 'Murder and police'. She stated Cronin claimed her husband was beating her, but she did not see any blood on her face as it was too dark.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Sarah Pittendreigh testified that the prisoner, her sister, separated from her husband, Mr. Studholme, in July 1873. This establishes the initial family conflict that precedes the alleged libels.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Pittendreigh testified to receiving numerous letters from the prisoner, sometimes two a day for months. She identified a letter from Dec. 31st as being in the prisoner's handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Under cross-examination, Pittendreigh was made to admit that she had previously been a witness in several court cases and had once been charged with stealing a watch, of which she was 'honourably acquitted'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mr. Besley, counsel for the defense, declined to address the jury. The transcript notes it was 'as he could not justify the libels.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Despite the evidence and the Recorder's direction, the jury found the defendant NOT GUILTY.

public.game_background: Henry Brannan, a police sergeant, was indicted for wilful and corrupt perjury based on his inconsistent testimony in the case against Thomas Parrock and Mary Ann Cook for the theft of a portmanteau. The prosecution alleges Brannan fabricated evidence to secure a conviction, while the defense claims his conflicting statements were due to confusion and stress, not malicious intent.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Henry either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
