information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: In his first deposition on Feb 19th, Sergeant Brannan stated he found a portmanteau in an abandoned cart after seeing Thomas Parrock and Mary Ann Cook nearby. He claimed they returned with another man, went back and forth near the cart, and that he pursued but lost them.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Thomas Parrock, the man Brannan accused, testified he was with his friend George Richmond on the night in question. He stated they passed the cart and spoke innocently with officers Young and Hannah, and that he did not touch the cart or associate with Mary Ann Cook.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Caroline Charlwood, the woman with Parrock that night, testified that Brannan took her to his solicitor and attempted to suborn her testimony, asking her to falsely swear that she saw Parrock get up on the wheel of the cart and touch the portmanteau.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Officers Young and Hannah testified that Brannan stated he wanted to take charge of the case because 'this portmanteau belongs to a big man, a Member of Parliament,' and an inspector might 'do you out of it.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Inspector Jonas Hole testified that Brannan bore the character of a 'diligent, careful, and truthful officer.' He added that Brannan had just returned to duty after being on the sick list for 30 days due to 'worry and grief for the loss of his father.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The court clerk testified that in the original case, Magistrate Knox discharged Parrock, stating the non-production of the jemmy and knife was 'conclusive to his mind.' Mr. Knox then offered Parrock the process to file a perjury summons against Brannan.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Defense witness John Hornblow testified that he saw the cart on the night in question and observed a policeman, whom he identified as Brannan, concealed in a doorway watching it. Hornblow also saw two other suspicious-looking men approach the cart.

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: When Hussey returned the horse, the prisoner initially offered only £6 or £7 back. Later, his associate offered to return £20 of the £25.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: When Hussey gave the prisoner into custody, the responding policeman stated, 'It is not Conolly, his name is King-well, I know him well.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: During cross-examination, Hussey admitted, 'I should have had nothing to complain of if he had returned the 25 l.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Frederick Kingwell Sr. also testified that the signature 'Conolly' on the receipt was 'not a bit similar' to his son's handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev14]

[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Thomas Tanner testified that the prisoner offered the horse on a week's trial, with the understanding that if it was not liked, it could be returned and the money paid back.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Joseph Evans, a job-master, testified that he had driven the mare regularly and found her 'perfectly quiet in harness, and goes very well indeed.' He valued her at about thirty guineas.

[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: George Harding Field, a civilian, testified that on the morning of November 19th, he saw Rogers with the roan mare and also found the stolen cart hidden near a shed with its harness inside. He added that two other men approached and referred to the hidden cart as a 'plant'.

[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Charles Mat and Samuel Trevor testified that Roberts was with them on the afternoon of November 19th, from about 2:40 PM until 6:20 PM, traveling to Peckham.

Description: Ann Cox, a neighboring shopkeeper, testified that she received the florin from the first transaction for change, discovered it was bad, and returned it to the prosecutrix's shop shortly after.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Herbert Poole testified that the defendant Murphy approached him, stating that a conductor's pay was insufficient and offered to show him how to supplement it. Murphy allegedly said Poole should give the driver, Bridegroom, 'a dollar a day' and could keep 'four or five hog or bob' for himself.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Poole testified that Bridegroom, angry at the high takings being paid in, publicly accosted him in the Earl Lonsdale pub in front of other omnibus men. Bridegroom allegedly shouted, 'You will get all the b----lot of the men sacked,' after which other men threatened Poole.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Road inspector Joseph Wild testified that on the morning of January 14th, Poole came to his office and made a complaint. Wild described Poole as being 'in rather an excited state'.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In his cross-examination, Poole admitted to having been previously charged with perjury at Taunton, though the bill was ultimately ignored. He also admitted to being known as 'The hedge lawyer' and switching political allegiances after a payment dispute.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Defense witness John Forrester, a shoeblack, testified that Murphy was not on the omnibus when it arrived at the Lonsdale pub for the first journey. He stated he would have seen Murphy if he had been there.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Frank Morgan, a waiter at the Lonsdale, testified that he heard Poole say he had made a 'pretty fine mistake' by putting down fourpences instead of twopences. He also said Poole counted his money and said he was short because he forgot to account for his wages.

public.game_background: William Mackay was indicted for wilful and corrupt perjury. He had previously testified under oath that he was robbed at the Halifax Arms public house by William Furze, at the direction of the proprietor, Charles Davis. The prosecution alleges that this testimony was intentionally false, fabricated as part of a scheme to extort money from Davis.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Bugby, a police sergeant, testified that on February 2nd, William Mackay came to the station and signed a charge sheet accusing Charles Davis of being concerned with William Furze in stealing £5 and valuable papers from his coat pocket.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: James Towsey, lessee of the Rose Tavern, testified that he heard Mackay swear in court that at the Halifax Arms, he heard Davis tell Furze 'Go round and get a card out of him, he has some quids,' after which Furze put a hand in his pocket and he missed his pocket-book and £6.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: James Towsey testified that on the way to the police station, Mackay threatened that he would 'b---- up the license at the Halifax Arms unless his friends came down in the morning with 150 quid.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Henry Payne testified that Mackay was drunk and disruptive, and that he and Davis had to call two policemen to remove him from the premises around 12:25 am.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William Furze, the man Mackay accused of physically robbing him, had a prior conviction for which he served seven years' penal servitude.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In a letter written after the perjury summons was issued, Mackay stated: 'I hereby withdraw from both my actions... with regard to the charge I made against Mr. Davis, I entirely exonerate him from the same.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: James Towsey, a key prosecution witness, formerly owned the Halifax Arms and sold it to Davis. He had known Davis for several years and was interested in transferring the house to him.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Charles Davis admitted to having been tried for manslaughter (though acquitted) and being imprisoned for attempted theft twelve years ago. He was also a professional pugilist.

public.game_background: Thomas Kirwin was indicted for feloniously wounding Police Constable Robert Clapp with intent to murder, prevent lawful apprehension, or do grievous bodily harm. The incident occurred during an alleged burglary at a factory in Bow, where PC Clapp confronted two men, one of whom he identified as the prisoner, leading to a violent struggle.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Police Constable Robert Clapp testified that he heard a warehouse door being forced, looked through a window, and saw two men inside, one of whom he recognized as the prisoner, Thomas Kirwin. Clapp stated he knew Kirwin's appearance from having seen him more than twelve times in 1870 or 1871.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The factory foreman, Joseph Schmidt, testified that when he showed the prisoner the signs of the break-in, Kirwin said, 'I dreamt there was something wrong in the factory, I could not sleep after two o'clock.' The break-in occurred around 2 a.m.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Detective James Howlett testified that the prisoner told him to investigate the saw mills next door, stating, 'I believe that was made there,' referring to the jemmy used in the assault. Kirwin did not work at the saw mills.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Both the factory clerk and the foreman testified that the prisoner, Kirwin, was the only man employed on the premises who wore a cheese-cutter style cap.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: A detective found a bottle of iodine on Kirwin's person upon his arrest. Kirwin gave a confused explanation, first saying he bought it from a doctor, then saying a woman upstairs gave it to him.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Christiana Bakes, who lived with the prisoner, provided an alibi, stating that Kirwin was in bed with her and their two children the entire night of the burglary and did not leave.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Martha Stent, a co-worker, testified that she had given Kirwin the bottle of iodine paint three weeks before the incident to treat his rheumatism.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Robert Waite testified that the prisoner had complained to him of rheumatism on the Sunday before the incident. Waite also stated that he gave Kirwin some 'horse oil' on the Thursday after the incident, which he said could blister a horse in twenty minutes.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Several witnesses, including the prisoner's brother who was a railway guard, testified to the prisoner's good character as an honest, respectable, and hard-working man with no prior charges against him.

The defense argued that a clerk in Ault's employ, named Shown, was responsible for the forgeries.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Court clerk Charles Coulthery testified that his office received two irregular affidavits from the defendant's agency. One, affidavit B, was returned for initials to be added to alterations. A substitute affidavit, A, was sent for the other, and affidavit B was returned with what appeared to be forged initials.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: John Comfort Bennett testified that the signature 'J. C. Bennett' on affidavit A was not his writing. He confirmed he had sworn a different, legitimate affidavit related to the same debt.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Henry Albert Lovett, a commissioner for oaths, testified that the signature 'H. A. Lovett' on affidavit A was not his, although it was a 'close imitation.' He confirmed his genuine signature was on affidavit B.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Handwriting expert Charles Chabot testified with confidence that the body of affidavit A and the forged signature of 'J. C. Bennett' were written by the same hand that wrote letters Ault admitted were his.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Lovett, the commissioner whose signature was forged, testified that while he was 'inclined to think' the body of affidavit A was Ault's writing, he had 'some little doubt' and was also 'inclined to think' the forged Bennett signature was not Ault's writing.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Hubert Charles Holland, a merchant who did business with Ault, testified that the signature 'Bennett' was 'decidedly not his writing' and that the commissioner's signature was also not Ault's writing.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Ault proactively went to the court to discuss the affidavits after hearing about an issue from Mr. Lovett, before he could have received the court's official letter. He stated, 'it is a serious business for me.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The prisoner received a good character. Multiple witnesses, including court clerk Charles Coulthery, testified that Mr. Ault had borne a 'very respectable character' for many years. Furthermore, clients J.C. Bennett, F.B. Guerrin, and H.C. Holland all stated their firms continued to employ him even after his committal.

Description: Policeman George Cheeseman testified that on August 25th, he was 15 yards from the prisoner and saw him stoop, pick up a stone about the size of an egg, and throw it at a passing passenger train.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: John Beare, the station-master at Willesden Junction, testified that the train was the Liverpool and Manchester express and that there were 200 passengers on board at the time.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On September 11, 1875, Henry Wainwright was arrested while transporting two parcels containing the dismembered and decomposed remains of a female body. His associate, Alfred Stokes, who was helping carry the parcels, discovered their contents and alerted the police.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Police Inspector Matthew Fox searched Henry Wainwright's business premises at 215 Whitechapel Road and discovered a grave, 5 feet long and 2 feet deep, dug under the floorboards. The earth in the grave was heavily mixed with chloride of lime and contained light-colored human hair matching the victim's.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Harriet Louisa Lane, who was in a long-term relationship with Henry Wainwright (known to her as 'Percy King'), was last seen alive on Friday, September 11, 1874, the day after the tools and lime were procured.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Medical examination determined the cause of death was two gunshot wounds to the head and a severe cut to the throat. Surgeons testified that either injury would have been fatal and that the wounds could not have been self-inflicted.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edward Pallett, a gatekeeper and father of the main witness, testified that defendant Joseph Colleman approached him asking about his employer, Mr. Ellis, and offered him money for information regarding chemical colours for some 'Frenchmen'.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Ellis testified that defendant Bobrownicki had previously worked at the factory in 1872, where he tried and failed to develop a patent. During that time, Bobrownicki was aware of the experiment book and had even made an entry in it himself.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Ellis testified that the book was of 'very great value' to him, containing the results of his research, trade secrets, and the groundwork for future patents.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In summing up, the Common Serjeant instructed the jury that the 'real question was whether they intended permanently to deprive the owner of the possession of the book,' likening the situation to a schoolboy borrowing a key to his lessons, which would not be larceny.

public.game_background: William Clarke was charged with two counts of unlawfully uttering counterfeit florins. The first incident involved passing a counterfeit florin to Sarah Ann Williams at the Crown pub, and the second involved passing another to William Turner at the nearby Royal Oak pub approximately twenty minutes earlier.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Sarah Ann Williams, a barmaid at the Crown pub, testified that on January 11th at about 3 p.m., the prisoner William Clarke handed her a florin to pay for gin. She immediately identified it as counterfeit and told him so.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: William Turner, landlord of the Royal Oak pub, testified that about 20 minutes before the first incident (at 2:40 p.m.), the prisoner bought cigars from him and paid with a florin which he suspected was counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Upon searching the prisoner, Policeman Hobbs found 2 shillings and 1 penny in good money.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: William Webster, an Inspector of Coin for Her Majesty's Mint, provided expert testimony that both florins recovered from the two pubs were counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The two pubs, the Crown and the Royal Oak, are located only a quarter of a mile apart, and the two uttering incidents occurred within twenty minutes of each other.

public.game_background: Samuel Charles Phillips and Isaac Cohen were indicted for unlawfully conspiring to obtain a valuable Sevres china dessert service from Matthew Vehovar by means of false pretenses. The prosecution alleged that Phillips, with Cohen's assistance, deceived Vehovar into exchanging the china for paintings of little value by fabricating a story about his wealth and noble connections. The defense argued that it was a legitimate, if poor, business transaction and that Cohen was an unwitting servant merely following orders.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Samuel either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Defendant Phillips approached Matthew Vehovar, presenting himself as a connoisseur and a distinguished person, the son of a lord, to gain Vehovar's trust and see the valuable china service.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Phillips claimed the six paintings offered as a 'guarantee' had cost him £10,000 and were by famous old masters like Poussin and Salvator Rosa.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The deposition of Mr. Edward Lumley stated he had sold the entire contents of the chambers, including the six paintings, to Phillips for £350. An inventory valued the paintings themselves at only £50.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: On September 22nd, Phillips had Vehovar sign a receipt stating the 49 pieces of china were received 'in exchange for six old paintings, my property.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The day after receiving the china, Phillips disappeared from his Albany apartment. The housekeeper told Vehovar he was out of town, and Vehovar was unable to gain entry.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: That same evening, Cohen delivered all six paintings to Vehovar's hotel, claiming a 'lord' had arrived unexpectedly and that Phillips wanted Vehovar to have them immediately for a viewing the next morning.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Christian Macle, a waiter at Vehovar's hotel, corroborated the conversation, testifying that Cohen said 'the prince came and master sent me away with the pictures directly'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: When confronted after Phillips's disappearance, Cohen first claimed not to know where his master was, and later read a letter to Vehovar stating Phillips was at a hunting party in Beckenham.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Multiple witnesses, including Vehovar, Mr. Lumley, Phillips's father, and others, described Cohen as Phillips's servant, errand boy, or acting in a menial capacity since he was a youth.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: On cross-examination, Vehovar admitted to pawning multiple pieces of the Prince's china collection on several occasions to raise money for his personal expenses.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Vehovar admitted to having been declared bankrupt in Vienna in 1864.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Court records showed Phillips had filed for liquidation in June 1874, with debts of £6,266 and assets of only £350.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On July 26th, the prisoner, Albert Richardson, approached pawnbroker Robert Percy Attenborough to borrow money, offering forty-three sable skins, a gold watch, and a ring as collateral.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Maurice Davis of 'W. Davis Co.' testified that the invoice was one of his firm's printed forms but that the transaction described was fabricated and the writing was not from anyone in his firm.

public.game_background: Robert John Young was indicted for wilful and corrupt perjury, based on his previous testimony in which he accused William Pilley of directing him to steal two boxes of brass bands belonging to a Mr. Warren, and then gifting them to him.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Robert either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The deposition by Robert Young upon which the perjury charge is based. It states that William Pilley directed him to secretly remove two boxes containing brass bands from a property and later gave them to him as a gift, saying he considered it giving him £5.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Testimony from William Pilley that he had recently evicted the defendant, Robert Young, from premises he had allowed Young to occupy rent-free for nearly two years.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Pilley testified that he gave permission for a Mr. Benson, the former leaseholder, to store some fixtures in his stable. He stated that Mr. Benson, not he, gave the defendant, Young, instructions to move the items.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: William Pilley stated he was completely unaware of the two boxes of brass bands until he received a letter about them from their owner, Mr. Warren, a week after the removal.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Pilley testified that upon receiving Warren's letter, he confronted Young, who eventually admitted taking the boxes. Pilley claims he immediately ordered Young to return them to Mr. Warren and offered his own pony and cart for the purpose.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Pilley testified that he saw the boxes at his home for the first time that evening and again told Young, 'Now, see that they are delivered safely to Warren.' He explicitly denied ever making a gift of the boxes to Young.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Holton testified that after leaving with the boxes, Young said, 'Blow taking them to Mr. Warren's that night,' and proceeded to take the boxes to his own house instead.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: William Pilley testified that in late November, he saw two men carrying the boxes near Young's shop. He confronted a drunken Young, who admitted they were going to a scrap dealer's (McMillan's) but promised they would be returned the next morning.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Defense witness James Wilmot McMillan testified that he bought the boxes of brass bands from Young. He stated that when Pilley arrived on the scene, Pilley said of the boxes, 'they are mine'.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Alfred Westoby, the new tenant of the Buttesland Street property, testified for the defense. He stated that he saw the boxes on the premises and that Pilley also saw them, knew they contained brass bands, and was told they belonged to Mr. Warren before they were removed.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Henry Thomas Warren, the owner of the brass bands, testified that he wrote four or five letters and called on Pilley several times about the missing goods. He stated that Pilley never told him that Young had taken the boxes.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Under cross-examination, William Pilley admitted to being involved in a previous legal action (Saint against Lockyear) which involved an affidavit he made and some fixtures on a property.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Robert Pearce, a solicitor, testified for the defense that he 'would not believe Mr. Pilley upon his oath.' He stated Pilley had filed a bill against his firm and actions against him personally.

public.game_background: Thomas Beavis was indicted for unlawfully possessing thirteen counterfeit florins, ten counterfeit crowns, and other counterfeit coins, with the intent to utter them. The charges stem from two separate incidents of him passing bad florins at a public house, leading to his arrest and the discovery of a large quantity of counterfeit currency on his person.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Thomas Beavis either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Webster, an inspector of coin for the Mint, testified that the florin uttered on April 24th, the piece of the broken florin, and the florin passed on the previous Thursday were all counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The Mint inspector, William Webster, further testified that eight of the thirteen counterfeit florins found on Beavis were made from the same mould as the florin passed on the previous Thursday.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: In his statement, the prisoner alleged that he received the coins from people he accidentally met and that his intention was to bring them to justice.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: After being found guilty, the prisoner pleaded guilty to a previous conviction of felony in March, 1871.

public.game_background: James William Nutt and Carl Ferdinand Nikell were indicted for unlawfully conspiring to obstruct the course of justice by swearing false affidavits. The affidavits claimed they attempted to serve writs on Charles Edward Bannister at his father's house in Willesden on November 20th, 1875. They swore they saw Bannister there, whom Nutt knew personally, but were violently ejected by his father, making personal service unsafe. The prosecution sought to prove the affidavit was perjured, while the defense maintained its contents were true.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. James either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: An official clerk produced two affidavits sworn by both defendants. The affidavits stated that on November 20th, 1875, they went to Buckingham Villas to serve writs on Charles Edward Bannister, who was personally known to Nutt. They claimed to have seen Bannister, but his father denied his presence, took them by the neck, and ejected them with violence, making further service unsafe.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Samuel Weymouth Hopwood, the solicitor who administered the oaths, confirmed both defendants swore the affidavits. He also testified that the defendant Nikell was an employee in his firm, stating, 'I entertain a very good opinion of Nikell, and but for this trial he would have remained in our service.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Sophia Green, who lived in the adjoining villa to Elisha Bannister, testified that on the evening of November 20th, she heard voices and went to her back door. She witnessed the encounter and swore on her oath that Mr. Bannister 'never put his hands upon them at all' and was not near enough to do so.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mrs. Green stated that about two weeks after the incident, Nutt came to her house and insisted Charles Bannister lived there. He claimed to have seen Charles sitting in her room on the night of November 20th, to which Mrs. Green replied it was her husband. Nutt argued, 'Oh, no, it was not, it was Charles Bannister, I know him very well.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William Harrington testified that at the Bow Street court, Nutt did not recognize Charles Bannister, prompting Harrington to point him out. Harrington also produced a note from Nutt which he interpreted as an attempt to get him to perjure himself by denying this, which read in part: 'why should I ask you to point him to me when I knew him personally'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Annie Blanche, a neighbor, testified for the defense that on the afternoon of November 20th, she saw Charles Edward Bannister at his father's property in Buckingham Villas. She specifically stated she saw him 'chopping wood in the back yard' between 2:30 and 3:00 o'clock.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: William Jeffery Stagg testified that he accompanied Nutt to Buckingham Villas on December 6th to serve a writ. He stated they saw Charles Bannister, and Nutt called out, 'Here, Charlie, I want you,' but Bannister went inside the house to avoid them.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective John Davis testified that on June 14th, he and Officer Tansey watched the church of St. Martin and saw Hawksby and Richards leave together around 7:50 a.m. He stated, 'seeing that they looked bulky I followed them.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: When Detective Davis arrested Joseph Richards later that day, he told him the charge of stealing lead from the church. Richards replied, 'You will have to prove it.' When told he was seen taking lead to Jackson's, he denied having been there.

Description: Charles Stock, the foreman at the church, testified that lead had been missed from the job where Hawksby and Richards were employed. He stated he had mentioned in the hearing of the prisoners that lead was missing.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On 13th July, Detective Osborn saw Hopkins and Stroud speak, enter the warehouse together, and after a few minutes, Hopkins came to the door, looked up and down the street, and then Stroud came out carrying a salmon openly in his right hand.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Henry Marshall testified that he did not give Stroud a salmon on the day in question, nor authorize him to get one.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Benjamin confirmed that a man named Dick worked in the warehouse for another tenant, though that tenant (Mr. Harris) did not sell salmon.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Benjamin testified that Stroud frequently came to the warehouse for legitimate purposes or to see Dick, and that 'there was nothing unusual in his coming.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mr. Benjamin stated that his business is strictly wholesale, they 'very seldom sell a single salmon,' and he had recently refused to sell one to Henry Marshall's son.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: When asked by the jury if he would be able to miss the stolen salmon, Mr. Benjamin replied, 'I should not have been able to miss the salmon... we are always a lot short.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The transcript notes that 'Stroud received an excellent character.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On July 12th, 1876, the defendant, John James Parland, attended a sale at Tattersall's and successfully bid on four horses, with the total price amounting to £186.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: On July 25th, Parland transferred the horses to a creditor, Mr. Calisher, as collateral for a further loan of £60. The transaction was documented as a 'sale' for £100 to protect Calisher.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The defendant's solicitor, Milton Bradford, testified that on July 21st or 22nd he called on Tattersall's solicitors, explained that Parland had ample means, and offered to pay the £186. The offer was not immediately accepted.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The defendant's solicitor, Milton Bradford, testified that Parland had significant assets, including a reversionary interest in an estate worth £20,000 and property in St. Petersburg, Russia, worth an estimated £12,000.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: At the time the cheque was written, Parland's bank account was overdrawn by £43 14s. 11d.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Parland failed to appear in court in answer to a summons issued on August 14th, leading to a warrant for his arrest on August 21st. He had left for Russia on July 25th.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Herring, a creditor, testified that he met Appleby in Paris in late November 1874. Appleby denied his identity, claiming the debtor was his uncle, and according to Herring, had 'plenty of money in his possession'.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Richard Sparkes, a clerk from the Bankruptcy Court, produced the official file. Appleby's statement of affairs, filed March 4, 1875, listed debts of £595 and assets of only £35 plus some book debts. A significant debt owed by David Collins was not mentioned.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: David Collins testified he owed Appleby £40. After the bankruptcy, Appleby instructed him not to pay the trustee, attempted to collect the money for himself, and offered to provide false receipts to show the debt had been paid.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Elijah Croucher testified that in January 1876, Appleby sold him a piano, harp, and harmonium for £20. The trustee later sued Croucher and recovered the items, establishing they were property of the bankrupt estate.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: John Murray, a relative of Appleby, testified that in October 1874, the prisoner gave him a van-load of furniture, enough to furnish two rooms.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: A pawnbroker testified that on November 14, 1874, Appleby pawned a silver service for £50. The pledge was subsequently renewed by Appleby and his wife in January and June 1875, after the bankruptcy.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: John Reeves testified that he purchased Appleby's business and goodwill for £750 in October-November 1874. Payments included several bills of exchange, with some accepted as late as November 16th and 17th.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: On cross-examination, William Herring confirmed that the trustee had agreed to pay Mr. Budget a 20% commission for information leading to the recovery of assets, and that about £70 had been paid to him.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Thomas Chambers, a solicitor's clerk, testified that his firm obtained a judgment against Appleby on November 17, 1874. When they attempted to levy execution, they found the sheriff was already in possession for another creditor.

public.game_background: Charles De Chastelaine and Nathan Wetherall were indicted for unlawfully obtaining a valuable security (a partnership agreement) from William Le Hunt Doyle by false pretences and conspiracy. The prosecution's position is that the defendants, along with the deceased Sir Edward Cunningham, deliberately exploited Mr. Doyle's chronic alcoholism to induce him to sign an agreement to join their wine business, with the intent to defraud him of his money.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Charles either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Doyle's solicitor, Mr. Mitton, testified that in his professional opinion, Doyle was 'a man that I considered incapable of entering into any engagement whatever'. He described receiving evasive replies from Wetherall when attempting to see the partnership agreement.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Court records showed De Chastelaine and Sir Edward Cunningham were undischarged bankrupts with no assets, and Wetherall had a history of financial difficulties including a past liquidation.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: A creditor, James Homan, testified that De Chastelaine boasted of partnering with 'a wealthy gentleman with capital of the name of Doyle... and he can suck every penny of it if he chooses.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Dr. Thorn, who had treated Doyle, testified that his patient's 'usual condition was one of intoxication' and that alcohol rendered him 'excessively stupid' and mentally impaired, even when he could still walk about.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The judge, in his summary to the jury, stated the central question was whether the defendants took advantage of Doyle's drunken state to induce him to sign the document, knowing he was unaware of its nature and liability.

public.game_background: James Stanford Scott was indicted for willful and corrupt perjury. The charge stems from his testimony in a civil case (Scott v. Wood), where he allegedly falsely swore under oath that he had not mortgaged or disposed of his interest in certain properties, denied executing deeds to that effect, and denied employing the solicitors Messrs. Overton and Hughes in these matters.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Testimony from James Towell, a shorthand writer, who stated that at the trial of Scott v. Wood, the prisoner James Scott was sworn in and systematically denied his signature on every deed and document placed before him. Scott did this despite being repeatedly cautioned by counsel that witnesses who saw him sign were present in court.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The defense moved to quash the indictment on the grounds that it did not allege that the matters Scott swore to were material to the issue in the original civil case. The Recorder declined to quash the indictment but noted the matter was on the record for potential appeal.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The defense submitted that there was no case to answer due to the absence of the original writ from the civil trial. They argued that without it, there was no proof of what matter was actually at issue before the Vice-Chancellor, making it impossible to prove perjury.

Description: William Murphy testified that his mother came home, beat him with her hand because he was asleep, and threw him against the door before ordering him to bed.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: In his cross-examination, William Murphy stated that when his mother attacked him, she was 'very drunk'.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Murphy testified that his mother had complained about him taking things and had beaten him for it that day, prior to using the poker. He mentioned stealing a shilling.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Witness Sarah Hazlehurst stated she found the boy on a doorstep three days later, trembling with cold. Upon examining him, she found 'both burns and bruises' and took him to the police.

Description: Medical witnesses testified that the child was not badly nourished and did not appear to be in want of food.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Policeman Taylor testified that on the road to the station, the prisoner also said, 'I don't care as long as I can get rid of them'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Florence Rennison, a neighbor living in the same house, testified 'I have heard the boy crying, and heard him say "You burn me more than you do Louey mother."'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry James Page testified that he and his wife passed four young men, identifying the prisoners Uwins and Patten as two of them.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: After getting up, Mr. Page chased and caught up to Uwins, who was wearing Page's hat. Page took the hat off Uwins's head and placed it on his own.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: While trying to assist her husband, Mrs. Page heard money rattle on the pavement but could not see their hands.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mrs. Page stated she followed the two prisoners, holding both their coats and calling 'Police', and that Patten said 'Let us run off'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Martha Richards claimed that during the scuffle, she was punched in the mouth by Uwins, and a tooth was knocked out.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Under cross-examination, Martha Richards stated, 'I did not see the little dark man shove Patten, but I saw him pick up some money and run off'.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Mr. Faulkener also testified that he himself was struck by one of the men in the group who was not in custody.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Before the magistrate, John Faulkener stated, 'I did not see Patten strike Page,' though he maintained Patten was one of the persons on Page.
[EVIDENCE ev19]
Description: Police Constable David Stapley testified that when the prisoners were charged, no money was found on either of them.
[EVIDENCE ev20]
Description: Charles Arnold, a witness for the defense, testified that he stopped Uwins as he was running because a woman was shouting 'Stop him!'.
[EVIDENCE ev22]
Description: Due to Mr. Page's conflicting statements, Mr. Arnold testified that he said to Page, 'I believe you are lying'.

public.game_background: Louis Staunton, Patrick Staunton, Elizabeth Staunton, and Alice Rhodes were indicted for the wilful murder of Harriet Staunton, wife of Louis Staunton. The prosecution alleged that the defendants systematically starved and neglected her to death, motivated by her fortune and Louis Staunton's affair with Alice Rhodes. The defense argued that her death resulted from natural causes, such as tubercular meningitis, and not from any malicious neglect.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Before her marriage, Harriet Staunton was described by her mother as simple-minded but in good general health, well-dressed, and clean. She was possessed of a fortune of approximately £3,000.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Louis Staunton and his wife Alice Rhodes were engaged in an affair. The servant, Clara Brown, discovered a letter from Louis to Alice which said, 'there will be a time when Harriett is out of the way, when we shall be happy together.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The nurse, Ellen Gooding, testified that Harriet was unconscious until her death. When she told Louis and Elizabeth Staunton that Harriet was dying and asked if they wanted to see her, Elizabeth replied 'Do not ask him, nurse, you worry him so,' and neither entered the room.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The defendants' servant, Clara Brown, admitted in court that her initial exculpatory testimony before the Coroner was false. She stated that the prisoners had instructed her on what to say to conceal their guilt.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The defendants gave depositions to the Coroner which were read in court. They claimed Harriet was in 'perfect health' until a few days before her removal, was a 'great eater,' well-clothed, clean, and free to come and go as she pleased. They also claimed her removal was her own idea.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Harriet's infant son died at Guy's Hospital on April 8th, just four days before she was moved to Penge. Mr. and Mrs. Patrick Staunton had taken the child there, giving a false name ('Henry Stormton') for the father.

Abel stated he signed the documents, one of which was the affidavit in question, but was never sworn to it. Abel declared the related receipt for £50 for the furniture was fictitious.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: James Frederick Dibbin, a clerk for Willshire's solicitor, testified that Willshire personally handed him the affidavits, including the one from Abel, on the morning of the interpleader summons, stating they were to be used in court that day.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Alice Gregory, Willshire's former fiancée, testified that he told her the furniture in his house was his own. He also claimed he had to move it all out to raise money to pay a £90 debt, showing a clear motive to protect it from seizure.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Frederick Saunders Herbert, solicitor for Willshire's ex-wife, confirmed receiving a copy of Abel's affidavit before the hearing. He stated that although the affidavits were not discussed, he had read them and "expressly requested that it should be filed."
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Samuel Nicholls, a defense witness, testified that in August 1876 he lent £25 to the defendant upon some furniture. The defendant repaid the loan in October 1876 and removed the goods.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edmund Francis, manager of the Mambré Saccharine Company, testified that in early January, he found the coal account was 'rather heavy' and the coal was 'going very fast.' This suspicion led him to secretly employ another clerk, Ryan, to check the coal quantities reported by the defendant Harrison.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Francis testified that when he compared Harrison's daily reports of skips unloaded with the quantities on Johnson's advice notes, they always corresponded. He 'never found that Harrison's weights were less than Johnson's,' which seemed suspiciously perfect.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: When questioned by Francis about the sloppily written invoices, Burton admitted that he or Johnson wrote them personally at Johnson's house. Burton claimed they 'did not wish the clerk to know the price they were selling at.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Thomas Tisdale, who lodged with Harrison, testified that Harrison admitted to him, 'I am in a mess about the coals,' and confirmed they were short. When asked if Johnson knew, Harrison reportedly said, 'he knew but he would not know.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Thomas Dickenson, a clerk for the Powell Duffryn Steam Coal Company, provided official records of coal loaded into Johnson's barges. For example, the barge 'Jack' was loaded with 49 tons 15 cwt, but Johnson's subsequent advice note to the Mambré Company for a delivery by the 'Jack' claimed 60 tons.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Harrison received a good character reference. His employer, Mr. Francis, described him as a 'hard working industrious man.' The jury, in their verdict, strongly recommended him to mercy, 'believing him to be a tool in the hands of Johnson.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: A great number of witnesses gave Johnson a good character. Richard Marfell, an agent for the coal company who had known Johnson for about ten years, stated he 'always found him honourable in his dealings' and considered him a 'respectable man.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: In cross-examination, Richard Marfell of the Powell Duffryn company admitted that they had received reports of short weights from the collieries and had made allowances for them. He recalled one dispute over a truck being 1 ton 11 cwt short, which he thought was sold to Johnson.

Description: Detective Henry Taylor arrested the defendant, Charles Cherwood, on the afternoon of March 2nd. The arrest was initially for a separate suspicion of forging letters of credit on the London Joint Stock Bank.

Description: A search of Cherwood's lodgings revealed camel-hair brushes, a small piece of sponge, and bottles containing various liquids, including one with size. These are known to be tools used for forgery.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Caroline Burgess, the landlady, confirmed Cherwood lived at her address under the alias 'Sherwood.' She testified that he left the house between 9 and 10 a.m. on March 2nd, the day the cheque was cashed.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: While in Newgate prison, Cherwood wrote a letter to the governor. In it, he claimed to be an associate of 'noted forgers' and an expert chemist, offering to establish an anti-forgery service for £5,000 per annum to prevent all professional forgeries in London.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Detective Sergeant Moss testified under cross-examination that he had searched for Mr. Cross but was unable to find him. He added that what he had heard about Cross 'would be against him.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: At the defendant's lodgings, police found a loaded seven-barrel revolver and a box of cartridges.

public.game_background: William Gattie is indicted for forging and uttering a receipt for securities with intent to defraud Colonel Fielding. His alleged accomplice, Horace Keymer, has pleaded guilty. The prosecution argues Gattie, a Bank of England clerk, conspired with Keymer to create a fictitious receipt, making it appear that Colonel Fielding's stocks were securely deposited at the bank as collateral for a loan, when they had already been sold by Keymer. The defense, supported by Keymer's testimony, claims Gattie was an unwitting pawn, believing he was merely acting as a 'nominal holder' for the securities and was unaware of the forged receipt or the underlying fraud.

public.game_background: Jean Menier was indicted for stealing jewelry and other articles from the dwelling-house of William Henry Weldon. The prosecution, led by Mrs. Georgina Weldon, alleged that Menier, who was entrusted with some responsibilities for the house while she was abroad, abused this trust by moving in, stealing, and pawning her property. The defense argued that Menier and Mrs. Weldon had an intimate business partnership, that he was authorized to pawn items on her behalf due to her financial difficulties, and that the items he took were in lieu of money owed to him from their joint ventures.

Description: Testimony from three different pawnbrokers (King, Wilson, and Cordell) confirmed that various articles identified by Mrs. Weldon as hers, including silver cutlery, china, and jewelry, were pawned in November 1877. The pawn tickets were found at Tavistock House, and Menier was identified as the one who pawned some of the items.
[EVIDENCE ev5]

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: James Bell, the possession man, testified that the prisoner was living in the house 'as man and wife' with a mistress, a Miss Nicholls. This was discovered when he was put in charge of the house on behalf of Mr. Weldon in late March.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: William Henry Weldon, the prosecutor's husband, testified that he had indeed taken steps to assess his wife's mental state. He stated, 'twelve months before I had consulted two medical men... the moment she returned to England I took steps to see as to the state of her mind.' He denied any conspiracy or malicious motive.

Description: Arthur Lamplough, a publican, testified that he owed money to Watson Ansell. A £200 bill was cancelled and replaced with a new £150 bill, which was drawn up by the defendant, Josiah Ward, on Ansell's behalf. Lamplough accepted the bill, but it had no drawer's name on it at that time.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Under cross-examination, Ansell admitted that in 1877 he owed Ward 'something like 70 l.' and that Ward had done repair work for him for which he had not yet received an account.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: It was established in cross-examination that Ansell had previously offered his creditors a composition of only 6d. in the pound. Lamplough also expressed doubt that Ansell's own signature would have been sufficient to get the bill discounted.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Ansell admitted that before bringing this criminal charge, he had first served Ward with a demand in bankruptcy to 'get the money civilly'. Furthermore, he had brought a previous charge at the police-court which was dismissed.

The defense argued that Marks was not present at the time of the fire and provided alibi witnesses.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Fireman John Hawes testified that he found two separate and non-communicating fires in the house. One fire was well alight in the second-floor front room, while another was discovered in the first-floor back room, where a bed was on fire.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Police officer Joseph Marriott testified that the prisoner, Aaron Marks, offered him and his colleague Sergeant Thick a bribe. Marks allegedly said, 'Well, it won't do you any good to put me in prison; you might as well have 7 l. or 8 l. for yourself,' later increasing the offer to £12.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: William Armstead, an assessor for the insurance company, testified that the prisoner filed a claim for goods valued at £129. Armstead, upon inspection, valued the total goods on the premises at only £60.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The prisoner provided conflicting statements to the police. He first denied returning to his house between 3-4 p.m. and the time of the fire, but later admitted he 'did come back just before half-past 5.' His alibi involving a man named Friedenberg was directly contradicted by Friedenberg.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Caroline Robinson testified that the prisoner's mother-in-law, Mrs. Kruntz, had the second key to the house that day. The prisoner initially told police there was only one key, which the servant had, before later admitting a second key existed.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: George Walton, a neighbor, testified that when he entered the burning house, he found an old lady, Rachel Kruntz, on the first floor in a 'stooping position' and carried her out. He stated she was confused but able to walk.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Rachel Kruntz testified that she did not enter the house. She claimed she saw the flame from the street, 'lost her senses,' fell down, and was carried into a neighbor's shop, and did not remember being inside No. 19 at all.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Defense witness Gersau Weissm testified he was with the prisoner in Goulston Street between 4 and 5 p.m. He stated they walked to the prisoner's house but did not go inside.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The prisoner received a good character from two men for whom he worked, William Keen and Thomas Croft. Mr. Keen stated, 'during the time I knew him I found him a very respectable, well-conducted man.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Harry Warland, a labourer outside the shop, testified he heard the noise, saw a scuffle, and witnessed the prisoner knock the waitress against a partition and shove him aside to escape. Warland gave chase and, with the help of a cabman, stopped the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Harry Warland also testified that 'another man came up with the prisoner, but did not go inside.' He stated he saw the prisoner pass by daily with another man.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Policeman Thomas Butcher testified that he arrested the prisoner after hearing cries of 'Stop thief!'. Upon searching him, he found 3s. 6d. in silver and 3d. in bronze, all good money. The prisoner was very violent during the arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Policeman Robert Dill Bell testified that the prisoner was 'very violent' at the station and, once in his cell, broke a thick plate-glass window and the seat of the closet. When asked why, he replied, 'What did you put me in here for?'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Webster, an expert from the Mint, testified that the half-crown was bad. He also stated that based on Mr. Chick's description of bending the florin, he had 'no doubt it was bad,' as a good coin would not bend in that manner.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: After the guilty verdict, the prisoner was charged with a previous conviction of a similar offence, to which he pleaded guilty.

The defense maintains that Costello was an innocent bystander who had coincidentally asked Williams for directions just before the police intervened, a claim supported by the testimony of both Williams and Adams.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prosecutor, Harry Green, testified that the four prisoners rushed him as he left a pub, threw him to the ground, and went through his pockets. He stated he was then knocked unconscious by kicks to the head and later found his money, amounting to 3 shillings and 10 pence, was missing.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: A bystander, John Smith, testified that he witnessed defendant Grimshaw knock the prosecutor down and that both Grimshaw and defendant Coombs kicked him. He added that defendant Campbell threatened him to make him leave.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Another bystander, Frederick Barry, testified that he saw defendant Campbell kick the prosecutor two or three times while he was on the ground. He also testified to seeing defendant Coombs kick him in the back.
[EVIDENCE ev6]

[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Dr. Gamale Butler, the house physician, confirmed that the prosecutor was admitted to the hospital insensible, suffering from a concussion and multiple wounds to his head and face. He could not state whether the prosecutor had been drinking.

information:

Description: Samuel Foulsham, a clerk of the peace, produces an impounded lease dated 3rd December, 1878, which purports to be signed by E. W. Alexander and John Litton. He also produces a receipt for £100, also purporting to be signed by E. W. Alexander.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: A death certificate shows that Elizabeth Whitfield Alexander died on 13th January, 1879, at the age of 67.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Ann Strange, a cousin of Mrs. Alexander, testifies that the signatures on the lease purporting to be Mrs. Alexander's are not her handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Henry Deacon Woodman, a close friend of Mrs. Alexander, testifies that he considered her to be somewhat under the prisoner's influence.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Woodman testifies that after Mrs. Alexander's funeral, the prisoner discussed her affairs but never mentioned that she had assigned the lease of the house to him.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Detective Stammers testifies that he found a notice on the prisoner from the Treasurer of the Middle Temple, dated 25th Jan 1879, calling on him to show cause why he should not be disbarred for a prior conviction of obtaining money by false pretenses.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Solicitor Sidney Smith testifies that in all correspondence, the prisoner referred to an assignment dated 23rd October, but the assignment produced in court as evidence was dated 3rd December.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Solicitor Smith testifies that when a new lease was arranged in September 1878, just a few months before the alleged assignment, it was decided it would be in Mrs. Alexander's name only, not the prisoner's.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Samuel Foulsham produces a mutilated, or torn, assignment deed that was among the documents impounded by the court.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Moir Clark testified that the defendant, Francis Thompson, was his office manager and cashier. Thompson himself designed and was solely responsible for maintaining the cash book where the discrepancy was found.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Following Thompson's dismissal, an annual audit of the company's books revealed a deficiency of £200. An investigation was launched to find the source of the discrepancy.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: An entry for a cheque in the cash book was written as £393, but the column total was correct only if the entry was £193. This created a £200 shortfall. Mr. Clark testified the alteration was in Thompson's handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mr. Clark testified that Thompson repeatedly suggested getting rid of the auditors to save money and had also requested authorization to sign cheques for the firm, which Clark declined.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Under cross-examination, Mr. Clark admitted that 'things in the office were rather in a muddle' and that delays arose in bookkeeping.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The defendant was hired after being selected by the company's own auditor, Mr. Fletcher, from a pool of 400 applicants. The transcript also notes that 'The prisoner received a good character.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: After being discharged by Mr. Clark, Thompson brought a successful action against him for wrongful dismissal, which was settled out of court. The embezzlement charge was brought after this lawsuit.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mr. Clark pointed out another instance of an incorrect entry in November, where £124 was carried forward as £324 but was later altered back to the correct sum at the end of the month.

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: James Fry testified that he paid his coal account of £2 14s. on January 26th, 1877, and received a receipt signed by the prisoner, 'C. B. Smith'. Mr. Lupton later re-examined the receipt and confirmed it was in the prisoner's handwriting.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: A letter written by the prisoner to a member of the committee of inspection was produced. In it, the prisoner wrote, 'I was unfortunately foolishly tempted to make use of some of the moneys received in payment of the coals... I throw myself at your feet and ask that you will allow me to pay back the amounts by instalments.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: During cross-examination, Mr. Lupton admitted his business was failing, with liabilities of £2,800 against assets of only £300 when he filed his petition for liquidation.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Lupton admitted that he had sometimes put money received from a customer into his own pocket, but claimed he always entered it in the books.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Lupton admitted to moving office furniture and giving some soiled neckties to a creditor just before filing his petition. The trustee, Mr. Edward Smith, described the latter act as something he would 'look on as a fraudulent preference'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: During cross-examination, Lupton denied ever telling Mr. Fry that 'if the prisoner had not given so much information to the receiver I should never have brought these proceedings'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Prosecutor Hodson Stanley, a theatrical manager, testified that he organized the 'grand dramatic ball' at the request of numerous friends in the theatrical profession, implying it was a respectable event connected to the dramatic arts.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Stanley stated he took precautions for respectability by instructing ticket agents not to sell tickets directly to ladies and hiring a former police superintendent, Limeburn, to refuse entry to known 'questionable characters'.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: At the ball, defendant Edward Ledger allegedly congratulated Stanley on its success and the 'nice gathering.' Stanley then asked Ledger to chair the supper, which he declined.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Stanley testified that the ball was orderly, with no indecency, and that he recognized no women from the 'demi-monde,' directly refuting the libel's description of the event as an 'orgy' attended by 'painted harlots'.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Stanley's letter, written to rebut the 'Cannon Ball' article in the 'Era', was delivered to the defendant's office but was never published or referenced in the newspaper.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Under cross-examination, Stanley admitted the ball was primarily for his own financial benefit, with no profits going to any dramatic charity.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Stanley admitted that a previous ball he organized at the Freemasons' Tavern was attended by many members of the 'demi-monde' because he gave complimentary tickets to attractive-looking women he saw at other balls there. The proprietor closed the doors because he 'did not like the people that were coming in.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Robert Villiers, a respected theatrical manager and a witness for the prosecution, testified that the ball was orderly. However, he also admitted to seeing 'seven or eight' women of the 'demi-monde' present.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mrs. Thorn, a defense witness from the demi-monde, testified that Stanley refused to sell her a ticket because he recognized her profession, yet she attended anyway after a gentleman gave her one.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Clement Scott, a respected dramatic critic, testified that he wrote the 'Cannon Ball' letter at Ledger's request to provide a candid review and affirmed his description was a 'perfectly accurate account' of what he saw.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Hotel manager Edward Rand testified that he had received a warning about the ball's likely 'questionable character' beforehand and, due to his concerns, referred Stanley to the hotel's solicitor before agreeing to let the room.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Famed actor Henry Irving testified to Ledger's honorable character and the 'Era's status as the 'recognized organ of the dramatic profession.' He stated the article was 'not severe enough' because it was 'infamous to connect such a calling as ours with anything so disgraceful.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Frederick William Blunt, a partner at Hawkes and Co., testified that the London agent, Henry Woollett, was first mentioned to the firm by the defendant, John Martin Wall. Wall was then sent to London to obtain references for Woollett, which the firm subsequently approved.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: One of Wall's principal duties, as described by Mr. Blunt, was to check the agents' accounts. This specifically included reconciling Woollett's daily cash diaries and weekly advice notes with the firm's cash book to ensure all collected money was paid in.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The investigation began when Mr. Wigan, a partner, opened a letter from Woollett addressed to Wall and marked 'Private.' Mr. Blunt testified that until seeing that letter, the partners had no reason to believe Wall and Woollett were on intimate terms.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Two cheques for £100 each, drawn by Woollett and payable to Wall, were discovered. Wall initially denied any financial transactions with Woollett but later admitted to having borrowed £200 from him, which he claimed was still outstanding.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Wall endorsed a cheque for £126 1s. 4d. from Woollett in the name of the firm. The partners testified that they had expressly forbidden Wall, or any other clerk, from endorsing cheques in the company's name.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mr. Blunt testified he had made inquiries at Wall's previous employer (Sedgwick's brewery) and was told Wall had been 'discharged summarily... because there was something wrong in his accounts.' This contradicted a positive written testimonial from 1875.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: After finding him guilty, the jury recommended Wall to mercy 'on account of the small benefit he received, and his previous good character.'

[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Henry Symons, the prosecutor's manager, testified that no discussion of six months' credit occurred during the initial meetings with the defendant. He stated, 'nothing had been said in our office or in the public-house about six months' credit... I never heard of the six months' credit until I heard it in the affidavit in the action.'
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Symons testified that he received £5 from the defendant's employee as a payment 'on account' for the work done. He stated he signed a document that read 'Received of Mr. Lawrence, the sum of 5 l. on account.'
[EVIDENCE def2]
Description: The defendant's original testimony was that he questioned Symons's request for £5 because 'my credit has not expired,' and that Symons explained he needed it as a personal loan to repay a friend, explicitly stating it was not a payment on account.
[EVIDENCE def5]
Description: The Defense Counsel submitted to the Recorder that the perjury allegations concerning credit and the £5 payment depended on the evidence of 'only one witness.' This questions whether the prosecution met the high standard of proof for a perjury conviction.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police Sergeant B Taylor testified that when arrested for perjury, the defendant said, 'As to the perjury, I know what they are driving at; I said no more than any other young fellow might do.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The Recorder, in summarizing the case for the jury, stated that the only two questions for them to decide were regarding the six months' credit and the payment of the £5 on account, effectively dismissing other aspects of the alleged perjury.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Messenger, the deceased's husband, testified that the prisoner, William Herbert, lived at his house for five months. On August 16th, Herbert left the house around the same time as the deceased, Jane Messenger, who left without her husband's permission.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The prisoner came to England to claim an estate he believed was worth nearly £175,000 a year for his wife's family. This was a constant subject of conversation and he was under the impression its value was enormous.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Sarah De Boo, his landlady, described the prisoner as 'strange,' noting he would 'creep about the house' without his shoes on. She thought it was 'funny for a man to do that.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Sarah De Boo testified that Mrs. Messenger told her she had found a bottle of laudanum in the prisoner's pocket. Mrs. Messenger accused him of intending to poison her, to which he simply replied 'Eh, what?'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Eyewitness John Bradley saw the prisoner and deceased in Finsbury Park. After hearing a pistol shot and seeing the woman run away, he saw the prisoner chase her, grab her shawl, and fire into her breast. As she knelt imploringly, he fired at her again from about three feet away.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Policeman Samuel Taylor testified that the prisoner stated to his cousin, 'I took a tramcar to Finsbury Park... with the full intention of doing what I have done if the interview with her was not satisfactory to me.' The statement was taken down in writing.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: A common butcher's knife, which appeared to have been recently sharpened, was found on the prisoner after he threw it from a cab window while in custody.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mark Partridge, a gunsmith, testified that the prisoner bought a cheap revolver and a box of cartridges a few days before the incident. The prisoner claimed he was going to Australia and wanted it 'only as a matter of show.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Dr. Arthur Wharry, under cross-examination, acknowledged a form of disease known as melancholia. He stated that 'a suicidal tendency is an evidence of melancholia' and that disappointment may produce it.

[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Tobacconist Lindsay Giles Till testified that on December 20th, the prisoner paid for a cigar with a counterfeit florin. When confronted, the prisoner offered another coin but then fled the shop without his change before a constable could arrive.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: On both occasions of his arrest (Dec 20th and Jan 5th), the prisoner gave his name but refused to provide his address. He stated to police that he did not want his friends to find out he had disgraced himself.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When arrested, the prisoner was found to be in possession of a significant amount of good money. On Jan 5th, he had three good half-crowns, a florin, and pennies; on Dec 20th, he had 6s. in good silver.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: William Webster, an expert witness, examined the half-crown, florin, and shilling in question and testified that all three coins were counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner's sole defense was the statement, 'I was not aware that the coins were bad.' He provided no further evidence or explanation to support this claim of ignorance.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: After the guilty verdict, the prisoner pleaded guilty to a previous conviction for a 'like offence' from October 1879. This demonstrates a prior history of committing the same type of crime.

Description: Charlotte James testified that on February 19th, the prisoner purchased one penny's worth of sweets and tendered a shilling in payment.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Webster, an inspector of coin for Her Majesty's Mint, provided expert testimony confirming that the shilling in question was counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Policeman Cavanagh testified that the prisoner, when apprehended, stated, "You have made a mistake, I have not been into your shop."
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: During cross-examination by the prisoner, Charlotte James stated that there was no other shilling on the counter.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Officer Cavanagh stated that the prisoner declared at the police station she was 'an unfortunate woman, and had no home,' and that nothing was found on her.

public.game_background: George Frazer and James Benjamin were indicted for unlawfully uttering counterfeit coin. Frazer pleaded guilty at the outset of the trial. The case proceeds against Benjamin, who is accused of acting in concert with Frazer in a scheme to pass counterfeit crown pieces in various public houses on March 9th, 1881.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. George either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Barmaid Esther Serjeant testified that after she refused a suspicious crown piece from Frazer, he returned with Benjamin. Benjamin laughed when told of her refusal, ordered a drink, paid with a good shilling, and then both men left together.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Chemist John Bailey stated that Frazer and Benjamin were in his shop at the same time, but he saw no sign of recognition pass between them.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Barmaid Margaret Denham testified that Benjamin paid with a good coin and specifically asked for a five-shilling piece (a crown) in his change, stating he wanted it 'for my young lady.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Eliza Busbridge, Sr., testified that Frazer paid her with a crown piece. About an hour later, her daughter took that same coin to give as change to Benjamin, who had specifically requested one.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Landlord John Lane testified that Benjamin asked for a crown piece. Mr. Lane jokingly offered him a known bad crown (a 'duffer') for a reduced price, explicitly stating it was counterfeit. Benjamin purchased the bad coin for 4 shillings and left immediately.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Upon his arrest, Constable David Wating found four bad crown pieces in Benjamin's possession.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Constable Warner testified that when asked for his address, Benjamin refused to provide it.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Graves testified that the prisoner, George Harris, was his cashier and manager of the books for about eight years. It was the prisoner's duty to pay cheques handed to him into the firm's account at Glyn's bank.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Graves admitted that he first discovered matters were wrong in early October but did not give the prisoner into custody until February. He stated that if Harris made restitution, he did not wish to prosecute.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mr. Graves testified that another employee, Croxford, left of his own accord when the financial issues were discovered and has not been seen since. Croxford's father also assigned a life policy as part of the restitution.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mr. Graves was questioned about giving the prisoner a character reference after dismissing him. Graves stated he did not vouch for his honesty but may have said 'he was a very clever fellow'.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mr. Graves stated that the total amount of the prisoner's defalcations amounted to £13,000, a figure arrived at after an accountant went through the books.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Following the guilty verdict, a previous conviction was proved against the prisoner from April 1872, for stealing a book from his employer.

information:

Description: George Gibbs Stark testified that the prisoner, Edmund Gridley, was employed at a salary of £200 a year. He stated Gridley had signed a letter agreeing to obey his instructions punctually in everything.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Bonthron, a baker, testified that he purchased 23 sacks of flour from Gridley at 45 shillings a sack. He stated that on March 15, 1880, he paid for this purchase by giving Gridley a cheque for £51 15s.
[EVIDENCE ev10]

Description: Caroline Palmer, a greengrocer's wife, testified that on the evening of June 4th, the prisoner Ellen Bow came to her shop to purchase two bundles of rhubarb, which cost 9 pence.

The prosecution argues the motive was to claim insurance money for fraudulently over-insured property.
