information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Robert Harris, a journalist who lived in a house opposite, testified that he was awakened by the fire. Looking through the fanlight over the shop door, he saw the flames 'were reaching up both sides of the room, forming an archway,' with a dark space in the center.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Nash had insured his household goods for £50, trade effects for £20, and stock-in-trade for £50, for a total of £120. After the fire, he made a claim on the Yorkshire Fire Insurance Company for £79.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: John Darvill, the landlord, testified that Nash was seven weeks behind on rent and also owed money for gas. Darvill had been pressing him for payment frequently, and had asked him to give up the shop portion of his tenancy just days before the fire.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Robert Harris, fireman Frank Moore, and Policeman Thomas Leech all testified to fetching the fire escape. They were adamant that Nash, who claimed in his deposition to have gone for the escape, did not assist them in any way.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Upon her arrest after the inquest, Inspector Charles Phillips testified that Annie Wright said, 'It is all through the insurance.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Fireman Charles Boulter, who examined the ruins, stated the basement under the shop was not burnt at all and that the shop's fireplace was bricked up 16 inches thick, making a fire from below or from the adjoining house impossible.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Fireman Boulter testified that the gas meter in the basement was disconnected from the supply pipe, and the cock was turned full on. He stated that while the fire's heat would melt the connecting pipe, it would not turn the cock on.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Francis Flamme, a workman employed to clear the ruins weeks after the fire, testified for the defense that he saw smoke and sparks coming down from the chimney into the basement. He claimed there was a hole in the fireplace above the bricks.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: In rebuttal, Fireman Boulter testified he re-examined the fireplace after hearing Flamme's claim. He found a hole had been made, but stated 'the mortar was quite fresh' and 'the hole appeared to have been quite freshly made,' dismissing it as the cause of the fire.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Louisa Maria Bond testified that the prisoners were at her house on the evening of the fire, leaving around 11 p.m. She stated it would take them 7 to 10 minutes to walk home.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective William Hutt received information that led him to watch the prosecutor's warehouse on August 12th. He observed George Whitehead arrive with a pony and barrow.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: At the police station, Whitehead told two officers (Hutt and Davis) that it was his 'first transaction' with the man and that he had 'never seen him before'.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: During cross-examination, Detective Hutt confirmed that Whitehead answered his questions 'perfectly frankly and without hesitation' and that the barrow had Whitehead's name on it.

public.game_background: Charles Kent, William Hayes, James Mayers, Alfred Leeson, and Thomas Robinson were indicted for obtaining money by false pretences and conspiring to defraud through purported debt-collecting agencies. Kent, Mayers, and Leeson pleaded guilty to conspiracy, while the trial proceeded against Robinson and Hayes.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Charles either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Albert Steadman, a bootmaker, testified that Thomas Robinson solicited him to join the 'London and Provincial Mercantile Society' for a fee of 25s. Believing it to be a legitimate debt collection service, Steadman paid the subscription and provided Robinson with a list of his debtors.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: John Grimes testified that he owed money to a Mr. Wybrow and was contacted by the agency. He paid Robinson 10s. in person and his wife later sent another 10s. by post office order.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Thomas Popplewell testified that when he confronted William Hayes at the agency's new office about the money collected for Wybrow, Hayes claimed he 'knew nothing of Robinson, only he had taken the business over from him.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: George William Carey, the landlord at the previous office, testified that 'while Robinson was there Hayes assisted him for about twelve months or more.' He also noted that numerous people came to the office making complaints, 'saying they were a lot of swindlers.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Edmund Bolton, a clerk at the Newgate Street building, testified that Kent and Hayes occupied an office there, first as the 'London and Manchester Mercantile Office' and later changing the name to 'Powell and Co.' He saw both men there constantly until their arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Detective Sampson Foster testified that when he visited the Newgate Street office, Hayes denied that 'Mr. Powell' (an alias for Kent) was present. Foster then heard a movement, looked behind a partition, and found Kent hiding there.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Detective Foster also testified that his inquiries revealed ten different fraudulent debt-collecting offices in the City were all connected, and that he had received complaints linking Robinson to the Newgate Street operation.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Upon his arrest, Robinson stated his books were with Mr. Kent and that if Kent did not produce them to support him, 'he should open on the lot.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Robinson, in his defense, stated that the agency was a 'bond-fide concern' as far as he knew, and that he only worked in the 'outdoor department' and was not competent to manage accounts.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Hayes, in his defense, stated that 'in all he did he was acting as clerk to Kent and afterwards to Robinson.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Numerous other tradesmen testified that they had subscribed to the 'London and Manchester Mercantile Office' (the Newgate Street operation) and never received any money collected on their behalf, showing a consistent pattern of fraud by the organization.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Frank Beaton, a licensed victualler, testified that on August 16th, George Wilson and Thomas Walwyn came into his house with another man called Captain Lee. Wilson produced a cheque book, wrote a cheque for 20l., and handed it to Captain Lee, who then left in a brougham.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: William Draper testified that he previously had an account at the bank and, upon going into liquidation, gave his pass-books and an unused cheque-book to his accountants. He stated, 'at that time Walwyn was a clerk of theirs, and I gave him the cheque-book with the other books.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Police Inspector John Tunbridge testified that when arrested, Wilson denied writing a cheque at Beaton's and claimed he had nothing to do with Walwyn or Lee there. A search of Wilson's house found a cash-box containing letters belonging to Walwyn.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police Sergeant Charles Berry testified he overheard a conversation in the gaoler's room. Wilson said to Walwyn, 'You know that when we were in Beaton's you gave Lee a blank cheque, and he filled it in.' Walwyn replied, 'I did not give Lee a blank cheque... you are as deep in the mud as I am in the mire.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Police Sergeant Alfred Brown testified that upon his arrest, Walwyn confessed, 'I have passed some of the cheques, but have not written one of them. George Wilson... is the man that wrote them, and a man named Lee was with us in Long Acre when some of them were written.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Richard Hicks, Walwyn's former landlord, testified that Walwyn had lodged with him for four years and conducted himself well during that time. He stated Walwyn would go to his employment regularly in the morning and return in the evening.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Wilson, in his defense, alleged that he was at Beaton's on his own business and that Walwyn and Lee came in. He claimed Lee asked to borrow money, was declined, and then Lee took the cheque away. He denied writing the cheque or having it in his possession.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: John Walter Clement Stone testified that on June 23rd, the prisoner, Charles Longman, called on behalf of Messrs. Oliver and Smith to collect an account. 1l. 12s. was paid to Longman, for which he gave a receipt.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Sarah Jane Wyatt stated that she paid the prisoner a total of 30s. (1l. 10s.) in advance for a headstone. The prisoner claimed he was a partner in the firm of Oliver and Smith.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Martha Baker, a widow, testified that the prisoner arranged for a monument for her husband. She paid him a total of 12l., for which he gave receipts.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Smith, a partner in the firm, stated that the prisoner's duty was to attend the office every morning and to account for all moneys he received either at night or the next morning.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Oliver testified that he later met the prisoner in Camden Town, gave him a pint of beer because he was "hard up and hungry and dry," and the prisoner said "Don't lock me up; I can't help it."
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: During cross-examination, William Smith admitted, "I have been to your house perhaps half a dozen times since this embezzlement; I don't know exactly on what business—your wife asked me to give you employment."

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Jacob Lion, the employer, testified that defendant Hall had been a foreman but left the company's employment in September after another foreman, Chandler, was dismissed. Following their departure, the men in their department went on strike.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Lion stated that after the strike began, he constantly saw the three prisoners and others outside the premises in Sun Street. He testified that on October 6th, he saw them with a crowd of nearly fifty people hooting, shouting, and hissing at him and the men during the dinner hour.

Description: Witness James Passfield testified that he saw the defendants near the shop "almost day by day" after the strike. He stated they behaved disorderly, shouting "scabs and such things" and using threatening language day after day.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In their statements before the Magistrate, the prisoners denied having used any intimidation to Harris or any one while on strike.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mary Ann Foster testified that on December 12th, a well-dressed man calling himself 'Mr. Ramsay' agreed to purchase her two prize-winning Yorkshire terriers for £60 at a dog show.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Richard Hunter, a carman, testified that on December 14th, the day after the dogs were taken, Carling sought him out at a pub, asking for a man named Bevan who buys dogs to 'send away.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: John Randall, a potman, corroborated Hunter's account, testifying he saw Carling and Hunter leave his pub together, joined by two other men, one of whom carried a 'long square basket made of brown cane.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Robert Cinderley, a barman at another pub, testified he saw Carling with three other men and a basket, from which a 'long blue-coated dog' was produced. Carling paid for the drinks.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The prisoner received a good character. Mrs. Foster testified that she had known him for nine years and he was 'respectable up to this affair.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Mrs. Foster described 'Ramsay' as having a 'full moustache and a little whisker.' Hunter described 'Jackson' as having 'whiskers, beard, and moustache.' Foster stated that Hunter's description did not 'exactly' correspond with her memory of Ramsay.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Wylde, a solicitor, testified that his client, Charles Dell, brought a successful civil action against John Swanson for a debt of £81 19s. 7d. A judgment was issued, and a writ was sent to the Sheriff to seize Swanson's property, which initiated the legal conflict over the ownership of a horse and van.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Charles Dell testified that in September, Swanson told him he had recently bought the horse for ten guineas at Stapleton's Repository and that the stables belonged to him.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Henry Seabrook, a letting agent, testified that it was defendant Swanson himself who hired the stable at the end of August.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Edward Fox testified he witnessed Swanson sign the stable rental agreement and that Samuels, when paying rent, stated he received the money from 'the governor' to pay bills.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The horse was identified by employees of the Great Eastern Railway Company (GERC) as their former property. George Bird, a foreman, testified the horse was sold at Stapleton's auction on September 8th.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: James Henry Target, a clerk for the auctioneer Stapleton, testified from records that the GERC horse was sold to defendant Swanson for £36 on December 7th or 8th.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The GERC horse was positively identified through a unique number, 4202, found cut into its hoof. GERC foremen Bird and King confirmed this was their marking system and recognized the horse by other features as well.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William Wylde testified that three cheques from Farrer to Swanson, produced at an earlier hearing, were dated late June and early July for a total of £30 2s. 6d.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The transcript notes that the 'Prisoners received good characters.'

Description: Police Constable William Hewitt testified that after he intervened in a disturbance involving young men taking an old gentleman's hat, he followed them into Nottingham Court where he was struck with a pickaxe-handle, felled to the ground, and received several more blows, rendering him insensible.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Eyewitness George Clifton testified that he saw Fitzpatrick strike PC Hewitt on the forehead with a pickaxe-handle 'a good many blows'. Clifton also heard Fitzpatrick later say, 'I hope to Jesus Christ the policeman dies.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: George Clifton testified that he saw Michael Barry throw a pickaxe-handle over people's heads, hitting PC Hewitt at the back of the head. Clifton, who had known Barry for four or five years, later identified him in a police line-up and stated he had 'no doubt whatever' it was him.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Charles Burtenshaw, a 13-year-old witness, saw Fitzpatrick hit the constable with a pickaxe-handle. He also testified that he saw Barry at the scene with sticks in his hand, giving them to his comrades, but 'did not see him do anything to the constable'.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Eyewitness Thomas Spikens testified he saw Fitzpatrick strike the constable with a stick. However, when asked about Barry, he stated, 'I did not see Barry there at all; I knew him before.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Police Constable Isaac Ward testified that upon his arrest, Fitzpatrick confessed, stating 'I struck him two or three times; I know I struck him two or three times... with a stick.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police Constable William Dodge testified that when he arrested Michael Barry, Barry stated: 'All right, I thought I should be locked up for this, but I can get witnesses to prove I am innocent. I was lying in the tap of the Black Horse drunk when this occurred.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Dr. Charles Robinson Crane, the house surgeon, testified that PC Hewitt suffered a deep wound extending to the bone, multiple bruises, and a concussion of the brain. The symptoms were 'very serious and might have proved fatal,' and the injuries were consistent with being struck with great violence by a stick.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense statement to PC Hewitt, Fitzpatrick admitted his own involvement but exonerated his co-defendant, stating: 'I own I hit him once... Barry was not there.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Defense witness Joseph Guiver testified that Barry was not at the disturbance. He claimed he saw the assault and that the stick was thrown by a man named Dempsey, who he knew by sight and who 'is a good deal like Barry in dress and everything' such that 'they might easily be mistaken'.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Defense witness Rose O'Connor, who claimed to have known Barry from infancy, testified that he was 'not in the disturbance' and 'was not there at all'. On cross-examination, she denied being drunk that night.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Henry Light testified that on the night of April 17th, his beerhouse was secured, but the next morning he discovered it had been broken into via a back window. He reported that a watch, a clock, boots, two coats, and some cash were stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Crucially, John Barnett was not in physical possession of any stolen items at the time of his arrest. All recovered property was in the bundle carried by his wife, Ellen.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The defendants, John and Ellen Barnett, are husband and wife. They lived together near the scene of the crime.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Joseph Winter, a grocer's assistant, testified that around the middle of April, the male prisoner, John Barnett, showed him a watch and said he had it for sale.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Nelly Moore, a barmaid, testified that on April 19th the prisoner paid for rum with a half-crown. She saw it was bad, and it broke when tested. When confronted, the prisoner made no reply, paid with good money, and left.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Policeman John Potter testified that when he arrested the prisoner for the first offense, he searched him and found no other money on his person.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Policeman Potter stated that upon arrest, the prisoner refused to give his name and address, saying it would take all the policeman's time to find it. At the police-court, he gave the name of Sullivan.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Alick Rickaby, another barmaid, testified that on May 20th, the prisoner purchased 11d. worth of whisky and gave her something that represented a half-sovereign. She thought it looked too bright and called her mistress.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Alick Rickaby testified that the prisoner gave the name of James Green and the address 63, Tollington Road, Holloway.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: According to Alick Rickaby, the prisoner asked for the coin back but was refused. He then stayed in the bar and was given into custody without attempting to flee.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Policeman Potter, on recall, stated that upon inquiry, nothing was known against the prisoner and that he is respectably connected.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Policeman Potter also testified that the prisoner had been out of work since August last (approximately 10 months).

[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Upon being taken into custody and having the warrant read to him, the defendant said, 'All right, I can get out of this.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mortlock testified that during the six weeks he was at the office, the defendant 'never did any business'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: James Matthews, the landlord, testified that the previous tenant, Mr. Simpson, had a business that 'fell off very much' and that 'public-house brokers have been doing very little for the last year or two.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: During cross-examination, it was revealed the cash-book contained entries for July showing 'receipts £13, expenditure £8 4s. 4d.' and for August 'receipts £8 7s., expenditure £10 1s. 3d.'

Description: Powell testified during cross-examination that the money entered in the cash book as received 'was not money received in the business, but by pawning his clothes and the money he had from me.'

Description: On Saturday, October 28th, Inspector Gillick found nine pieces of tortoiseshell in a box in the ivory storeroom. This room was under the prisoner's charge, and the tortoiseshell did not belong there.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: On Monday, October 30th, Inspector Gillick stopped the defendant and found two pieces of tortoiseshell concealed under his coat and waistcoat, held by his braces.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Catherine Ormiston, the publican's wife, testified that on the evening of December 22nd, the prisoner called for a pint of ale and gave her a half-crown, which she put in the till. Soon after, he called for another and gave her a second half-crown, which made her suspicious. Upon examination, she found both coins were bad.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: After the prisoner had left, Mrs. Ormiston searched her pocket, which contained money she had taken that evening, and found another bad half-crown and a bad florin. Her husband then went after the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Police Constable James Sullivan testified that when he located the prisoner at the Snow Shoes public-house and told him he was wanted at the brewery tap for passing bad money, the prisoner claimed he did not know the place.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Emma Thomas, landlady of the Star, testified that on December 14th, eight days prior to the main offense, the prisoner came to her house with a woman. The woman attempted to pay with a bad two-shilling piece, which Mrs. Thomas detected and returned.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Elizabeth Knight testified that about six weeks before the incident, she was in the Snow Shoes pub when the prisoner asked her to go with him to Eaton Square because "he had got six bad two-shilling pieces to change." She declined his offer.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Police officers David Rawlins and Walter Boyd testified to knowing the prisoner under the aliases John Collins (in 1875) and Charles Brown (in 1878).
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The prisoner PLEADED GUILTY to a conviction for a like offence (unlawfully uttering counterfeit coin) at the same Court in October, 1875.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Upon being searched by Constable Sullivan, the prisoner was found to have 4 shillings and 6 pence in good silver, and 1 shilling and 3 3/4 pence in bronze, all good money. No other counterfeit coins were found on his person.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: William Webster, an expert, examined the coins in question (a half-crown and two florins) and testified that they are all counterfeit.

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Upon his arrest on February 2nd, Detective William Hurst testified that Hines gave a false name, 'Andrew Palmer', and claimed to have no fixed residence.

Description: At the police station, Detective Philip Bick witnessed Hines attempt to pass a pocket-book to Hooper. Bick intercepted the item.

Description: In his defense, Hines stated, 'I am innocent. I never had a shilling benefit by Mrs. Hooper's business transactions; in fact I cannot write my own name.'

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Jordan testified that he noticed the prisoner, Patrick Morris, among the group of five or six men at the street corner just before the attack. He recognized Morris because he had seen him regularly on his way to and from work.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: According to Jordan's testimony, after he shouted 'Police!', all the attackers fled the scene except for the prisoner, Morris.

Description: Policeman Samuel Duke testified that he was on duty nearby when he heard someone shouting 'Police!', which prompted him to go to the location.

[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The prisoner stated in his defense that he had told an Inspector Holloway the names of the two men who actually committed the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The trial record contains no evidence that any of the stolen items—the watch, chain, or money—were found in the prisoner's possession upon his arrest.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective Robert Child testified that on January 23rd, he searched Zion College and discovered large quantities of stolen property concealed in multiple locations, including an attic, a dark cellar, under a trapdoor in the hall, and inside fire-buckets.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: James Larkman, a foreman scaffolder at the fire site, testified he saw Goddard inside the dangerous, restricted area on two separate mornings. On both occasions, Goddard was carrying away goods (calico and shawls) and falsely claimed to be working for the Salvage Corps.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Inspector Float testified that he overheard Goddard say to his sister, 'You know that these men have brought parcels to my place,' to which she replied, 'No, I know nothing at all about it.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: William Burland, a cabman, testified that on the evening of January 17th, he was hired to go to Zion College. There, two men—neither of whom was Goddard—loaded several large parcels into his cab and directed him to drop off the parcels near the homes of policemen Johnson and Batchelor.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Under cross-examination, Sergeant Batchelor admitted to a disreputable incident where he got a young barmaid, Ellen Morrison, drunk and then lied to her parents about it. He confessed to them, 'I was drunk, or I should not have done it,' and begged them not to report him.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Sergeant Batchelor also gave contradictory testimony regarding three umbrellas, suspected to be from the fire, which he left at a public-house. Witnesses contradicted his claim that he had merely 'lent' them, suggesting he gave them away and that a mysterious parcel was also involved.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Detective Child stated that the college windows looking into Philip Lane had a space of only 5.75 inches. He testified it would be impossible to get the larger parcels, like the calico and winsey, through the windows.

information:

Description: Louisa Bult, a clerk at the Hatton Garden Post-office, testified that on May 1st, the prisoner Jeremiah M'Grath asked for 16s. 1d. worth of stamps. He tendered four half-crowns and six shillings, which she tested and determined were counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev3]

[EVIDENCE ev5]

Description: In his defense, M'Grath argued that it was 'very unlikely he would have put down all the 10 pieces at the post-office at once' if he had known they were counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: After being found guilty of the current charge, the prisoner pleaded guilty to a previous conviction for having counterfeit coin in his possession.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Charles Thorn Williams testified that after seeing an advertisement for 'Merrick, Williams, and Co.', he corresponded with them and sent two cheques totaling £5 15s for the purchase of bonds. He stated that he never received any bonds or any money back.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The key prosecution witness, Thomas Paul, who worked for the firm, testified that the defendant Langridge was the one who provided the reference to the landlord for the office, and that Langridge arranged for and ordered the firm's printed letterheads and circulars.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police Sergeant Benjamin Morgan testified that when he arrested Langridge and charged him with respect to the three cheques, Langridge replied, 'I can prove that I rendered value to Williams for these amounts.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The landlord, Edward Rogers, testified that Langridge not only acted as a reference for the firm but that the rent was frequently paid by a cheque signed by Langridge for the exact amount due.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: A deposition from the late Rev. George Robert Clack was read, stating that he communicated with 'Merrick, Williams, and Co.' in October 1882, sent a cheque for £21 5s for the purchase of five Brussels bonds, but never received the bonds or his money back.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Thomas Paul testified that during the firm's operation, only one bond was ever bought and delivered to a client in London, and one other that Langridge bought and sent away. These were the only legitimate transactions he knew of in eight months.

The prosecution alleged that the three men were working together in a conspiracy to pass bad sixpences in public-houses in St. James's.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mary Jane Hancock, manageress of the Feathers public-house, testified that Williams tendered a bad sixpence for tobacco. She identified it as counterfeit, bent it, and returned it to him.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Laura Warren, a barmaid at the Golden Lion, testified that May tendered a bad sixpence for tobacco. She broke the coin into three pieces and returned two to him.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Upon searching the men, the police found only good money on all three. Smith had two shillings, eight sixpences, and 4s. 4d. in bronze; Williams had 11d.; and May had 1s. 11d.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: In his defense, Williams denied being the man at the Feathers and highlighted that only good money was found on his person.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: May and Smith both stated in their defense that they had merely stopped because Williams asked for a light, and that their meeting was coincidental.

The prosecution presents eyewitness testimony and evidence of a prior similar offense, while the defense relies on an alibi provided by the prisoner, his mother, and a lodger.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Eliza Carlton, the landlady of the Plough Tavern, testified that the prisoner, Daniel Geehan, gave her a counterfeit shilling for a glass of ale. She broke the coin in a tester, returned a piece to him, and he then paid with a good sixpence. She positively identified him in court.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: John Northcott, the potman at the Plough, testified that he watched Geehan leave, saw him meet another man (Hogan), and pass something to him. When Hogan was approached, he dropped an object which Northcott recovered and identified as the counterfeit shilling. Northcott also strongly identified Geehan.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Policeman Alfred Nicholson testified that when he arrested Geehan on October 3rd for the uttering on October 2nd, Geehan stated, 'I was not out of bed all day yesterday.' Nicholson did not mention the date of the offense to Geehan before this statement was made.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Webster, an Inspector of Coin to the Mint, testified that the shilling passed at the tavern and the piece recovered were both counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Antonio Bendy, a landlord, testified that on September 12th, about three weeks prior to the current charge, the prisoner came into his pub and gave him a bad two-shilling piece. Bendy called a policeman and gave Geehan into custody.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Policeman Nicholson testified that when he searched the prisoner's residence, he did not find an ulster.

Description: Detective Oldhampstead testified that after the prisoner was remanded for a week, he escaped from custody.

Description: Police Constable William Williamson testified that on the night of November 25th, he saw the prosecutor, Arthur Orpin, who was 'the worse for drink,' being accosted by the prisoner, Martin Griffin, who offered to sell him matches.

public.game_background: Police Constable Frederick Whisker was indicted for wilful and corrupt perjury for his testimony in a prior police-court hearing. Whisker had sworn that another constable, Sharp, did not strike the complainant, James English, but rather that English was drunk, using abusive language, and fell down on his own. The prosecution alleges this testimony was false and intended to wrongly exonerate Sharp.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Police either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Augustus Halle, a clerk at Hammersmith Police-court, read the sworn testimony of Frederick Whisker from the prior hearing. The testimony stated that Whisker arrived before the complainant (English) fell, that English fell backwards on his own, that the other constable (Sharp) did not strike him, and that English was using abusive language.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Lewin Fenner, a letter-carrier, testified that he was at the corner to empty a letter-box when he saw Constable Sharp knock English down. He stated unequivocally that at that time 'there was no other constable there' and he saw Whisker arrive only afterwards.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police Sergeant James Crampton testified that when English arrived at the police station around 12:45, he was 'under the influence of drink; he was not drunk; he was not excited.' He also stated that English 'talked scarcely intelligibly.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Eliza Pavitt and Mary Ann Giblett, two servants, testified for the defense. They stated that on returning from a chemist's shop, they saw a woman (Thompson) followed by a constable (Sharp) and heard loud talking. They then met Whisker and told him, 'You had better hurry on, as I think there is some jangling up there.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Eliza Pavitt testified that she followed Whisker back and saw a man fall. She stated, 'I saw the prisoner at the time the man fell, I should say he was about two yards off, he was just going up and I was close behind him.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Police Inspector John Bates testified that Whisker had been in the force for 18 months and had borne an 'unexceptionable character,' describing him as a 'very sober, truthful, honest young man.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Constable Sharp, whose assault charge prompted Whisker's original testimony, was convicted in both cases against him (the assault on English and the incident with Thompson). Sharp's sentence expired before Whisker's trial, but he was not present to testify.

public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Joseph either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Police Sergeant Thick testified that upon entering the prisoners' room, he found Christy and Donovan at a table with counterfeit coins spread out. Christy was actively filing the edge of a coin.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Upon the police entering the room, Christy jumped up, rushed to the open window, and threw something out. A rattling sound was heard from the yard below.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: As police entered, Margaret Donovan rushed out of the room and downstairs. She was stopped by Inspector Peters after Sergeant Thick shouted, 'Stop that woman.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police found 22 counterfeit florins in the room. Sergeant Thick testified that some of the coins were warm and there was a bright fire burning.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: A search of the room by Officer Bolton revealed a file under the table and a spoon, covered with coal, which had metal residue on it.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William John Webster, an expert from the Mint, testified that all 23 florins were counterfeit and made from the same mould found on the roof. He also confirmed the spoon and files were instruments used for counterfeiting.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: When questioned by police, Christy stated: 'They don't belong to me, I have been to work all day, they were made in my absence by a man I don't know.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Donovan claimed to the police, 'I know nothing about it, I was not in the room.' She repeated on cross-examination, 'I was on the landing.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Charlotte Nevill, the deputy landlady, testified that she let the room to Donovan and that both Christy and Donovan had occupied it together since late December.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: In his defense, Christy claimed he went to the yard early in the morning, 'kicked against something,' and found the coins and tools in an object he thought was a purse.

public.game_background: Edward Hales was indicted for feloniously forging and uttering a bill of exchange for £250 5s., purporting to be accepted by George Russell Miller, with intent to defraud.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Edward Hales either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edward Barton testified that the prisoner, Edward Hales, owed him about £600 in mid-1881. Barton was almost constantly asking him for payment.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Barton testified under oath that he wrote the entire body of the bill, including the amount and the drawee's name (Mr. Miller), before handing the blank, unaccepted document to Hales.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: George Russell Miller testified unequivocally that the signature on the bill is not his writing. He stated it was not written by his authority and was 'not even a plausible imitation of my signature.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Barton claimed Hales admitted to writing 'Accepted payable at' on the bill because Mr. Miller was unused to accepting bills. Miller also testified that he believed this part of the writing was Hales's.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: A handwriting expert, George Inglis, testified that in his opinion, the words 'G. Russell Miller' and 'Accepted, payable at...' were written by the same hand, though slightly disguised.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Barton testified that when he questioned Hales about the bill's genuineness, Hales replied, 'That is all right, I have got the very blotting-paper it was written on, I have taken care of myself.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When confronted by Barton and Mr. Daniels that Miller repudiated the signature as a forgery by him, Hales allegedly said, 'Let him prove it,' and that nothing could be done until the bill matured.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The Jury, after examining the bill with a magnifying glass, stated their belief that the body of the bill (written by Barton) was written after the acceptance.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Barton's own financial situation was extremely precarious. He filed for bankruptcy for £7,000 in September 1881, shortly after this transaction, and the Sheriff had previously seized his goods.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mr. Daniels, the bill discounter, testified that Barton told him the bill had been drawn on Miller as payment for hops. Barton denied saying this, creating a direct conflict in testimony.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mr. Miller testified that around the same time, he gave Hales two blank acceptances (i.e., bills with only his signature on them) for Hales to get discounted on his behalf. Miller later got these back.

public.game_background: George Augustus Evett, a solicitor, was charged with conspiring with his clients, Richard Cobden Cox and Richard Johnston Railton, to create a fraudulent bill of sale. The purpose of the conspiracy was to transfer the assets of Railton's newspaper to Cox, thereby depriving a third party, Henry Munster, of the financial compensation (£489 in costs) awarded to him in a libel verdict against Railton.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. George either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Brewer, solicitor for Henry Munster, testified that the case of Munster v. Railton resulted in a verdict for the plaintiff with costs taxed at £489 18s. 11d. on July 18th. The defendant, George Evett, was present throughout the trial as solicitor for Mr. Railton.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Thomas Thorpe, the managing clerk at Evett's firm, testified that in the firm's bill book, the original heading for the client in the Munster case was 'Mr. R. C. Cox'. This had been struck through and 'Railton' was substituted.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Thomas Thorpe, the firm's managing clerk for 11 years, testified that the first time he ever heard of a partnership between Cox and Railton was after the bill of sale was executed.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Thomas Goodman, a solicitor from Brighton who had known Evett for seven or eight years, testified on cross-examination, 'he is a most honourable man... I have had hundreds of transactions with him... and I always found him to act up to his word.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Solicitor Thomas Goodman testified that on April 9th, 1881, he prepared a partnership deed between Richard Cox and Richard Railton. This established that they were business partners long before the libel action began.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The victim, Kate Clark, testified that after a verbal dispute, Timothy Donovan's mother-in-law ran out with a knife. She claimed Donovan took the knife from his mother-in-law and stabbed her (Clark) in the side of the face.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Kate Clark testified that the prisoner had kicked her on the night previous to the stabbing.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mary Brett, a fellow tenant, testified that she saw Mrs. Clark, who was drunk, smash the shop's counter and a lamp. She claimed Clark then fell across the counter and cut herself with the nails and broken glass.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: In response to the accusation, Ms. Hurley testified the prisoner said, 'No, I did not,' though the witness admitted she was not entirely certain of the exact words.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Ms. Hurley testified that bad money had been found eight or nine times after the prisoner had been in the shop, and that the prisoner always brought sixpences.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Policeman May further testified that when formally charged with the uttering on the 16th, the prisoner said she 'did not know anything about the one on the 16th.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner was searched upon arrest, and three half-crowns of good money were found on her person. No other counterfeit coins were discovered.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Webster, an Inspector of Coin for Her Majesty's Mint, testified that both sixpences were counterfeit and made from different moulds.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner's statement before the Magistrate was read: 'I was not sure of the coin. She is mistaken about the first coin; I did not give it to her.'

The prosecution alleged he knowingly participated in the theft orchestrated by a fellow employee, Rust, while the defense contended he was an unwitting accomplice.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Inspector James Henry Pearson testified that he and Superintendent Coffin confronted the prisoner, John Acton, on May 30th regarding a bale of leather stolen in January. They informed him that he and a man named Rust were suspected of taking the leather from the station in a van of empties and transferring it to a man with a pony and cart.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Inspector Pearson confirmed under cross-examination that the alleged robbery was in January, but Acton was not arrested until May, and that Rust was in a slightly superior position and a much older man.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: George Brewer ('Ginger') also testified that Rust had previously asked him to take a bale of leather away, but he had refused.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: John Davis, a warehouseman, testified that on January 30th he packed a bale of leather in the specific wrapper shown in court and handed it to a carman of the London and North-Western Company.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The value of the stolen leather was stated to be £27 0s. 5d.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Colonel Charles Richard Binney testified that he had known the prisoner for about two years, had business transactions with him, and that the prisoner knew he had an account at the London and County Bank.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Colonel Binney stated that the cheque for £50 was not drawn or signed by his authority. He also noted that it was not his habit to draw cheques on plain paper.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Owen Aves, the prisoner's former business partner, testified that he had frequent opportunities to see the prisoner's handwriting and believed the £50 cheque was written by him.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Samuel Hammond, the chief cashier, testified that the prisoner presented the cheque. Due to it being on plain paper, he questioned the prisoner about it.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Francis Piggott, the chief clerk, corroborated Hammond's account. He testified that he asked the prisoner why the cheque was on blank paper, and the prisoner repeated the story that Colonel Binney had no cheque book and needed the money for wages.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Detective Sergeant William Wright testified that upon being arrested and told of the charge, the prisoner's initial reaction was to say, 'It is a bad job, I was in difficulties and very much pressed for money at the time'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner stated that on the day in question, he was in the company of a man named Walters from 10:30 a.m. until 6 p.m., and therefore could not have gone to the bank.

Description: Constable Patrick Doherty testified that upon arrest, the prisoner said 'I am innocent.' On the way to the station, she added, 'I make my living on the streets, and am liable to get a bad shilling as well as any other body.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]

The magistrates dismissed the case against the Girdlers and subsequently granted a summons for perjury against Constable Hall.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George Brodie Clark, clerk to the Brentford Justices, confirmed he administered the oath to Charles Hall and produced the official notes of Hall's testimony. In this testimony, Hall claimed he was on duty when he encountered men rioting, was struck a severe blow under the chin, kicked in the ribs, and hit on the back of the head. He specifically accused William Girdler of striking him on the chest.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Kenyon, the prosecutor, testified that he was in the Cricketers beerhouse and saw the prisoner, Charles Robinson, who was 'making a big noise and drinking.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Witness George Retchless contradicted Kenyon's testimony about his walking stick, stating that Kenyon's first act in the pub was to offer the stick for sale for fourpence.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Policeman Tyrrell stated that the prisoner 'had been drinking, but was not so drunk as not to know what he was about.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The prisoner's statement before the Magistrate was, 'I am not guilty. I was so drunk I don't know what happened.'

public.game_background: Frederick Mansell, a poor rate collector for the parish of Fulham, was indicted for failing to account for and pay in several sums of money he had received on behalf of the ratepayers.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Frederick either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_macey_payment]
Description: Ratepayer John Macey testified that on August 23rd, he sent a cheque for £1 18s 9d to the prisoner, Frederick Mansell, and received a receipt signed by him. The prisoner personally delivered the receipt about a week later.
[EVIDENCE ev_ladd_payment]
Description: Ratepayer Henry Ladd testified that on August 25th, he paid the prisoner £1 13s 6d in person at his house and received an official receipt.
[EVIDENCE ev_hubbard_payment]
Description: Ratepayer Philip John Hubbard testified that he left £1 10s at the prisoner's house in late September and subsequently received a receipt.
[EVIDENCE ev_saunders_collection]
Description: James Saunders, a house agent employed by Mansell, testified that he collected rates from defaulters and gave Mansell three cheques for £50, £70, and £26 4s 6d. All three cheques were endorsed by Mansell and paid by the bank.
[EVIDENCE ev_cockburn_duty_last_entry]
Description: William Powell Cockburn, the Vestry Clerk, testified that it was the prisoner's duty to pay all collected moneys into the overseers' banking account. He stated that the prisoner's last entry of a receipt was on September 11th, with no entries after that date.
[EVIDENCE ev_cockburn_absence]
Description: Vestry Clerk Cockburn stated that Mansell was absent on September 29th, the day the rate collection was to be closed. Cockburn did not know that he was planning to be away.
[EVIDENCE ev_jones_flight]
Description: Police Inspector Henry Jones testified that on October 1st, he found the prisoner's house unoccupied. He eventually located and arrested the prisoner on October 7th in Debenham, Suffolk.
[EVIDENCE ev_character_good]
Description: Vestry Clerk Cockburn testified that as far as he knew, the prisoner had borne an honourable character up to this time. The transcript also notes that 'The prisoner received an excellent character.'
[EVIDENCE ev_father_cooperation]
Description: Vestry Clerk Cockburn testified that on the day Mansell was absent, his father came to inquire about the payment day and, upon request, provided all of the prisoner's accounting books.
[EVIDENCE ev_jury_mercy]
Description: After finding the prisoner guilty, the jury strongly recommended him to mercy, a recommendation in which the prosecution concurred.

public.game_background: The defendants, Frederick Pope, Harry Hughlings, Edward Errington, James Pryor, and Caleb Titcomb, were indicted for unlawfully conspiring to obtain money by false pretences from various persons with intent to defraud, primarily through the promotion and operation of several limited liability companies, including the International Fish Dinner Company, the House Property Distribution Society, and the City and Suburban Bank.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. The either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Sidney Casbourne, a clerk from the Registrar of Joint Stock Companies, presented official files for the companies in question. He detailed the subscribing shareholders and officers, linking Errington, Hughlings, Pryor, and Titcomb to one or more of these entities based on official registration documents.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Jay Leather, a builder, testified that Edward Errington, promoter of the International Fish Dinner Company, told him that if Leather would 'remunerate him,' he would get him the contract for fitting out the Barbican premises. Leather stated he later paid Errington £5 for securing the job and a bill for payment.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Clara Potter testified that Hughlings and Errington induced her to buy 100 shares in the International Fish Dinner Company by telling her it was a requirement to secure the position of manageress. The business failed to open properly, and she lost her investment, which Errington admitted was used to pay for expenses at another location.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Shareholder Robert Frewer stated that defendant Hughlings persuaded him to invest by showing him a printed statement from the company's auditor which certified that a profit of not less than 7 percent had already been made by the company's operations.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Samuel Weekes, a builder who was never paid for his work, testified that defendants Hughlings and Pope told him that the philanthropist Mr. Burdett-Coutts was expected to visit the premises and was likely to become a major shareholder, thereby encouraging Weekes to continue working without pay.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The company chairman, R.A. Riddell, testified that he was consistently prevented from seeing the company's bank pass-book by Hughlings, who made 'phantom excuses.' He also stated that Hughlings and two directors friendly to Errington issued numerous cheques without the Board's knowledge, which were subsequently dishonoured.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Alexander McChristie, a cashier at the Grosvenor Bank, testified that James Pryor introduced Frederick Pope to open an account. He confirmed Pope's account was frequently overdrawn and many cheques were dishonoured, with payments from the overdrawn account being authorized by the manager, Caleb Titcomb.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Thomas Waram and Thomas Rintoul, directors of the Grosvenor Bank, testified that a £300 loan application from Pryor was explicitly refused by the board. They stated that Caleb Titcomb, the manager, later obtained a director's signature on the cheque by falsely claiming the board had approved it, and the loan was granted against what proved to be worthless security.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The company chairman, R.A. Riddell, testified on cross-examination that he never knew Pope to have any official connection with the company. Jay Leather, the builder, also testified that Pope never gave him instructions or induced him to take shares, and seemed to be present only as a friend of the other parties.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: William Birch, a director of the Grosvenor Bank, testified that Caleb Titcomb, then the bank's manager, proposed a £200 loan for the International Fish Dinner Company. The loan was declined after the bank's chairman investigated, visited the premises, found they were 'doing no business,' and declared, 'Good God! don't lend those people any money.'

Description: John Kyle, the co-defendant, pleaded guilty to the charge of receiving the stolen cloth.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: A search of Evelyn's person revealed County Court and judgment summonses against him.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Defense witnesses Robert Banks and J. Lupton testified that Evelyn ran a legitimate, recognized business as a 'piece-broker,' buying and selling remnants over the counter.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mr. Letts, on behalf of himself and his partner Peel, approached solicitor Henry Dinn to borrow about £200. He left a bill of sale for the ship Ætna as a preliminary form of security, stating it was to defray expenses on the ship.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: When Dinn requested a formal mortgage on the ship for the small loan, Letts refused, stating that 'to give a mortgage for such a small sum as 200l. would damage our credit in Liverpool.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The ship's vendor, Charles Pile, testified that in addition to the £3,000 mortgage to Forward Brothers, the defendants had also given him a mortgage for £9,000 secured against the ship.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Dinn admitted under cross-examination that he made no search at the Customs House to verify the encumbrances on the ship, stating, 'I took their word for that, holding their bill of sale as sufficient evidence.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Henry Dinn stated in his testimony, 'I have at no time had any wish to prosecute—I wished to get my money back.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Lehman, a partner at the brickmaking firm, testified that in August, William Barrett was appointed as a commission agent for the sale of their bricks. As an agent, Barrett would not be personally responsible for the price of bricks he arranged for sale.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Charles Linnett testified that he was a printer's warehouseman who was out of employment and had never been a builder. He stated that Chalcraft, whom he knew well, was aware of his situation.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Linnett testified that a note shown in court, addressed to the station-master and signed 'E. Linnett,' authorizing a carman to unload the bricks, was not in his handwriting. Linnett's first initial is 'C.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Henry Brooks, the station-master, stated that Barrett came to the station to collect the bricks, claiming to be the agent for the senders and to have an 'interest in the consignees.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The carters, Lester and Barker, testified that Barrett hired them to move the bricks. However, Chalcraft was also involved; he recommended Lester to Barrett and was present with Barrett when Barker was engaged.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: James Young Pattison, a solicitor's clerk, testified that his search of the Middlesex Registry revealed that Chalcraft had conveyed the building land to Miss Mayho in 1883. Therefore, Chalcraft was not the freeholder in November 1884.

They are accused of stealing four boxes of cigars, tobacco, a bottle of whisky, and 5 shillings in bronze coins. The charges also include breaking out of the premises and receiving stolen goods.

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The prosecutor, Oswald Halliwell, testified that on the morning of March 25th, he was awoken by police and found his bar in great disorder. A tap-room window's hasp was unfastened, allowing entry. All his spirit taps were turned on and running, and two plates with human excrement were left on the counter.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Oswald Halliwell, the prosecutor, testified that he had seen the prisoner Webb in his house on the night of the burglary, just before closing. The pub's potman, William Carter, confirmed seeing both prisoners, Webb and Leonard, go out of the house together at closing time.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Police Sergeant Frank Bryars testified that he searched the room occupied by Webb and found four boxes of cigars, a bottle of whisky, tobacco, and 1s. 7d. in bronze coins.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Upon being arrested, Webb exhibited behavior indicating consciousness of guilt. He gave a false name, 'Tommy Pushpin', to Officer Cooper. He made no reply to Sergeant Bryars when told what was found at his house, and falsely claimed he had been 'walking about all night.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: City Policeman Charles Mansell testified that on the way to the station, Leonard stated, unprompted: 'I did not turn the b——taps on... I suppose you have got Joe Towell as well.' 'Joe Towell' was an alias used by Webb.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William Allen testified that Leonard gave him two packets of tobacco on the morning of March 25th. Sergeant Bryars added that in Leonard's presence, Leonard confirmed this by saying, 'Yes, I gave it to him.' The prosecutor identified this tobacco as being stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Sergeant Bryars testified that when Leonard was charged, he 'made no answer to the charge.' His only other response when questioned was to admit giving tobacco to Allen.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Oswald Halliwell testified, 'I believe Leonard was there that night, but I did not see him.' This contrasts with his positive identification of Webb.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Charles Mansell testified that he apprehended Leonard at the bar of the prosecutor's house at 1:30 PM on the day following the burglary.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George Aylward, master of the workhouse, testified that a fire broke out around 4 a.m. in a water-closet used as a storeroom. The fire had a firm hold, burning about 12 counterpanes and causing approximately 10 pounds in damage to the woodwork.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Aylward stated in his initial written report to the Guardians that he believed the fire was accidental, as he could not come to any other conclusion at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Thomas Vile, the labour master, testified that two days after the fire, on March 6th, he cautioned the prisoner, who then said, 'There has been one little fire, two little fires, but the next one will be a big one.' Vile reported this threat to the master.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Aylward, who had known the prisoner for 13 or 14 years, testified that Schuster 'is quite right in his mind so far as I can judge.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mr. Duffield testified that he understood Schuster was suicidal and had made an attempt on his life. Duffield 'regarded him as a man who might do something very dangerous if not watched.'

public.game_background: James Forbes and John Henry Gardner were indicted for conspiracy to defraud their creditors. The prosecution alleged that Forbes, through his firm Jones and Forbes, conspired with Gardner, who managed a separate firm named Bloomfield and Co., to create fictitious transactions, raise money on accommodation bills, and conceal assets, leading to significant financial losses for their creditors upon their respective bankruptcies.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. James either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Charles L'Enfant, a clerk from the London Bankruptcy Court, produced the proceedings for James Forbes's bankruptcy. The statement of affairs, signed by Forbes, showed gross liabilities of £23,571 and a deficiency of £14,243. Notably, it listed a loss of £6,161 on transactions with Bloomfield and Co.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Lellyett, a bank manager, testified that Forbes and Gardner came to his office together to open an account for 'Jones and Forbes'. Forbes introduced Gardner as his agent or manager in Brighton and gave him full authority to draw bills and cheques on the account.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: On cross-examination, Mr. Lellyett confirmed he received letters from Forbes's Scottish bank, dated June 1884, which expressed confidence in Jones and Forbes, noting they had kept an account there for 14 years.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Crossthwaite discovered a sack load of Bloomfield's papers hidden away in an empty house standing in Mrs. Gardner's name. These papers included financial records that were not entered in any of the official company books of either Jones and Forbes or Bloomfield and Co.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Crossthwaite testified that an insurance policy on a property, Cameron Villa, was found in Forbes's desk, yet this property was not disclosed in Forbes's statement of affairs. Forbes initially denied having anything to do with it.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: On cross-examination, Mr. Crossthwaite admitted that Forbes's ledger account with Bloomfield and Co. was visible in the books and that there was 'nothing concealed there.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Edward Woodham, a bank manager, testified that on the same day £120 was drawn from the Bloomfield and Co. account, it was paid into a new account opened by Mrs. Kate Gardner, the co-defendant's wife.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Crossley, a builder who did business with Bloomfield and Co., testified on cross-examination that 'every bill I signed I had goods for' and that Forbes was never a party to any of his arrangements.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Benjamin Wakefield, a decorator, testified that he accepted a bill for £35, drawn by Jones and Forbes and signed by Forbes, but he 'received no goods or consideration for that.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: John Sheldon, a builder, testified that Gardner told him Mr. Forbes was 'a very wealthy man' who was going to be 'at his back' in the new business, Bloomfield and Co., which was started because Gardner could not trade in his own name after his liquidation.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Roderick McKay, a chartered accountant, testified that 'Forbes's books were fairly kept' and 'posted up to date.' In contrast, he stated of Bloomfield's books, 'I could make nothing out of them; they cannot be relied upon; they are not at all complete.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: At the conclusion of the trial, 'Several witnesses deposed to the good character of Forbes.' This led the Jury to strongly recommend him to mercy, 'believing him to have been led into the fraud by Gardner.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Rose Ogborn, a barmaid at the Royal George, testified that on June 23rd, Hollaby came in and asked for a 'twopenny smoke.' He gave her a shilling which she bent and found to be counterfeit. She showed it to the landlady who detained Hollaby.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Aubrey Brown, a hosier's assistant, testified that on June 27th, Hollaby came to buy a collar and tendered a bad half-crown. When told it was bad, Hollaby said 'Then it will be of no use to me' and paid with a good half-crown.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: William John Webster, a Treasury expert, examined the coins involved in the case and testified that they are all counterfeit and made from different moulds.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Barmaid Eliza Dunlop testified that on August 14th, the prisoner paid for a 6d. glass of sherry with a sovereign. She served him and gave him change.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Eliza Dunlop testified that on August 18th, she served the prisoner again, who paid with another sovereign. She stated she knew by its light weight that it was bad and immediately gave it to Miss Ellison.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When detained on August 18th, the prisoner said that he would wait. He was also willing to pay for his drink with other money.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William John Webster, Inspector of Coin to Her Majesty's Mint, testified that all three coins were counterfeit, made of pewter metal, only half the weight of a sovereign, and cast from the same mould.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner's statement before the Magistrate was a simple declaration of innocence: 'I am innocent of the charge brought against me.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In his defence, the prisoner claimed, 'I deny being there on August 14th.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The prisoner admitted tendering a sovereign on August 18th but claimed he did not know it was bad. He stated, 'I received it at a race where I backed a horse.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: During the cross-examination of Mr. Barnes, it was revealed that the prisoner stated, 'I have no address.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev01]
Description: Police Sergeant David Francis found flour under a bed berth on the prisoners' barge on July 13th. The younger prisoner claimed it was an 'over' (an excess from a delivery) that they were making use of.
[EVIDENCE ev05]
Description: The dock returns for the steamship Longhurst, from which the prisoners loaded their cargo, showed a shortage of 32 bags of flour.
[EVIDENCE ev06]
Description: A clerk for the ship's agents and a shipping manager both testified that mistakes are sometimes made in manifests, receipts, and dock landing accounts, and that delivery errors are frequent.
[EVIDENCE ev07]
Description: Mr. Saunders, the prisoners' employer, testified that the elder Kirby had been in his employment for eight years. The prisoners received good characters in court.
[EVIDENCE ev08]
Description: Mr. Saunders described an unauthorized custom among lightermen to keep 'overs' (excess bags) to recoup themselves for financial losses from 'shorts' (shortages in delivery) for which their wages are docked.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The barge 'Lucy' made several other voyages after the flour delivery, including one with coal to London, with the flour sacks remaining on board without any attempt to sell or dispose of them.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Marmaduke Hare, the shipping clerk who tallied the flour onto the barge, produced his tally-book which showed that, according to his count of the tiers, the prisoners' barge was 9 bags short of its 500-bag complement.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Testimony from grocer George Whitehead that on March 5th, he paid the prisoner £2 18s. 4d. in cash for an outstanding debt to Messrs. Godey, Henshall, and Co., and received a receipt from the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Testimony from oilman Adfred Long that on May 15th, he paid the prisoner £2 18s. 4d. for goods and was given a receipt.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Testimony from oilman Frederick Brayziel that he paid the prisoner a total of £6 0s. 10d. for goods on two occasions, and the prisoner signed both receipts.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: On cross-examination, Henshall admitted he was 'obliged to institute the prosecution to recover money from the Guarantee Society' and stated that 'if the account had been settled up there would have been no prosecution'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective William Cross testified that on February 2nd, he observed Charles Lever hand an empty black bag to Samuel Freeman after they exited Newbury's establishment. Later that day, he saw them together again, with Lever carrying a parcel of perfume and Freeman holding his arm.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Knollys Hayes Walker, representing Bailey & Co., testified that the seized perfume, bottles, and labels were counterfeit. He stated their lowest wholesale price was around 20s 6d per dozen, and they had never sold them for 11s, making the prices quoted by the defendants impossible for genuine goods.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Barrett, manager for J. & D. Atkinson, testified that William Boyes was a former employee. He confirmed Atkinson's perfume was also counterfeited and identified a stone found by police in Bristol as having a portion of their trade-mark label on it.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Edward Pickering, of Francis Newbury and Sons, testified they had bought the counterfeit perfume from Lever for two years. Lever explained the low prices by claiming the goods were obtained in exchange for advertisements, a story Pickering believed.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Police found documents, including invoices and telegrams, at Freeman's house and on Lever's person. This correspondence related to transactions in scent and chemicals between Freeman, Lever, and Williams.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Dr. Robert Humphries, a witness for the defense, testified that he was present when Boyes stated he had 'made this spurious scent and sold it to Williams as a genuine article.' Boyes also said he would not allow Williams 'to suffer for his misdeeds.'
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The transcript notes that Williams and Freeman received good characters. Lever also received a good character.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Officer Cross testified in cross-examination that he believed Lever was 'very near sighted' and that on the first occasion he saw him, 'Freeman had hold of his arm leading him.'

Description: Richard Knowlden, one of the property's trustees and owners, testified that the fixtures were the property of the trustees and he did not sell them to Clark.

Description: John William Greenwood, a collector for Chandler and Co. brewers, testified from brewery ledgers that Clark's average trade was about 2.75 barrels per week, with a high of 18 barrels in the month of October.

[EVIDENCE ev16]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Cecil George Douglas, chief clerk at the Guildhall Police-court, testified that on March 9th, the prisoner William Tranter was duly sworn and gave evidence. In his testimony, Tranter alleged that Daniel Cleary and Dennis Brian stole his gold watch, worth 20 pounds, the previous night. Tranter signed this written statement as correct.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Ernest Kidner, a policeman, testified that Tranter reported the theft and identified Cleary and Brian. The two men were immediately stopped and searched, but no watch was found on them.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: At the police station, Tranter claimed the stolen item was a gold presentation watch, given to his father for long service as a manager of a coal mine, with an inscription on the back.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police Sergeant Simon Kurtz testified that when Tranter was arrested on another charge, 11 pawn-tickets were found on him, dating over the previous year. This included tickets for two other, cheaper watches pawned in January 1886.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Herbert Austin, a court clerk, stated that before Cleary's trial, Tranter applied for extra travel expenses from Wales. He falsely claimed his father had died, leaving behind young children to be cared for. He was paid over 4 pounds in expenses.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner's key witness, a friend named Murphy who was supposedly with him during the theft and testified before the Magistrate, did not appear at Cleary's trial. Tranter claimed Murphy had absconded with money Tranter had guaranteed for him.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense, Tranter maintained his story was correct. He suggested it was suspicious that he happened to accuse two men who, it turned out, both had prior criminal convictions.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: After the guilty verdict, it was stated by an inspector that Tranter had previously made a similar charge of robbery against another person, which was found to be groundless.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_skeels_loan]
Description: Hanry Skeels testified that on February 8, 1885, Forest Lycett, claiming to be in a 'terrible mess,' asked for a 450l. loan for a few days. Lycett stated he was a shipowner in the 'Charles Howard' and could provide shares as security which would amply cover the amount. Skeels agreed and gave him a cheque for 450l., induced by this representation.
[EVIDENCE ev_kirkaldy_purchase]
Description: John Kirkaldy testified that he had purchased two shares in the 'Charles Howard' from Lycett in June 1883. Although he held the bill of sale unregistered for his own convenience, he considered them his property and had unsuccessfully attempted to register the sale in January 1885, prior to Lycett's transaction with Mr. Skeels.
[EVIDENCE ev_share_value]
Description: Herbert Pelly, a clerk, testified that at a shareholders' meeting on February 9, 1885, the day after the loan was made, shares in the 'Charles Howard' were being 'freely offered there at 25l. each.'
[EVIDENCE ev_unsealed_bos]
Description: The bill of sale for the shares that Lycett provided to Skeels was delivered without a seal. Skeels testified he was 'perfectly ignorant of anything to do with shipping' at the time and did not notice the omission.
[EVIDENCE ev_sister_housekeeper]
Description: Mr. Skeels testified that the prisoner's sister, Miss Lycett, had been his 'lady housekeeper' for three months prior to the loan. He also stated that knowing Lycett was 'respectably connected' was partly the reason he lent him the money.
[EVIDENCE ev_good_character]
Description: The transcript concludes by noting that 'The prisoner received a good character,' implying that witnesses testified favorably regarding his general reputation and honesty.

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Walter Harris further testified that he saw the defendant himself outside the shop cleaning the window between 12 and 1 o'clock, and stated Barling 'must have seen the cutting in the window then'.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: William Patterson, the victim, confirmed that the report referred to him and that he is a pork butcher in the same business, with his shop located directly opposite the prisoner's.

[EVIDENCE ev11]

[EVIDENCE ev12]

information:
