Description: The defendant, Gurney, approached Ludwig Lichwitz claiming to have bought cigars for £310 and sold them for £380. He asked for a loan of £310 for half an hour to get the dock warrants, offering a £10 commission. Gurney claimed the introduction came from a solicitor, Mr. Stanskey, whom Lichwitz knew.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Gurney visited Lichwitz's solicitor, Mr. Rubenstein, asking for the warrants to supposedly collect the money from the buyer. However, when Rubenstein produced the warrants, Gurney immediately postponed the meeting, saying, 'I don't think we will go to-day.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: A ledger clerk from the dock warehouse, William Elsom, testified that the warrants were legitimately made out in Gurney's name. This was done at the direction of a respectable firm, John Meyer and Co., and the details on the warrants were correct.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mr. Slesinger testified that during the confrontation at Choppin's office, he heard Gurney say something to the effect of, 'It is very unfortunate, I should never have had the money to take up the warrants if I had not relied upon your paying.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Frederick Bertram Smart, the administrator of the estate of Ann Marshall, testified that the prisoner, Robert Marshall, claimed he knew the location of missing estate bonds. The prisoner stated that if he were given 75 pounds, he would return with the bonds by 6 o'clock that same day, or else return the money.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mr. Pritchard, the solicitor, testified that he warned both Robert and George Marshall before the money was handed over that 'they would be prosecuted if they did not bring the notes or the documents back.' He then personally handed the 75 pounds in notes to the prisoner, Robert Marshall.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Harold Tyrrell Granger, a beneficiary of the estate, testified that the prisoner had visited him on several occasions prior to the incident, claiming he could produce the bonds 'provided he had the necessary funds advanced for that purpose.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Mr. Granger also testified that after the prisoner had received the 75 pounds, he told Granger that 'his brother had the money, and he could not obtain it from him.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: George Marshall testified that he took the 75 pounds home and banked it in the Post Office, placing 30 pounds in his own name and 30 pounds in his mother's name.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mr. Smart testified that George Marshall had previously asked him for loans and he had declined. This suggests George was in need of money.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The defense argued as a point of law that because the cheque was drawn to George Marshall's order, the proceeds legally became his money, precluding a charge of stealing from Mr. Smart.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Frederick James testified that he was awakened around 2:20 AM by a noise in his drawing-room, which he had locked before bed. He heard footsteps on the landing.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Ernest Hill, a guest in the house, testified that he also looked out of the window and saw the prisoner, dressed in a fireman's uniform, get out of the drawing-room window.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Frederick James testified that after the incident, he checked the house and "missed nothing."
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Both Frederick James and Ernest Hill testified that the prisoner was drunk. James noted he was "the worse for liquor" and "walking along the road rather straddling," while Hill stated, "I considered him drunk when I spoke to him."
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Policeman Frederick Green testified that when he arrested the prisoner in his fireman's box, "he didn't appear to be drunk then."
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: David Hobbs, an engineer with the Fire Brigade, testified that when he was fetched after the arrest, the prisoner was "under the influence of drink." Hobbs also confirmed he had visited West at 10:26 PM, at which time he was "perfectly sober."
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: David Hobbs testified under cross-examination that West had been in the brigade for 10 months and his character was "very satisfactory."
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Officer Green testified that upon being arrested, the prisoner first said he "didn't know of any house," but later said he had "heard a noise and went to see what it was."
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: On re-examination, Mr. James stated that the drawing-room was on the first floor and the prisoner "could only get in there by getting on a wall."

public.game_background: Joseph Harcourt Smith was indicted for feloniously forging and uttering a conveyance of land and buildings. He is accused of creating fraudulent deeds for a fictitious property in Ardwick, Manchester, which he first used to secure a £500 mortgage investment from his employer, George Newnes, and later used the same deeds to secure a £1,000 loan from Glyn, Mills, Currie, and Co., bankers, with intent to defraud.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Joseph either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George Newnes, M.P. and proprietor of Tit-Bits, testified that the prisoner, Joseph Smith, was his business manager from 1882 to 1885. Newnes stated, 'I always had the highest confidence in him... on the contrary I had the greatest admiration for the way he conducted the business.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Multiple officials testified that the property and individuals named in the deeds were fictitious. A postman and a clerk to the overseers for Ardwick confirmed there is no 'Copenhagen Street' there. Police and the clerk confirmed that neither Thomas Cripps nor Charles Haynes could be found or were listed in rate books as property owners.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: William Henry Priest, a stationer, testified that the two main deeds (the conveyance and mortgage), which were dated February 1884, were actually engrossed in his office on September 25, 1884, for a 'J. Smith'. The mortgage deed was officially stamped at Somerset House on October 3, 1884.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Cyril Pearson, a clerk, testified that Smith dictated the answers to a requisition of title form. The form included the handwritten note by Smith, 'Answered personally by Haynes at office,' but Pearson confirmed that Mr. Haynes was not present at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Arthur Styleman, a solicitor's clerk, was present at a meeting where Smith was questioned about the deeds. Styleman testified that Smith was shown the attestation signatures on the deeds and was asked if it was his own handwriting, to which Smith replied that it was.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Smith offered conflicting accounts of his involvement. He told the bank's solicitor that the mortgagor, Haynes, was a 'personal friend' for whom he collected rent. Later, in a letter, he claimed that he had 'been a victim of an ingenious fraud' himself.

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Emma Sibley testified that Hughes told her that her 'pa had been dead seven years.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]

Description: On cross-examination, Elizabeth Hughes admitted that prior to her husband's death, the family had lived in Wales, had a house there, and kept horses and carriages of their own.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: On cross-examination, Charles David Evans admitted he made inquiries about all customers introduced by Allen through a Trade Society before supplying goods. He stated that 'nothing was registered against them.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Frederick Ash testified that after receiving a satisfactory reference from Allen for Manning, he supplied goods. Manning paid for the first small order, but then took a much larger order of butterine worth £36 and disappeared without paying.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: James Griffiths testified that Allen ordered tea and gave J. S. Manning as a reference. Manning wrote back stating he had known Allen for six years and done 'considerable trade with him.' Griffiths became suspicious and did not send the goods.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Thomas Brett, Allen's landlord, testified that Allen rented a front parlour and kitchen, where he 'never has any sign of business.' This contradicts Allen's business persona as 'Allan & Co., foreign flour merchants.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Testimonies from landlords Frederick Grover and William Collins established that Manning had a recent history of not paying rent at two different business premises. Both landlords had to take legal action to distrain for rent.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Herbert Muskett, a solicitor, found a letter book at Allen's residence containing an entry in Allen's own writing from September 1887 regarding Manning: 'I have never done anything with him.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Detective Murphy testified that when Manning was arrested and the warrant mentioning Allen's name was read, his immediate response was, 'Where is Allen? What about Allen?'
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Joseph Dymond, who bought Manning's bakery business for £350, testified he was 'perfectly swindled' as the business was 'falsely represented entirely.' He found goods obtained from other victims on the premises, not being used for trade.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Police found numerous pawn-tickets and unpaid invoices totaling £759 at Manning's residence, indicating a pattern of obtaining goods and immediately pawning or defaulting on them, rather than engaging in legitimate trade.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: At 10:10 pm on April 19th, police inspectors met the prisoner, George Harris, about a mile and a quarter from the crime scene. They noted he was in a 'heated state'.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Upon searching Harris, police found two silver bracelets, a gold watch and chain, an enamelled brooch, and a gold turquoise ring in his right-hand trousers pocket. These items were identified by the victim as hers.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Inspector Andrews testified that Harris's 'knees were wet with mud as if he had been kneeling in the road' and that it was 'pouring with rain'.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: A search of the prisoner revealed the blade of a table-knife.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The victim, Ann Ashton, testified that the prisoner had lodged in her house for a few days about two weeks before the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mrs. Ashton testified that at about 9:40 pm she heard the 'landing window bang down and a door slam, and I then heard the kitchen window smash.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: During cross-examination, Mrs. Ashton stated that the house was open until 11 o'clock and that 'any person could get upstairs while the house was open.'

public.game_background: Frank Talbot is charged with stealing a gelding, a set of harness, and a whip, the property of Charles Joyce. Description: A witness named Daws testified that on May 29th, he purchased a pony, cart, and harness from the prisoner, Frank Talbot, for £4. He specified it was a chestnut pony with a white face.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Richard Howell, an ostler, testified that the prisoner came to the White Bear inn on May 29th and offered to sell him a pony, cart, and harness for £10.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Daniel Hawtrey, a publican, testified that on May 29th, the prisoner asked to sell him his pony and cart, describing it as 'the little lot outside'.
[EVIDENCE ev7]

[EVIDENCE ev8]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Huntingdon was appointed as a receiver for the Fry v Fry estate, which required him to submit regular accounts and vouchers for all payments made on behalf of the estate.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Huntingdon delayed submitting his accounts for several months. He claimed a fire at a neighboring office had drenched and spoiled his vouchers, forcing him to obtain duplicates.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The account eventually submitted by Huntingdon included two items for ground rent paid to the Licensed Victuallers' Asylum, one for £99 7s. 6d. and another for £99 5s. 6d.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: An affidavit, purportedly signed by Huntingdon, was sworn to verify the accuracy of the submitted accounts, including the fraudulent payments. The affidavit was presented to the court by the solicitor.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: When approached by the Asylum's solicitor for the overdue rent, the defendant pretended to be the receiver's brother, falsely claiming the actual receiver (himself) was dying from an accident.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The defendant had legally changed his name from Sydney Thomas Raven to Thomas Sydney Huntingdon. He failed to disclose that his clerk, Raven, and his surety, Joseph George Raven, were both his brothers.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The defendant was in financial difficulty. He owed the estate a balance of over £300, had received £189 for repairs of which he only spent about £20, and was subject to court orders to pay over £1,000.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Upon his arrest, the defendant immediately denied the charge, stating, 'It is absolutely false, I know nothing about it,' and later claimed, 'I was away at Eastbourne then.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Thomas Rossiter testified that he was approached by the prisoner, Martin Brabazon, and a Mr. Toll. Toll stated, in Brabazon's presence, that Brabazon had a reversionary interest under a will which was free from incumbrance, except for a £50 mortgage to a Mr. Coulson.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The prisoner handed Rossiter a letter dated May 25, 1888, purporting to be from the trustee, his aunt Mrs. Salter. The letter stated that she had not received notice of any charge on the property.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: During cross-examination, Rossiter admitted, 'If I had seen any chance of getting the money by fair means I should not have thought of a prosecution.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Clement Cheese, a solicitor, testified that he attested the execution of a deed on October 24, 1879, and identified the prisoner as the man who executed it. This deed concerned the same reversionary interest.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Under cross-examination, Mr. Cheese confirmed that after the current criminal proceedings began, a writ to cancel the 1879 document was served on him.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mr. Timbrell, another solicitor, testified that in August 1887, the prisoner had also come to him, represented that the same interests were uncharged, and obtained an advance of £21.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Detective Sergeant Samuel Lythel testified that upon arresting the prisoner, he replied that he had been 'ill advised.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The jury found the prisoner guilty but strongly recommended him to mercy 'on account of his youth and his having been led away by money-lenders and other people.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: David Shaw Batchelor advertised two sets of harness for sale in the 'Bazaar, Exchange, and Mart'. He received a letter in response from the prisoner with a printed heading: 'Edward G. Creasey, Wholesale Potato, Fruit, and Poultry Merchant' of 21, May-day Green, Barnsley. The letter offered to purchase the harness and remit money upon receipt.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: James Kay testified that 21, May-day Green is a private cottage where a Mrs. Sarah Rose lives, and no wholesale business is carried on there. He also stated that when the prisoner gave him a receipt, he tore off the printed 'Wholesale Potato Merchant' heading.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Sergeant Joseph Parker testified that upon seeing him at Barnsley's Midland Railway Station, the prisoner immediately ran away. The officer chased him for a quarter of a mile before the prisoner rushed into a house and tried to slam the door shut.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: During a search of the prisoner's lodgings at 21, May-day Green, Sergeant Parker found a great quantity of the fraudulent bill-heads and several copies of the 'Bazaar, Exchange, and Mart' newspaper, where the first victim had placed his advertisement.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Detective Sergeant Aaron Tidswell testified that he intercepted the thirty baskets of plums addressed to Creasey at the train station. He also testified that he had known the prisoner for two years and that Creasey had never carried on business as a wholesale fruit merchant.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: On cross-examination, Detective Tidswell added that he had seen the prisoner selling fruit in the market, but 'when he has seen me he has run away and left it'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: After the guilty verdict, the prisoner pleaded guilty to a prior conviction for obtaining goods by false pretences in January 1888, at Wakefield. Sergeant Parker also stated that eleven other similar cases had been traced against the prisoner since his arrest.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Bryant, a publisher, testified that Adolf Francis arranged to have letters addressed to his office at 11, Red Lion Court. Several letters arrived for 'Cassell and Co,' which Bryant refused as Francis never paid the agreed fee. The real Cassell and Co. does not operate from that address.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Henry Munro, a printer, stated that he printed 88 pages of a book for Francis, who ordered only twenty copies as specimens. Francis never paid the £10 owed for the printing work.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Percy Walker testified that George Price claimed to be a canvasser for 'Messrs Cassell and Co,' who were supposedly bringing out a cyclists' road-book. Price explicitly confirmed it was the famous firm 'Cassell, Petter and Galpin.' Believing this, Walker paid £2 for an advertisement.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Henry Adams testified that Francis personally called on him, claimed to be from 'Cassell and Co,' and obtained a £1 cheque for an advertisement, which he gave believing the publishers were the famous firm.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Peter Picarney testified that when he questioned Francis about the Red Lion Court address on the receipt, Francis claimed it was 'one of their publishing offices' and was the same as Belle Sauvage Yard.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Frederick Cook testified that Francis explained the different address by saying 'they are bringing out a work that was brought out by Griffith and Farran, and they are keeping it distinct from their other business.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: James Anthony testified that when he asked Price if he meant 'Cassell, Petter, and Galpin,' Price replied, 'Yes, that was the name of the old firm,' convincing him to pay for an advertisement.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Edwin Barnard, manager for publishers Griffith and Farran, testified that the 'Cyclists' Road-book' used by the defendants was their property and was taken 'bodily from ours,' with a different title page. It had never been sold or transferred to Cassell and Co.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Walter Woods, a manager at the real 'Cassell and Co, Limited,' confirmed the defendants had no connection to their firm and were not authorized to represent them. He noted the book was a fraudulent composite and the signature on the cover was deceptively similar to their director's.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Sydney Jennings testified that when he noted the address on the receipt was not Cassell's, Francis said, 'Oh, I think they are cousins.' Jennings then remarked, 'Then I suppose there is another Cassell, as there is a Castell Brothers?'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: In January 1889, Thomas Brinsmead's firm, which had supplied timber worth £273 to Pound's company, declined to supply further timber without receiving security for payment.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: During cross-examination, Brinsmead revealed that the defendant 'asked me particularly not to make any inquiry at the War Office as to the delivery.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When Brinsmead grew suspicious and asked for an explanation, Pound showed him a list of entries in a book that corresponded with the amounts given, which Brinsmead did not consider a satisfactory explanation.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: A few days after obtaining legal process, Brinsmead visited the company's factory in Deptford and found that none of the timber he had supplied was there; it had all been consumed.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: James Martin, the manager of the company's Deptford works, testified that the timber from Brinsmead was used for the War Office contracts and that work was carried on with as much despatch as possible. He claimed approximately 1,700 of 2,046 ammunition boxes were nearly complete.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The defense witness Martin stated that when a petition was filed against the company, 'everything came to an absolute standstill' and he had to take orders from a liquidator, preventing further deliveries.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: John Siggers, the company secretary, testified that the defendant had a large interest in the business and had advanced large sums of his own money to the company, with £5,000 to his credit.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: The jury found the defendant guilty, but recommended him to mercy 'on account of the hard and striving manner in which he tried to uphold his business, and believing that he had no intent to defraud.' The defendant was discharged on his own recognisances.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Douglas Gordon Macrae testified that his newspaper, the Financial Times, had published critical comments about companies connected to the prisoner, Alfred Green. This led to a libel action by Green against the newspaper and a subsequent application to commit Green for contempt of court, setting the stage for the hostile cross-examination.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Barry O'Driscoll, a reporter for the Financial Times, testified he saw Green move a revolver from his hip pocket during the examination. A fully loaded five-chamber revolver was later found on Green at the police station.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Defense witness Stuart James Butt, a barrister's clerk from a nearby chamber, testified that he heard a scuffle and saw two gentlemen, later identified as Green and Macrae, 'striking one another with sticks' on the landing.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Both defense witnesses, Butt and Blunden, testified that they were close to the altercation but did not hear Green use any expression like 'I will murder you.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Three defense witnesses (Arthur Russell, Liddell, and James Cock) testified that Green had recently been on his yacht, where he and friends amused themselves by shooting with the revolver. They stated he bought new cartridges on September 19th or 20th because foreign ones purchased in Antwerp did not fit properly.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Police Constable Frederick Kitson testified that when he arrived, he found a 'thick knotted wangee cane' on the steps between the two men. He stated, 'I believe Mr. Macrae claimed it; I am not certain of that.'

public.game_background: John Walton and his wife Emily Walton were indicted for forging and uttering a deed to fraudulently obtain a loan of £1,100 from Alfred William Carpenter, a banker. The prosecution alleged that Emily Walton impersonated her sister-in-law, Annie Walton, the true owner of two freehold houses, to secure the loan against the property, with John Walton acting as an accessory. The defense argued that Annie Walton had authorized Emily to raise the money on her behalf.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1_carpenter_loan]
Description: Alfred William Carpenter, a banker, testified that a woman calling herself Miss Annie Walton borrowed a total of £1,100 against two freehold houses. He advanced the money on the strength of her being the rightful owner and signing the mortgage deed. He later discovered she was Emily Walton and that the deed was a forgery when the real Annie Walton initiated legal proceedings against him.
[EVIDENCE ev3_annie_theft]
Description: Annie Walton, the victim, testified that she was the rightful owner of the property. She stated she gave the title deeds to her sister-in-law, Emily, at her request for her brother John to look at, but never authorized either of them to raise money on the property.
[EVIDENCE ev8_guillaume_prior_forgery]
Description: Thomas Gillaume, a solicitor, testified that Emily Walton had previously come to him, using the name 'Miss Walton', and forged a mortgage on another of Annie Walton's properties (Overstein Road). He discovered the forgery and communicated with the real Annie Walton, who then executed a genuine deed to pay off the debt.
[EVIDENCE ev12_inwood_confrontation]
Description: Harriet Inwood testified about a confrontation she arranged between John and Annie Walton. In Annie's presence, Inwood told John that Annie had called him 'a thief, a drunkard, and a liar' for having her deeds. When John asked Annie if he had cheated her, she replied, 'I never said so.'
[EVIDENCE ev13_smith_admission]
Description: Alice Smith, sister of Emily Walton, testified that in August 1885, Annie Walton visited her in Liverpool. When asked how the Waltons were, Annie allegedly replied, 'They are all right now; Emily has been in some difficulty about money matters, but I have helped her out by getting money on the houses up the road.'
[EVIDENCE ev14_forbes_authorization]
Description: Hugh Forbes testified that in January 1886, at Annie Walton's request, he wrote out a document authorizing Emily Walton to raise £300 on the New Brighton property. He stated that he then saw Annie Walton sign this document.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Ernest Burrows, the victim, testified that he knew the prisoner, John Wallington, for three to four months as a friendly workmate at a bakery. Burrows trusted Wallington enough to arrange for his Post Office bank book to be sent to Wallington's address.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Burrows stated that the signature on the withdrawal application for £6 and the subsequent receipt were not his, and that he did not authorize anyone to sign his name or withdraw money on his behalf.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: In a letter to Burrows and a later statement to an investigator, Wallington claimed that a strange man called at his home, stating he had Burrows' orders to collect his box and bank book, and that his (Wallington's) wife gave them to him. Wallington claimed he did not see the man himself.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Annie Gordon, Wallington's landlady, stated that the prisoner and his family left her house on February 27th, the day after the money was withdrawn. He gave no forwarding address and two letters for him from Dover were subsequently returned to the postman.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William Feeley Lovell, a Post Office investigator, confronted Wallington on April 30th. In Lovell's presence, Wallington wrote a statement reiterating his claim that his wife had given Burrows' book and clothes to a strange man.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: During his interview with Mr. Lovell, Wallington's manner was described as 'very excited, and he looked frightened.' This led Lovell to limit his inquiries to the bank book rather than the withdrawal itself.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: On cross-examination, Ernest Burrows stated that he found the prisoner 'very straightforward' while he was associated with him. Burrows said, 'I should not have thought such a thing of him.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The transcript notes that two additional, unnamed witnesses were called to testify to the prisoner's character.

The defense argued that Harding was not responsible for his actions due to insanity, citing a family history of mental illness, his own erratic behavior, and the potential effects of a prior syphilitic infection on his brain.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Elizabeth Collier, the victim's mother, testified that the prisoner, a married man, was paying attention to her daughter, Florence Varney. Harding had told her he was legally separated from his wife, but this was untrue. For eight months, he had been taking his meals at their house.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Police Inspector Charles Miller testified that when he informed Harding that Florence Varney was dead, the prisoner's only response was to ask, 'Is she dead?' Miller noted he 'appeared quite callous' and expressed no sorrow or regret.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mrs. Collier, though a prosecution witness, stated that both she and her deceased daughter had remarked on the prisoner's behavior, saying, 'He cannot be right in his mind to do such a silly thing as that,' referring to his extreme jealousy.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Richard Brazier Harding, the prisoner's father, testified that his son had a lifelong excitable temperament and had undergone a marked change in the last 18 months, showing 'vacancy of mind.' He also provided a detailed history of insanity on both sides of the prisoner's family.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Dr. Pearce and Dr. Lattey, who both treated the prisoner, testified that he had contracted syphilis. They described him as not being 'well-balanced' and 'most excitable,' and stated that in a person with hereditary taint, syphilis could irritate the brain and develop latent mental weakness.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Ernest Fry, the prisoner's next-door neighbor, testified to numerous eccentric acts. These included running down the street in his nightshirt, shouting and playing the piano late at night, and having no memory of these events afterwards. The witness stated the prisoner was known in the neighborhood as 'Mad Tom.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Arthur Smith, a commercial traveller, testified that just two days before the murder, the prisoner's conduct was 'extraordinary.' Harding suddenly seized him by the collar and appeared to be talking to an imaginary third person, before darting after a random girl in the street. Smith's impression was that Harding was 'out of his mind.'

An employee testified it would be possible for a person to reach the portfolio through the pigeon-holes in the counter's protective netting.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: As Head Policy Clerk for six years, the prisoner Frederick Oelrichs's duties included writing and checking insurance policies, ensuring the correct stamps were affixed. Thirty-one policies that he had initialed as correct were found to have been tampered with.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: A fellow clerk, Mr. Wardell, testified that about a year prior, he pointed out a suspicious, altered stamp to Oelrichs. Oelrichs replied, 'let the matter pass, and don't say anything more about it.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Another clerk, Mr. Nicholls, found three tampered policies in the prisoner's personal clause-box. When confronted, Oelrichs said, 'Oh, yes; I can account for it,' but never did.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: A clerk named Magrath was in charge of all policy stamps, kept under lock and key. He was dismissed around the time the fraud was discovered for 'inattention to work' after a discrepancy was found in his stamp balance.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mr. Merriman testified that Oelrichs's character had been good for his six and a half years of employment. He did his work well and the manager had no fault to find with him before this incident.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Thomas Goodson, a barman, testified that on September 22nd, the prisoner Thomas Shepherd tendered a two-shilling piece for a cigar. Goodson examined the coin, found it to be bad, and broke it in half with his fingers in front of Shepherd.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The barman, Goodson, stated that while Shepherd and his companion had been drinking, they were not drunk and knew what they were doing. He added that he would not have served Shepherd if he had been drunk.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Policeman Herbert Swain, who arrested the men, testified that 'Shepherd was drunk; Davis was sober.' He also noted that Shepherd was very quiet in his presence and did not use any reckless language.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Upon searching Shepherd at the police station, Policeman Swain found a significant amount of good money (£1 in gold, 19s. 6d. in silver, 1s. 5d. in bronze) and one additional counterfeit florin.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Policeman Swain also searched Shepherd's companion, Davis, and found a counterfeit florin and a portion of a second one, along with good money.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The cab driver, Horace Brewer, testified that after dropping the prisoners at the police station, a subsequent fare found packets of coins in the cab. A further search revealed a total of eighty-eight counterfeit coins in seven packets.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William John Webster, Inspector of Coin to the Mint, testified that all the coins were bad. He stated the florin found on Shepherd and one found on Davis were from the same mould. He also noted the large quantity of coins from the cab were wrapped in newspaper, a method typical of 'smashers' (coin counterfeiters).
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Under cross-examination, James Davis stated that he had met Shepherd for the first time that day around midday at a police court. He claimed to have only known him for about ten minutes before spending the rest of the day with him.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Policeman William Bennett testified that he was on duty at the Bow Street Police-station door when the cab carrying Shepherd and Davis arrived. He stated that he watched the cab until the driver returned and drove away, and that 'no one entered the cab during that time or put anything into it, or I must have seen it.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Testimony from Alfred William Knapp, a clerk for the Financial News, that he bought a copy of a pamphlet libelling Harry Marks in Threadneedle Street. The pamphlet was being openly sold in large numbers throughout the City with placards advertising it.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: A clerk to Marks's solicitors testified that following a prior lawsuit which Marks won, Butterfield was presented with costs of £940 and was made bankrupt by Marks's party when he could not pay.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mrs. Annie Koppel testified that after her husband's death, Harry Marks exploited her vulnerability, convincing her to grant him power of attorney over her late husband's newspaper and estate, effectively seizing control.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: During cross-examination, the prosecution produced numerous receipts signed by Mrs. Koppel for hundreds of dollars from Marks. They also produced a sworn statement, signed by her, retracting all accusations and affirming Marks had acted in a 'most upright, honest, and gentlemanly way.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Harry Marks testified, denying all of Koppel's allegations. He claimed the relationship was consensual, he bought the newspaper fairly (producing a receipt for $500), supported her financially (referencing the receipts), and only had her arrested after she violently destroyed his office.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Frederick John Smith testified that he was paid £200 by Marks to act as a nominee 'vendor' for the Rae Gold Mining Company. Smith had no property to sell; he merely signed documents and endorsed cheques worth tens of thousands of pounds which were immediately returned, in a sham transaction.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Harry Marks testified that using a nominee vendor for the Rae Mine is standard practice. He claimed the large share applications from nominees were a legitimate modification of the vendor contract, and that advice in his newspaper was based on an honest belief in the mine's potential.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Process servers testified they were unable to locate and serve subpoenas on key witnesses connected to Marks and the Rae Mine, including his stockbroker Frank Barnard and his sub-editor Arthur Cohen, who was reported to be ill.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: The jury found the defendant, Butterfield, NOT GUILTY. They added that they found the alleged libel was true, that the plea of justification was made out, and that the publication was for the public benefit.

Description: On October 1st, the prisoner, Jacob De Kromme, visited the warehouse managed by Samuel Dash. De Kromme offered Dash a sovereign, stating, 'I will make it all right with you,' which Dash returned.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: John Thomas Whitley, the senior partner of the firm, testified that the 184 pieces of floorcloth were valued at £150 and that Dash had no authority to sell them for less.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Frank Mason, a clerk, testified that on October 1st, after making a purchase, De Kromme pointed to another, more valuable lot of goods not sold to him and tried to claim it, saying 'Now that lot.' Mason refused to let him take it.

[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mr. Whitley testified that a stocktaking on June 30th had revealed a large deficiency in stock over the entire business. Dash was put in charge of the warehouse on July 1st.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Carver testified that on November 16th, the prisoner Richard Hirst, who was acting as a picket during a strike, followed him from his workplace. Carver stated that Hirst was abusing him with 'filthy language' and calling him a 'b------ old rat' for taking work from other people.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Arthur Skeats, a clerk, testified that he saw Hirst following Carver and talking to him 'rather excitedly.' He stated, 'at the corner of West Harding Street a cart intervened; after the cart had passed I saw them turning down West Harding Street.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Edward Tickner, a fellow picket, testified for the defense. He stated, 'I did not lose sight of the two men all the time—Hirst did not raise his hand to Carver, or strike him in the face—if such a thing had occurred I must have seen it.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Defense witness Edward Tickner directly contradicted prosecution witness Arthur Skeats on a key detail. When asked by the jury about the cart Skeats mentioned, Tickner stated, 'There was no cart in the road; it was quite clear.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Richard Shaw Bond, a partner in the firm and Hirst's former employer, testified on cross-examination that Hirst 'bore the character of a peaceable man while with us.' Hirst had been with the firm for about two and a half years.

public.game_background: Bernhardt Burghardt Assmus was indicted for unlawfully obtaining money by false pretences from Theodore Buhl and Maurice Giwelb by selling them forged and chemically altered postage stamps, representing them as rare and valuable genuine items.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Bernhardt either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Theodore Buhl, a stamp dealer, testified that the defendant, Assmus, showed him a black penny English stamp with 'V.R.' in the corners. Buhl immediately identified it as a forgery, stating the original Maltese crosses had been erased and the 'V.R.' letters printed in their place. Assmus became indignant, claiming it was genuine.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Maurice Giwelb, another stamp dealer, testified that on August 12, 1890, he purchased the forged black 'V.R.' stamp from Assmus for £4. Assmus claimed he acquired it from an old gentleman who had owned it for twenty years. Giwelb later discovered the stamp was a forgery.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Charles Reya, a bookseller, testified that on December 2, 1890, Assmus sold him a forged four-anna Indian stamp with an inverted head for £7 10s. Assmus claimed it was worth £20-£25. Reya, not an expert, subsequently sold it to the dealer Stanley Gibbons for £15.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Harry Hilckes, manager for stamp dealers Bright and Son, testified that the defendant, using the name 'A. Bernhardt,' sold them forged Baden stamps. In subsequent letters, he introduced a 'Dr. Assmus' (also the defendant) as a separate party involved in a larger collection, thereby using aliases to further his dealings.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: A search of the defendant's person at the time of arrest yielded a wallet containing about 900 stamps, including 99 forged Baden stamps similar to those sold to Mr. Bright. The search also revealed two dies, one with the figure 10 and another with a French post-mark.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: To establish his personal status, Fryer claimed to have a 'large estate at Greenhithe, in Kent, for which I paid £20,000,' which provided an income of 'over £700 a year.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Fryer claimed, 'I am one of the surveyors of the London School Board,' and showed Cawthorn a letter related to a valuation to support this.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: As security, Fryer gave Cawthorn a cheque for £100 on the Western Bank of London, stating he always kept a 'good substantial balance'. The cheque was later returned marked 'account closed'.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Fryer's landlord, Arthur Horne, testified, 'this is a most rotten prosecution.' He stated he thought the paper would be a great success and was prepared to finance it himself before the legal proceedings began.

public.game_background: Frederick Pemberton Peach and Walter Selwyn were indicted for unlawfully obtaining £890 from George Willis Scears by false pretences, and for conspiracy to defraud, by inducing him to lend money on stolen Turkish Priority bonds.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Frederick either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Testimony from French railway and shipping agents (Porche, Pombei, Carpentier, Dubois) established that a sealed parcel containing 425 Turkish Priority bonds was dispatched from Paris on January 11th, 1890. A porter at Folkestone (Downes) testified that when the safe containing the parcels arrived, two of the four parcels were missing.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: George Scears, the victim, testified that Peach approached him in July 1891 proposing a loan of £1,000 for a Mr. Archibald Melville (the defendant Selwyn). Peach claimed Melville was a wealthy man about to marry an American banker's daughter and needed the money for a deposit on an estate, with the bonds to be placed in a marriage settlement.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: George Kennett, a clerk at the Union Bank, testified that in November, Scears deposited the bonds. When Kennett cut off the September coupons and sent them to the Imperial Ottoman Bank for payment, he was informed they had been stopped because the bonds were stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Joseph Hunter, a solicitor from Eastbourne, testified that he witnessed the signature 'A. H. Melville' on the loan agreement. He identified the defendant Selwyn as the man who signed the document, with Peach also present.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Sarah Beeby, a boarding-house keeper in Eastbourne, identified Selwyn as the man who stayed at her establishment as 'Mr. Melville' from July 22-25. She further testified that after Peach's arrest, Selwyn returned in January and stayed under the alias 'Retlaw Newles'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Testimony from various bank cashiers and pawnbrokers traced the bank notes given by Scears. Several £100 notes were quickly exchanged for foreign currency on July 27-28. One note, paid into Barclay, Bevan, and Co., was endorsed 'A. H. Melville'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: John Serjeant Blake, a house agent, testified that in March 1891, he let a house to Peach, who used the alias 'Henry Harcourt'. Peach gave as his reference 'Mr. Walter Selwyn' of a nearby address, and Selwyn provided a written recommendation for him.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: James Montague, a bank clerk, testified that the defendant Selwyn opened an account in September 1890 under the name 'Percy Melville'. He did so using a letter of introduction from 'Captain Selwyn' (himself), recommending his 'brother-in-law' Melville.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Inspector Abberline testified that he found 62 coupons, dated March 1890 and cut from the bonds given to Scears, inside a hat-box left at Victoria Station. The cloak-room ticket for the box was recovered from a known bond thief named Powell, who was arrested at Calais.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Inspector Abberline testified that on December 29th, after Peach's arrest, he went to Peach's house. With the assistance of Mrs. Peach, he recovered a parcel containing the remaining 23 stolen £100 Turkish Priority bonds from a hiding place under the floorboards.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: On cross-examination, Scears admitted that he had many previous financial dealings with Peach over 12-13 years, including transactions involving bonds, which had all been satisfactory.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Alfred George Cole, manager for Edward Pink, testified that the fruit baskets are loaned to his firm and are meant to be returned to their owners. They are not sold or otherwise disposed of.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mr. Cole stated that his carman, Langham, had no legitimate business reason to take baskets to 5 Sandys Street, the location where the baskets were found.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Joseph Lockhart, a carman, testified that he was hired by the defendant, Isaac Griszard, to move 500-600 baskets from the cellar at 5 Sandys Street at 2 a.m. and dump them in Covent Garden.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Dancie Donelly, another carman, corroborated Lockhart's account, stating he also helped load five to six hundred baskets from the cellar onto vans at 2 a.m. at Griszard's direction.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Detective-sergeant Abbott testified that upon searching the cellar at 5 Sandys Street, he found 122 fruit baskets, along with a paint-pot and a brush.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Constable Fulcher testified that the prisoner told him, 'This comes from not being a scholar; if I could read I could have taken the baskets back to the owners.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Several fruit salesmen, including James Fowler, admitted under cross-examination that they do not keep a strict account of baskets leaving or being returned from large customers like Pink.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: William Burgess and other defense witnesses testified that they had known the prisoner for years in the market as a legitimate buyer and seller of baskets and barrels.

public.game_background: John Stewart Smith was charged with the wilful murder of his wife, Amelia Stewart Smith. The prosecution alleged that during a drunken quarrel, he deliberately beat her to death with a poker. The defense contended that the victim, a habitual drunkard, provoked the attack by throwing the poker first, and that the defendant's subsequent actions, while unlawful, amounted to manslaughter rather than murder.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Mary Burden testified that on the night before the death, the prisoner came home, was angry that his drunken wife was lying down, and she heard him pull her out of bed followed by the sound of a blow.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Mary Burden testified that on the evening of the death, the prisoner came home very drunk. After a 'little bit of wrangling' over tea, his wife went out and returned at 10:30 PM, after which the fatal altercation began.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When Mary Burden tried to intervene in the assault, the prisoner told her to 'go upstairs and mind my own business, or else I should have the same result,' and then said, 'You can have me locked up if you like.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The son, John Smith, testified that after the altercation, his mother came downstairs with 'a little blood on the back of her head.' He offered to tie it up, but she declined.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: When the son discovered his mother was dead the next morning, the prisoner came upstairs and exclaimed, 'Oh, my God! she is dead. What shall I do?' and immediately went to find a doctor.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Inspector Sewell described the scene, noting 'a very great quantity of blood on her clothes and on the bed,' blood on the stairs, and a blood-soaked pillow found downstairs.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The medical examiner, Dr. Knight, determined the cause of death to be syncope (heart failure) from loss of blood from a 'contused, bruised wound' at the back of the head. An iron bar like the poker could have caused it.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Dr. Knight also found a severe internal bruise to the bowel and pancreas, consistent with a fall onto a hard object or a kick, which would have produced considerable shock.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Dr. Knight testified that death resulted from a slow bleed over hours and 'her life might have been saved' with medical attention. He also noted her poor health (alcoholic liver, diseased heart valve) made her more susceptible to syncope.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: A writ was issued against Isaac Beeman's firm on March 18th, 1893, in an action for £600 on a bill of exchange. This indicated imminent financial distress and potential legal proceedings.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Egerton Spencer Grey, the Assistant Official Receiver, testified that due to missing books, particularly the journal, he was unable to trace transactions or understand Beeman's business methods. He stated there was 'no book to enable me to trace goods from the vendor to the purchaser.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Thomas Asshenden, a hop grower, testified that Beeman, acting as his agent, sold his hops and sent him a cheque for £343. Mr. Asshenden was never told who the buyer was and believed this was the full price obtained, minus commission.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The Official Receiver, Mr. Grey, pointed out large summary entries in Beeman's ledgers under 'Extra commission, differences, etc.', amounting to thousands of pounds annually (e.g., £6,087 in 1885, £3,070 in 1889). No details for these substantial sums were found in the remaining books.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The defense, through cross-examination of Alfred Springett, brought up a transaction where Beeman and a partner took over hops at £6 3s. and sold them at £6, incurring a loss. This was presented as evidence that Beeman took on market risks.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Multiple witnesses testified that it was common for merchants to be given credit (e.g., payment via acceptances), while factors like Beeman were expected to pay growers 'prompt' cash on the second Saturday after a sale. This meant Beeman often financed the deals himself, taking on risk.

[EVIDENCE ev1]

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Hawse, a neighbor living opposite, testified that he heard a 'very loud smash, as if of glass' and saw that the top landing window was 'smashed completely through.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: When Policeman William Spencer arrested the prisoner for wilfully throwing his wife through the window, Arthur Crouch replied, 'Yes, and I wish she had stopped there, the b—'.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Police officers Spencer and Bristow both testified that the prisoner, Arthur Crouch, was 'sober' or 'perfectly sober'. In contrast, they and other witnesses noted Mary Crouch had been drinking, though she was not incapacitated.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The landlady, Rosina Porter, stated that on the day of the incident Mary Crouch had been 'out all day drinking' and was a 'very bad-tempered woman'. She said when the prisoner came home, there was no tea or fire ready for him.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The landing window was described as being very low to the floor (about two feet), and the landing itself was very narrow (about 3 feet 4 inches wide). The landlady testified that in a scuffle, someone 'could easily fall through if slightly pushed.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Dr. George Robertson testified that the victim's primary injury was a severe lacerated wound on her left hand, consistent with being caused by glass. She had no signs of collapse from the fall.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The night before the murder, the victim, Alfred Carter, confronted the prisoner about money owed and for scolding the children. The prisoner, who was 'very tipsy,' called his brother a liar and threatened to hit him.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: A note written by the prisoner was found: 'Dear Brother and Sister,—My troubles are too much on one brother. Kind love to all.—W. J. C.' His niece testified he wrote it the night before the murder, telling her, 'Your mother will hear of this to-morrow.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The victim's widow, Sarah Ann Carter, testified that the prisoner had a history of self-harm. On one occasion in September, he attempted to cut his throat and later tried to drown himself in a water closet. On another occasion, he ingested carbolic acid.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The prisoner's sister, Anna Maria Riley, testified to a family history of mental illness. She stated that another brother, Frank, has suffered from epileptic fits from birth, and an aunt became insane after childbirth and died in a lunatic asylum.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Eyewitness George Hitchen testified he saw the prisoner 'come down the shop with a knife in his hand... passed me up to his brother very quietly, and thrust the knife into his breast, and said, “That is how I will serve you”. He then dropped the knife, and walked quietly out of the shop, as if nothing had happened.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Multiple witnesses who saw the prisoner in the market shortly before the murder testified that they saw 'no difference in his manner' and that 'he seemed all right, as usual.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Upon being charged with murder, the prisoner stated, 'I am very sorry, I did not know what I was doing.' He consistently maintained in statements and letters that he had no recollection of the stabbing.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: On the morning of the murder, the prisoner was present while a set of butcher's knives were being sharpened. He later took one of these knives and used it to kill his brother.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: A few months before the murder, the prisoner told his sister that he suffered from 'queer pains in his head and felt very queer at times, he hardly knew what he was doing at times.'

[EVIDENCE ev_ww_01]
Description: Prosecutor George Warwick testified that in a prior transaction, the defendant purchased a diamond necklet from him using a bill and a cheque for approximately £200. Both the bill and the cheque were dishonoured upon presentation.
[EVIDENCE ev_ww_06]
Description: Warwick admitted under cross-examination that no one else was present during his conversations with the defendant. He stated, "I can call no one to corroborate my statements... we were the only two there."
[EVIDENCE ev_ww_07]
Description: Warwick testified that he was an uncertificated bankrupt and his discharge had been suspended for ten years. The defense suggested the Registrar called it "the worst case he had ever heard."
[EVIDENCE ev_ww_08]
Description: Under cross-examination, Warwick admitted to having gone through a bigamous marriage in 1885, though he claimed it was a "matter of error."
[EVIDENCE ev_ww_10]
Description: Warwick admitted to seeing the defendant three or four times in the City after the alleged theft but did not attempt to have him arrested. His reason was that he "did not want to throw more money away over a prosecution."
[EVIDENCE ev_ww_11]
Description: Warwick admitted he was aware from the beginning that the defendant's defense was that the jewellery was given to him to pawn. This defense effectively accused Warwick of committing perjury.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Victim William Thompson testified that at 12:35 a.m., the three prisoners approached him, hustled and tripped him up. When he attempted to rise, they knocked him down again. He stated he felt John Davis's hands in his pockets.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Constable Arthur Bird testified that about two minutes after seeing Thompson, he went round the corner and saw the prosecutor lying in the road with the three prisoners over him. When he approached, the prisoners ran away.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: In his defense, John Davis claimed that he had been pushed onto the prosecutor by two other men. He stated that William Davis and James Heath were innocent and that he had given the police the names of the real culprits.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Inspector James Nairn testified that he made inquiries based on information from John Davis and Heath about two other men. He stated he could find no corroboration of their story.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: George Stoneman, who lives in the same house as William Davis, testified that he saw William Davis come in at 12:25 or 12:30 a.m. on the night of the assault. He later heard Ada Davis knock at the front door.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: On re-examination, both William Thompson and Constable Bird testified that it was not raining at the time of the assault. Constable Bird stated, 'when I went on duty at 9.30 p.m., I had not to wear my cape... there was no rain after that.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: After the verdict, John Davis pleaded guilty to a prior conviction of felony from 1891, and William Davis pleaded guilty to a prior conviction from 1889.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Following the verdict, Constable Bird testified that Heath was an 'expert pickpocket' and did no work to his knowledge.

The prosecution contended the note was for Watson's legitimate share of their dissolved partnership.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: In an affidavit, the prisoner Alfred Letts stated that he was not indebted to the plaintiff Walter Watson, that the promissory note for £500 was made for the accommodation of the plaintiff without consideration, and that the plaintiff was not a holder for value.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Walter Watson testified that he had invested approximately £500 in the partnership business with Letts. He stated he entered the business supposing Letts understood it.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The prosecution produced a letter written by Letts two months before the dissolution. In it, Letts confirmed an offer for Watson to give up his share in the business and receive £500, with Letts taking over all liabilities and assets.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Solicitor George Grant testified that after the dissolution, he asked Letts for payment of interest on the note about half a dozen times. Letts never suggested the note was for accommodation, but would say he was not in a position to pay.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Grant testified that in 1888, Letts approached him at a club and asked him to use his influence with Mr. Watson to accept £50 in full discharge of the £500 promissory note, as he was not in a position to pay the full amount.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: On cross-examination, Walter Watson admitted that the partnership lost money, that towards the end there was 'no business doing', that at least one execution was put in before he left, and that the landlord eventually took the office furniture.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: When questioned about taking out a summons at the Police-court for perjury, Walter Watson admitted, 'I suppose I thought that would be a good way of getting the money.' He added that the idea was not necessarily that friends would find the money, but simply 'to get the money'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The Recorder expressed his opinion to the jury that after hearing the evidence (particularly from Watson), 'it would be difficult for the JURY to find a verdict of Guilty.' The jury immediately returned a unanimous verdict of NOT GUILTY.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: An officer from the Bankruptcy Court testified that the defendant, B. F. Nash, was an undischarged bankrupt as of April 1893. He had failed to attend his public examination, and a warrant had been issued against him.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: A clerk from the Registrar of Joint Stock Companies produced a file for the 'National Provincial Press Company, Limited,' but testified that he found no registration for the 'Brighton News Company, Limited.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Thomas Hewitt paid Nash £100 for preference shares and a £50 premium to become an 'articled pupil' of the company, based on the promise of a job with a salary of £120 a year.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Violet Moss testified that she had advanced Nash £320. As security, he gave her 4,000 ordinary shares, stating they were 'worth nothing then' but hoped they would be if the venture was a success.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Edward Roland Crawley, a solicitor, testified that he never consented to be the solicitor for the Brighton Newspaper Company or to have his name appear on its prospectus.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Perry Fisher Sparks, a clerk at the Union Bank in Brighton, testified that the bank never consented to act as bankers for the Brighton Newspaper Company, and no authority was given for its name to appear on the prospectus.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Hewitt admitted under cross-examination that numerous replies to advertisements began to arrive and that by Christmas a large number of requests had been received. He also stated his impression that the business 'could be made a very good business.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: During cross-examination, Hewitt stated, 'I think if you had worked as you ought to have done the thing might have been made a success.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: In his defense, Nash stated he believed the Brighton paper would be a success but did not procure enough money. He claimed Hewitt knew it was a risk and that his plans failed due to illness, not an intention to defraud.

The prosecution argued Pledger sold spoiled goods at a steep discount, while the defense claimed the milk was merely 'thick' at the time of sale and deteriorated later.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Porter, the grocer who bought the milk, was previously fined £5 and costs because fifty-nine tins of the milk seized from his counter were deemed unfit for human food.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The tins had no labels on them when sold to Mr. Porter; they had been stripped off, though remnants were visible.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: A few days after his second purchase, Mr. Porter noticed some tins were 'bulged' and would not stand flat. Knowing this indicated fermentation, he hid them in the center of his display pile.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Mr. Porter testified that he sold the first batch of milk successfully, it 'sold fairly,' and his 'customers were satisfied with it.' He sold it as 'thick, but sweet.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: According to the court clerk's notes from Porter's trial, Pledger testified that he bought the milk at an auction for 5s. a case and sold it to Porter for 2s. a case.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mr. Porter stated in re-examination that the prisoner, Charles Pledger, was not present at either sale on August 21st or 28th. The sales were conducted by the prisoner's brother.

public.game_background: George Wilson was indicted for burglary in the dwelling-house of Thomas Randle. His co-accused, George Howden, pleaded guilty to the burglary. The prosecution argued that Wilson was a direct participant, evidenced by his possession of stolen goods and burglary tools shortly after the crime. The defense contended that Wilson was unaware the goods were stolen and was merely pawning them as a favor for Howden. The jury found Wilson guilty of receiving stolen goods.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Police Sergeant John Martin testified that when George Wilson was first shown two stolen spoons found in his possession, he claimed, 'a little man with a fair moustache, wearing a dark coat, gave me the spoons on that morning.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Upon being searched, a 'brace top' was found on Wilson's person. This was identified as part of a larger brace and bit tool found elsewhere.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Thomas Randle, the homeowner, testified that his house was locked up on the night of February 26th and he discovered it ransacked the next morning. A window had been forced, and he missed several silver spoons, sugar tongs, and an overcoat, which he identified in court.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Ernest Pearson, clerk at the lodging-house, confirmed that both Wilson and Howden had lodged there for about a year and he had seen them together. He also stated Wilson used the alias 'Oxley' and had taken a ticket for a bed on the night of the burglary.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George Sewell, a builder, testified that lead and chandeliers, worth about 12s. and 25s. respectively, were stolen from a property he was repairing. He stated they had been 'absolutely wrenched away from the building'.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: George Drechster, who lives near Holloway's shop, testified that he saw George Smith (the co-accused who pleaded guilty) come out of a nearby house with something bulky under his arm on the evening of the theft.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: During the police investigation, the confessed thief, Smith, pointed at Holloway and said, 'I believe that is the man I bought it of.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Sergeant Badcock testified that when he first questioned Holloway about buying lead pipe, Holloway replied 'No'.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Sergeant Knott testified that Holloway and his brother gave him a description of Smith and pointed out the neighborhood where he could be found, assisting in his capture.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William Brown, the employee, testified that Holloway's wife looks after the business when he is out, and that another boy is also employed. He also stated that another lot of lead could have been bought when Holloway was out.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Sergeant Badcock testified that when he went to search Holloway's property, there was a quantity of old lead in the stable which Holloway did not attempt to conceal.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The court record states that 'Holloway received a good character.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Alfred Saville testified that after leaving a club, the prisoner, Frank Tarbeaux (Taylor), asked for a lift and then invited him and Arthur Cockburn into his home for a drink.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Constable Matherson noted that all parties, including the prisoner and victims, 'seemed to have been drinking heavily.' The room showed signs of a struggle, with broken glasses and a broken vase.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Inspector Michael Crawford testified that the prisoner, Frank Tarbeaux, was admitted to bail and subsequently failed to surrender for his trial. He was eventually arrested on a warrant at the Cape of Good Hope. His accomplice, Woodward, also absconded.

The funds, amounting to approximately £470 from 'Bennett's Charity', were intended for the benefit of poor parishioners. The defendant was accused of selling the trust's stock and depositing the proceeds into his personal, overdrawn bank account.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: An official from the Probate Registry produced the original will of Mary Bennett, executed in 1849, which bequeathed funds in trust for the benefit of twelve poor persons of the parish of Great Greenford. This established the existence and purpose of the trust fund in question.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Testimony established the defendant's precarious financial situation. His net income from the living was only about £260-£375 per year, and he had significant financial obligations and debts to moneylenders, leading to bankruptcy.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: When churchwarden Alfred Perkins first inquired about the location of the Bennett's Charity stock, Mr. Biscoe told him it was with the Charity Commissioners. This was the first of several misleading statements.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: When threatened with arrest, Mr. Biscoe stated that taking out a warrant would do no good as his living was under sequestration, meaning no money could be recovered from him.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: A stockbroker, Julius Warberg, testified that he sold stock on the defendant's behalf on two separate occasions: £200 in April 1892 and the remaining £263 in December 1893, giving the proceeds directly to Mr. Biscoe.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The defendant stated he hoped the money would be all returned and that he would communicate with his friends to achieve this. He also stated this in a letter to his solicitor.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Despite having sold the trust's capital, the defendant continued to distribute funds to the charity's beneficiaries. The chief prosecution witness saw him personally distribute the funds at Christmas 1894.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: At the conclusion of the trial, evidence was presented that the prisoner received an excellent character.

Description: Samuel Norman, the homeowner, testified that he left his premises secure around midday on Christmas Day and returned in the evening to find the police there and the lock on a side-door tampered with.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Witness Tom Brown testified that upon leaving a pub opposite the victim's house, he saw two men, heard his brother-in-law's dog bark, and then went to the side door. He stated that the defendant, Walsh, came down, mumbled something, pushed past him, and ran away.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: According to multiple police officers, upon his arrest Walsh stated he was innocent and provided an alibi. He claimed he was indoors from 3 pm until 8 pm, then went out with friends for beer and cigars, returned home, and later went to Baker Street station to see friends off.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Inspector William Richardson testified that he heard the co-accused, Bradock, say in Police-court that Walsh was not the man who was with him during the burglary.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Harriet Chatten, Walsh's landlady, testified that on Christmas night Walsh was in her rooms from between 3 and 4 p.m. until nearly eleven. She stated he sat and played dominoes with her son and other guests and did not leave the house.

Description: A letter written by the prisoner, dated July 18th, 1895, was presented as evidence. In it, he signed off as 'Your affectionate husband and father, Thomas Haydon,' acknowledging his relationship with Marie Hoyez and their children.

Description: William Smith, clerk to the Guardians of the Romford Union, testified that in 1886 the prisoner was at the workhouse and recognized his wife, Marie Hoyez, and their children when they arrived after being deserted by him.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Midwife Eliza Randall testified that the twin children were 'very healthy and bright' at birth and for the first few days of their lives.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The landlady, Harriet Morgan, testified that about a month before they died, the twins 'became very frail, and threw their food up.' She stated that Julia Price said she did not have sufficient food or money to care for them.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The family's room was described by the landlady as poorly furnished, with the older children sleeping on the floor on 'a piece of coarse sacking.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The post-mortem examination on Frederick William, conducted by Dr. Turtle, found no evidence of disease. The doctor attributed the child's 'fearful emaciation' to 'the want of proper nourishment.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The defendant's employer, Frederick Truby, testified in his defense that he had never seen Charles Price drunk and 'always found him steady while he worked for me.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: In his defense, Charles Price claimed the children were 'murdered by Mrs. Morgan's sour milk' which caused them to vomit and waste away.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Joseph Cowell testified that he encountered a police constable in Alfred Mews, asked him if he had cleared out the 'roughs,' and replied 'To my superior, I suppose' when challenged. Immediately after, he was struck down and lost consciousness.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Dr. Wills and Dr. Mudd testified that Cowell suffered severe injuries, including multiple facial fractures, requiring 'great violence.' Both suggested the injuries were consistent with being struck by the lens of the police lantern produced in court.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Inspector Stewart testified that the defendant, PC Lewis, came to the station at 10:25 PM asking for an ambulance, stating 'a man drunk had fallen down in Alfred Mews.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: On cross-examination, Cowell admitted to a prior conviction for assault where he was fined £4. He also admitted to being disliked in the neighborhood and stated, 'The police don't like me and I don't like them.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Inspector Sutherland testified that on October 31st, he examined the defendant's truncheon and clothing and found no marks or blood-stains.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: James Herbert Field, the employer, testified that on January 13th he missed his cheque book from his locked private drawer. He confirmed the forged £50 cheque presented at the bank was a leaf from this stolen book.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Bank cashier William Board positively identified the defendant, Edward Gyseman, in court as the individual who presented the forged cheque.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: When Detective Pollard arrested Gyseman, Gyseman replied, 'I do not know anything about the forgery; why don't you look for the thief'.

Description: Upon seeing Lear at the police station, Gyseman said to him, 'Halloa, you here? had it not been for that telegram I should not have been here'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In his own defense statement to the court, Gyseman's only remark was: 'I burnt the cheque book; I did not want nothing found on me'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Nathaniel Hopkins Burgh, a clerk for the prosecuting firm, testified that the defendant, William Crawford, represented himself as having an option in Lake View Shares that would yield a 'handsome profit of £2,000 or £3,000.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Testimony from four separate lodging-house keepers (Thomas Winter, Christina Beckton, Elizabeth Cowell, Oscar Clausseon) established that between 1896 and the end of 1897, Crawford consistently failed to pay his rent, absconding while owing money.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Bank clerks Philip Mortimer and Chas. James Farley testified that Crawford's accounts at the London and County Bank and the City Bank had been closed by the banks in December 1896 and February 1897, respectively, due to overdraft or lack of funds.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Lodging-house keeper Thomas Winter testified that Crawford gave him a cheque for £10 15s. on the London and County Bank account, which was returned because the account was closed.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: James Muir, a partner at another stockbroking firm, testified that Crawford had transactions with his firm and left owing £594. A judgment was obtained against Crawford for the balance but was never satisfied.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: James Thompson Currie, an acquaintance of the defendant, testified that Crawford had been unemployed for about four years. He also stated that Crawford passed a bad cheque under the assumed name 'William Seaford' and borrowed money from him.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Upon being arrested and having the warrant read to him, the defendant stated, 'I did not think they could take criminal proceedings against me.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: In his defense, Crawford produced a written statement admitting he had gambled on the Stock Exchange, had been unable to meet his liabilities, and had 'merely exaggerated his position with a view to obtain credit.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The victim, Charles Fay, testified that after leaving a theatre, he was followed by the prisoner and others. He stated they knocked him down, kicked him, put their hands in his pockets, and stole 8 shillings. Fay identified the prisoner as one of the assailants.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Charles Shaw, a 13-year-old boy who was with Fay, corroborated the victim's account. He testified that the prisoner and another man followed them, pushed him away, knocked Mr. Fay down, kicked him, and went through his pockets, at which point he ran to find a policeman.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Policeman John Harris testified that upon his arrival, the prosecutor immediately identified the prisoner and another man, stating, 'Constable, there are the two men that robbed me.' Harris then saw the men run, shouted 'Stop thief,' and pursued the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Policeman Harris stated in his testimony that 'The prosecutor was under the influence of drink.' He also observed that Fay 'had been handled very roughly' and was lying on the ground when he arrived.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Constable Herbert Tanbury, who assisted in the chase, testified that upon catching the prisoner, he said, 'I am done for, if you will leave go of my neck I will go quietly, I have done nothing.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Constable Tanbury also gave his assessment of the victim's state, testifying that 'the prosecutor had been drinking, but he knew what he was doing.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Constable Tanbury stated that the prisoner gave his correct name and address upon arrest. He also noted that the prisoner 'has been doing some work' but that he had 'lost sight of him lately.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner's defense was that he saw the prosecutor asking for someone to see him home. He claimed he and his companion were giving Fay 'a hand' and that when his companion ran, he ran with him, having only just met the companion.
