information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Victim Henry Drury testified that on April 15th, he was kicked in the ankle by James Smith, who then snatched his watch chain. He stated this was the beginning of the assault and robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In his statement, Smith claimed the prosecutor wrongly accused him of stealing his chain. He said Drury laid hold of him, and he only 'struck him in the face with my hand' in response, after which several other men started 'knocking me about.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In his defense, Allen claimed he heard 'Stop thief!' and ran with others to help. He stated he was wrongly caught and nearly choked by two men, and denied seeing the struggle on the ground. He also admitted, 'I own I kicked the man.'

public.game_background: Marcus Leon and James Blumenthal were indicted for conspiracy to defraud, obtaining money by false pretences, and aiding others in the same. The prosecution alleged they operated a scheme where they introduced business owners in financial distress to supposed capitalists. They would then facilitate the creation of fraudulent 'accommodation bills,' disguised as payment for goods that were never supplied. These bills were discounted at banks, the proceeds split between the parties, and the defendants would take a large commission. The bills were ultimately dishonoured, leading to substantial losses for the banks and other financiers.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Marcus either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Witness Ernest Burckhardt testified that, being in financial difficulty, he advertised for a partner with capital and was contacted by the defendants, J. Blumenthal and Co. This establishes the initial contact and Burckhardt's vulnerability.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: John Howard, another victim, testified that Blumenthal explicitly proposed a scheme to 'draw or accept bills and divide the proceeds,' with the acceptor planning to fail before the bill's maturity. Leon also instructed him to tell the bank the bills were 'trade bills.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Henry Strachan Pringle testified to a nearly identical scheme. He was introduced to firms by the defendants, instructed by Leon to draw bills for odd amounts to appear as 'genuine trade bills,' and told to lie to bankers that they were for 'goods supplied.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Official Receiver Egerton Spencer Grey testified that the defendants kept no proper account books, only small 'Where is it?' address books. He also found commission notes relating to over 300 persons, of whom approximately 140 had become bankrupt.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: After Burckhardt pleaded guilty, Leon contacted him and said, 'if a search warrant is issued nothing will be found in our offices to embarrass us.' He also mentioned his doctor suggested he should go out of the country.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Evidence was produced of Leon's prior history, including three bankruptcies (one with liabilities of £40,000 and assets of 5s 8d) and a conviction in Hamburg in 1886 under a different name.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The defense's position, articulated in Leon's statement, was that they were legitimate financial agents making introductions and were not responsible for the subsequent actions of their clients. Leon's statement claimed 'misfortune had been the reason of his failure' and sought to 'exonerate his son-in-law (Blumenthal) from all blame.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Baron de Gourelli testified that while imprisoned, he wrote to a solicitor, Mr. Cudley. The defendant, Mason, visited him claiming to be Cudley's clerk. Believing him, the Baron gave Mason instructions for his defense and signed an authority for him to collect his luggage from the Covent Garden Hotel for safekeeping.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Charles George Cudley, the solicitor, testified that Mason was never in his employment. He stated he never authorised Mason to see Gourelli, collect his luggage, or undertake his defense, and had told Mason he wanted nothing to do with the case.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Policeman William Cranston testified that when he arrested the defendant and charged him with stealing the articles, he replied, 'I have nothing to say.'

The prosecution alleges he intentionally kept the money, faked being robbed, and gave the funds to a woman he was living with. Picken's defense suggests he was in debt to the firm, became intoxicated, and cannot fully account for his actions or the loss of the money.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edward Pitt, managing foreman, testified that on May 27th, the prisoner Walter Picken failed to account for goods amounting to £28 1s. 5d. Picken was responsible for selling goods from his van and returning either the collected cash or signed credit vouchers to the company, neither of which he did.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Mr. Pitt found that the strap of Picken's satchel was cut.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Multiple customers, including Walter Musto, Charles Bishop, and John Street, testified that they paid Picken in cash on the afternoon and evening of May 27th. All of them stated that Picken was 'perfectly sober' at the time of their transactions.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: John Street testified that about three weeks before the incident, Picken had asked to borrow £3 or £4 from him.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The van guard, John Haley, testified that while at the Sailor Prince pub, Picken 'went out for about ten minutes.' He noted that Earlsfield Station was about 'half a minute's walk' away.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Four labourers who were in the tap-room of the Sailor Prince at the same table as Picken all testified that they 'did not interfere with him' and that no one else interfered with him.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: In his defense, Picken stated he was in debt to the firm 'owing to the bad season' and that 'There is a system in the firm that when a man gets into debt to make a loan of it, and give them I O U's.' He admitted giving £24 to the young woman.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edward James James testified that Horner, a regular customer, vouched for George Emmett, calling him a 'big exhibition man,' and persuaded Mr. James to cash a cheque for £2 15s., saying 'stand on me; it will be all right.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Mary Ann Popham testified that on April 23rd, Horner, whom she knew as a customer, asked her to cash a second cheque for £1 7s. 6d. This cheque was drawn by 'H. L. Collins' in favour of Horner.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Charles Borham testified he saw Emmett outside the Pophams' pub on April 25th, that Emmett claimed to be 'stony' (broke), and that Emmett and Horner left the pub together.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Ed. Stewart Brand testified that the cheque cashed by Mrs. Popham was one of two missing from his personal chequebook, which he had left in Emmett's office. He stated he gave no one authority to fill it up.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: On cross-examination, Mr. Brand stated that Horner had visited Emmett's office on previous occasions because Emmett owed Horner money.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Detective James Ballard testified that upon arrest, Horner stated, 'Emmett owed me some money... Emmett got the cash, and gave me £1.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Walter Turner, a witness for Emmett, testified that he saw Mr. Brand hand Emmett a cheque and say, 'You had better keep this in case we want it.' He also stated he did not believe the 'Collier' signature was in Emmett's writing.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In his defense, Emmett stated that he gave Horner the first cheque to cash, promising him £1 of it, and claimed he did not intend to defraud Mr. James, later sending money via Horner to pay the cheque.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Mr. James testified that Horner asked him not to prosecute.

information:

Description: Walter Trip testified that on May 20th, Donald Stewart and a man known as 'Baron Romer' came to his tavern, and Stewart asked him to cash a £5 cheque signed by Romer.

[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: William Bond, manager to Imre Kiralfy, testified that they had no dealings whatsoever with the prisoner regarding the purchase of rifles.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: In his defense, Stewart claimed Trip invited them to play cards and that Romer suggested cashing the cheque, with Stewart merely vouching for him as a friend.
[EVIDENCE ev13]

[EVIDENCE ev14]

Description: Michael Shea stated that Johnson then struck him, and after being knocked down by others, Johnson kicked him while he was on the ground.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The victim, Percy Cornelius Pare, testified that he was walking in the City Road when he was hustled and received a severe blow to the side of his head. He stated he remembered nothing else after the blow.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Thomas Ryan, an errand boy, testified that he was two yards away and saw the defendant Taylor go behind Mr. Pare and strike him across the head with a stick. He stated that the defendant Cates was standing alongside Taylor at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Dr. Herbert Munday, the house-surgeon, testified that Mr. Pare had a cut on the right side of his head about 1.5 inches long, going down to the bone. He stated the wound could have been caused by a rather heavy blow from a stick.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Police-Sergeant Simon Court testified that a warrant was issued for the arrest of Alfred Golsborough, the other eyewitness with Ryan, to give evidence in the case. He stated that he had been unable to execute the warrant.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Police Constable Thomas Lye testified that when he arrested Taylor, Taylor stated, 'I am a ratepayer, and know nothing about it.' At the station, he added, 'I am a respectable man and rent four houses, and you will know about this to morrow.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Philip Goorvitch testified that he discovered multiple discrepancies between the ledger, kept by Bronstein, and the invoices for goods sold to customer Mr. Grosse. For example, on September 22nd, the ledger showed an entry of £2 17s. 11d., while the invoice was for £4 17s. 11d., a difference of exactly £2.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: After a warrant was issued for both Grosse and Bronstein, Goorvitch discovered Bronstein was gone. Police Sergeant William Reid confirmed he went to Bronstein's address but could not find him.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Henry Franks, Goorvitch's brother-in-law, testified that Bronstein came to his shop and confessed, saying, 'I have done a lot of trouble to him and I am guilty, but for the sake of my own children I ask for forgiveness.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Bronstein testified that he was paid only 5s. a week, plus a cup of tea or plate of soup, and some old clothes. Goorvitch confirmed the pay was 5s. a week and board, stating he was 'completely a father to him'.

Description: Abraham Levin, a skin merchant, testified that on June 20th, a case containing 967 musk skins and 250 skunk skins was stolen from the ground floor passage of his warehouse. He reported the theft to the police.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Walter Parker, a manager for the firm Nicholai, identified a fur coat found on the prisoner's premises as their property. He testified it was last seen in their stock in May and had not been sold.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Frederick George Ensor, a furrier, identified six pieces of squirrel skin, a black Thibet skin, and eight pieces of sealskin found on the prisoner's premises as his property. He testified they were never sold by him and he did not know how they were taken.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Morris Woolf, another furrier, identified 62 black Thibet skins as his property that he had lost. He also noted that 12 Persian skins found were very similar to some he had missed.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Solomon Bernard Lothien identified 15 opossum skins and other furs as his property. He testified they were part of goods lost during a burglary at his premises on February 24th.
[EVIDENCE ev8]

[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: A bank cashier testified that the prisoner's bank balance had declined from £116 in January to £1 8s. 10d. by the end of July, the month of his arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Officer Charles Douce testified that he went to the prisoner's premises on July 24th to make inquiries about stolen skins. He saw the skins and took the prisoner into custody.

information:

Description: Jeanie Barr testified that she is the sister of Catherine Green and was a witness at her wedding to the prisoner on December 29th, 1893, in Glasgow. She stated the prisoner was described as Henry Baranowski on the register which she signed.
[EVIDENCE ev_hg_02]
Description: Jeanie Barr, the first wife's sister, stated, 'I saw my sister last Sunday night.'
[EVIDENCE ev_hg_04]

public.game_background: Albert Edward Cavendish was charged with obtaining credit from De Keyser's Royal Hotel Co., Ltd., by false pretences. The prosecution argued that Cavendish checked into the hotel without luggage or the means to pay, falsely claiming that his luggage was arriving later, with the intent to defraud the hotel of payment for his room and board.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Albert either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Gustav Schmitzer, a clerk at De Keyser's Royal Hotel, testified that the prisoner arrived on the afternoon of April 27th without any luggage and asked for a room.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The next day, the general manager, Carl Saler, gave instructions to have the prisoner's bill (£1 1s. 6d.) presented to him.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The prisoner was found with two cloak-room tickets. Detective William Mathews followed up on them.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When asked by the detective if it was worth making inquiries at an address in Portsmouth he had given, the prisoner admitted, 'No, it is false.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Officer Alfred Lucas searched the prisoner and found on him 1.5d in cash, various small personal items, and a pawn-ticket for an overcoat.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: After the prisoner was found guilty, five previous convictions were proved against him.

Description: Thomas Norris testified that on April 10th, Mary Webb visited his home and gave him several pawn-tickets. He stated she said, 'As you have been a great friend to me you can have the tickets. I cannot afford to get them out myself.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Henry Pugh Lloyd, the homeowner, testified that he left his house locked at 3 p.m. on June 17th. Upon returning at 9:50 p.m., he could not enter and heard the door being bolted from inside. After a policeman helped him gain entry, he found the house in disarray, though nothing was stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Detective William Brogden testified that he saw the prisoner, whom he knew previously, passing near the prosecutor's shop around 9:20 p.m. on the night of the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Detective James Collins testified that upon arresting the prisoner and informing him of the charge, the prisoner replied, 'All right, sir'. The prisoner then refused to provide his address or any account of himself.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The prisoner's statement before the Magistrate was read, in which he stated, 'I have never been inside the house in my life.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: After being found guilty, the prisoner pleaded guilty to a previous conviction of felony from 1898. Three other convictions were also proven against him.

public.game_background: Thomas Frost Hubbard was charged with the manslaughter of Reginald Arthur Bolter, a four-year-old child. The prosecution alleged that Hubbard, while driving a Great Western Railway van in a state of intoxication, negligently drove onto a street refuge, running over and killing the child. The defense contended that the incident was a tragic accident caused by one of the horses shying, and that the prisoner was not drunk.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Thomas either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Simeon Mall, an eyewitness, testified that he saw a van and two horses 'going fast.' He stated the van 'ran into the middle of the refuge, where there were two or three little boys' and the wheel went over one of them.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Frederick Medhurst, a police pensioner who stopped the van, testified that the prisoner 'was drunk.' He described him as 'rolling about on the dicky, and his eyes were almost closed.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner received a good character reference.

The prosecution alleges that on the night of September 17th, 1900, Bell, in an intoxicated state, assaulted Chenning on the street after being refused a drink. The assault, witnessed by several neighbors, allegedly caused Chenning to fall and sustain head injuries, which resulted in fatal blood clots on the brain, leading to her death on September 26th.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Sarah Powis testified that she was with the deceased at the Greenland Fishers public-house when the prisoner, Sarah Bell, entered and asked the deceased for a drink. The deceased refused, stating, 'Not to-night, Sarah; I think you have had sufficient.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: When charged, the prisoner's defense was, 'I do not remember anything about it.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Witness Arthur Owen testified that he saw the two prisoners, Pinnock and Gibson, going through the pockets of a man who was surrounded by a small crowd. He stated that the man was waving a flag and the prisoners went through his pockets while he was standing up.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The victim of the alleged theft was never identified and is referred to in the indictment as "a man whose name is unknown." Consequently, the victim did not testify or report a crime.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In his defense, Pinnock stated on oath that he was on Ludgate Hill when he began sneezing and his false teeth came out. He claimed he stopped to replace them and was then suddenly seized by a man who accused him of stealing a watch.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Both Pinnock and Gibson, in their respective defenses, stated that they did not know each other. They were charged as co-conspirators.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Francis Mantelli testified that he hired the prisoner's cab (No. 120), loaded his luggage, and told the prisoner to wait at Ludgate Circus. When Mantelli returned 12-15 minutes later, the cab, the prisoner, and all his luggage had disappeared.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mantelli stated that at the police station, he identified the prisoner not by his appearance, but by his distinct stutter when he heard him speak.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Cab driver Arthur Shepherd testified that the prisoner hired him to transport the portmanteau and bag from his lodgings to the cloak-room at Portland Road Station.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The address on the prisoner's cab license was found to be a place he never resided, but only used to receive letters.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Henry Rendles testified that the prisoner had been in a 'bad state of health' since his wife died, suffering from gout and 'one thing and another'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective James Brown re-arrested the prisoner, John Bayley, and charged him with breaking and entering a shop in the City. Bayley responded, "I do not know the place."
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: In response to the charge, Bayley also stated, "You will have to prove I stole it; there was another man with me, but I will not round on him."
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Sidney Herbert Perrien, the London publisher for the victim's firm, testified that the show case produced in court was the property of the firm and was kept inside the office window.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Constable Dennis Daly testified that he was on duty in Fleet Street and saw the show case in the window, unbroken, at 3:35 a.m. on the early morning of December 30th.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner's statement before the Magistrate was, "Who saw me break the window?"

public.game_background: Edward Kingston Pike was charged with assaulting James Bristow and occasioning him actual bodily harm. The prosecution alleges that Pike, after being ejected from a public-house, assaulted Bristow outside, striking him twice and breaking his jaw in four places without provocation. The defense cross-examines witnesses to explore the context of the altercation and any events preceding the assault.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Edward either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1_bristow_attack]
Description: James Bristow testified, "the prisoner came out and knocked me down—while I was on the ground he hit me under my jaw... two blows were struck."
[EVIDENCE ev3_bristow_motive]
Description: James Bristow testified about his prior relationship with the prisoner: "I knew him before; we were always on friendly terms—we had no quarrel." He could not suggest any reason why Pike struck him.
[EVIDENCE ev5_dean_disturbance]
Description: Charles Dean, the manager, testified, "I turned the prisoner out—he was disorderly and causing a disturbance... he had been quarrelling with the prosecutor, who was in there."
[EVIDENCE ev7_williams_struggle]
Description: Henry Williams, a waiter, testified, "I saw the prisoner and the prosecutor struggling on the floor in the bar—the manager and the head barman parted them—the prisoner was turned out."
[EVIDENCE ev9_mouflet_attack]
Description: John Mouflet, a bystander, testified: "I saw the prisoner being ejected by the manager—a little while after I saw the prosecutor come out—the prisoner said to him, 'Now then,' and hit him a blow with his left hand, which knocked him down, and then he hit him again with his right hand on the jaw."
[EVIDENCE ev10_bell_medical]
Description: Dr. Victor Bell, the house-surgeon, testified that the prosecutor suffered "from a fractured jaw, caused probably by two blows—it was broken in about four places." He also detailed the severe and lasting nature of the injury.
[EVIDENCE ev11_prido_intoxication]
Description: Constable Daniel Prido testified that the prisoner "had been drinking, but he was not so drunk as not to know what he was doing."
[EVIDENCE ev12_bristow_compensation]
Description: James Bristow testified on re-examination: "I was offered ₤10 as compensation—I said I would not accept it, but would let justice take its own course."

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective Samuel Bacon testified that around 11:15 p.m., he saw the defendant, Patrick Barry, with James Devine and another man. After the three spoke, Devine entered the doorway of No. 98 Cheapside, while Barry positioned himself at the corner of the turning, 'looking up and down the street.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Samuel Alderton, the proprietor of the shop at 98 Cheapside, testified that his shop had been securely fastened and that he later saw an attempt had been made to open it.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Upon his arrest, a search of Barry revealed only about 4 1/2d. and a pen knife. No housebreaking instruments were found on his person.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: James Devine, the co-accused who was caught with the tools, pleaded guilty to the charges.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Rose Isaacs, a linen-draper, testified that on January 31st, Mary Blackalley attempted to pay for socks with a counterfeit half-crown. Isaacs bent the coin, returned it, and identified it as bad, after which Blackalley paid with good money.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Detective Sergeant David Cox testified that he and another sergeant had all three prisoners under observation for five weeks prior to the arrest. They were frequently seen together at the Bear public-house in Oxford Street.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: After the guilty verdict, it was revealed that Collins had nine previous convictions and Smith had five. Mary Blackalley was stated to be a known 'associate of coiners'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Hermann Kantorowich, the victim, testified that he left his workroom securely locked with both a padlock and a standard lock. When he returned on April 6th, he found the door had been forced open, with the staple for the padlock pulled out.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Solomon Levinski, a tailor, testified that he saw the prisoner, Paul Stockel, standing on the stairs near the warehouse. At that time, Mr. Levinski observed that the warehouse door was locked.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Robert Louis Cohen, a hairdresser in the building, heard a noise, saw the door was broken, and then witnessed the prisoner running away. Mr. Cohen immediately gave chase, shouting 'Stop thief!'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: After being arrested, the prisoner spoke in German to Robert Cohen, the witness who chased him, and asked him not to press the charge.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: In his official defense, the prisoner claimed that he was leaning against the warehouse door when it unexpectedly gave way.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prisoner gave a separate explanation to police at the station, stating, 'I only went there to beg.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The police investigation confirmed that nothing inside the warehouse was disturbed and no items were stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Two keys were found on the prisoner after his arrest, but police detectives confirmed that neither of them fit the lock on the victim's door.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Detective Sergeant Lyon testified that the prisoner gave an address at a lodging house where, upon investigation, it was found that he was known but did not actually live there.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Garbit, Mrs. Lewis's father, testified that he was in the Ivy House pub and saw the three prisoners enter around 10:30 p.m. He stated he saw them go out around 11:00 p.m., and about 10-15 minutes later, he heard a scream and saw a large group of them rush back in through a side door.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Detective Samuel Cox testified that upon being arrested, Amos said, 'All right, don't take hold of me... you are taking me for nothing; I would not mind if I had it.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Policeman George Wright testified that on the way to the station, O'Brien said, 'You are taking a liberty with me because you know me; O'Brien will tell you I did not do it; I was not on the job.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Police Sergeant James Laing testified that he went to the pub and summoned the manager and potman to attend court. He stated that neither of them gave a decided answer, and they were called in court but did not appear.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: After the jury returned a guilty verdict, all three prisoners pleaded guilty to previous convictions. Numerous other convictions were also proven against each of them.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: George Smith, manager to Mr. Wilson Barrett, testified that the cheque for £27 was not Mr. Barrett's signature, nor did Mr. Barrett have an account at the bank named on the cheque, the National Provincial Bank.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Elizabeth Smith, the prisoner's landlady, stated that the prisoner, using the name Charles Benjamin, boarded at her house and had fallen into arrears on his payments, owing her £3 10s. on March 5th.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: The prisoner told his landlady, Elizabeth Smith, that Mr. Wilson Barrett was his trustee and that he received money from his family, building a connection to the famous name.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: After receiving the prisoner's cheque for £23 10s., he left immediately and never returned to the boarding-house. He was later arrested in Manchester.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner stated on oath that he received the cheque from a friend named Bailey (or Bennett), who told him it was from Mr. Barrett, and that he believed it was genuine.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Mrs. Smith testified that when the prisoner gave her the cheque, he said there was another man outside, although she did not see him.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner pleaded guilty to a previous conviction of felony from 1892, and another conviction was also proven against him.

information:

Description: Lady Annette Carson testified that four or five boys, including the three prisoners, came along while she was walking. She stated that Snell seized her purse, which was attached to her wrist by a chain, and that she tried to push him off with her umbrella.

Description: Detectives Walter Smith and John Laving testified that when they located Sines and Snell, the two gave false names. Sines claimed to be "Jones" and Snell claimed to be "Williamson."

public.game_background: William Henri, Thomas Benjamin, and James Anderson were charged with breaking and entering the shop of Harry Alston and stealing 19 watches. Henri pleaded guilty, while Benjamin and Anderson were tried for their alleged roles as lookouts and accomplices.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. William either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Testimony from shop assistant Harry Glover established that on the morning of October 11th, the shop at 69 Cornhill had its window broken and 19 watches, valued at £322, were stolen.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: According to Constable Webber, upon being charged at the station, William Henri confessed, 'I have got the stuff,' and produced 18 of the stolen watches. Henri also stated, 'I did not break the window.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Constable Webber testified that at 2:10 a.m., he saw Benjamin and Anderson together with Henri and two others acting suspiciously near the crime scene, shortly before the robbery.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Constables Walker and Davis both testified to hearing the distinct sound of smashing glass, which prompted them to run towards the jeweller's shop.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Upon arrest, Benjamin claimed, 'You have made a mistake; I am going to Covent Garden Market to work.' His defense was that he met Henri by chance, got on a van, and was at a coffee stall when the whistles blew.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Anderson's defense was that he was walking home from the Star Music Hall in Bermondsey. He claimed that upon hearing police whistles near the Bank, he saw people running, ran after them out of curiosity, and was then arrested.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Constable Walker testified that after the arrests, defendant Thomas Benjamin provided a false address to the police at the station.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Constable Turner testified that when Benjamin and Anderson were searched after their arrest, none of the stolen property was found on either of them.

public.game_background: George Woolfe was charged with the wilful murder of Charlotte Cheeseman. The victim's body was discovered in a ditch on Tottenham Marshes on January 26th, 1902, with multiple severe incised wounds to her head, face, and hands. The prosecution alleges that Woolfe, the victim's boyfriend, was with her on the night of the murder, had a motive to kill her, and that his subsequent actions and the physical evidence (scratches on his face) point to his guilt. The defense argues that Woolfe was elsewhere at the time of the murder and that the scratches were sustained in an unrelated fight.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. George either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The body of Charlotte Cheeseman was found in a ditch on Tottenham Marshes on the morning of Sunday, January 26th. She was dressed in a green jacket, and her face and hands were covered in blood.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Edward Matthews, the victim's brother-in-law, saw fresh scratches on the prisoner's face on Sunday, January 26th, the day after the murder. There was one scratch on each cheek and several on his forehead.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Witnesses Elizabeth Schmidlin and Gilbert Dent both identified the prisoner and the deceased as being together in the Rosemary Branch public-house between 9:00 PM and 9:30 PM on the night of the murder.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Witnesses Edward Wilcocks and Charles Clapham testified to seeing a couple matching the descriptions in the Park Hotel, Tottenham, near the marshes, around 10:10 PM. They noted the man appeared 'cross and disagreeable' while the woman seemed to have had too much to drink. Both identified the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Alfred Jones, a workmate, testified that the prisoner told him he was in 'serious trouble' with the deceased, that she was 'six weeks gone' (pregnant), and that he would 'get shut of her.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The prisoner admitted to writing an anonymous letter to the victim's employer, under the false name 'Alfred Dixon,' falsely accusing her of theft and drunkenness in an attempt to get her fired.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Ernest Sapsford and Frederick Izant testified that on Boxing Day, about a month before the murder, they saw the prisoner in possession of an object described as 'a piece cut out of the middle of a file, sharpened at each end.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: On Monday, January 27th, the first working day after the murder, the prisoner did not return to his job. Instead, he enlisted in the East Surrey Regiment at Kingston Barracks under the false name of 'Slater'.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The prisoner's defense and alibi is that he was with the deceased until about 7 PM, after which he left her and went alone to the World's Fair until 11:25 PM, before returning home and getting into the fight with Sapsford. He claims he never went to Tottenham that night.
[EVIDENCE ev19]
Description: Upon being arrested, the prisoner said, 'I am innocent of it.' When charged, he said 'I hope I shot her,' and later remarked, 'She won't ruin another man's house.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The victim, William Andrew Gilfillan, testified he was drunk, got into conversation with four men including McCarthy, and after leaving the pub was pushed down and robbed. He stated, 'I cannot swear positively to O'Shaughnessy... I cannot say if either of the prisoners were there when I was pushed down.'
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Henry Ellerington, a cab driver, testified he saw McCarthy pick the drunken prosecutor up. He explicitly stated, 'I did not see O'Shaughnessy there, there was a man similar to him, but I can swear he was not there—the man who was there was not so tall as O'Shaughnessy.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: William Devenish, a decorator, testified he saw both prisoners with the prosecutor. He stated, 'I saw McCarthy hold the prosecutor down by his shoulders—they all ran away, and I saw some silver in McCarthy's hand.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Constable Mould testified that when he caught McCarthy, McCarthy said, 'I have just come from the boozer,' and claimed it was the Shakespear. Mould also found 2s. 6d. in silver and 9d. in bronze on him.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: O'Shaughnessy, upon his arrest, provided an alibi, stating: 'I was miles away buying meat in York Street with my missus.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: McCarthy's defense was a statement admitting his presence but denying participation: 'I was there, but I had nothing to do with the robbery.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective Sergeant George Pryde testified that he observed the group in the Oxford Arms public house and saw Frederick Williams hand a packet to Charles Pitman.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Detective Pryde also testified that he saw Frank Morris take a shilling from his packet and hand it to Emma Bell, who examined it before handing it back.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Upon his arrest, Williams attempted to hide a packet containing ten counterfeit shillings and said to the arresting officer, "I knew you would have me, Mr. Pryde."
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Detective Sergeant Randell Hodgson testified that when he arrested Pitman, Pitman said "I do not understand you" and threw a parcel containing twenty counterfeit florins into the roadway.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: A search of Pitman revealed a half-sovereign, 6s. in silver, and 1s. 1 1/2d. in bronze, all of which were good money.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Detective Thomas Rathby arrested Bell, who stated, "I have got none." A search confirmed she only possessed a small amount of good money.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: A search of Bell's house revealed a paper of silver sand and a paper of whitening.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Jane Mumford, Bell's landlady, testified that she had opened the door for Williams, Pitman, and Morris to visit Bell on several occasions.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Mr. Webster, Inspector of Coins to His Majesty's Mint, testified that all the seized florins and shillings were counterfeit and "fairly well made."
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Bell, in her defense, stated she went to the pub because a man who owed her 1s. took her there to get change to repay her.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Bell testified that while she was in the pub, the other prisoners came in and they all had drinks together, implying the meeting was a coincidence.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Frederick Williams and Frank Morris, who were arrested as part of the same group and observed distributing the packets, both pleaded guilty to possessing counterfeit coin.

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Frederick Charles Nunn, an inspector of postmen, stated that the prisoner was on duty at the sorting office from 11:10 p.m. to 2:25 a.m. on the night the letter was posted. His role stamping letters would have given him direct access to it, though he noted about ten other postmen and four stampers were also on duty.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Mason, the betting agent's employee, also testified that his company had no record of any customer named Albert Edward Yates or 'F. Morgan,' the alias the prisoner claimed to have used for betting.
[EVIDENCE ev5]

[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Constable Holtham stated that a search of the prisoner's house revealed five more postal orders (one for 1s. and four others, including a 2s. one). All were crossed and made payable to G. Stoddart.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The house search also uncovered stamps which appeared to have been re-gummed and a quantity of 2.5d stamps that had 'undoubtedly been torn off letters.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Elizabeth Edwards testified that on December 25th, her house was burgled and a gold watch was stolen from a jewel-case. She identified the watch found in the prisoner's possession as her property. The prisoner's pub is located 200-300 yards from her house.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Detective Albert Clarke, who was with Pike, testified that while Pike was searching, the prisoner was left with him and said, "I have been put away, I may as well give him everything."
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Detective John Martin testified that at the Islington Police Station, the prisoner said, "I am done; I have been a fool, and when I get out of this I shall never take the game on again."
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: William Drummond, a blacksmith, testified for the defense. He stated that on Boxing Day he was in the prisoner's pub and saw a man ask the prisoner to "oblige me with 15s. on this watch until to-morrow?" He saw money pass and the prisoner take a watch he thought was the gold one in question, all done openly in the bar.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: James Herbert Cranley, manager of the brewery that owns the pub, testified as a character witness. He stated that the prisoner had been a tenant for six or seven years and that "we have always found him an excellent tenant and an honest man."

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Paul Gustavus Friend testified that he answered an advertisement in the Daily Telegraph for a secretary position with a salary of £250. He met the prisoner, Lawrence Rodgers, who claimed to be the principal of the 'Anglo-American Syndicate' for producing stage plays.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Howard Crunden Cleaver, a friend of the Duke of Rovigo, testified that the signature on the agreement was not the Duke's. He confirmed the Duke was never asked to be a partner and was not involved in the syndicate.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William Reginald Smell of the Mutual Depositors' Banking Company testified that Rodgers had only a personal account with them, not a syndicate account. On the day Friend paid his money, the account balance was 12s. 7d. before the deposit, and was reduced to 8s. 8d. within five days.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: In his defense, Rodgers claimed Friend agreed to become a partner, denied the agreement produced was the one he drafted, and asserted the money was a genuine investment in the 'Sousa Girl' play. He claimed Friend was 'swearing falsely.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The defendant, Rodgers, admitted that he had been convicted of obtaining goods by false pretences at Lewes on April 3rd, 1900, and three times previously.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Defense witness William John Ridsdale testified that Rodgers had told him that Friend was his partner and that they were going to produce the 'Sousa Girl.'

information:

Description: Barnard Buse, a grocer, testified that on April 9th, the prisoner George Hall came into his shop and gave his wife a florin for a 5d tin of sardines. Mr. Buse tested the coin, breaking it, and recognized the prisoner as the same man who had passed a counterfeit florin two weeks prior.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Hume, an assistant at a newsagent's shop, testified that on the same day, the prisoner came in and offered a bright florin for a 4 1/2d item. His employer, Mr. Weaver, sounded the coin, declared it bad, and the prisoner took it back and left without the item.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner pleaded guilty to a prior conviction for unlawfully uttering counterfeit coins on April 2nd, 1901.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: City Policemen Hugh Maclean and George Gale testified that on April 11th, they observed Webb and Ford acting suspiciously together for about twenty-five minutes. They saw Ford tampering with letter boxes in Aldermanbury while Webb was nearby, 'apparently keeping watch outside.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Adolph Baltzer, secretary of the victim company, confirmed the four bills of exchange were stolen from their letterbox at 55 Hatton Garden. A postman, Charles Worsfold, testified he delivered letters there around 8 a.m., and Baltzer testified he arrived at 10:20 a.m. to find the box empty.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Webb's father, Alfred Coomber, testified that Webb left their home, which is a fifteen-minute walk from Hatton Garden, at about 8:45 a.m. on the morning of the crime. Webb was out of employment at the time.

The prosecution alleges they operated a coining operation from a confectioner's shop at 10, Station Road, Twickenham, a property owned by the defendant Russell.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The previous tenant of 10, Station Road, Julia Beard, testified that the defendant Russell, her landlord, gave her a sovereign to vacate the premises in early April. Shortly after, the defendant Trowbridge was seen at the property.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: A lodger, William Erricker, testified that Russell gave him notice to leave, stating, "I have got a man coming who is bringing out a new patent." The attics, where the coining implements were later found, were empty when Erricker left on April 29th.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Police Constable Charles McFenning testified that he had seen all three prisoners together on multiple occasions outside 10, Station Road, at various times of the day and night. He described one specific late-night incident where he saw Russell and Jewell acting suspiciously.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: During the police raid on May 5th, Detective-Inspector Dew stated that upon police entry, defendant Trowbridge went into the back parlour and shouted, 'Go, Charlie; go, Charlie,' apparently as a warning to the defendant Jewell.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: In a locked back attic at 10, Station Road, police found a complete set of counterfeiting equipment. This included plaster moulds for sovereigns, half-crowns, and florins, plaster of Paris, white metal, a ladle, a battery, and other tools.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The defendant Russell made several contradictory statements to police. He alternately described Trowbridge as his 'partner' and 'joint owner' of the shop, and later as merely a 'lodger'. He also told Sgt. Taylor he had 'never seen this man before' when pointing to Jewell.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: On the way to the police station, Trowbridge remarked to an officer, 'Well, I suppose I must expect a blank sometimes in our lives.' At the station, Jewell told another officer, 'if that inspector had promised me something definite... I might have told him something... I never give something for nothing.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William John Webster, an Inspector of Coins from H.M. Mint, examined the seized items. He testified they constituted the 'regular, complete stock in trade of a counterfeit coiner' and confirmed that the counterfeit florins passed by Trowbridge and found in the garden were made from the specific moulds found in the attic.

public.game_background: Herbert Madden was indicted for the wilful murder of his son, Herbert Patrick Madden, and daughter, Mary Beatrice Madden. The prosecution argued that Madden intentionally killed his two young children with a hammer, evidenced by multiple confessions and his sober state at the time. The defense contended that he was insane and not responsible for his actions, citing a history of a severe head injury, chronic alcoholism, and delusional behavior leading up to the event.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: The day before the killings, the prisoner, while drunk, confronted a neighbor, Ada Strange, about 6s. 6d. that had gone missing. He threatened, 'I intend to set fire to the place from top to bottom,' and then struck another neighbor. Mrs. Strange testified that she told him to keep away from her as he was 'mad'.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Alice Roberts testified that on the evening of the murders, her stepfather had been home for about half an hour and was having his supper. She stated, 'He may have had some drink that evening, but he was not very drunk'.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: After committing the act, the prisoner locked the kitchen door from the inside. When his wife and step-daughter gained entry, the lamp inside was extinguished, and the prisoner then came out of the room.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Multiple witnesses who encountered the prisoner immediately after the murders, including civilians and police officers, testified that he was 'quite sober' and 'perfectly rational'. Divisional Surgeon Dr. Caunter examined him shortly after and found he 'showed no signs of having been drunk'.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police found a hammer on the floor near the bed where the children's bodies lay. Dr. Cotter testified that it had wet blood, hair, and brain matter on it, and the children's extensive skull fractures were consistent with being caused by that hammer.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: An envelope was found on the table on which was written in pencil, 'The last of the Maddens by Herbert'. When questioned by Inspector Briggs, the prisoner confirmed that he had written it.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: In 1898, the prisoner fell down stairs and hit his head, after which he was ill for over a week. Both his wife and step-daughter testified that he was 'different' and 'like mad' after the fall, and had taken to drinking more heavily.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Multiple witnesses, including the prisoner's wife, step-daughter, and neighbours, testified that the prisoner was always kind to and fond of the children, and they were not afraid of him, even when he was in drink.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Clara Wolff testified that on October 23rd, the prisoner asked for change for a half-sovereign. She sounded it on the wet wooden counter and gave him change. She later doubted the coin as it was light, took it to the post office where it was found to be light, and later gave it to a barman.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Sarah Wickham testified that on October 24th, the day after the Wolff incident, the prisoner gave her a gilded Jubilee sixpence in change for a half-sovereign. She immediately realized it was fake and ran out to find a policeman.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Upon searching the prisoner, Constable West found a good half-sovereign, two half-crowns, two florins, three sixpences, and 6 1/2d. in bronze.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police Sergeant Charles Walters testified that when the prisoner was formally charged with uttering the coin to Wolff, he said, "I know nothing at all about it."
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: William John Webster, Inspector of coin to His Majesty's Mint, testified that the coins were gilded sixpences which resemble half-sovereigns and were officially called in as a result.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner in his defence stated that he believed the coins were good.

Description: Upon Mr. Dresel's imprisonment for debt, Louis Waterman sent a letter to Mrs. Dresel, whom he did not know, explaining the situation and offering his assistance. The letter mentioned he was known to her relatives and suggested meeting at her solicitor's office.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: A memorandum written by Mr. Dresel was enclosed in the defendant's first letter to Mrs. Dresel, which stated, 'Mr. Waterman will explain all.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Frederick Smith, the court bailiff who arrested Mr. Dresel, testified that Dresel knew the defendant and 'suggested that the prisoner should write or communicate with Mrs. Dresel to try and procure the money to get his release.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The bailiff, Frederick Smith, who was from the Croydon County Court, also testified that the defendant 'never came to settle any' of the other actions against Mr. Dresel at that court.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: In her testimony, Mrs. Dresel stated that after the legal proceedings had started, she 'was quite willing to forgive him for playing me such a dirty trick' and 'did not wish to go on with the case'.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: During the trial, the defendant stated that he was willing to refund the £4 to Mrs. Dresel. The refund was then made in open court.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Alfred James Brown, a clerk at the Gandy Belt Manufacturing Company, testified that on January 1st, Brotherton presented a forged order from D. B. Hook. He stated Brotherton signed for the goods as 'W. Kempson' and was later positively identified by him in a police lineup of nine or ten men.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Henry Prince, a salesman at Ridley, Whitley & Co., testified that Brotherton used forged orders from D. B. Hook to obtain American cloth on three separate occasions (Jan 7, 8, and 11). Prince stated he had 'no doubt' that Brotherton was the man, who signed for the goods using the alias 'Jackson'.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Thomas Frederick Edwards, manager at C. J. Edwards & Son, testified that on January 12th, Brotherton presented a similar forged order. Growing suspicious, Edwards detained Brotherton and gave him into custody, at which point Brotherton claimed he was 'sent by somebody else.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Charles Matthias Field, manager for the real D. B. Hook, confirmed his firm's address was 155, not 177, Upper Thames Street, and that none of the orders were genuine. He testified that No. 177 is occupied by a coffee house, a baker, and a blacksmith.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Upon searching Brotherton, Inspector Ottaway found a slip of paper with 'D. B. Hook, Upper Thames Street' written on it.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Inspector Ottaway found a pocket book on Brotherton containing the entries 'Ridley, Whitley, Newgate Street, and Cheapside, £4 12s.' and 'G. Jackson, to-morrow.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Inspector Ottaway testified that when shown receipts, Brotherton denied involvement in the Gandy Belt incident (signed 'Kempson') but admitted his involvement in the others (signed 'Jackson').
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In his defense, Brotherton testified that the other prisoner, Bugden, asked him to take a letter for him and wait for an answer. He claimed he did not know what was in the letter and that he is innocent.

He stated she was afraid to seek a separation because her husband threatened to kill her. Mr. Lowman found her to be in the 'greatest distress through the neglect and ill-treatment of her husband.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The prisoner confessed the act to her sister, Florence Harding. Harding testified that the prisoner 'started to scream and cry' and said, 'I have drowned him... in the Lea about 12 o'clock.' She explained that her husband had been abusing her and that she was going back to drown herself.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Both William Lowman and the prisoner's sister, Florence Harding, testified that the prisoner was very fond of her children. Mr. Lowman stated she appeared 'to be very fond of her children, and desirous to do the best she could for them,' and was especially fond of the deceased, her youngest child.

[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: A clerk from the Bank of England testified that the large notes obtained from cashing the draft were exchanged for smaller notes. The person making the exchange endorsed a £50 note as 'F. J. Vincent, 18, Guildford Street,' a signature the prisoner admitted was his.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: A clerk for H. S. King & Co. testified that on August 10th he handed a registered letter addressed to 'Mrs. K. J. Dacre' to the prisoner, who signed a receipt for it. This was the letter mailed from Melbourne.

Description: A porter at the Charing Cross Hotel testified that the prisoner stayed at the hotel under the name of 'Ward' from August 7th to August 15th, the period when he had told his wife he was in Gloucestershire.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Upon his arrest on August 22nd, a police search revealed the prisoner was carrying £25 in £5 notes in his jacket pocket, and a further bundle of twenty-two £5 notes (£110) hidden in his trousers pocket. He later revealed another twenty-four £5 notes (£120) hidden in his sock.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Frederick Chopping testified that Markfeldt claimed his printing invention was taken up by Messrs. Spalding & Hodge, who offered him a £500 salary, and that Markfeldt then offered Chopping an assistant position at £5 a week, a significant raise for him.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Chopping testified that Markfeldt later claimed he could secure him a government directorship at Enfield related to his invention, and obtained another £2 10s. for 'official papers,' providing a written promise of repayment.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Robert Cooper, an employee at Spalding & Hodge, testified that the firm had never employed Markfeldt, were not in the printing business, and that no director lived at Leighton Buzzard, directly contradicting Markfeldt's claims.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Walter Jones testified that Markfeldt claimed to have improved Marconi telegraphy and that the Admiralty was taking up his patent, appointing him a 'Colonel' at £2,000 per year with a factory at Enfield.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: William Badcoe testified that Markfeldt falsely claimed to be a Member of the Royal College of Surgeons (M.R.C.S.), prescribed for his wife, and obtained £1 16s. 3d. for medicine.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Badcoe stated Markfeldt showed him a periscope invention and a forged Admiralty document claiming it was accepted. Markfeldt induced Badcoe to put his name to the 'invention' and finance it for a share of the profits.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Sir John Durston, Engineer in Chief to the Fleet, testified that he does not know Markfeldt, never received any invention from him, attended no trials, and that the supposed 'Admiralty document' was a fake.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Gilbert Forrest Cowell, Secretary to the College of Surgeons, testified that there is no member of the Royal College of Surgeons named Markfeldt.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: During cross-examination, Frederick Chopping admitted he had seen Markfeldt's actual invention for printing in colours, thought it was novel, and that a doctor at the hospital paid Markfeldt for his work, knowing he was penniless.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Detective George Smith testified that after his arrest, Markfeldt claimed he expected £3,000 for his invention and that all his troubles were caused by the Admiralty's refusal to acknowledge him, while maintaining he had no intent to defraud.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Clara del Rey testified that she purchased the second lot of furniture from the Official Receiver for £2. She stated, 'I gave the money to my husband to pay; he conducted the purchase.'
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Mrs. del Rey explained why the furniture was stored in the prisoner's name: 'I put the furniture at Gibson's in the prisoner's name, as the first was in his name, and I thought it would be safe.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: According to Mrs. del Rey, the prisoner said he was going to move the furniture to 'Thomas Connolly's, a great friend of his, to warehouse it.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mrs. del Rey testified that on September 27th, she went to the prisoner's house 'where I saw a black cabinet, some vases, and a palm stand' that belonged to her.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: In cross-examination, Alfred del Rey admitted that he had listed the prisoner as a £25 creditor in his bankruptcy and sworn to the Official Receiver that he had paid him. He then admitted, 'it was not true—I know that it is perjury.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: William Gibson, the warehouseman, testified that on August 2nd, at the prisoner's request, he moved the furniture 'to his house at 30, Mayflower Road, Clapham, which is Slingsby's address.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: William Gibson also testified, 'I never said anything to the prisoner about desiring that he should remove the goods—so long as the charges were paid we did not mind keeping them.'
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: George Slingsby, at whose house the furniture was stored, testified that the prisoner told him 'part of them were probably going to be returned to Mrs. Del Rey.'
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Detective Charles Tobutt testified that upon arrest, the prisoner stated, 'I do not see how I could steal my own goods, they were warehoused in my name.'
[EVIDENCE ev20]
Description: In his defense, the prisoner denied telling the Del Reys he had taken the furniture to Connolly's, claiming he only said he 'would see if he was the best man to take the furniture to.'

public.game_background: Joseph William Bryant, a 53-year-old Post Office porter, is charged with stealing a postal packet containing post cards while on duty. The prosecution alleges he concealed the packet in his apron and left the sorting office with it, later confessing to this and previous thefts. The defense argues that while he took the packet, he did not intend to keep it, but merely wanted to inspect its contents out of a 'bad desire' related to indecent post cards.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Joseph either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Arthur Jonas Watts, a clerk at the General Post Office, testified that he saw the prisoner, Mr. Bryant, look around the sorting office, take a packet from a basket, hurriedly conceal it in his apron, and walk out of the sorting office.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: When confronted by Mr. Watts, the prisoner took the packet from his apron and said, 'I was not going to keep it; I was going to look at it.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The prisoner further explained his departure from the sorting office by stating, 'I was going to the w.c. and I did not intend to keep the packet.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: A search of the prisoner's locker revealed two packets of post cards (thirty-two unstamped, sixteen stamped) and three French periodicals.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Referring to the post cards found in his locker, the prisoner confessed, 'They belong to the office; they are what I stole. The unstamped ones I stole out of a packet about a month ago.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: It was established in cross-examination that the prisoner had served in the Post Office for fifteen years and previously in the Navy, coming to the job with an 'excellent character'.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The packet Mr. Bryant took contained two French school books of a harmless character and its appearance did not indicate that it contained money.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Watts testified that the stolen unstamped post cards were 'of a broad character' and that marks made on them by the prisoner made them indecent.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Overseer Thomas Bradford testified that on February 14th, the day before the main incident, he saw the prisoner place a packet in his coat pocket, leave the office, and go to the w.c.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: In his defense on oath, the prisoner admitted taking the packet, stating he was 'infected with a bad desire' and was going to open it to see if there were more French cards inside.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The prisoner stated under oath that he had 'no intention of keeping it,' referring to the packet he took.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The prisoner claimed that the French mail was often searched for indecent materials and he wanted to see what this packet, which had been passed as decent, contained.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: The prisoner admitted that the alterations on the previously-found post cards were his, but he 'did not know what induced him to make them.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Ivy Kirby, the deceased's fiancée, testified that she and the deceased were walking when they were approached by two men. One of the men, whom she identified as the prisoner, appeared drunk and was rolling about.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Walter Lester, a thirteen-year-old boy, testified that he saw the prisoner kick the deceased when he was in the road. He later picked the prisoner out of a lineup.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Alfred Philip Smith testified that he saw the prisoner push the deceased into the road and then raise his foot three or four times in the action of kicking. He also identified the prisoner.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Dr. John Jenkins, who performed the post-mortem, stated that the deceased died from an extensive fracture at the base of the skull, consistent with a fall onto the head or a kick by a boot, requiring violent force.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Ellen Dowden testified she saw the prisoner in a pub with a 'very bad black eye' shortly before the incident. She later saw the assault but could not identify the attacker.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Frederick Robert Tomlin testified that he had a fight with the prisoner a week before the incident and struck him in the eye, which would have caused a black eye.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Detective-Sergeant Bedford testified that a boy named Watson, who was the first person to come to the station and name 'Jim Rice' as the culprit, subsequently failed to pick the prisoner out of a lineup.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Mrs. Bridgen, a neighbor, testified that she went to the scene and heard the prisoner say, 'I have done it, Mrs. Bridgen. I intended to do her in and myself too.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The prisoner, James Rice, provided a defense on oath stating he was at a friend's (Watson's) house from about 8:45 p.m., where he drank beer, fell asleep, and did not wake until 3:30 a.m., thus providing an alibi for the time of the incident.

[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Constable James Saren testified that both prisoners denied having seen the bag. They also lied, stating there was no other room on the floor when he asked.
[EVIDENCE ev7]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective James Brown testified that on February 16th, he and Detective Anderton followed the two prisoners, Williams and Leeke. He stated they were walking together, in 'deep conversation,' and followed a strange, wandering path through various streets and alleys near St. Paul's Churchyard for eight or nine minutes.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Detective Anderton testified that he had also observed Williams and Leeke together on the previous day, February 15th. He stated they were in 'deep conversation and walking about the various streets,' going into doorways and looking at the name plates on the doors.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: According to Detective Anderton, when he arrested Williams, Williams 'resisted very violently' before being told who the officer was. After being informed of the charge, Williams said, 'I have only walked round with him.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: John Carter, a salesman at Messrs. Copestake and Co., testified that cloths like those produced were in their warehouse on February 16th. He also remembered Leeke coming into the shop and speaking to him on that day.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Alfred Williams made a statement asserting his innocence. He claimed he met Leeke a fortnight prior, who offered to help him find employment. He stated, 'I perfectly innocently went with him, not thinking he would do anything like that. I knew no more that he had the things about him than any stranger in the street.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Ernest Leeke pleaded guilty to the charge of stealing the four tea cloths.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Ambrose Turner testified that he had an arrangement for the prisoner, Woodward, to use his shop for a paper business in exchange for weekly rent. Turner stated he missed between £40 and £60 worth of stamps, including five sheets sold by the prisoner to Mr. Wallace. Turner asserted that the prisoner had no authority to deal with the stamps for himself.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Detective Edward Marriott testified that upon being cautioned, Woodward claimed he was in partnership with Turner when he sold the album. Later, Woodward asked Turner to withdraw the charge, stating he had tried to buy the album back for double the price. Marriott also noted that when first investigating, Woodward knew another man was suspected but said nothing about a partnership.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: John Wallace, a stamp dealer, testified that the prisoner sold him the stamps for 10s. He stated that Woodward had previous dealings with him and had said he was taking over a business.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Woodward claimed that when Turner first told him about losing £50 worth of stamps, he advised Turner to go to the police, as he knew he had not taken them.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Woodward testified that about ten days before his arrest, a High Bailiff came to the shop to levy a distress warrant against Turner. Woodward claimed that Turner 'distinctly stated to the High Bailiff that the whole of the business in that shop belonged to me,' which resulted in the bailiff not levying the distress.

public.game_background: John Devlin is charged with the murder of Agnes Hankers, the woman he lived with. The prosecution argues he intentionally killed her with a chopper, citing his confession and a note he left as evidence of a rational, motivated act. The defense contends that Devlin was not legally responsible for his actions due to a temporary state of insanity, pointing to his personal and extensive family history of mental illness, his local reputation as 'Mad Jack', and the extreme violence of the crime.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Alice Balham, the prisoner's half-sister, testified that on the night before the murder, the prisoner and the deceased seemed to be on 'very good terms' and were not excessively drunk.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Alice Balham testified that upon entering the prisoner's room in the morning, she found the deceased on the floor covered in blood and the prisoner lying at the head of the bed.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: A note written by the prisoner was found at the scene, stating, 'She is a bad woman. I have give her all I can, but it done no good, and she has ruined (or marred) my life.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Inspector Nichols testified from a police 'occurrence' book that in 1903, the prisoner was committed to the Horton Asylum after behaving violently, threatening to cut someone's throat, and claiming his 'head was on fire.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Testimony from Mrs. Hines and Inspector Nichols established that the prisoner was widely known in the neighborhood by the nicknames 'Mad Jack' and 'Jack the Devil'.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Inspector Nichols provided evidence that there was a significant history of insanity in the prisoner's family, noting his son, half-brother, and half-sister had all been in asylums.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Alice Balham testified that the deceased used to nag the prisoner and threaten to leave him, which 'appeared to upset his mind very much.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Dr. Jacquet, the medical officer, described the victim's injuries as extensive and brutal, with at least twelve wounds to the head inflicted with a chopper, and a skull that was 'literally smashed into little pieces.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: After the inquest, the prisoner asked to see the body of the deceased and, upon being granted permission, 'kissed it several times'.

public.game_background: Michael Harnett and Edwin Thomas Baker are charged with assaulting Reuben Rottstein with intent to rob. The incident also involved a severe assault on Edward Hemms, a friend accompanying the Rottsteins.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Reuben Rottstein testified that three men came from behind a pillar and caught hold of him. After his son Isaac came up and identified him as his father, one of the men (Harnett) said, 'All right, we've made a mistake,' and they let him go.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Under cross-examination, Reuben Rottstein stated, 'Harnett did not try to rob me; he had no change. Baker never touched me at all.' He also stated he was struck at the back of the head but could not say who struck him.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Edward Hemms, the seriously injured man, testified that he was struck from behind and became unconscious. He stated, 'I did not have a fight with these men.' His skull was fractured and he was unconscious for a week.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Thomas Patten, an independent eyewitness, stated 'Harnett struck Hemms a heavy blow and knocked him over the ropes... Hemms did not attempt to fight; he made no insulting remark to the men, and did not draw them on in any way.' He also testified that both prisoners struck Hemms.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: On cross-examination, Thomas Patten admitted, 'I did say at the police court, "I came over and found you quarrelling"; they were jawing to one another, but not kicking up a row.' He also stated, 'Baker was trying a bit to pacify them; he said, "Let them go".'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Detective Thomas Gale testified that when he and another officer went to Harnett's house at seven in the morning, both prisoners 'saw me and another constable (who was in uniform) they ran away.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Detective Gale testified that Baker, while waiting at the police court, said, 'It was a fair fight; he set about me; I am very sorry.' Sergeant Brown also testified Baker said, 'they took a liberty with us; he put his fists up at me.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Police-constable Henry Bamfield, who arrived after hearing a police whistle, testified about the scene where Hemms was injured: 'His coat was off; it was rolled up under his head.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Thomas Patten was recalled and testified in direct contradiction to the constable: 'When we got Hemms out of the trench his coat was on; I am positive of that.'

The defense argued that Schafer genuinely believed his first wife was deceased at the time of his second marriage, based on an anonymous letter and subsequent inquiries.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Annie Mary Woolley, sister of the prisoner's first wife, testified that she witnessed the marriage of the prisoner to her sister, Emma Harriet Rumboldt, on January 30, 1893. She stated they lived together until about four years prior to the trial, when her sister left him.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Annie Mary Woolley testified, "As late as Christmas, 1905, I saw him in a public-house and we talked about his wife, and he knew perfectly well then she was alive."
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Annie Margaret Pilkington testified that she went through a form of marriage with the prisoner at Bermondsey Parish Church on November 12, 1904.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Annie Margaret Pilkington stated that the prisoner and his mother told her his first wife had died. She testified, "they showed me a letter to that effect... the postmark on the envelope was Victoria Park."
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Police-constable William Harris testified that the prisoner was in court on December 23, 1902, charged with detaining his wife's property, and was ordered to return it to her.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The prisoner stated that he lost the anonymous letter when he moved to a new residence. He testified, "When I moved to Spa Mansions a lot of my letters got lost, and this one amongst them."

Description: Marcus Miller, the bookmaker, testified that his registered telegraphic address was 'Puddler, London' and that Warner's agreed-upon code name was 'Cane.' He accepts bets wired up to five minutes before a race's advertised start time.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Marcus Miller stated that at 3:05 p.m., after the 2:45 p.m. race had finished, he heard a telegram drop through his letter-box. He immediately looked out the window but saw no messenger, which he found suspicious.

Description: Miller testified that he met Warner in a public-house on the evening of the race, and Warner asked, 'How about my £25?' in reference to the bet on 'Charles Edward.'

[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: After the guilty verdict, Detective-sergeant Burton stated that on the night Warner was arrested, a known criminal with a long record, associated with a rival bookmaker, came to the police station to inquire about bail for the prisoner.
