information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Frances McCarthy testified that the prisoner, Frederick Helbert, told her that her solicitors, Messrs. Cohen and Cohen, were 'the greatest scoundrels in the profession.' This alarmed her, as they were holding over £600 of her money at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Helbert induced McCarthy to transfer 300 shares in the Kalgoorlie Electric Lighting Company, worth approximately £300, for investment in a new motor-car company.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Helbert agreed to pay McCarthy £5 a month, which he paid for about 10 months. McCarthy stated he described this as interest until the shares were bought; Helbert claimed it was interest on a loan.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: In a letter not dictated by Helbert, McCarthy referred to the money she had given him, writing: 'At the time I lent you the first £550... and gave you the other £300'.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: In February 1907, after pressure from McCarthy's new solicitor, Helbert gave her a written receipt stating that the sum of £850 was to be invested in the Kitlene Company.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Helbert testified that he told McCarthy at the outset that he was an undischarged bankrupt. McCarthy emphatically denied ever being told this.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Helbert admitted in court that he had used all of the £850 and that none of it was left. He claimed it was spent 'for the purposes for which it had been lent me' in 'pushing these businesses'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: After McCarthy's solicitor wrote to him demanding a settlement, Helbert left London for Paris, giving his address as Cook and Son.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Several letters signed by McCarthy were produced, in which she ratified his actions, approved his negotiations, and gave him a 'free hand'. These letters were written at Helbert's dictation.

public.game_background: Richard Sansom, a motor-omnibus driver, is charged with unlawfully causing bodily harm to cabman John William Dove through wanton driving, wilful misconduct, and neglect. The incident involved a collision between Sansom's omnibus and Dove's cab in Park Lane on a foggy night. A key component of the charge is the allegation that Sansom was drunk and unfit to drive at the time of the accident.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Richard either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The victim, cab-driver John William Dove, testified, "I do not remember anything about the accident until I found myself in St. George's Hospital." He could not recall the motor-bus striking him.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Henry Williams, a passenger on the bus, testified that the accused drove in a "serpentine" course, weaving between traffic rests. He stated, "he took the corners that we passed at the same speed as when going straight, which was too fast."
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: After the collision, passenger Henry Williams testified that he threw himself on the bottom of the bus, which "went into the Hyde Park railings like greased lightning." This suggests a very high speed at the moment of impact and after.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police-constable Green testified that the defendant stated, "The driver pulled to the right into me, and struck my steering wheel, rendering it powerless."
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Police-constable Green, who arrived at the scene, testified that he observed the defendant to be drunk. He noted Sansom "smelt very strongly of drink; he was unsteady in his gait; and...his speech was thick."
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The defendant, Richard Sansom, testified that his omnibus had broken down earlier in the day, causing him to be stuck from 1 p.m. until nearly 10 p.m. before he could resume his route.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Sansom testified that after the serious accident, he felt "rather shaken up and very much distressed" and went to a nearby public-house where he drank one or two glasses of "mild and bitter." He claims this was the only drink he had after 6 p.m.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Henry Jeffries, the omnibus conductor, corroborated the defendant's account. He testified that the bus's front wheel "seemed to skid into the cab" and that the driver was "not either reckless or drunk."
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Robert Hugh Montgomery, a member of the London County Council who arrived at the scene shortly after, testified, "During my conversation with the accused he struck me as being absolutely sober. I should have been quite content he should have driven one of my cars."
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: Francis Deverell, a mining engineer who also arrived soon after, stated that the defendant "did not seem to have had anything at all in the way of drink. I have a very keen sense of smell, and I think I should have noticed it if he had been drinking."
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Mrs. Elizabeth Kirton, a passenger on the bus, testified, "I should say the pace was moderate. If he had been driving furiously I should have been too nervous to stay in it... He was decidedly not drunk."
[EVIDENCE ev19]
Description: Walter Henry Plowman, an inspector for the bus company, testified that he went to the scene, had a conversation with the driver, and "smelt his breath in the company's interests... I formed the opinion that he was absolutely sober."

[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mrs. Dalgleish testified that while she could not swear the attacker was the prisoner, she believed it was him. She had known him before as a plumber and recognized his eyes, though his cap was pulled down and his collar turned up.

Description: Edith Rosina Johnson testified that she believed she saw the prisoner in Lewisham (near the crime scene) at about 7:15 p.m. on the evening of the assault.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prisoner, Harry Smith, testified that he was at home all afternoon on December 17, from approximately 2 p.m. until 9 p.m., and was not in the neighborhood of Lee at all.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The defense counsel, Mr. Lawless, submitted that there was no evidence of an intention to rob. He argued the attack was equally consistent with spite or some other motive.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The Recorder, the presiding judge, observed in court that 'the evidence of identity is very slight.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The Recorder also commented that the evidence of handwriting, based on a signature only, was 'not very satisfactory.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Frederick Ward, a warehouseman, testified that on the evening of January 31, he locked up the warehouse safely. The next morning, he found the shop in disorder, cupboards burst open, a top floor window open, and a case of medals and 880 fountain pens missing. The total value of the stolen property was estimated at £1,247.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: During cross-examination, Catherine Sager testified that while Laurence lived with her, she saw nothing suspicious. He was not living extravagantly, seemed like a respectable man, and his bed appeared to have been slept in on the night of the burglary.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Detective Edward Marriott testified that upon arrest, Laurence said, 'You have made a mistake.' At the station, when charged, he said, 'I did no breaking and entering,' but admitted he had dealings with the property through a third person.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William Warner testified that he went to the prisoner's door to ask his sister to be quiet, and Jacobs attacked him without provocation, striking him on the head and stabbing him.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Julius Muller, a bystander who came to the scene shortly after the incident, testified that he heard the prisoner say to his wife, "Put the knife away."
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Dr. Edward Goss, the house surgeon, testified that Warner's multiple head wounds were consistent with a knife and that it was "impossible" for all four wounds to have been caused by a fall against sharp brass handles.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Inspector Charles Clark found a knife wrapped in paper inside a small box on the table in the prisoner's room. Dr. Goss stated the wounds might have been caused by this knife.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: On cross-examination, Jacobs admitted he was convicted seven years prior for living on the prostitution of a woman, for which he received three months' hard labour.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: PC Kitch observed Jacobs with a lot of blood on his face and hands. Jacobs's wife testified that Henry Warner had a black eye and claimed his daughter had given it to him.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Zalig Waller, the prosecutor, testified that he had known the prisoner for twelve months and that she had pawned items for him on three or four previous occasions, always accounting for the money.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Waller stated that he went to an address Cooke had previously given him but discovered she did not live there.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Waller admitted that at the time of the transaction he was an undischarged bankrupt and that he kept no books or banking account.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Horace S. Bull, a pawnbroker, testified that the ring in question was pledged with him on February 24 for £26 in the name of Mrs. Maud Cooke.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Detective-sergeant Whitehorn testified that a warrant for Cooke's arrest was issued on March 5, but he was unable to find her until May 5, when she was being detained on another charge.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Cooke testified that after pawning the ring, she met friends, had several drinks, and became intoxicated. She remembered having the £26 in her purse at a Tube station but missed the purse upon getting out at Clapham Common.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Cooke stated she did not raise the story of the lost money at the police court because the magistrate had indicated the case must go to trial, and her defense was reserved.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The jury deliberated for three-quarters of an hour but were unable to agree, and they were discharged. A subsequent retrial resulted in a verdict of 'Not guilty'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Annie Foster, a civilian witness who saw the crowd, testified that she saw the man on the ground (Hathaway) drop several coins that looked like two-shilling and half-crown pieces.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: On the way to the station, Hathaway said to Detective Course, "Now, don't be wicked and I will tell you how to catch those people that do make them."
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Harriett Small, the landlady at 28 Ethel Street, testified that Hathaway and a woman lodged there under the name of Smith, and that the other arrested man, English, was a frequent caller at Hathaway's room.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The landlady, Harriett Small, identified the burnt saucepan found by police as one she had lent to Hathaway for cooking.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The landlady's 10-year-old granddaughter, Margaret Lenham, testified that two days after Hathaway left, she found a piece of metal and a spoon (later identified as used for pouring molten metal) while sweeping his room.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: During cross-examination, Hathaway challenged Detective Course on procedural details, such as why the saucepan was not taken to the station immediately and whether certain evidence was produced at the police court, implying police misconduct.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: The landlady testified that after Hathaway's arrest, the woman living with him ('Mrs. Smith') brought some rubbish down, threw it in the dusthole, and then left in a great hurry.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: A cheque (No. 70042) for £6, payable to 'F. Marks' and signed 'John Scott', was discovered to have been torn from a new Ordnance College cheque book. This cheque was presented at the 'Freemason's Hotel' on the afternoon of July 9.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Thomas Gurrin, a handwriting expert with 23 years of experience, testified that after comparing the writing on the forged cheque with samples from the prisoner, he concluded they were written 'by the same hand.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner, John Swain, had an exemplary military record, having served 12 years in the Middlesex Regiment with medals for multiple campaigns. Captain Tandy also confirmed he had borne a good character at the barracks.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: From the moment of his arrest, John Swain consistently protested his innocence. He told Sergeant Hutchings, 'I do not know anything about it. I shall not worry; I am innocent,' and repeated this claim to Colonel Townsend and in a letter from prison.

Two factory hands, Elizabeth Taylor and Eliza Cope, had already pleaded guilty to stealing the items from their employer. Bignell was also charged with receiving a stolen gold ring, but this indictment was not proceeded with. The trial focused on whether Bignell and Simpson knew the goods they received were stolen.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Ely Marshall, general manager of Marshalls, Limited, identified the articles produced by the police as the property of his firm, valued at £2 or £3. He also confirmed that Taylor and Cope, who had pleaded guilty to the theft, were in their employment. Under cross-examination, he acknowledged that partly damaged goods are sometimes described as 'throw-outs.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The transcript notes that the co-defendants, Elizabeth Taylor and Eliza Cope, 'pleaded guilty' to the charge of stealing the articles from their masters, Marshalls, Limited.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: After the verdict, Bignell confessed to a previous conviction at the same court on February 9, 1903, for receiving stolen property.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The police gave Bignell 'a very bad character,' describing him as 'a noted receiver of property the proceeds of burglaries and the constant associate of some of the cleverest housebreakers and burglars.'
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: It was stated that the original thieves, Taylor and Cope, 'bear a good general character.'

Description: The prisoner's father gave evidence that his daughter was well-educated, had taught in Sunday school, was 'always a girl of very good character,' and knew that her home was always open to her.

Description: Police-constable Thomas Russell testified that on the night of October 24, he saw the prisoner, William Connelly, drunk and quarrelling with a woman in the street, surrounded by a crowd and using very obscene language.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: PC Dennehy stated that a hostile crowd of 50 to 100 people gathered around them, but no one came to their assistance.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Two civilian Post Office employees, George Henry Harper and Frederick Jocelyn, gave evidence that they assisted the police during the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev12]

[EVIDENCE ev17]

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Charles Walter Chilman, a clerk, testified that on January 11, 1909, he drew a cheque for £10 13s. 4d. in favour of Richard Hawkins and Co. and crossed it with indelible ink. The cheque was signed by his principal the next day and posted around noon.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mr. Wheeler also testified that the endorsement 'Richard Hawkins and Co.' on the cheque was not written by himself or Mr. Hawkins, and no one else had the authority to endorse cheques.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Frederick Archibald Nash, a bank cashier, testified that the defendant presented the altered cheque on January 13. Mr. Nash noticed that the signature appeared to have been inked over and that the crossing had been obliterated with a penknife.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Edward Herring Martin, the bank manager, testified that the defendant stated a man in the street, who'd had a 'row with the manager,' had asked him to cash the cheque for a payment of 2s. or 3s.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The cashier, Mr. Nash, stated in cross-examination that the defendant waited at the counter after being told to wait and 'did not attempt to move'. The defendant waited for 15 to 20 minutes before a constable arrived.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Upon being charged at the police station, the prisoner remarked, 'Damned hard luck.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Detective-sergeant Jesse Crouch testified that he compared a handwriting sample willingly provided by the prisoner with the endorsement on the cheque. In his opinion, it was the same writing.

[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Sarah Ann Staker, prisoner Brooks's mother-in-law, stated that on the morning of March 11, Brooks and another man brought a barrow with ironmongery to her house. Brooks took them away the same evening, saying he was going to sell them.

Description: In his defense, Edward Norris testified that he was a self-employed carter and had never met Brooks before. He stated that on Thursday, March 11, Brooks approached him in a pub and offered him half a sovereign to move some goods, which he accepted as a legitimate job.

[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: A former employer gave Brooks a good character as a workman. A police officer also testified that Brooks had previously borne a good character but had been out of work, suggesting 'it was really poverty that had brought him to this.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Wilfrid Dulken, a partner in the affected timber merchant business, testified that on March 27, a fire broke out at their wharf involving a large quantity of jute and other inflammable fibres. The fire lasted for about four days, and the total loss amounted to at least £40,000.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: William Caygill, a foreman at the wharf, stated that around noon on the day of the fire, he noticed the prisoner, Henry Davey, loitering in the area. Caygill confirmed that Davey was not an employee at the wharf.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The court was informed that 'Nothing criminal is known against the prisoner,' indicating a lack of any prior criminal record.

public.game_background: Hester Roberts was charged with maliciously wounding Joseph Henry Grinsey. The defense claimed that her actions were in self-defense.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Joseph Henry Grinsey, the prosecutor, testified that eleven months prior to the incident, he had refused to serve the prisoner, Hester Roberts.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: According to Grinsey, when he told the prisoner to leave, she refused, saying, "No; this will be a fair, stand-up b——fight," and then attempted to kick him.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Grinsey testified that prior to this incident, the prisoner had threatened to blind him and to kill him.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Cartwright testified that he did not hear the prisoner scream, "Let go my throat; you are choking me," and that the prosecutor did not strike her.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Constable McBean stated that when the prisoner was charged with malicious wounding, she made no reply.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Helen McCarthy testified that she sent in three claims for an income tax repayment but never received the money order for £7 9s. 6d. or the funds. She stated the signature on the cashed order was not her writing and she never gave anyone authority to sign it.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Inspector Cope testified that he obtained handwriting samples from Mrs. Clarke, asking her to write the names and words present on the money order.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Post office assistant Ada Perry testified that she seemed to recollect paying the order to a boy she thought was Mrs. Clarke's son, but 'could not swear positively'.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Inspector Cope testified that Mrs. Clarke initially provided a false alibi, stating she was away with Mrs. Palmer on Sunday night and did not return until Monday evening. Mrs. Palmer later stated that Mrs. Clarke returned home on Monday morning.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Inspector Cope testified that Mrs. Clarke told him, 'I have offered to pay Miss McCarthy back. I will pay it back, but I never took it.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Mrs. Clarke testified under oath that she did not write the signature on the Post Office Order and knew nothing about it.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The defendant and victim lived in a tenement house with a common letter-box on the outer door. Mrs. Clarke testified that there were 12 or 13 people living in the house, suggesting anyone could have intercepted the letter.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Three witnesses, Mrs. Toomey, Mrs. Woollard, and Mr. Strong, testified that Mrs. Clarke was a 'hardworking and respectable woman' and that no charge had ever been brought against her.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The post office assistant testified that she did not compare the signature on the order with the letter of advice, which would have revealed the spelling errors, and that it was not Post Office regulation to require the payee to sign in her presence.

public.game_background: Charles West was indicted for the manslaughter of Oliver William Howard, known as Jack Hearne. The death occurred following a fight outside a public house. The prosecution alleges that after the initial fistfight had concluded and the deceased had won, West struck Hearne on the head with a belt, causing a wound that later became septic and led to a fatal brain abscess. The defense contends that Hearne was the aggressor, that West does not remember using a belt due to being knocked down and intoxicated, and that the fatal infection could have been caused by the deceased's occupation.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Dr. Edward Parrott, the surgeon, testified that the deceased suffered from a septic wound that led to a fatal brain abscess. He stated that a blow from the buckle end of the belt in evidence would cause such a wound.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Isaac Williams acknowledged on cross-examination that the men had an 'extra glass or two' due to the holiday and that he did 'not suppose they were exactly sober.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Isaac Williams testified that the deceased, Jack Hearne, came to the public house the morning after the fight to 'beg my pardon for the row.' Williams also noted that Hearne 'seemed then all right.'

public.game_background: Charles Gurron and Charles Knight are charged with breaking and entering the shop of Mappin and Webb, Limited, and stealing jewellery valued at over £43,000. The prosecution alleges they acted as lookouts for three other men who committed the robbery and grievously wounded the watchman. The three primary perpetrators pleaded guilty to breaking and entering.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Detective-sergeant Arthur Woollard testified that he had seen prisoner Gurron in the company of William Russell (one of the men who pleaded guilty to the robbery) on two recent occasions, including August 2 and August 20.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Charles Gurron testified that he was in Cheapside because he had an appointment with a friend named Barker at the Bank corner, but his friend never arrived. He stated he later met another friend, Alfred Green, by chance and had a drink with him.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: When arrested, Knight was mounting his bicycle as if to ride away just after the three robbers were arrested. Detective Dyer pulled him off the bicycle.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Charles Knight testified that he had borrowed the bicycle the previous day for a planned holiday. He stated that on the day of the robbery, the bicycle's free wheel was faulty, causing the pedals to revolve without moving the wheel, and he stopped at Bird-in-Hand Court to fix it.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Henry Ernest Wrixon, the owner of the bicycle, testified that he had lent it to Knight on August 21. He confirmed that the bicycle was not perfectly sound and had a fault in the free wheel where 'the pedals sometimes revolving without the bicycle going forward.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Knight testified that he was at the 'Crown' public-house until 3 p.m., then rode to Petticoat Lane to buy trouser clips and fried fish, leaving for the Bank around 3:40 p.m. and arriving at Bird-in-Hand Court around 4:10 p.m., disputing witness Sutton's claim of seeing him there at 3:10 p.m.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The police investigation could not ascertain any connection between Knight and the other prisoners. Knight gave his correct name and address when arrested.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Three witnesses, including a furniture dealer, a furniture porter, and a house agent, gave prisoner Knight a good character.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: No stolen property was found on either Gurron or Knight upon their arrest. No tools or other incriminating items were found on Knight.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Police-constable William White testified that he observed Crispin and Thorpe acting suspiciously near the crime scene. He heard Crispin say to Thorpe, 'You get the stuff and I will take it away.' He also saw Stone on the other side of the road, appearing to watch his movements.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: P.C. White testified that after the barrel was loaded, Crispin and Thorpe pushed the trolley down the street and were joined by Stone. All three men then pushed the trolley up Tower Hill together before being stopped.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: P.C. White stated that when he seized Crispin and Stone, Crispin said, 'All right; we're copped.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: P.C. White testified that when he returned to the warehouse and questioned Lara, asking if he had 'sent any metal away,' Lara replied, 'No, only an old barrel.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Lara's employer, Mr. Jacob, testified that Lara had been in his service for seven years and bore a good character, stating he was 'honest, sober, and industrious.' Sergeant Brown confirmed that police inquiries found Lara's character to be 'very good.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Mr. Jacob, Lara's employer, identified the trolley, barrel, and solder as his property and confirmed that he had given no authority to Lara or anyone else to dispose of it.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Jacob testified that on the morning of the theft, Lara asked for his week's wages of 16s. to be paid in advance, stating that his father wanted the money.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Joseph Thorpe, in a statement not under oath, claimed he was a dock labourer out of work and simply asked Lara in the cellar, 'Have you got any old iron you do not want?' He stated Lara agreed to give him some and offered the use of his trolley.

public.game_background: Percy Charles Weston, a 28-year-old postman, is charged with fraud against a bookmaker, Arthur Peters. The prosecution alleges that Weston used his position at the Post Office to place bets on horses after the races had already been run and won. The scheme involved date-stamping an envelope with a time prior to a race, waiting for the results, enclosing a winning bet slip, and then introducing the letter into the mail stream for delivery, making it appear as a legitimate, pre-race wager.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Alfred Riley, an overseer, testified that on October 15, he saw the defendant Percy Weston leaving the post office at 2:15 p.m. This was more than an hour after Weston's duty had ended at 1:09 p.m., and he had no official reason to be on the premises.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Overseer Riley discovered a letter in Weston's handwriting, addressed to the bookmaker Arthur Peters, on the Wandsworth dispatching table around 2:20 p.m. The letter was postmarked 12:15 p.m. and should have been sent out for delivery by 1:09 p.m. at the latest.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Upon Weston's arrest on November 4, Investigator John Crompton found a letter in his pocket addressed to Peters. It was lightly sealed, bore a 12:30 p.m. postmark from that same day, and contained only a blank sheet of paper.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Investigator Crompton testified that after October 15, Weston was observed engaging in similar behavior. Specifically, on October 26, he was seen putting a letter down at 2:30 p.m., which was date-stamped 1:30 p.m., after two winners in early races were already known.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Arthur Peters stated that Weston had a suspicious betting pattern, frequently backing the first two winning horses of the day. He testified that Weston only backed losers twice, and those bets were posted early in the morning.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Lord Robert Cecil gave a character reference for Weston, stating that when Weston was in his service for six to eight months around 1900, he 'gave complete satisfaction, and, as far as I know was perfectly honest, straightforward, and truthful.'

The defense argues the shooting was an accident, claiming Brannan, in a drunken and foggy state, pulled out his revolver to frighten her by firing it at the ground, and it discharged unintentionally, striking her.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Ada Evans testified that just before the shooting, she heard the deceased, Florence Staples, say to the prisoner, "If you've got a couple of shillings you may come, otherwise keep away, but I shall be at London Bridge Station in the morning at 11."
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Police-constable Charles Mackay, who was off duty, heard the shot and found Florence Staples. When he asked what was wrong, she said, "I've been shot by a sailor."
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The morning after the shooting, prisoner Luke Brannan went to Highgate police station and turned himself in, stating, 'I am sorry to see by the papers that the young woman who was shot...is dead. I did not know she was dead or I should have done it on myself.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The revolver used in the shooting was found by a member of the public in County Terrace, about a five minutes' walk from the crime scene. The prisoner admitted to throwing it away.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: The prisoner, Luke Brannan, had 'very good' discharge papers from the Royal Navy, covering his service from December 1901 to September 1909.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Police-constable Charles F. Palmer testified that on October 18, he had arrested the prisoner for wantonly discharging the same revolver in the street while drunk. The prisoner pointed it towards the ground on that occasion.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Several witnesses who were with the prisoner and deceased earlier in the evening, including Clara Tregea and Helen Williams, testified that there was no quarrelling between them.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: A gunmaker, Dudley Williamson, testified that the prisoner purchased 25 cartridges on October 12 and another 14 at the end of October, stating he wanted them for practice.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Maud Morton, an assistant at the post office, testified that on December 8, 1909, at 9:10 p.m., she secured the premises with a padlock. The safe within was locked and contained over £1300 in cash, postal orders, and stamps.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Sergeant Herbert Dickison testified that at 10:35 p.m. on December 8, he examined the post office and found the door open, the padlock forced, and the office in disarray, strewn with postal orders.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Sergeant James Laing testified that at 12:30 a.m., he followed marks to 79 Rosebery Avenue, heard a chipping noise, and saw a light. Standing on a rear wall, he observed the prisoner and six other men attempting to force open a safe in the back parlour.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Police-constable Harry Woodley produced a plan of 79 Rosebery Avenue, testifying that the rear wall was 8 ft. high and 14 ft. from the window, and that anyone standing on the wall could see down into the room.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: John Wilkinson, the prisoner, testified that he was at 79 Rosebery Avenue to collect a one-shilling debt from an Italian named Gianicoli. As Gianicoli could not pay, he invited Wilkinson to sleep in a chair in his room upstairs.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: William Henry Blackwood, a barman called by the defense, testified that he did not remember the prisoner being in his pub on December 8, contrary to the prisoner's alibi.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Albert Gianicoli, the Italian, testified that Sergeant Leach burst into his room and took him downstairs. He confirmed he owed the prisoner a shilling, but made no mention of the prisoner being in his room.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Blanche Edith Knowles, an assistant clerk at the post office, testified that the prisoner, George Vincent, was one of three men present at the counter when 71 postal orders were stolen on the morning of December 7. She identified him at a police line-up.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Charles Saville, a grocer, testified that on the night of December 11, the prisoner purchased items from his shop and paid with a forged 8s. postal order, which was later identified as one of the stolen items. He picked the prisoner out of a line-up.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Cecilia Sarah Montford, a post-mistress, confirmed that the stamp and initials on the postal order tendered at Mr. Saville's shop were forgeries.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Sergeant Frederick Goodey testified that upon arrest, the prisoner said, 'I did not steal them; is it identification?' and later asked, 'Have you got the others, or am I the only one?'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William Armstrong, the prisoner's employer, testified that on December 7, the day of the theft, the prisoner was working for him in Spitalfields Market from seven o'clock until eleven o'clock in the morning. The theft took place at approximately 10:05 a.m.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Inspector Thomas Hollis testified that he prepared a plan of the accident scene. He noted the road was dirty with thin mud, and there were no signs of wheel marks, suggesting the motor-cab's wheels were likely in the tram lines.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Sergeant William Blake, who examined the cab after the accident, testified that the near-side tyre of the front wheel had been punctured and the offside steering arm was bent. He stated the road surface was wet, but not greasy.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Dr. Arthur C. Robinson, who performed the post-mortem, stated in cross-examination that he found no bruises on the deceased that he would have expected to find had the victim been dragged under the cab for 15 or 30 yards.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: When questioned by the jury, Ealey explained he had no time to sound his horn because his attention was entirely focused on the steering gear and the brakes during the emergency.

public.game_background: Frank Turner, a pianoforte tuner, was charged on three counts: first, with unlawfully and fraudulently converting a bicycle entrusted to him by Walter Busby for a specific purpose (to sell it for £5); second, with fraudulently converting the proceeds of that sale to his own use; and third, with fraudulently converting the sum of £5 received on account of Walter Busby to his own use and benefit. The prosecution accepted a plea of guilty to robbery without violence, but the transcript details the trial on the conversion charges.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev_turner_01]
Description: Walter Busby, a cycle maker, testified that the prisoner, a customer, asked for a lady's bicycle, stating he had a customer for one for £5. Busby let him have the bicycle on the understanding that Turner was to sell it for £5 or bring it back the same evening, and could keep any amount he made over £5.
[EVIDENCE ev_turner_02]
Description: Edith Nichol testified that she bought a bicycle from the prisoner in May 1904 for £5. She stated that the prisoner told her he was selling it for a Mr. Busby.
[EVIDENCE ev_turner_05]
Description: After Busby gave him two or three days to find the money, Turner disappeared. Busby testified, 'I never saw him again until last week at the police court.' This was corroborated by Detective Wedden's testimony that a warrant was issued in 1904 and Turner was only arrested in June 1910.
[EVIDENCE ev_turner_06]
Description: Detective Richard Wedden testified that upon being arrested years after the event, Turner's statement was, 'Oh, that's years ago. I did not think it would come to this.'
[EVIDENCE ev_turner_08]
Description: Elizabeth Scultze, a mission lady, corroborated the prisoner's statement that after he ran away from home, she met him, gave him food and lodging, and found him a job.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Detective Alfred Kirby testified that he saw the three prisoners, Murray, Patten, and Jones, acting suspiciously together at the corner of Queen Street and kept them under observation.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Upon arrest, a counterfeit florin was found on Murray, and 11s. in small silver and 3s. 6d. in bronze was found on Patten. Nothing was found on Jones.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William John Webster, Inspector of Coins, testified that the florin from the 'Boar's Head', the ones from Lockhart's, and the one found on Murray were all counterfeit and made from the same mould.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Mabel Mary Sander, the publican's wife, testified that on September 24, the prisoner John Beck ordered a penny drink and tendered a florin. She recognized it was counterfeit, bent it with her fingers, and returned it to him. Beck apologized, paid with a penny, drank his beer, and left.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Henry James Walter Sander, the proprietor, corroborated his wife's testimony about the florin on September 24. He added that two days earlier, on September 22, Beck had come in and paid for a drink with a counterfeit crown piece, which Sander only discovered after Beck had left.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Henry Bagley, the potman, testified that on September 24 he saw Beck in the bar and went to fetch a constable. When Beck left, Bagley followed him, at which point Beck saw him and began to run before being stopped.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: When questioned by PC Wild on the way to the station, Beck stated, 'I am as innocent as anybody about the two-shilling piece. I changed a half-sovereign at the 'Swan,' Shoreditch, last night, and I must have had it given to me in change.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William John Webster, Inspector of Coins at H.M. Mint, confirmed that both the florin and the crown piece were counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Prisoner's statement before the magistrate was, 'I know nothing about the 5s. piece.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: PC Wild testified that when arrested, Beck was found to be in possession of 6s. 8d. in good money (three florins, a sixpence, and 2d. in bronze).

[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edith Richmond, the prisoner's wife, testified that on the evening of November 8, the prisoner came up behind her, confirmed her identity, and then 'drew something sharp across my throat.' She then ran home to her mother.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Edith Richmond stated that the prisoner had left her on August 13, nearly three months before the assault.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: PC Dicker also testified that at the station, the prisoner said to his wife, 'You are a lucky girl.'
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: PC Dicker found a pair of scissors in the prisoner's inside jacket pocket during the arrest.
[EVIDENCE ev14]

[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: The surgeon testified that the wounds 'might have been caused by the scissors (produced).'

information:

Description: William Edward Stratford, a clerk for the General Post Office, testified that following complaints of theft, he created a test packet. This packet was addressed to a fictitious company and contained two half-crowns, one sixpence, two pennies, and five penny stamps, all of which bore his private mark.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Multiple witnesses testified to the prisoner's good character. It was stated that he had been employed by the Post Office for about ten years and had borne a 'very good character.' His father and the secretary of his Oddfellow's Lodge also gave evidence to his good character.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Under cross-examination, Overseer Maryon stated that there would have been 'about 15 or 16 men at the sorting table' at the time the incident occurred. Stratford also noted about 400 people are employed at the office.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prosecution confirmed that only the marked coins were ever found. The test letter itself, the match-box it contained, and the five marked postage stamps were not recovered from the prisoner or his home.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: It was stated that a pattern of thefts had been occurring at the post office prior to the prisoner's arrest. During the preceding four to five months, 40 to 50 complaints had been received regarding lost letters containing a total of £12 in coin, and such complaints ceased after the prisoner was arrested.

public.game_background: Stinie Morrison was indicted for and charged on the coroner's inquisition with the wilful murder of Leon Beron on Clapham Common on January 1st, 1911. The prosecution alleged that Morrison bludgeoned and stabbed Beron, robbing him of his money and a gold watch. The defense argued an alibi and provided alternative explanations for the circumstantial evidence presented.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On the morning of January 1, 1911, the body of Leon Beron was discovered on Clapham Common. The body was lying in some bushes near a path, with a trail of blood indicating it had been dragged. A black handkerchief was laid over the crown of his head.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Dr. Freyberger, a pathologist, testified that the cause of death was concussion and fracture of the skull from approximately eight blows with a blunt instrument. He also found knife cuts on the face and stab wounds to the body which he believed were inflicted after death.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Cab driver Edward Hayman testified that at around 2:00 AM, he picked up Morrison and another man in Sidney Street (East End) and drove them to Lavender Gardens, Clapham, setting them down a short distance from the murder scene.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Cab driver Alfred Stephens testified that at around 3:00 AM, he picked up a man he identified as Morrison at the cab rank at Clapham Cross (near the murder scene) and drove him to Kennington.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: On January 1, the day of the murder, Morrison, who had limited known income, changed ten sovereigns for two £5 notes and cashed a cheque for another man, paying him in gold. When arrested, he had two £5 notes and over £4 in gold.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Forensic experts Dr. Smith and Dr. Willcox testified that they found small spots of human blood on the collar, tie, and shirt cuff that Morrison was wearing around the time of the murder.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: On January 1, Morrison deposited a loaded revolver and cartridges in a cloakroom at St. Mary's Station. When arrested, he was a convict on license who had failed to report his change of address.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Hector Munro, the acting manager of the Shoreditch Empire, testified in rebuttal that on that specific night, the theatre was sold out by 8:55 PM, the ticket price was 1s 6d (not 1s as claimed), and that certain acts the Brodskys described were not performing.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Morrison testified that the large sums of money in his possession were not from the robbery. He stated they came from £20 sent by his mother in Russia in late November and a £35 win at a gambling club on December 1.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Morrison testified that the bloodstains on his clothing were the result of a nosebleed he suffered while washing in the yard on the morning of January 8. He claimed that splashing water caused blood to get on his cuff, and he then transferred it to his collar with his fingers.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: In a dramatic late intervention, Police-constable George Greaves testified that he was on duty in the charge room and heard Detective-sergeant Brogden tell Morrison he was being brought in 'on a serious charge—on suspicion of murder.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Alfred Thomson, a barman, testified that the prisoner, George Merriman, tendered a 2s piece (a florin) to pay for ale. Thomson was doubtful about the coin and passed it to the licensee, Mr. Lindsay, for inspection.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Police-constable Gibson testified that he was called by the landlord and took Merriman back into the middle bar. He conducted a 'temporary' search of Merriman there but did not find the second counterfeit coin.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Sidney William Smith, Assistant Assayer for H.M. Mint, testified that both florins (the one uttered and the one found in the pocket) were counterfeit and made from the same mould.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The court was informed that Merriman had five previous convictions, mostly for loitering, and was an associate of bad characters. However, he had no felony conviction for five years.

public.game_background: Zurka Dubof, Jacob Peters, John Rosen, and Nina Vassileva were indicted for conspiring and agreeing together and with other persons unknown to break and enter the jewellery shop of Henry Samuel Harris with intent to steal his goods. The charge arose from a failed burglary attempt at 119 Houndsditch on December 16, 1910, which was interrupted by police and resulted in the murder of three officers, allegedly by deceased members of the same group.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Zurka either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On the night of December 16, 1910, Max Weill, whose property at 120 Houndsditch adjoined Harris's jewellers, heard drilling and boring noises against his back wall. He reported this to the police, which led to their arrival and the interruption of the burglary.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Multiple witnesses, including neighbors Bessie Jacobs and Joseph Da Costa, testified they frequently saw Nina Vassileva at 11 Exchange Buildings, suggesting she was living there and taking down the shutters in the mornings.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Witnesses identified Zurka Dubof and John Rosen in or near Exchange Buildings prior to the crime. George Richardson claimed to see them leaving with bags, while Solomon Abrahams saw them entering No. 11 with Gardstein.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Witness Joseph Da Costa identified Jacob Peters as a man he had seen on several occasions loitering near Nos. 9 and 11 Exchange Buildings, often in the company of another associate named Federof.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Vassileva's landlords, Isaac and Fanny Gordon, testified that she returned to her lodgings on December 17 with her hair dyed black. They saw her burn papers and clothes, wash the dye out of her hair, and express great fear of arrest and deportation to Russia, where she said she would be hanged.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: John Rosen gave a false statement to police, denying he knew Fritz or the other key figures. He later retracted this, admitting he was with the group at 59 Grove Street on the day of the crime and claimed Vassileva told him she had been living at Exchange Buildings.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: On the afternoon of December 16, hours before the crime, key members of the group gathered at 59 Grove Street. Witness Nicolai Tockmacoff saw Peter the Painter, Fritz, Rosen, Josef, Federof, Dubof, and Gardstein all present.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Chairs found at the operational base in 11 Exchange Buildings were identified by police as being 'en suite' with chairs found in Peter the Painter's room at the group's social hub, 59 Grove Street.

He identified the alteration and the handwriting on the envelope as the prisoner's. The prisoner's account with his bank was closed at the time.

Description: Detective-inspector John Collisson testified that upon arrest, the prisoner denied the charge, stating, 'I sent a cheque to the manager of the Delhi Bank and it was for £11,000'. The detective noted the prisoner was very excited and did not seem to realise the gravity of the offence.

[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Dr. Sidney Reginald Dyer, Medical Officer at Brixton Prison, testified that he had the prisoner under observation and, in his opinion, the prisoner is insane and has been suffering from delusions for some years.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Charles Ainsworth Mitchell, an expert in the chemistry of ink, testified that the endorsement on the cheque was in the same ink as the body of the cheque.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Official records proved that both Forbes and Harrison were undischarged bankrupts at the time they were operating the firm and taking investments.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: William Cash, a chartered accountant who examined the firm's books, testified that the firm's actual stock market dealings with brokers resulted in a net loss of over £3,800.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Stockbroker Charles Wood testified that his firm closed its account with Harrison upon learning he was a partner in Duncan Forbes & Co., as their advertising methods suggested they were not conducting a 'fair business.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Testimony revealed that after the business began to fail, defendant Forbes rented new offices under the alias 'Rupert Scott' to start a new, similar business called 'The Prudential Exchange.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Defendant Isaacs claimed he was only a paid employee or manager, not a partner, and was following the instructions of Forbes and Harrison. This was supported by testimony from staff members like Cyril Gall and Minnie Harling.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Previous convictions for fraud and false pretences were proven against both Forbes and Harrison. Harrison had a particularly bad record for 'bucket-shop' frauds.

public.game_background: Henry Charles Richardson, an upholsterer, was charged with endeavouring to obtain £6 9s. 5d. from a bookmaker, William Walker, by means of a forged instrument. The prosecution alleged that Richardson conspired with a postman, Albert Sweet, to intercept a letter, insert a post-dated winning betting slip after the race had run, and have it delivered under the false pretence of it being mis-sorted, thereby forging the postmark's implication of timely posting.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Henry either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Ella Walker testified that the envelope contained a betting slip in Richardson's handwriting for races that day, which showed he had won £6 9s. 5d. She also testified Richardson later requested payment for the bet.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Harry Turner of the Exchange Telegraph Company testified that the result of the final race on the betting slip was known at 3:39 p.m. on October 19. The envelope containing the slip was postmarked at 2:45 p.m. the same day.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: William Stratford, a G.P.O. investigator, testified that when he returned to Richardson's house with Sweet and a uniformed officer, Richardson caught sight of them and 'ran away very quickly.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: William Drake, Richardson's employer and a master builder, testified under oath that he personally went to 32, St. Leonards Square at 3:30 p.m. on October 19 and 'went round the premises with Richardson.' He stated, 'there is no mistake about it.'

public.game_background: Alfonso Manuello, an 18-year-old engineer, is charged with stealing one diamond ring from the shop of John Lazarus, a jeweller. The prosecution alleges that Manuello used distraction tactics to steal the ring and was caught with it in his possession after denying the theft. The defense argues that the incident was a misunderstanding exacerbated by a language barrier and that there was no intent to steal.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Alfonso either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: The jeweller, John Lazarus, testified that the prisoner, Alfonso Manuello, entered the shop and spoke in 'broken English, but quite clearly,' asking to see diamond rings.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Lazarus stated that after being shown a tray of about 80 rings, Manuello began 'talking very earnestly about one of the other rings,' creating a distraction.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: When stopped by a staff member at the door, Lazarus asked for the ring, and Manuello replied, 'I have no ring.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The police constable testified that on the way to the station, Manuello placed his hand in his mackintosh pocket, and upon removing it, the officer 'saw a ring glitter.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: When formally charged, Manuello stated in English, 'I did not intend to steal the ring; I would have paid for it.'
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: The jury returned a verdict of Guilty, but included 'a recommendation to mercy on account of his youth.'

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: William David Shepherd, a foreman for the firm, testified that at about 5 p.m. he saw the prisoner, John Sales, in the company of another man, carrying a box that he recognised as belonging to his firm just outside their premises.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Inspector James Dunning testified that when he told the prisoner he was being charged with stealing the box, the prisoner replied, "I did not steal the box."
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: The day after his arrest, while being taken to the police court, the prisoner said, "I may say I shall plead guilty to this charge and have it settled at the police court."
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Mr. Shepherd testified that "anybody could easily steal the box, as travellers and other people were continually coming in and out."
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner confessed to a previous conviction at the County of London Sessions in 1908 for burglary, for which he received six months' hard labour. Several other convictions were also proven.

public.game_background: Frederick Henry Seddon and Margaret Ann Seddon were charged with the wilful murder of their lodger, Eliza Mary Barrow, by administering arsenic. The prosecution alleged a financial motive, stemming from an annuity arrangement where the Seddons inherited Miss Barrow's assets upon her death. The defense contended that Miss Barrow died of natural causes (epidemic diarrhoea) and that all financial transactions were legitimate.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Frederick either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Miss Barrow, a woman of considerable means, transferred approximately £1,600 in India Stock and two leasehold properties to Frederick Seddon. In return, Seddon was to pay her a life annuity, which ceased upon her death, making him the sole beneficiary of these assets.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: A chemist, Walter Thorley, testified that the Seddons' daughter purchased a packet of Mather's arsenical flypapers on August 26, 1911. These papers were seen in Miss Barrow's room, and analysis showed each contained a potentially fatal dose of arsenic.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Dr. Willcox, Senior Scientific Analyst to the Home Office, conducted a post-mortem on the exhumed body and found 2.01 grains of arsenic. He concluded that death was caused by acute arsenical poisoning from one or more large doses.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Immediately after Miss Barrow's death, Frederick Seddon arranged a cheap £3 7s 6d funeral in a common grave, telling the undertaker the deceased had left only £4 10s. He failed to properly notify her relatives, who were unaware of the funeral.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Miss Barrow withdrew £216 in gold from her bank in June 1911, yet only £10 was found in her room after her death. On the night she died, two insurance agents in Seddon's office testified to seeing him count a large sum of gold, estimated at over £200.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: The day after Miss Barrow's death, Frederick Seddon took her diamond ring to a jeweller to be enlarged to fit his own finger. At the same time, he arranged to have the family inscription erased from her mother's gold watch.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Over several months, Mrs. Seddon cashed 27 of Miss Barrow's £5 bank notes. When doing so at shops where she was not known, she frequently gave the false name 'M. Scott' and a false address.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Upon being arrested for murder by arsenic poisoning, Frederick Seddon immediately asked police, 'Have they found arsenic in her body?' and suggested alternative causes like carbolic acid or Sanitas.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Margaret Seddon was the primary caregiver during Miss Barrow's illness but was acquitted of the murder charge. The prosecution suggested she was an accomplice, while the defense portrayed her as a dutiful but dominated wife.

public.game_background: John Larkins was charged with unlawfully uttering counterfeit coin on two separate occasions, February 16 and March 5, 1912, with the key issue being whether he knew the coins were counterfeit.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. John either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Daisy Paine, a barmaid, testified that on February 16, John Larkins tendered a 2s piece for a glass of ale. She tested the coin with acid, found it to be counterfeit, and informed him. Larkins said nothing, paid with a good coin, and took the bad one back.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Police-constable John Bassett Smith testified that after being called to the 'Lord Nelson', he approached the prisoner and said, 'I am informed you have got bad coin.' The prisoner took a 2s piece from his waistcoat pocket and said, 'Yes; I must have got it in change for half a sovereign last evening.'
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Upon searching the prisoner on February 16, PC Smith found 1s. 6d. in silver and 15d. in bronze, all of which was good money. No other counterfeit coins were found on his person.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Edward Lindsay, licensee of the 'White Thorn', testified that on March 5, the prisoner ordered ale and tobacco. He handed Lindsay a 2s piece, which Lindsay bent and found to be counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: According to Edward Lindsay, after the first coin was rejected, the prisoner handed him another 2s piece, which was also found to be bad. The prisoner then ran away.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Police-constable Thomas Birch testified he found the prisoner in the backyard of a house in Sherwood Street. When asked what he was doing, the prisoner said, 'I came in here for a rest.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Sergeant Horace Travers testified that when the prisoner was formally charged, he said, 'All right; I was drunk when I done it.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Sidney William Smith, Assistant Assayer at H.M. Mint, testified that all three florins produced in evidence were counterfeit and 'moderately well made.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: In a written statement, the prisoner claimed, 'The coin I passed at the 'Lord Nelson' I did not know was bad.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: The prisoner's statement explained his possession of the second bad coin: 'I afterwards sold a couple of razors for 2s. and received in payment the 2s. piece I tendered to Lindsay.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Regarding his flight from the pub, the prisoner wrote, 'I...ran away because I did not want to be shown up in that neighbour hood where I am known.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The prisoner's statement denied the most damaging part of the second incident: 'I did not tender him the other florin. He must have had that passed on to him, and he accused me of passing it to have his own back.'

public.game_background: Frederick Chandler was charged with feloniously wounding Frederick Harris, a jeweller, with intent to do him grievous bodily harm. The prosecution argued that Chandler orchestrated a series of meetings under the false pretense of purchasing jewellery, with the ultimate goal of luring Harris to his flat to assault and rob him. The defense contended that Chandler acted on a 'sudden impulse' after mistakenly believing Harris was behaving improperly with his wife.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Frederick either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: On March 6, jeweller Frederick Harris received a letter signed 'Fred Chandler' requesting a viewing of a 'stone, about 2 carats, white' at 9, Duchess Street. Harris had not known Chandler before this.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Upon being found by police, Chandler said to Harris, 'Harris, you won't charge me, will you, after all these years I have known you?' He later said, 'I am sorry. It was a sudden impulse.'
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Dr. Thomas Rose, the divisional surgeon, testified that Harris suffered from a contused, H-shaped wound on his head and that his tongue and lip were inflamed by a corrosive fluid. He confirmed a ginger-beer bottle could have caused the wound and that a syringe found at the scene smelled strongly of ammonia.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Police-constable John Welsby testified that he chased and caught Chandler running away from the scene without a hat and with blood on his face. Chandler admitted to the officer, 'I have had a row with a friend of mine, and I hit him with a bottle.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: At the flat, PC Welsby found the broken ginger-beer bottle, which Chandler identified as the weapon. When a syringe was found, Chandler asked the constable, 'What would you do, constable, if he assaulted your wife?'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Jacob Pewy, the landlord, testified that Chandler and his wife rented the rooms on March 4, paid only a small deposit, and brought no luggage, claiming it would follow later. It never did.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Lionel Green, a jeweller with a prior conviction for receiving stolen property, testified for the defense. He stated that he believed Harris and Chandler were in his shop together looking at a ring about a week before December 29, suggesting they knew each other previously.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Nellie Roberts, the prisoner's wife, testified that as the door opened, she was handing Harris back a hairpin when Chandler attacked him. She claimed she became frightened and ran out without seeing the assault itself.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Nellie Roberts also testified that she had bought ammonia for Chandler to clean clothes. She said he had broken the original bottle and put the ammonia into a rubber bottle (the syringe).
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: In his unsworn statement, Chandler claimed he believed prosecutor was behaving improperly with his wife, lost his temper, seized the ginger-beer bottle, and struck him on the spur of the moment. He denied any attempt to throw ammonia.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: After the verdict, it was revealed that Chandler had previous convictions for obtaining money by false pretences, housebreaking, and conspiracy to defraud.

public.game_background: George Fitzgibbons was charged with the wilful murder of his wife, Annie Fitzgibbons. The prosecution argued he intentionally stabbed her, while the defense claimed it was an accident or an act of manslaughter resulting from a sudden quarrel and loss of control.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Cornelius Holland testified that he was with the prisoner and his wife shortly before the incident, and they were on 'quite friendly and happy' terms. As they parted, the prisoner expressed that he was 'heartbroken at the thoughts of losing my girl,' referring to their daughter who had died a fortnight prior.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: The son, Richard Fitzgibbons, testified that he was awakened by a scream and saw his mother 'in a half lying, half sitting position on the floor near my bed... bleeding from the right breast.'
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Richard Fitzgibbons testified that immediately after seeing his injured mother, he saw his father in the kitchen, who said, 'I have done it,' or something to that effect.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: The daughter, Mary Fitzgibbons, testified that the knife used in the incident was one she had used for supper and left on the kitchen table for her parents to use.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Dr. John Rees Gabe, the Divisional Surgeon, testified that the fatal wound was directed 'downwards and inwards,' puncturing a major vein near the heart, and that 'considerable force had been used to inflict it.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Detective-inspector Gough testified the prisoner stated upon arrest: 'I don't know how it was, but she would nag, nag, nag... It was not done with any murderous intent.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: The children, Richard and Mary, both testified that after their sister's death a month prior, their mother took to drinking and became 'very aggravating and annoying.'

public.game_background: William Webster, a porter, is charged with feloniously receiving 136 stolen postal packets from Alfred Henry Titheradge, a postman. Titheradge pleaded guilty to the theft. Webster pleads not guilty, claiming he was unknowingly used by Titheradge to hold the stolen goods.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. William either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.

public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Police-constable James Kibby arrested Webster near his home in King's Cross. The brown bag he was carrying was opened at the G.P.O. and found to contain 136 postal packets with uncancelled stamps.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Edward Stratford of the G.P.O. testified that when questioned, Webster denied knowing Titheradge, denied going into the lavatory, and claimed he had found the bag on top of a bus.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Alfred Titheradge, the thief, testified that he had arranged to meet Webster with the bag but did not tell him what it was for. Titheradge stated, 'I should not think he knew I was going to put letters into it.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: When Titheradge and Webster were charged together, Titheradge did not say that Webster was innocent or knew nothing about the theft. He testified, 'When he was charged with stealing these letters I did not say that he did not know anything about it; I did not know what to say.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: In his defense, Webster testified that Titheradge had asked him to 'take a parcel home to mind for him till he called at the shop for it,' providing an innocent reason for meeting him with the bag.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: When asked why he did not provide his explanation at the Police Court, Webster stated, 'I did not give my explanation at the Police Court, because I knew the case was going for trial. I have given it to-day for the first time.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: After the guilty verdict, it was revealed that Webster had multiple previous convictions, including for stealing a contribution box in 1907 and burglary in 1907.

public.game_background: Harry Day, an ex-policeman, was indicted for the wilful murder of his wife, Mary Jane Day. The prosecution argued that Day beat his wife to death, citing medical evidence of numerous severe injuries inconsistent with an accidental fall and prior threats made by the accused. The defense contended that the deceased, a chronic alcoholic, sustained her fatal injuries from falling down a flight of stairs and that the accused was a patient husband dealing with a difficult situation. The jury ultimately returned a verdict of 'Not guilty'.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Police-constable Thomas Bennett, the deceased's brother, testified that on the last night he saw his sister alive, the prisoner said, 'I will do her in if she does not stop it,' referring to her drinking.
[EVIDENCE ev3]
Description: Mrs. Martha O'Brien, a lodger, testified that on the day before the death, she heard the prisoner and his wife quarrelling and heard him say that he would 'cut her throat.' She also heard strikes 'as if a person was being thrashed' and heavy thuds.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Police-constable E. Avery, a lodger, testified that the deceased was often 'very drunk' and used to 'fall about a lot.' On the day of her death, he witnessed her fall down eight or nine stairs.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Dr. Arthur Head, who performed the post-mortem, testified that the cause of death was 'direct violence to the abdomen,' which ruptured the mesentery. He found 50 bruises and stated the injuries could not have been caused by her falling downstairs.
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Dr. Head testified that the deceased's wedding ring was bent, and that this condition could not have been caused by a fall; 'the finger must have been trodden upon.'
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Police-constable E. Avery, who lodged in the house, testified that the prisoner 'had a good deal to put up with from her' and, in his opinion, 'was most patient and kind with her.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: George Marshall, a lodger, stated he was with the prisoner in the kitchen from 8.35 p.m. to 10.20 p.m. on the night of the death, during which time the prisoner only went upstairs once for a couple of minutes and no sounds were heard from upstairs.
