information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Prosecutrix Elizabeth Nice testified that the prisoner, a stranger, invited her for a drink, and later followed her, struck her, knocked her down, snatched her purse containing 3s. 6d., and ran away. She stated she never lost sight of him before giving him into custody.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Upon searching the prisoner, the police found a half-crown and a shilling (3s. 6d.) in his jacket pocket, the exact amount the prosecutrix claimed was in her stolen purse.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: No purse was found on the prisoner when he was searched.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: The prisoner, Thomas Sullivan, testified that he met the prosecutrix with a friend, William Bennett, and that she asked them to treat her to a drink.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Under cross-examination, defense witness William Turner admitted he and the prisoner 'have been boys together and we were soldiers together'.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Alfred Turner, the pub licensee, testified that the prosecutrix began dancing and using bad language, and he told all three (prosecutrix, Sullivan, and Bennett) to 'clear out'.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: The pub licensee, Alfred Turner, also testified that the prosecutrix was sober at the time of the incident.
[EVIDENCE ev14]
Description: Detective-sergeant Albert Chatt testified that upon inquiry, he found the prosecutrix's general reputation to be 'very bad both as regards drink and immorality' and that she caused her husband 'a great deal of trouble'.
[EVIDENCE ev15]
Description: Detective-sergeant Chatt also testified that the prisoner 'bears an excellent character for sobriety and honesty' and had been working in the area for 14 years.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: The detective testified that the prisoner had drawn 7s. 8d. in wages on the day of the incident.

public.game_background: Charles William James Harden, an auxiliary postman, was tried for conspiring with Walter Ambrose and a man named Darby to defraud a bookmaker, Thomas Campbell. The scheme allegedly involved submitting late betting slips on winning horses in envelopes bearing forged, earlier postmarks, which Harden is accused of introducing into the sorting office.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Charles either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Ambrose provided a signed statement detailing the scheme. He stated that Darby suggested the plan, and that he (Ambrose) obtained postmarked envelopes, which Darby filled with winning betting slips. Ambrose claimed he then gave these envelopes to Harden to take into the sorting office, and that he gave Harden 1s. for his role.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Thomas Campbell, the bookmaker, testified that on July 23rd he saw Ambrose meet Harden near the sorting office. He stated they went to a lavatory, where Ambrose handed Harden 'something white, resembling an envelope,' after which Harden entered the sorting office.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Thomas Campbell also testified that he had seen a similar event on July 19th, where Ambrose gave Harden something white outside the sorting office, and Harden then went inside.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Upon his arrest, Detective Albert Blake testified that Harden said, 'Yes, sir, but I never had any of the money. I never knew what was going on to tell you the truth.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: William Edward Stratford testified that Harden had been in the Post Office for four years and has a 'very good record.' He also works as a coachman, where he has a good record.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Frederick Charles Nunn, an inspector of postmen, testified that Harden came on duty at about 4:10 p.m. on July 19th and 23rd, in time for the 5:30 p.m. delivery.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Thomas Campbell testified that he became suspicious because the date stamp impression was on the back of the envelope but not on the betting slip enclosed within, leading him to contact the Post Office.

public.game_background: Charles James Dabinet, an auxiliary postman, is charged with stealing a postal packet containing 5 shillings (in two half-crowns) and one silk handkerchief, the property of His Majesty's Postmaster-General, while being an officer of the Post Office.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Charles either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Edward Joseph Stratford, a G.P.O. clerk, created a 'test packet' addressed to an imaginary person at a real address. He enclosed a specially designed white silk handkerchief and two half-crowns bearing his private mark.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: After the verdict, it was stated that 'a hundred complaints had been received during five weeks before prisoner's arrest relating to losses on his walk. Those losses had ceased since his arrest.'
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: It was also stated post-verdict that 'Eight or nine postal packets which had been tampered with were found in the possession of prisoner on his arrest.'

public.game_background: John Mansfield is charged with the wilful murder of his brother, Charles Mansfield, by shooting him with a revolver. The prosecution argues it was an intentional act following a quarrel over money and the deceased's unemployment. The defense claims the shooting was an accident that occurred when the deceased produced the weapon during the argument and it discharged as the prisoner tried to disarm him in a struggle.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. John either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Herbert Sanderson, a neighbour, testified that he heard the prisoner and deceased quarrelling loudly just before the shot. He heard the prisoner say, "I keep you... you would b—well starve me... Starve you—I will, too—you f—bastard."
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Dr. William Willcox, a scientific analyst, testified that based on the bullet's trajectory and the holes passing through four layers of bedclothes, it would have been "practically impossible" for the deceased to have shot himself, either intentionally or accidentally, while sitting up in bed.
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Dr. Burton testified that the deceased, in what was presented as a dying declaration, was asked who shot him and replied, "I did." When asked what he was doing, he said, "Trying to load it."
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Robert Churchill, a gun expert, testified that based on experiments with the bedclothes, the muzzle of the revolver must have been over five or six inches away when the shot was fired.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: Detective-sergeant Howard testified that in the prisoner's room, he found a locked box containing 43 live cartridges that fit the murder weapon.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: The prisoner testified that he was always on good terms with his brother, joined the same army battalion to be near him, and had been financially supporting him while he was out of work.

public.game_background: Henry Hyde and William Dempsey were charged with unlawfully uttering counterfeit coin twice on the same day. The prosecution alleges that Hyde first attempted to pass a counterfeit half-crown at a public-house, and shortly thereafter, Dempsey successfully passed the same or a similar coin at a nearby newsagent's shop.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Arthur Grace, a barman at the 'Kentish Drovers' public-house, testified that on November 9, around 9 p.m., Hyde and Dempsey came in together. Hyde ordered drinks and tendered a counterfeit half-crown, which Grace rejected as bad. Both men then left together.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Sidney William Smith, an assistant assayer from H.M. Mint, examined the half-crown and testified that it was counterfeit.
[EVIDENCE ev9]
Description: During cross-examination, PC Burchell's trial testimony was shown to contradict his police court deposition regarding who had money on them at the time of arrest. The deposition stated Hyde had 1s. 9d., while at trial he said it was Dempsey.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Testimony from Michael Burns, a carman. On the afternoon of November 22, he saw the two prisoners hanging about outside the wharf around 3 PM and again at 5 PM. On the second occasion, Cornish had a barrow with some sacks on it.
[EVIDENCE ev2]
Description: Testimony from Charles Living, a van guard. He saw the two prisoners loitering in the yard, with Cornish carrying a sack under his arm. He then witnessed both men enter an unoccupied warehouse and informed the police.
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Testimony from Police-constable Arthur Hunt. After gaining access to the roof, he saw Hall standing by a quantity of lead that had been ripped off. When addressed, Hall said, 'All right, governor; my mate is along there on the right.'
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Testimony from Police-constable Alfred Rawlings. He stated that while waiting to be charged, Cornish said, 'It is hard luck; I was outside with a barrow.' After being identified, Cornish allegedly said, 'My luck is right out; he did not see me go in.'
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Testimony from Arthur Trenton, cashier for the Standard Life Assurance Company. He confirmed the company owned the warehouse and stated the value of the stolen lead was approximately £6.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Baslie Leslie Tilton Pattinson testified that after advertising for a motor cycle, he received a letter from a 'T. G. Thomson' and consequently sent a £20 money order to an address at 224a, Shaftesbury Avenue.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: Charles Henry Rogers, a prison warder who knew the prisoner's handwriting, testified that he believed the fraudulent letters were written by Davis.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: In his statement to the court, Albert Davis claimed he obtained the goods with 'no criminal intent.' He asserted his real purpose was to use the crime to bring his personal troubles and past grievances to the attention of the Public Prosecutor.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev1]
Description: Between Christmas Eve and December 30, 1912, the office of solicitor Donald Henry Pettitt at 27, Chancery Lane was entered. A cheque-book was left in an unlocked drawer, and on returning to the office, ten cheques and their counterfoils were found to have been torn out.
[EVIDENCE ev7]
Description: Mr. Dunslow, the tobacconist, identified the prisoner at the Bridewell police lineup on January 14. He described it as 'the fairest identification I have ever seen.'
[EVIDENCE ev8]
Description: Inspector Herbert Hine testified that upon searching the prisoner, he found a set of nine keys, describing four of them as 'skeleton or master keys.' A further bunch of 28 keys, some of which were also skeleton keys, were found in a locked attache case in the prisoner's rooms.
[EVIDENCE ev10]
Description: When prisoner was arrested, he gave evasive answers. He claimed the £20 note was from a stockbroker whom he refused to name, and stated that 'Maxwell Williams,' the name he gave the tobacconist, was his 'racing nom de plume.'
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: John Radley, a clerk, testified that in October he saw the prisoner with a man named Will Gill. Radley claimed the prisoner brought out some keys and said, 'I can open some offices in Chancery Lane with these keys.'
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Inspector Hine testified on cross-examination that he 'brought nine people to the Mansion House to look at you in connection with the forgery charge. None of them identified you.'

public.game_background: Philip Sullivan was charged with feloniously possessing a mould for making counterfeit half-crowns and unlawfully possessing counterfeit coins. The prosecution alleged Sullivan planted the coining equipment at the home of an acquaintance, Albert Kemp, while the defense contended that Kemp was the true coiner and Sullivan was framed after attempting to act as a police informant.
public.state_space: State: Guilty vs Innocent. Philip either committed the crime (guilty state) or is wrongly accused (innocent state).
public.prior_belief: Prior: Equal probability of guilt/innocence before evidence examination. Presumption of innocence requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
public.action_space: Court: Find guilty (evidence proves guilt) vs not guilty (insufficient proof for conviction).
public.sender_utility: Prosecution utility: Highest for convicting guilty defendants properly, positive for proper procedures, negative for wrongful convictions or dismissed cases due to improper methods.
public.receiver_utility: Court utility: Maximum for correct convictions and protecting innocents, positive for maintaining legal standards, negative for wrongful convictions or procedural failures.

information:
[EVIDENCE ev4]
Description: Detective-sergeant Goodwillie testified that after the first arrest, Sullivan offered to help arrest the 'maker of the coins,' a man named Kemp in Hoxton, in exchange for being released from the charge of possessing counterfeit coins.
[EVIDENCE ev5]
Description: Goodwillie stated that after raiding Kemp's flat based on Sullivan's tip, he arrested Sullivan, who was waiting outside. Sullivan pointed at Kemp and said, 'I did not make them; he made them.'
[EVIDENCE ev6]
Description: Upon searching Sullivan after his second arrest, Goodwillie found two very bright, good half-crowns dated 1912, kept separate from his other money. These coins appeared suitable for use as patterns for coining moulds.
[EVIDENCE ev11]
Description: Charles More, the rent collector, testified that he called on the Kemps on February 11. He stated that he did not see Sullivan, but admitted that he stood at the door and someone could have been in the room without him noticing.
[EVIDENCE ev12]
Description: Detective Goodwillie recalled that in an earlier statement, Kemp had said he would 'tell the police that a man named Sullivan left them here.' This contradicted Kemp's trial testimony that he did not know Sullivan's name at the time.
[EVIDENCE ev13]
Description: Sullivan testified that he had known Kemp for months and on February 11, he witnessed Kemp making counterfeit coins. He claimed Kemp gave him three of the coins to pass, which were the ones later found by the police.
[EVIDENCE ev16]
Description: Sidney Smith, an assayer from the Royal Mint, testified that the coining moulds were made from one of the genuine half-crowns found on Sullivan. He also confirmed that the coins from Kemp's flat and one of the mutilated coins were made from these moulds.
[EVIDENCE ev17]
Description: William Reading, a barman, recalled seeing Sullivan and Lipman together in his pub, the 'Railway Tavern,' on February 12 between 12 and 1 p.m., which partially supports Sullivan's alibi.
[EVIDENCE ev18]
Description: Kemp claimed that in the cells, Sullivan urged him to tell his wife to lie about who brought the parcel, saying it was a man 'very much like me,' and bragged about having 'bluffed' the court in a past case.
